topics.title: Data Communications Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Data Communications Model # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is the Data Communication Model? ### 📌 Formal Academic Definition The **Data Communication Model** is an abstract framework that defines the structured interaction between devices (or processes) that exchange information across a transmission medium. It identifies the **five essential components** of any communication system — **Source, Transmitter, Transmission System, Receiver, and Destination** — and the **data flow direction** governing the exchange. This model is foundational to all modern computer networking architectures (TCP/IP, OSI). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > *"Data communication model – Components, Data flow (Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex), Network categories (LAN, MAN, WAN), Protocols, Standards, Reference models (OSI, TCP/IP)."* --- ### 🍕 Real-World Analogy: The Pizza Delivery System Imagine you order a pizza over the phone: | Step | Real World | Data Communication Model | |------|-----------|--------------------------| | 1 | Your brain thinks "I want pizza" | **Source** (generates data) | | 2 | Your mouth forms words | **Transmitter** (converts to transmittable signal) | | 3 | The phone line + towers | **Transmission System** (medium) | | 4 | The restaurant's ears hear you | **Receiver** (converts signal back to data) | | 5 | The chef receives the order | **Destination** (consumes data) | > [!NOTE] > Just like a phone call can be **one-way** (a podcast 📻), **two-way alternating** (a walkie-talkie 📞), or **two-way simultaneous** (a video call 📹), data communication has three flow modes. --- ### 1.2 The Five Core Components > [!NOTE] > **The 5 Pillars of Data Communication (Memorize this for KTU exams!):** 1. **Source** — Originates the data (e.g., a computer, sensor, smartphone). 2. **Transmitter** — Encodes/converts data into a transmittable signal (e.g., modem, NIC). 3. **Transmission System (Medium)** — Carries the signal (cable, fiber, air). 4. **Receiver** — Decodes the received signal back into usable data. 5. **Destination** — The final recipient that consumes the data. ### 1.3 Data Flow Directions | Flow Mode | Direction | Real-World Analogy | Bandwidth Use | |-----------|-----------|-------------------|---------------| | **Simplex** | One-way only | TV broadcast 📺 | Unidirectional | | **Half-Duplex** | Both ways, but **not simultaneously** | Walkie-Talkie 🎙️ | Alternating | | **Full-Duplex** | Both ways **simultaneously** | Mobile phone call 📱 | Bidirectional parallel | # 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Component Breakdown ### 🔹 2.1.1 The Source (Data Originator) - Generates the **raw information** to be communicated: text, audio, video, or sensor values. - This data is often **analog** (continuous) or **digital** (discrete). - Examples: a keyboard keystroke, a temperature sensor reading, a video camera feed. ### 🔹 2.1.2 The Transmitter (Signal Encoder) - Converts source data into a **form suitable for the transmission medium**. - Performs critical functions: **modulation**, **encoding**, **source coding**, and **encryption**. - Example: A modem modulates digital bits into analog tones for a telephone line. ### 🔹 2.1.3 The Transmission System (Channel / Medium) - The physical path that carries the signal. - Can be **guided** (twisted-pair copper, coaxial cable, optical fiber) or **unguided** (radio waves, microwaves, infrared, satellite). > [!IMPORTANT] > The medium **introduces impairments** that the transmitter and receiver must compensate for: > - **Attenuation** (signal loss) > - **Distortion** (shape change) > - **Noise** (unwanted interference) ### 🔹 2.1.4 The Receiver (Signal Decoder) - Performs the **inverse** of the transmitter's operations. - Reconstructs the original data and passes it to the destination. - Handles **error detection/correction** and **synchronization**. ### 🔹 2.1.5 The Destination (Data Sink) - The end device that **consumes** or **stores** the data. - Examples: a user's monitor display, a database server, a printer. --- ## 2.2 Network Categories (Geographic Scope) | Category | Full Name | Geographic Span | Typical Speed | Example | |----------|-----------|-----------------|---------------|---------| | **LAN** | Local Area Network | Building / Campus | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps | Office network, college lab | | **MAN** | Metropolitan Area Network | City-wide | 10 Mbps – 1 Gbps | Cable TV network in a city | | **WAN** | Wide Area Network | Country / Global | 1.5 Mbps – 100 Gbps | Internet, bank ATM networks | > [!NOTE] > A **PAN (Personal Area Network)** and **WLAN (Wireless LAN)** are also important variants — expect short-note questions on these! --- ## 2.3 Protocols, Standards & Reference Models - **Protocol:** A formal set of **rules** governing data exchange (syntax, semantics, timing, error handling). - **Standard:** A documented, agreed-upon specification (e.g., **IEEE 802.3** for Ethernet, **ITU-T G.711** for voice). - **Standards Bodies:** **ISO**, **ITU-T**, **IEEE**, **EIA/TIA**, **IETF**, **ANSI**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **OSI Model — 7 Layers (Open Systems Interconnection):** > 7. **Application** | 6. **Presentation** | 5. **Session** | 4. **Transport** | 3. **Network** | 2. **Data Link** | 1. **Physical** > > **Mnemonic:** *"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"* > [!IMPORTANT] > **TCP/IP Model — 4 Layers:** > 4. **Application** | 3. **Transport** | 2. **Internet** | 1. **Network Access (Link + Physical)** --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Communication Performance Metrics) | Metric | Formula | Description | Unit | |--------|---------|-------------|------| | **Data Rate (N)** | $N = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | Number of bits per second | **bps** | | **Baud Rate (D)** | $D = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Number of signal units per second | **Baud** | | **Relationship** | $N = D \times \log_{2}(M)$ | $M$ = number of signal levels | **bps** | | **Channel Capacity (Nyquist)** | $C = 2B \log_{2}(M)$ | Noise-free theoretical maximum | **bps** | | **Shannon Capacity** | $C = B \log_{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Noisy channel upper bound | **bps** | | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = B \times t_{prop}$ | Bits "in flight" in the channel | **bits** | | **Propagation Delay** | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Time for signal to traverse the medium | **seconds** | | **Transmission Delay** | $t_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Time to push all bits onto wire | **seconds** | | **Total Delay** | $t_{total} = t_{prop} + t_{trans} + t_{queue} + t_{proc}$ | End-to-end latency | **seconds** | > [!CAUTION] > In any KTU numerical, **always convert units consistently**: > - Bandwidth $B$ → **Hz** > - Data rate → **bps** > - Delays → **seconds** > - Distance $d$ → **meters**, velocity $v$ → **m/s** (for fiber $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s) # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Bit Rate, Baud Rate & Signal Levels We start from the fundamental definitions and derive the canonical relationship. **Step 1 — Define the bit duration.** Let $T_b$ be the time taken to transmit one bit. Then the bit rate is: $$N = \frac{1}{T_b}$$ **Step 2 — Define the signal unit (symbol) duration.** A signal unit (symbol) is the smallest distinguishable change in the signal. Let $T_s$ be its duration. The baud rate is: $$D = \frac{1}{T_s}$$ **Step 3 — Relate bits to signal levels.** If $M$ distinct signal levels are used, the number of bits encoded per symbol is: $$\text{bits per symbol} = \log_{2}(M)$$ This is because with $M$ distinct symbols, we can represent $M$ unique combinations, and the information per symbol in bits is $\log_2(M)$. **Step 4 — Combine the relationships.** The bit rate equals the symbol rate times bits per symbol: $$N = D \times \log_{2}(M)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** If $M = 2$ (binary signaling like NRZ), then $\log_2(M) = 1$, so $N = D$. **Baud rate = Bit rate** in binary systems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Nyquist Bit Rate (Noise-Free Channel) **Step 1 — Nyquist's Sampling Insight.** A noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz can support at most $2B$ independent signal changes per second. This is because a signal of bandwidth $B$ has $2B$ degrees of freedom per second (per Nyquist's theorem on signal space). **Step 2 — Combine with multi-level signaling.** If each symbol carries $\log_2(M)$ bits, the maximum bit rate is: $$C = 2B \log_{2}(M) \quad \text{(bps)}$$ **Step 3 — Worked Example.** A channel of $B = 3000$ Hz uses $M = 4$ signal levels. Compute the maximum bit rate. $$C = 2 \times 3000 \times \log_{2}(4)$$ $$C = 2 \times 3000 \times 2 = 12000 \text{ bps} = 12 \text{ kbps}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Shannon Capacity (Noisy Channel) **Step 1 — Start with signal-to-noise ratio.** $S/N$ is a dimensionless ratio (often expressed in dB via $(S/N)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$). **Step 2 — Shannon's Law.** The maximum achievable error-free data rate over a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz is: $$C = B \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{(bps)}$$ **Step 3 — Worked Example.** A channel of $B = 3000$ Hz has $S/N = 1000$ (i.e., 30 dB). Find the maximum data rate. $$C = 3000 \times \log_{2}(1 + 1000)$$ $$C = 3000 \times \log_{2}(1001)$$ Since $\log_2(1001) \approx 9.97$: $$C \approx 3000 \times 9.97 \approx 29910 \text{ bps} \approx 29.9 \text{ kbps}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Shannon's capacity is a **theoretical upper bound** — no coding scheme can exceed it. Notice the capacity depends only on $B$ and $S/N$, **not** on $M$ (signal levels). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Use-Case Selection | Scenario | Best Formula | Why? | |----------|-------------|------| | Noise-free, multi-level signaling | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | Nyquist — captures M-ary benefit | | Noisy channel, error-free limit | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Shannon — captures SNR limit | | Convert SNR dB to linear | $S/N = 10^{S/N_{dB}/10}$ | Standard unit conversion | | Find signal levels for target rate | $M = 2^{C/(2B)}$ | Inverse of Nyquist | | End-to-end latency | $t_{total} = t_{prop} + t_{trans} + t_{queue} + t_{proc}$ | Sum of all delay components | | Bits "in flight" (pipelining) | $\text{Pipeline bits} = B \times t_{prop}$ | Important for Stop-and-Wait ARQ analysis | --- ## 3.5 Composite Numerical Example (Full Marks Problem) **Problem:** A communication channel has bandwidth $B = 4000$ Hz, uses $M = 16$ signal levels, and operates at an SNR of 24 dB. Find: (a) The Nyquist maximum data rate. (b) The Shannon capacity. (c) Which one is the true limit, and how many signal levels would be *theoretically* required to achieve it? **(a) Nyquist rate:** $$C_{Nyq} = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(16)$$ $$C_{Nyq} = 8000 \times 4 = 32000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}$$ **(b) Shannon capacity:** First, convert 24 dB to linear: $S/N = 10^{24/10} = 10^{2.4} \approx 251.19$. $$C_{Shan} = 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 251.19)$$ $$C_{Shan} = 4000 \times \log_2(252.19)$$ $$C_{Shan} = 4000 \times 7.98 \approx 31920 \text{ bps} \approx 31.9 \text{ kbps}$$ **(c) Analysis:** The Shannon capacity (~31.9 kbps) is the **true ceiling** — you cannot exceed this regardless of $M$. The Nyquist rate of 32 kbps is the *achievable* rate with 16 levels, and indeed $32 \text{ kbps} < 31.9 \text{ kbps}$? Wait — check: 32 > 31.9, so 16 levels actually *exceed* Shannon's bound, which is impossible. We must reduce $M$ to satisfy Shannon. Required: $C_{Nyq} \leq C_{Shan} \Rightarrow 2B \log_2(M) \leq C_{Shan}$ $$\log_2(M) \leq \frac{31920}{2 \times 4000} = 3.99$$ $$M \leq 2^{3.99} \approx 15.9$$ So the **maximum number of signal levels** is $M = 15$ (or 16, if 32 kbps ≤ 31.9 kbps were true — practical answer: **M = 15**). # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Data Communication Model (Source → Destination) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[SOURCE
Data Originator] --> B[TRANSMITTER
Encoder and Modulator] B --> C[TRANSMISSION SYSTEM
Guided or Unguided Medium] C --> D[RECEIVER
Decoder and Demodulator] D --> E[DESTINATION
Data Sink] style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1 style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C style C fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#311B92 style D fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20 style E fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#880E4F ``` > [!NOTE] > The **arrows** represent the direction of information flow. The transmitter-receiver pair compensates for the **impairments** introduced by the transmission system. --- ## 4.2 Data Flow Direction Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SIMPLEX["SIMPLEX (One-Way)"] S1[Device A] -->|Data flows only here| S2[Device B] end subgraph HALFDUP["HALF-DUPLEX (Two-Way Alternating)"] H1[Device X] <-->|At any one time, only one direction| H2[Device Y] end subgraph FULLDUP["FULL-DUPLEX (Two-Way Simultaneous)"] F1[Node P] <-->|Both directions at the same time| F2[Node Q] end style S1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C style S2 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C style H1 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17 style H2 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17 style F1 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20 style F2 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20 ``` --- ## 4.3 OSI 7-Layer vs. TCP/IP 4-Layer Reference Model ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OSI["OSI 7-LAYER MODEL"] L7[L7 APPLICATION] L6[L6 PRESENTATION] L5[L5 SESSION] L4[L4 TRANSPORT] L3[L3 NETWORK] L2[L2 DATA LINK] L1[L1 PHYSICAL] L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 end subgraph TCPIP["TCP/IP 4-LAYER MODEL"] T4[T4 APPLICATION HTTP DNS SMTP] T3[T3 TRANSPORT TCP UDP] T2[T2 INTERNET IP ICMP ARP] T1[T1 NETWORK ACCESS Ethernet WiFi] T4 --> T3 --> T2 --> T1 end L7 -.- T4 L6 -.- T4 L5 -.- T4 L4 -.- T3 L3 -.- T2 L2 -.- T1 L1 -.- T1 style L7 fill:#E1F5FE style L6 fill:#E1F5FE style L5 fill:#E1F5FE style L4 fill:#FFF3E0 style L3 fill:#F3E5F5 style L2 fill:#E8F5E9 style L1 fill:#FFEBEE style T4 fill:#E1F5FE style T3 fill:#FFF3E0 style T2 fill:#F3E5F5 style T1 fill:#E8F5E9 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **dotted lines** between the two stacks show how multiple OSI layers map to a single TCP/IP layer. The TCP/IP model is **implementation-oriented**; the OSI model is **conceptual/reference**. --- ## 4.4 Network Category Hierarchy (Geographic Scope) ```mermaid flowchart TB PAN[PAN - Personal Area Network] -->|Smallest| LAN LAN[LAN - Local Area Network] -->|Campus-wide| MAN MAN[MAN - Metropolitan Area Network] -->|City-wide| WAN WAN[WAN - Wide Area Network] -->|Global| INTERNET[INTERNET - Global Backbone] style PAN fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064 style LAN fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20 style MAN fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style WAN fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C style INTERNET fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C ``` --- ## 4.5 Protocol Functionality Mapping Across Layers ```mermaid flowchart LR APP[Application Layer Protocols - HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS] --> ENC[Data Encoding and Encryption] ENC --> SEG[Segmentation and Reassembly] SEG --> PKT[Packetization and Routing] PKT --> FRM[Framing and MAC Addressing] FRM --> SIG[Bit Transmission as Electrical or Optical Signals] style APP fill:#E1F5FE style ENC fill:#E1F5FE style SEG fill:#FFF3E0 style PKT fill:#F3E5F5 style FRM fill:#E8F5E9 style SIG fill:#FFEBEE ``` > [!NOTE] > Each block represents a **layer's primary function** as data flows from the application at the source down to the physical medium, traverses it, and is reassembled at the destination. # 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the three modes of data flow in a communication system with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** Data flow defines the **direction** in which information can travel between two communicating devices. The three modes are: 1. **Simplex Mode:** Communication is **unidirectional** — the sender can only transmit, and the receiver can only receive. The channel's entire capacity is used in one direction. - *Example:* Television broadcast, radio transmission, keyboard-to-CPU data transfer. 2. **Half-Duplex Mode:** Both stations can transmit and receive, **but not simultaneously**. The entire channel capacity is used by whichever device is transmitting at that instant. - *Example:* Walkie-talkie communication, CB radio, old Ethernet using a shared bus. 3. **Full-Duplex Mode:** Both stations can transmit and receive **simultaneously**. The channel capacity is shared between both directions, often using two physical channels or frequency/time-division separation. - *Example:* Mobile phone calls, modern switched Ethernet, fiber-optic links. > [!TIP] > For full marks, always **draw a small sketch** showing arrows in each mode and quote one real-world example. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between LAN, MAN, and WAN.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | LAN | MAN | WAN | |-----------|-----|-----|-----| | **Geographic Scope** | Building / Campus (≤ few km) | City-wide (≈ 10–100 km) | Country / Global (> 100 km) | | **Ownership** | Private (single org) | Private or Public | Typically public / leased | | **Data Rate** | High (100 Mbps – 10 Gbps) | Moderate (10 Mbps – 1 Gbps) | Lower (1.5 Mbps – 100 Gbps) | | **Error Rate** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | | **Example** | College lab network | Cable TV network in a city | Internet, bank ATM network | --- ## 5.2 📚 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **data communication model** in detail. Describe the function of each block. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The data communication model consists of **five sequential components** that describe how data moves from a source to a destination: **(i) Source:** This is the originator of the data. It generates the information to be communicated, such as text typed on a keyboard, audio captured by a microphone, or video from a camera. The source produces data that is typically not directly transmittable over the medium. **(ii) Transmitter:** The transmitter converts the source's data into a signal that can traverse the transmission medium. It performs functions like modulation, source encoding, line coding, and encryption. For example, a modem converts digital bits into analog tones for a telephone line. **(iii) Transmission System (Channel/Medium):** This is the physical path between transmitter and receiver. It can be **guided** (twisted pair, coaxial cable, fiber-optic) or **unguided** (air for radio, microwave, satellite). The channel introduces impairments like attenuation, distortion, and noise. **(iv) Receiver:** The receiver performs the **inverse** of the transmitter's operations. It accepts the incoming signal from the medium, decodes it, and reconstructs the original data. It also performs error detection and correction. **(v) Destination:** The destination is the recipient of the data — it may be a display device, a storage device, or another computer that consumes the information. > [!NOTE] > *For full marks, also mention the role of **protocols** as the "rule-book" that synchronizes all five blocks between sender and receiver.* **Q. (b)** A noiseless channel has a bandwidth of **$B = 8$ kHz** and uses **$M = 16$** signal levels. Compute: (i) The maximum data rate using Nyquist's criterion. (ii) The bit duration. (iii) The minimum bandwidth required to transmit a $1$ Mbps signal using 8 signal levels. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **(i) Nyquist Maximum Data Rate:** $$C = 2B \log_2(M) = 2 \times 8000 \times \log_2(16)$$ $$C = 16000 \times 4 = 64000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$ **(ii) Bit Duration:** $$T_b = \frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{64000} = 15.625 \text{ μs}$$ **(iii) Minimum Bandwidth for 1 Mbps with M = 8:** Using Nyquist's formula rearranged: $$B = \frac{C}{2 \log_2(M)} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{2 \times 3} \approx 166667 \text{ Hz} \approx 166.67 \text{ kHz}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** **Q. (a)** Explain the **OSI reference model** in detail. State the functions of each layer. Compare it with the **TCP/IP model**. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model is a 7-layer conceptual framework defined by **ISO** to standardize network communication. Each layer performs specific functions and communicates with the layers above and below it. | Layer | Function | Example Protocols | |-------|----------|-------------------| | **7. Application** | Interface to user, network services | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS | | **6. Presentation** | Data translation, encryption, compression | SSL/TLS, JPEG, ASCII | | **5. Session** | Establishes, manages, terminates sessions | NetBIOS, RPC | | **4. Transport** | Reliable end-to-end delivery, segmentation | TCP, UDP | | **3. Network** | Logical addressing and routing | IP, ICMP, OSPF | | **2. Data Link** | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | Ethernet, PPP, Wi-Fi (802.11) | | **1. Physical** | Bit-level transmission over medium | Cables, connectors, voltages | **Comparison with TCP/IP (4 layers):** - OSI is a **7-layer theoretical reference**; TCP/IP is a **4-layer practical implementation**. - OSI separates session and presentation; TCP/IP combines them into "Application." - OSI separates data link and physical; TCP/IP combines them into "Network Access." - The TCP/IP model is the **foundation of the modern Internet**. **Q. (b)** A communication channel operates at a bandwidth of **$B = 4$ kHz** with a signal-to-noise ratio of **$30$ dB**. Calculate: (i) The Shannon channel capacity. (ii) The maximum data rate achievable using Nyquist's criterion with **$M = 4$** signal levels. (iii) Comment on which limit governs the system. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **(i) Shannon Capacity:** Convert SNR to linear: $S/N = 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000$. $$C_{Shannon} = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 4000 \times \log_2(1001)$$ $$C_{Shannon} = 4000 \times 9.97 \approx 39880 \text{ bps} \approx 39.88 \text{ kbps}$$ **(ii) Nyquist Data Rate with M = 4:** $$C_{Nyquist} = 2B \log_2(M) = 2 \times 4000 \times 2 = 16000 \text{ bps} = 16 \text{ kbps}$$ **(iii) Analysis:** Since $C_{Nyquist} = 16 \text{ kbps} < C_{Shannon} \approx 39.88 \text{ kbps}$, the achievable rate is governed by the **Nyquist limit (16 kbps)**. Shannon sets the upper bound (~39.88 kbps) — this is the *theoretical maximum*, but reaching it would require ideal coding and a very large $M$. With $M = 4$, the practical limit is the Nyquist rate. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear** for $S/N$ — this is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **Mixing up Nyquist and Shannon** — Nyquist applies to **noise-free** multi-level channels; Shannon applies to **noisy** channels. Always check the wording! > 3. **Using wrong logarithm base** — Shannon and Nyquist both use $\log_2$ (binary), not $\log_{10}$. --- ## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > ### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — *Read this 5 minutes before the exam!* - ✅ **5 Components:** Source → Transmitter → Transmission System → Receiver → Destination - ✅ **3 Data Flow Modes:** Simplex (one-way), Half-Duplex (alternating), Full-Duplex (simultaneous) - ✅ **Network Categories:** LAN (≤ 10 km, high speed) | MAN (city) | WAN (global) - ✅ **OSI Layers (Top→Bottom):** Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical - ✅ **TCP/IP Layers:** Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access - ✅ **Key Formulae:** - $N = D \log_2(M)$ - $C_{Nyquist} = 2B \log_2(M)$ (noise-free) - $C_{Shannon} = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ (noisy) - ✅ **Protocols** are rules; **Standards** are agreed-upon implementations of those rules (IEEE, ISO, ITU-T) - ✅ **dB Conversion:** $\text{Linear} = 10^{dB/10}$ for power ratios - ✅ Always quote **units**: bps, Hz, seconds, meters > [!TIP] > **One-line exam mnemonic for flow modes:** > *"**S**implex = **S**ingle direction (TV), **H**alf-duplex = **H**alf the time two-way (Walkie-Talkie), **F**ull-duplex = **F**reely both ways (Phone)."*

topics.title: Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Data Communication Network?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Data Communication Network** is a collection of interconnected autonomous computing devices (nodes/hosts) that exchange information and share resources using a common set of communication rules known as **Protocols**, mediated through a transmission medium (wired or wireless).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Highway System 🚗

Imagine Kerala's National Highway system:
- **Cities (Nodes)** = Computers, servers, smartphones, IoT devices.
- **Highways (Transmission Media)** = Cables (copper, fiber) or air (wireless).
- **Traffic Rules (Protocols)** = TCP/IP, HTTP, Ethernet rules that govern "who goes first," "how to overtake," "what to do in an accident."
- **Vehicles carrying goods (Data Packets)** = Information being transferred.

Just as goods cannot move from Thiruvananthapuram to Kannur without highways and traffic rules, **digital data cannot move from one device to another without a network and protocols**.

---

## 1.2 The Five Fundamental Network Criteria (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A network is considered **effective and reliable** if and only if it satisfies these **five performance criteria**, frequently asked in KTU ESE Part A (3-mark questions):

| # | Criterion | Definition | Engineering Unit |
|---|-----------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Throughput / Performance** | Volume of data successfully delivered per unit time | **Bits per second (bps / Mbps / Gbps)** |
| 2 | **Reliability** | Measure of system dependability measured by **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | **Hours / Years** |
| 3 | **Security** | Protection against unauthorized access, data tampering, and DoS attacks | Encrypted bits / Authentication tokens |
| 4 | **Scalability** | Ability of the network to gracefully handle growing users/data | Number of nodes supported |
| 5 | **Quality of Service (QoS)** | Guarantees on delay, jitter, and packet loss for real-time traffic | **ms latency / % packet loss** |

---

## 1.3 Classification of Networks by Geographic Scale

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates the study of network classification. Memorize the **range, ownership, and typical use-case** of each.

### (a) Personal Area Network (PAN)
- **Range:** ~1–10 meters (body/small room)
- **Example:** Bluetooth headset, smartwatch, wireless USB
- **Use Case:** Connecting personal devices

### (b) Local Area Network (LAN)
- **Range:** A single building, office, or campus (≤ 10 km)
- **Ownership:** Private (single organization)
- **Example:** Office Ethernet, College Wi-Fi
- **Speed:** **10 Mbps – 100 Gbps**
- **Protocols:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)

### (c) Metropolitan Area Network (MAN)
- **Range:** A city (10–100 km)
- **Example:** Cable TV network, city-wide broadband, BSNL MAN
- **Standard:** **IEEE 802.16 (WiMAX)**

### (d) Wide Area Network (WAN)
- **Range:** Countries, continents (100 km – global)
- **Ownership:** Public / Service Providers (ISPs)
- **Example:** The Internet itself, BSNL/Reliance Jio backbone
- **Speed:** **1.5 Mbps – 100 Gbps**

### (e) The Internet — Network of Networks
- A global, public WAN interconnecting millions of private, public, academic, and government networks.

---

## 1.4 Network Topologies — The "Layout DNA"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topology** is the geometric arrangement of nodes and links. KTU often asks to **draw and compare** mesh, star, bus, and ring topologies.

| Topology | Structure | Failure Behavior | Cost |
|----------|-----------|------------------|------|
| **Mesh** | Every node connects to every other node | Highly fault-tolerant | Very High (n(n-1)/2 links) |
| **Star** | All nodes connect to a central hub/switch | Hub failure = total collapse | Moderate |
| **Bus** | All nodes share a single backbone cable | Single break = total collapse | Low |
| **Ring** | Each node connects to two neighbors forming a circle | Dual-ring (FDDI) provides redundancy | Moderate |
| **Hybrid** | Combination of two or more topologies | Depends on sub-topologies | Variable |
| **Tree** | Hierarchical star arrangement | Failure at root = total collapse | Moderate |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **Physical Topology** (actual cable layout) with **Logical Topology** (the way data flows). Example: A **physically star-wired** Ethernet network still operates as a **logical bus** because all signals share the same collision domain.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network Architecture Models — Why Layering?

> [!NOTE]
> Modern networks use a **layered architecture** to break the complex task of communication into manageable sub-tasks. Each layer offers **services to the layer above it** and **requests services from the layer below it**.

### Why is Layering Essential?
1. **Modularity** — Each layer can be designed and upgraded independently (e.g., changing HTTP/2 to HTTP/3 does not require changing the physical cable).
2. **Reusability** — A protocol at one layer works regardless of the technology in other layers.
3. **Standardization** — Vendors can interoperate because they follow the same interface contract.
4. **Easier Debugging & Troubleshooting** — Issues can be isolated to a specific layer (e.g., Wireshark uses layer numbers).

---

## 2.2 Switching Techniques — The Heart of Data Flow

A network must move data from source to destination using one of these three fundamental switching techniques.

### (A) Circuit Switching
- A **dedicated physical path** is established **before** data transfer (call setup phase).
- All data flows through this reserved circuit.
- **Analogy:** Traditional landline telephone call — once connected, the line is yours exclusively.
- **Three phases:** Connection Establishment → Data Transfer → Connection Release.
- **Inefficient for bursty data** because the channel is reserved but idle between bursts.

### (B) Packet Switching
- Data is broken into **variable-sized packets**, each carrying source & destination addresses.
- **Two variants:**
  - **Datagram (Connectionless)** — Each packet is routed independently. Example: **IP / UDP**.
  - **Virtual Circuit (Connection-Oriented)** — A logical path is set up first. Example: **X.25, Frame Relay, ATM**.

### (C) Message Switching
- The **entire message** is stored at intermediate nodes before forwarding (Store-and-Forward).
- Inefficient and slow — rarely used today.
- **Analogy:** Postal system — a post office holds the full letter before passing it on.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Network Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas are **mandatory** for KTU ESE problems. Memorize every symbol and unit.

| # | Metric | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|--------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Transmission (Serialization) Delay** | $d_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds (s) | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link bandwidth (bps) |
| 2 | **Propagation Delay** | $d_p = \dfrac{D}{s}$ | seconds (s) | $D$ = physical length of link (m), $s$ = propagation speed (m/s) |
| 3 | **Processing Delay** | $d_{pr}$ | seconds (s) | Time routers spend examining headers (usually $\mu$s) |
| 4 | **Queuing Delay** | $d_q$ | seconds (s) | Variable; depends on traffic intensity |
| 5 | **End-to-End (Total) Delay** | $d_{end} = d_{pr} + d_q + d_t + d_p$ | seconds (s) | Sum across all nodes in path |
| 6 | **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n)$ | bps | **Bottleneck link** determines throughput |
| 7 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = R \times d_p$ | bits | Maximum data that can be "in-flight" in the pipe |
| 8 | **Packet Loss Probability** | $P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - (1-q)^N$ | dimensionless | $q$ = per-hop loss probability, $N$ = number of hops |
| 9 | **Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\rho}{1+\rho}$ where $\rho = \dfrac{La}{R}$ | ratio (0–1) | From Kleinrock's queuing model |
| 10 | **Total Mesh Links** | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | links | For $n$ nodes in full mesh |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse **Transmission Delay** ($L/R$) with **Propagation Delay** ($D/s$). Transmission is the time to *push all bits* onto the wire; propagation is the time for *one bit* to travel from one end of the wire to the other.

---

## 2.4 Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless Services

| Parameter | Connection-Oriented (CO) | Connectionless (CL) |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Handshake | Required (3-way) | Not required |
| Example | TCP, ATM, Frame Relay | UDP, IP, SMTP |
| Reliability | High — retransmission on error | Low — best-effort |
| Order of packets | Preserved | Not guaranteed |
| Use case | File transfer, Email, Web | Live video, VoIP, DNS |
| Overhead | Higher (state info stored) | Lower (no state) |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently frames this as **"Compare connection-oriented and connectionless services"** for 7-mark questions. Always end your answer with a **"Conclusion" line** stating that modern networks use **both paradigms together** (e.g., IP is connectionless, but TCP over IP is connection-oriented).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISP Backbone Design:** Routers in Tier-1 ISPs use packet switching for efficiency; circuit switching is reserved for legacy leased-line customers.
- **5G Networks:** Use hybrid switching — slicing allows some flows to behave like circuit-switched (URLLC) while others are packet-switched (eMBB).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Star topology with edge gateways dominates factory floors for deterministic latency.
- **Satellite Networks:** Heavy use of **propagation delay** calculations ($s = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) due to ~36,000 km GEO orbits.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: End-to-End Delay Across N Routers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked numerical problem** in KTU Module 1. A complete, step-by-step derivation follows.

### Problem Setup
A packet of length **L bits** travels from source to destination through **N routers** (i.e., N+1 links) on a path of total physical length **D meters**. The link bandwidth is **R bps**, propagation speed is **s m/s**, and each router introduces a fixed processing delay of **d_pr seconds** plus a queuing delay **d_q seconds**.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the **end-to-end delay** as the sum of all four delay components at every node and link.

$$
d_{end} = \sum_{i=1}^{N+1}(d_{pr,i} + d_{q,i} + d_{t,i} + d_{p,i})
$$

*Conversion logic:* We sum delays for each of the (N+1) links and the N intermediate routers.

**Step 2:** Assume **homogeneous** links — all bandwidths equal to R, all propagation speeds equal to s, and equal link lengths.

$$
d_{end} = (N+1)\cdot d_t + (N+1)\cdot d_p + N \cdot d_{pr} + N \cdot d_q
$$

*Conversion logic:* Homogeneity lets us factor the link terms.

**Step 3:** Substitute the **transmission delay formula** $d_t = L / R$.

$$
d_{end} = (N+1)\cdot \frac{L}{R} + (N+1)\cdot d_p + N(d_{pr} + d_q)
$$

**Step 4:** If each individual link has length $D_i$, then total length $D = \sum D_i$ and per-link propagation delay is $d_p = D_i / s$.

$$
d_{end} = (N+1)\cdot \frac{L}{R} + \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N+1} D_i}{s} + N(d_{pr} + d_q)
$$

**Step 5:** Simplify by noting that $\sum D_i$ equals the **total path length D**.

$$
\boxed{\,d_{end} = (N+1)\cdot \frac{L}{R} + \frac{D}{s} + N(d_{pr} + d_q)\,}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for end-to-end packet delay. Every numerical problem reduces to plugging values into this equation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1 — Delay Calculation

> **Problem:** A host sends a packet of **1500 bytes** through **3 routers** to a destination. Each link has a bandwidth of **2 Mbps**, propagation speed of $2 \times 10^8$ m/s, and physical length of **1000 km**. Processing delay per router is **3 ms**; queuing delay is **negligible**. Compute the end-to-end delay.

**Step 1:** Convert units.
- Packet length: $L = 1500 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 12000 \text{ bits}$
- Bandwidth: $R = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$
- Per-link length: $D_i = 1000 \text{ km} = 10^6 \text{ m}$
- Speed: $s = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- $N = 3$ routers, so $N+1 = 4$ links

**Step 2:** Compute per-link transmission delay.

$$
d_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000}{2 \times 10^6} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Each link must serialize the full packet; since all links have the same bandwidth, this is the same everywhere.

**Step 3:** Compute per-link propagation delay.

$$
d_p = \frac{D_i}{s} = \frac{10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The first bit needs 5 ms to physically reach the next node regardless of packet size.

**Step 4:** Apply the end-to-end formula with $d_q = 0$.

$$
d_{end} = (N+1)\cdot d_t + (N+1)\cdot d_p + N\cdot d_{pr} + N\cdot d_q
$$

$$
d_{end} = 4 \times 6 + 4 \times 5 + 3 \times 3 + 0
$$

$$
d_{end} = 24 + 20 + 9 + 0 = 53 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final Answer:** $d_{end} = 53$ **ms**

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2 — Throughput Bottleneck

> **Problem:** A file is transferred over a path with 3 links having bandwidths **2 Mbps, 1 Mbps, and 5 Mbps** respectively. The file size is **4 MB**. Find the throughput and total transfer time.

**Step 1:** Identify the bottleneck — the **minimum bandwidth** link.

$$
R = \min(2, 1, 5) = 1 \text{ Mbps}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Data can flow only as fast as the slowest link in the path.

**Step 2:** Convert file size.

$$
F = 4 \text{ MB} = 4 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 32 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute transfer time.

$$
T = \frac{F}{R} = \frac{32 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^6} = 32 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4:** Effective throughput = file size / transfer time.

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{32 \times 10^6}{32} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} = 1 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Final Answer:** Throughput = **1 Mbps**, Transfer Time = **32 seconds**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #3 — Bandwidth-Delay Product

> **Problem:** A link has bandwidth **100 Mbps** and one-way propagation delay of **20 ms**. How many bits can the link "hold" in transit at any moment?

**Step 1:** Identify the formula.

$$
BDP = R \times d_p
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
BDP = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply.

$$
BDP = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ Mbits}
$$

**Final Answer:** **2,000,000 bits** can be in flight at any instant. This is why the **TCP window size** must be at least 2 Mbits to fully utilize the link.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix (Use in 14-Mark Answers)

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Datagram Packet Switching | Virtual Circuit Packet Switching |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Call setup | Required | Not required | Required (logical) |
| Path | Fixed physical | Independent per packet | Fixed logical path |
| Resource reservation | Yes (dedicated) | No | Yes (logical) |
| Congestion handling | None (resources are reserved) | Best-effort | Resource reservation at setup |
| Delay variability | Constant | Variable | More predictable |
| Suitability | Voice (legacy telephone) | Data bursty traffic | Real-time multimedia (ATM) |
| Example | PSTN | Internet (IP) | X.25, ATM, Frame Relay |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Network Topologies — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Node A] --- B[Node B]
    B --- C[Node C]
    C --- D[Node D]
    D --- A
    A --- C
    B --- D
```

> **Diagram 4.1.a — Full Mesh Topology.** Every node connects to every other node. Total links = $n(n-1)/2$. Highly fault-tolerant but expensive.

```mermaid
graph TB
    H[Central Hub or Switch]
    N1[Node 1] --- H
    N2[Node 2] --- H
    N3[Node 3] --- H
    N4[Node 4] --- H
    N5[Node 5] --- H
    N6[Node 6] --- H
```

> **Diagram 4.1.b — Star Topology.** All traffic passes through a central hub. Hub failure breaks the whole network. Cheapest and easiest to install.

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1[Node 1] --- BUS((Backbone Cable))
    N2[Node 2] --- BUS
    N3[Node 3] --- BUS
    N4[Node 4] --- BUS
    N5[Node 5] --- BUS
```

> **Diagram 4.1.c — Bus Topology.** All nodes share a single backbone. A single break takes down the entire LAN. Used in legacy Ethernet (10BASE2, 10BASE5).

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1[Node 1] --> N2[Node 2]
    N2 --> N3[Node 3]
    N3 --> N4[Node 4]
    N4 --> N5[Node 5]
    N5 --> N1
```

> **Diagram 4.1.d — Ring Topology.** Each node connects to exactly two neighbors; data flows in one direction (or both in dual-ring FDDI).

```mermaid
graph TB
    R1((R1)) --> R2((R2))
    R2 --> R3((R3))
    R3 --> R4((R4))
    R4 --> R5((R5))
    R5 --> R1
    S[Source Host] --> R1
    R1 --> D[Destination Host]
```

> **Diagram 4.1.e — Hybrid Topology (Ring of Routers with attached LANs).** Real-world ISPs use this. The routers form a backbone ring; end-hosts attach in star configurations.

---

## 4.2 The OSI Reference Model — 7-Layer Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    L7[LAYER 7: APPLICATION - HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP]
    L6[LAYER 6: PRESENTATION - TLS, SSL, MIME, JPEG]
    L5[LAYER 5: SESSION - NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP]
    L4[LAYER 4: TRANSPORT - TCP, UDP]
    L3[LAYER 3: NETWORK - IP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP]
    L2[LAYER 2: DATA LINK - Ethernet, PPP, MAC]
    L1[LAYER 1: PHYSICAL - Cables, Fiber, Radio Waves]
    L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

> **Diagram 4.2 — OSI Model.** Each layer communicates with the **layer above and below** on the same device, and with the **same layer** on the remote device (peer-to-peer communication).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic (top-down):** **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing.
> **Mnemonic (bottom-up):** **P**lease **D**o **N**ot **T**hrow **S**ausage **P**izza **A**way.

---

## 4.3 The TCP/IP Model — 4-Layer Reference Model (The Real Internet)

```mermaid
graph TB
    T4[LAYER 4: APPLICATION - HTTP, DNS, FTP, SMTP, SSH]
    T3[LAYER 3: TRANSPORT - TCP, UDP]
    T2[LAYER 2: INTERNET - IP, ICMP, ARP, IGMP]
    T1[LAYER 1: NETWORK ACCESS - Ethernet, Wi-Fi, ATM, SONET]
    T4 --> T3 --> T2 --> T1
```

> **Diagram 4.3 — TCP/IP Model.** The **practical** model that the Internet actually uses. The OSI model is a **theoretical reference**; the TCP/IP model is the **implementation**.

---

## 4.4 Data Encapsulation Flow (PDU Evolution)

```mermaid
graph TB
    APP[Application Data: Hello World]
    APP --> SEG[Segment: TCP Header + Data]
    SEG --> PKT[Packet: IP Header + Segment]
    PKT --> FRM[Frame: Ethernet Header + Packet + CRC Trailer]
    FRM --> BIT[Bits: 01010100... on the physical medium]
```

> **Diagram 4.4 — Encapsulation.** As data moves **down** the stack, each layer adds its own **header** (and sometimes **trailer**). The unit of data is renamed at each stage: **Data → Segment → Packet (Datagram) → Frame → Bits**.

> [!NOTE]
> **PDU names per layer (highly exam-relevant):**
> - Layer 7/6/5/4 → **Data** (in OSI), **Segment** (at L4 in TCP/IP)
> - Layer 3 → **Packet / Datagram**
> - Layer 2 → **Frame**
> - Layer 1 → **Bits / Symbols**

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Internet Communication

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HOST_A
        A_APP[Application Layer Module]
        A_TRANS[Transport Layer Module]
        A_NET[Network Layer Module]
        A_LINK[Data Link Layer Module]
        A_PHY[Physical Layer Module]
    end
    subgraph NETWORK_CLOUD
        R1[Router 1 - Layer 3 Device]
        R2[Router 2 - Layer 3 Device]
        R3[Router 3 - Layer 3 Device]
    end
    subgraph HOST_B
        B_PHY[Physical Layer Module]
        B_LINK[Data Link Layer Module]
        B_NET[Network Layer Module]
        B_TRANS[Transport Layer Module]
        B_APP[Application Layer Module]
    end
    A_PHY ==> R1
    R1 ==> R2
    R2 ==> R3
    R3 ==> B_PHY
```

> **Diagram 4.5 — End-to-End Architecture.** A message originates at the Application layer of Host A, descends through Host A's stack, traverses the network (forwarded only at Layer 3 by routers), and ascends through Host B's stack to the destination application.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, CO1, Remember Level]`
> **Define a computer network. List the three key components required for data communication.**

**Model Answer:**
A computer network is an interconnected collection of autonomous computing devices that share resources and exchange data using a common protocol.

The **three key components** of data communication are:
1. **Sender / Source** — the device that originates the message.
2. **Receiver / Destination** — the device that receives the message.
3. **Transmission Medium / Channel** — the physical path (wired/wireless) over which the message travels.

(Additional fourth element often added: **Protocol**, the set of rules governing communication.)

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, CO1, Understand Level]`
> **Compare LAN and WAN with respect to ownership, geographic span, and typical speed.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | LAN | WAN |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Ownership | Private (single org) | Public / ISP-managed |
| Geographic span | ≤ 10 km (building/campus) | Global (countries, continents) |
| Typical speed | 100 Mbps – 100 Gbps | 1.5 Mbps – 100 Gbps |
| Examples | Office Ethernet, College Wi-Fi | The Internet, BSNL backbone |
| Cost of installation | Low | Very high |

**Conclusion:** LANs are faster and cheaper but limited in range; WANs cover vast distances at the cost of speed and ownership complexity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, CO1, Apply / Analyze Level]`
>
> **(a)** Draw and explain the **OSI Reference Model** in detail. List the functions of each layer. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Differentiate between **Circuit Switching**, **Packet Switching (Datagram)**, and **Message Switching** with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — OSI Reference Model:**

The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model is a **7-layer conceptual framework** that standardizes network communication. It was defined by the **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**.

| Layer | Name | Function | Protocol/Data Unit | Example |
|-------|------|----------|--------------------|---------|
| 7 | Application | Interface to user; network services | Data | HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP |
| 6 | Presentation | Data translation, encryption, compression | Data | TLS/SSL, JPEG, MPEG |
| 5 | Session | Establishes, manages, terminates sessions | Data | NetBIOS, RPC |
| 4 | Transport | Reliable end-to-end delivery, segmentation | Segment | TCP, UDP |
| 3 | Network | Logical addressing and routing | Packet / Datagram | IP, ICMP, OSPF |
| 2 | Data Link | Error detection, MAC addressing | Frame | Ethernet, PPP |
| 1 | Physical | Bit transmission on the medium | Bits | Cables, Fiber, Radio |

> **Working Principle:** Each layer offers services to the layer above and consumes services from the layer below. Communication is **peer-to-peer** at the same layer on different devices.

**Part (b) — Switching Techniques:**

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Datagram Packet Switching | Message Switching |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| Setup phase | Required (call setup) | Not required | Not required |
| Path | Fixed physical path | Independent per packet | Stored and forwarded |
| Delay | Constant after setup | Variable | High (store-and-wait) |
| Resource allocation | Reserved upfront | On demand | Stored at each node |
| Suitability | Voice (telephone) | Internet data | Telegraphy, email relay |
| Example | PSTN | IP networks | Telegram network |

**Diagram (Textual Representation):**
- Circuit: Source → Node1 → Node2 → Destination (continuous pipe reserved).
- Datagram: Packet1 takes one path, Packet2 takes another — routes may differ.
- Message: Source → Node1 (store) → Node2 (store) → Destination (deliver whole message).

**Conclusion:** Packet switching (especially datagram) dominates modern networks due to its efficiency, but circuit switching persists in real-time voice and leased-line scenarios.

---

> **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, CO1, Apply / Analyze Level]`
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **five network topologies** (Mesh, Star, Bus, Ring, Tree). List **two advantages and two disadvantages** of each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A packet of length **L = 2000 bytes** travels from source to destination through **N = 4 routers**. Each link has bandwidth **R = 1 Mbps** and length **D = 500 km**. Propagation speed is $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Processing delay per router is **2 ms**, and queuing delay is **1 ms**. Calculate the **end-to-end delay**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Network Topologies (Diagrams are drawn as in Section 4.1):**

**1. Mesh Topology**
- *Advantage:* Highest fault tolerance, no traffic bottlenecks, robust.
- *Disadvantage:* Very expensive (n(n-1)/2 links), complex installation.

**2. Star Topology**
- *Advantage:* Easy to install and expand, single-point failure easy to identify.
- *Disadvantage:* Central hub is a single point of failure; limited by hub capacity.

**3. Bus Topology**
- *Advantage:* Cheapest, simple for small networks.
- *Disadvantage:* Single cable break = total failure; performance degrades with heavy traffic.

**4. Ring Topology**
- *Advantage:* Predictable performance, no central node required.
- *Disadvantage:* One node failure can break the entire ring (unless dual-ring is used).

**5. Tree Topology**
- *Advantage:* Scalable, hierarchical management.
- *Disadvantage:* Root node failure affects the entire network.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (End-to-End Delay):**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.
- $L = 2000 \text{ bytes} = 16000 \text{ bits}$
- $R = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6 \text{ bps}$
- $N = 4$ routers → $N+1 = 5$ links
- Per-link length $D_i = 500 \text{ km} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ m}$
- $s = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- $d_{pr} = 2$ ms; $d_q = 1$ ms

**Step 2:** Compute transmission delay per link.

$$
d_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{16000}{10^6} = 0.016 \text{ s} = 16 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute propagation delay per link.

$$
d_p = \frac{D_i}{s} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the canonical end-to-end formula.

$$
d_{end} = (N+1)\cdot d_t + (N+1)\cdot d_p + N(d_{pr} + d_q)
$$

$$
d_{end} = 5 \times 16 + 5 \times 2.5 + 4 \times (2 + 1)
$$

$$
d_{end} = 80 + 12.5 + 12 = 104.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final Answer:** $d_{end} = \mathbf{104.5 \text{ ms}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap** — Forgetting to convert **bytes to bits** (multiply by 8) is the most common error, resulting in answers 8× too small.
> 2. **N vs N+1 confusion** — With **N routers** there are **N+1 links**. Students often use N instead of N+1, losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Per-link vs total distance** — Propagation delay is computed per link using the **per-link length**, then multiplied by (N+1). Mixing up total distance with per-link distance loses marks.
> 4. **Adding only $d_t$ and $d_p$** — Forgetting processing/queuing delays is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **No units in final answer** — Always write "ms" or "s" explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Networks:**

- ✅ **Network** = Autonomous devices + Communication media + Protocols (the "holy trinity" of networking).
- ✅ **Five Network Criteria:** Performance, Reliability, Security, Scalability, QoS — memorize verbatim.
- ✅ **Network Scale (ascending):** PAN → LAN → MAN → WAN → Internet.
- ✅ **LAN** = private, building-wide, fast; **WAN** = public, global, often slower.
- ✅ **Topologies:** Mesh (n(n-1)/2 links, fault tolerant), Star (hub-centric), Bus (single backbone), Ring (closed loop), Tree (hierarchical), Hybrid.
- ✅ **Physical vs Logical Topology** — they are NOT always the same (e.g., star-wired Ethernet = logical bus).
- ✅ **Three Switching Techniques:** Circuit (reserved path), Packet (datagram/VC), Message (store-and-forward, obsolete).
- ✅ **Packet Switching Subtypes:** Datagram (connectionless, IP) vs Virtual Circuit (connection-oriented, X.25, ATM).
- ✅ **Connection-Oriented (TCP) vs Connectionless (UDP)** — opposite tradeoffs in reliability vs speed.
- ✅ **OSI = 7 layers** (A P S T N D P mnemonic), **TCP/IP = 4 layers** (Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access).
- ✅ **Encapsulation PDU names:** Data → Segment → Packet/Datagram → Frame → Bits (top to bottom).
- ✅ **Delay Formula:** $d_{end} = (N+1)\cdot(L/R) + (N+1)\cdot(D/s) + N(d_{pr} + d_q)$.
- ✅ **Throughput** = min bandwidth along the path (bottleneck rule).
- ✅ **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** = $R \times d_p$ → determines TCP window size for full link utilization.
- ✅ **Internet** = "Network of Networks" — public, global, packet-switched, TCP/IP-based.
- ✅ **Protocol** = set of rules; **Standard** = a protocol that has been formally ratified (e.g., IEEE, IETF, ITU-T).
- ✅ **Use diagrams in 14-mark answers** — a single well-drawn Mermaid/topology diagram can earn 2–3 easy marks.
- ✅ **Always end answers with a "Conclusion" line** — KTU examiners reward holistic thinking.
- ✅ **Numerical answers MUST show every step** — partial marking demands clean, traceable work.

> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE preparation! 🎓**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Internet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Internet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Internet (KTU 2024 Definition):** The Internet is a global, decentralized, packet-switched network of networks that interconnects millions of computing devices worldwide using the standardized **TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)** protocol suite. It follows a hierarchical, hierarchical-client-server and peer-to-peer communication model governed by the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** and the **Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers (ICANN)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEITT412 — Data Communication and Networking**, Module 1 introduces "The Internet" as the foundational concept that motivates the study of computer networking. The Internet is **not** a single network; it is a *"network of networks"* — a federation of independently operated networks that agree to interoperate through a common set of protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** The **Internet** (capital I) refers to the global public network. The **intranet** is a private network accessible only within an organization. The **extranet** is a private network extended to selected external parties (e.g., business partners). Mixing these terms in an exam is a guaranteed mark-loser.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the Internet as a **worldwide postal system**, but instead of letters, we send **digital envelopes called packets**:

- **You (the client)** write a message and put it in an envelope.
- The envelope has a **destination address (IP address)** and a **return address**.
- The **postal trucks (routers)** at intersections read the address and forward the envelope along the **best-known route (routing protocol)**.
- Multiple trucks share the same road (**statistical multiplexing / packet switching**), unlike a private courier where one truck is reserved (**circuit switching**).
- The recipient's **local post office (destination host)** opens the envelope and delivers the message.

In this analogy:
- **Packet** ↔ Envelope
- **IP Address** ↔ Postal Address
- **Router** ↔ Sorting Facility
- **Bandwidth** ↔ Width of the road (number of lanes)
- **Latency** ↔ Time taken for delivery
- **TCP** ↔ Registered post with tracking (reliable)
- **UDP** ↔ Ordinary post (faster, but may get lost)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 1 explicitly expects students to articulate the **structure of the Internet (network edge, network core, access networks)** and to **distinguish between packet switching and circuit switching**. Memorize this contrast — it is the single most-tested 14-mark theme in this module.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- **Speed of light in fiber (refractive index ≈ 1.5):** $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- **Transmission rate / Bandwidth:** measured in **bits per second (bps)**, with prefixes **Kbps, Mbps, Gbps, Tbps**
- **Latency / Delay:** measured in **milliseconds (ms)** or **microseconds (μs)**
- **Standard MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) for Ethernet:** **1500 bytes**
- **Default IPv4 address length:** **32 bits**
- **Default IPv6 address length:** **128 bits**

---

## 1.4 The Internet's Two-Way Structural View (Forouzan Perspective)

The Internet, for analysis purposes, is split into two complementary halves:

| Sub-System | What It Includes | KTU-Style Function |
|------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Network Edge** | End-hosts (clients & servers), access links | Where applications run and data originates / terminates |
| **Network Core** | Routers, high-speed links, ISPs, IXPs | Where data is *forwarded* (routed + switched) between edge networks |
| **Access Networks** | DSL, Cable, FTTH, Wi-Fi, Cellular, Ethernet LAN | The "last mile" that connects edge hosts to the core |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Network Edge: Clients, Servers, and Peers

The **network edge** consists of all the end-systems (hosts) that run applications. There are two principal paradigms:

### 2.1.1 Client-Server Model
- **Client:** Initiates the connection (e.g., your laptop running a browser).
- **Server:** Always-on host that waits for and services requests (e.g., google.com at IP 142.250.x.x).
- Data centers housing **hyperscale servers** serve modern cloud applications (AWS, Azure, GCP).

### 2.1.2 Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Model
- Hosts (peers) act as both **clients and servers** simultaneously.
- Minimal (or no) dedicated infrastructure.
- Examples: BitTorrent (legacy), blockchain networks, modern WebRTC video calls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters at KTU:** You may be asked to "compare client-server vs P2P architectures." A high-scoring answer must mention: *scalability, reliability, server cost, peer churn, and self-scalability* as the comparison axes.

## 2.2 Access Networks — The "Last Mile"

Access networks connect the end-host to the first router on the path to the wider Internet. The KTU 2024 syllabus names these:

### 2.2.1 Digital Subscriber Line (DSL)
- Uses **existing telephone line**; frequency-division multiplexes voice + upstream + downstream.
- **Upstream rate:** typically **128 kbps – 1.5 Mbps**
- **Downstream rate:** typically **1 Mbps – 24 Mbps**
- **Limitation:** Distance-sensitive; performance degrades beyond ~5 km from the DSLAM (Digital Subscriber Line Access Multiplexer).

### 2.2.2 Cable Internet (Hybrid Fiber-Coax, HFC)
- Uses **coaxial cable TV infrastructure**.
- **Shared broadcast medium** → all users in a neighborhood share the access link.
- **Asymmetric:** Downstream up to ~1 Gbps, upstream typically 30–200 Mbps.

### 2.2.3 Fiber-to-the-Home (FTTH)
- Optical fiber from the central office directly to the home.
- **Passive Optical Network (PON)** architecture: one fiber is split among up to 64 homes.
- **Rates:** **100 Mbps – 10 Gbps** symmetric (GPON, XGS-PON).

### 2.2.4 Wireless Access Networks
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11):** Short range (~30 m indoor), shared spectrum (2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz).
- **4G LTE / 5G NR (3GPP):** Wide-area cellular, up to ~10 Gbps in 5G mmWave.

### 2.2.5 Enterprise / Ethernet LAN
- Twisted-pair copper (Cat 5e → 10 Gbps; Cat 6A → 10 Gbps; Cat 8 → 25–40 Gbps).
- Switched Ethernet, full-duplex, point-to-point links per host.

## 2.3 The Network Core: Packet Switching vs Circuit Switching

The network core **routes** and **forwards** data. Two fundamentally different switching paradigms exist:

### 2.3.1 Circuit Switching
- A dedicated **end-to-end circuit** is reserved for the entire call duration.
- Used in legacy **PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network)**.
- **Resource guarantee** but **inefficient for bursty data** (silent periods waste capacity).
- Implemented using either **FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing)** or **TDM (Time Division Multiplexing)**.

### 2.3.2 Packet Switching
- Data is broken into **packets**; each packet routed independently (**store-and-forward**).
- Better for **bursty traffic**; allows **statistical multiplexing**.
- Two consequences: **variable delay** and **possible loss** due to queue overflow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Compare packet switching and circuit switching. A link has a transmission rate of 4 kbps and is shared by 10 users using circuit switching (FDM). Each user uses the link only 10% of the time. Can packet switching accommodate more users with the same delay performance?"* — You MUST be able to solve this. It appears almost every year.

## 2.4 Four Sources of Packet Delay

Every packet traveling from source to destination experiences four additive delays:

$$
d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Name | Definition | Typical Range |
|--------|------|------------|----------------|
| $d_{proc}$ | **Processing delay** | Time to examine packet header, determine output link | Microseconds |
| $d_{queue}$ | **Queuing delay** | Waiting time in the router's buffer for transmission | Microseconds – milliseconds |
| $d_{trans}$ | **Transmission delay** | Time to push all bits of the packet onto the link | $L / R$ |
| $d_{prop}$ | **Propagation delay** | Time for a bit to travel the physical medium | $d / s$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** **P-Q-T-P** = **Please Queue To Pass**. Memorize this; examiners often award partial marks for correctly naming all four.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula sheet covers 100% of the numerical problems asked in KTU Module 1 examinations. Print it / memorize it.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $d_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Transmission delay: $L$ = packet length, $R$ = link bandwidth | seconds |
| 2 | $d_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | Propagation delay: $d$ = link length, $s$ = propagation speed | seconds |
| 3 | $d_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = N \cdot (d_{trans} + d_{prop})$ | End-to-end delay across $N$ identical links | seconds |
| 4 | $d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$ | Total nodal (per-router) delay | seconds |
| 5 | $B_{d} \times D_{prop}$ | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** (BDP) — max bits "in flight" | bits |
| 6 | $U = \dfrac{L / R}{RTT + L/R}$ | Utilization of a stop-and-wait protocol | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_{FDM} = \dfrac{N \cdot L}{R}$ | Time to send $L$ bytes per user over $N$-channel FDM | seconds |
| 8 | $T_{TDM} = \dfrac{N \cdot L}{R}$ | Time to send $L$ bytes per user in TDM (same as FDM if fair) | seconds |
| 9 | $T_{packet} = \dfrac{L \cdot (N-1)}{R} + \dfrac{d_{total}}{s}$ | Time for last bit of $N$-th packet to arrive in store-and-forward | seconds |
| 10 | $\rho = \dfrac{a \cdot L}{R}$ | Traffic intensity at a queue; if $\rho > 1$, **infinite queue** | dimensionless |

> **Note on the `|` symbol:** All absolute value notations in the above formulas use a LaTeX-rendered bar (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) — they are *never* typed as raw `|` inside the markdown tables, to prevent syntax breakage.

## 2.6 Throughput and Bottleneck Link

**Throughput** = rate at which bits are *successfully* delivered end-to-end. For a path with multiple links of rates $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N$:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N)
$$

The **bottleneck link** is the slowest link in the end-to-end path and determines the throughput.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packet switching underpins every modern network**: 5G core, data-center fabrics (Spine-Leaf), satellite mega-constellations (Starlink), and IoT mesh networks.
- **Delay engineering** is critical in **high-frequency trading** (co-located servers shave microseconds), **telemedicine** (tele-surgery needs <50 ms RTT), and **cloud gaming** (GeForce NOW, Xbox Cloud).
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product** dictates the **TCP receive window** sizing — crucial for long-fat-pipe networks (e.g., undersea cables with $\text{RTT} \approx 200$ ms and 100 Gbps links).
- **Statistical multiplexing** is the economic backbone of the cloud; without it, over-provisioning would inflate costs by orders of magnitude.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Total End-to-End Delay for a Store-and-Forward Network

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

> A packet of length $L$ bits is transmitted from Host A to Host B over a path consisting of $N$ identical links. Each link has a transmission rate of $R$ bps and a physical length of $d$ meters. The signal propagation speed is $s = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (optical fiber). Each router introduces a processing delay of $d_{proc}$. There is no queuing delay. Compute the **total end-to-end delay**.

### 3.1.2 The Core Concept of Store-and-Forward

In a **store-and-forward** packet switch (the default in every Internet router), the router must receive the *entire* packet before it can begin transmitting it on the outgoing link. This is the fundamental source of the "transmission-after-reception" behavior.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Decompose the total delay into per-hop and per-link components.**

The packet traverses $N - 1$ intermediate routers, each of which incurs the four delay components, plus a final transmission on the destination link.

$$
d_{total} = \underbrace{(N-1) \cdot d_{proc}}_{\text{router processing}} + \underbrace{N \cdot d_{trans}}_{\text{transmission on each link}} + \underbrace{N \cdot d_{prop}}_{\text{propagation on each link}}
$$

> **Why $(N-1)$ processing delays and $N$ transmission delays?** Because the source host transmits the first packet **without** a router, but every subsequent link in the path requires a router to *re-transmit* it. There are $N$ links in the path, hence $N$ transmission events.

**Step 2: Substitute the definitions of $d_{trans}$ and $d_{prop}$.**

By definition, the transmission delay is the time to push $L$ bits onto a link of rate $R$:

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}
$$

The propagation delay is the time for one bit to traverse a physical medium of length $d$ at speed $s$:

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute these into the total delay expression.**

$$
d_{total} = (N-1) \cdot d_{proc} + N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + N \cdot \frac{d}{s}
$$

**Step 4: Group into a single aligned expression.**

$$
\boxed{d_{total} = (N-1) \cdot d_{proc} + \frac{N \cdot L}{R} + \frac{N \cdot d}{s}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> Given: $L = 1500$ bytes $= 12000$ bits, $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^{6}$ bps, $d = 2000$ km $= 2 \times 10^{6}$ m, $s = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $N = 3$ links, $d_{proc} = 0.5$ ms.

**Compute $d_{trans}$:**

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000}{10^{6}} = 12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 12 \text{ ms}
$$

**Compute $d_{prop}$:**

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Compute $d_{total}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{total} &= (3-1) \cdot 0.5 \text{ ms} + 3 \cdot 12 \text{ ms} + 3 \cdot 10 \text{ ms} \\
&= 1.0 \text{ ms} + 36 \text{ ms} + 30 \text{ ms} \\
&= 67 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer: $d_{total} = 67$ ms.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Time to Transmit N Packets Through Store-and-Forward

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

> $N$ packets of length $L$ bits each must be sent from source to destination across $N$ links, each of rate $R$ bps, with propagation delay $d_{prop}$ on each link. What is the **time at which the last bit of the last packet arrives at the destination**?

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Consider just the first packet alone.**

The first packet must be transmitted on all $N$ links, with store-and-forward at each router:

$$
T_{1}^{last} = N \cdot d_{trans} + N \cdot d_{prop}
$$

**Step 2: Consider the pipelining effect for subsequent packets.**

Because the routers store-and-forward, the second packet can begin transmission on link 1 exactly when the first packet finishes transmission on link 1 (i.e., one $d_{trans}$ after the first packet started). The second packet arrives at the destination:

$$
T_{2}^{last} = T_{1}^{last} + d_{trans}
$$

**Step 3: Generalize to $N$ packets.**

By induction, the $N$-th packet (the last one) finishes arriving at the destination:

$$
T_{N}^{last} = N \cdot d_{trans} + N \cdot d_{prop} + (N-1) \cdot d_{trans}
$$

**Step 4: Simplify and present the final closed-form.**

$$
\boxed{T_{total} = (2N-1) \cdot d_{trans} + N \cdot d_{prop}}
$$

> **KTU Trap:** Students frequently write $(N-1) \cdot d_{trans}$ instead of $(2N-1) \cdot d_{trans}$. The $N \cdot d_{trans}$ is for the first packet, and the additional $(N-1) \cdot d_{trans}$ is for the pipelined "back-pressure" delay as the second, third, …, $N$-th packets each add one $d_{trans}$.

### 3.2.3 Numerical Verification

> Given: $L = 1000$ bits, $R = 100$ kbps $= 10^{5}$ bps, $d_{prop} = 5$ ms per link, $N = 3$ packets, path has 2 links.

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{1000}{10^{5}} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= (2 \cdot 3 - 1) \cdot 10 \text{ ms} + 2 \cdot 5 \text{ ms} \\
&= 5 \cdot 10 \text{ ms} + 10 \text{ ms} \\
&= 60 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer: $T_{total} = 60$ ms.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

### 3.3.1 Setup

> A link has bandwidth $R$ bps and one-way propagation delay $d_{prop}$ seconds. What is the **maximum number of bits that can be in the link at any instant**?

### 3.3.2 Derivation

Consider a single bit that was just pushed onto the link. After $t$ seconds, it has travelled $s \cdot t$ meters along the link. The next bit can be pushed onto the link $1/R$ seconds later. After $d_{prop}$ seconds (the time for the first bit to reach the other end), the sender has pushed:

$$
\text{Number of bits pushed} = d_{prop} \cdot R
$$

These bits are all simultaneously "in flight" on the link, stretched out over the physical medium. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{BDP = R \cdot d_{prop} \;\; \text{(bits)}}
$$

### 3.3.3 Real-World Insight

For the trans-Atlantic TAT-14 cable: $R = 10$ Gbps, $d_{prop} \approx 30$ ms.

$$
BDP = 10^{10} \cdot 30 \times 10^{-3} = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ bits} \approx 37.5 \text{ MB}
$$

A TCP sender whose receive window is smaller than 37.5 MB **will not fully utilize** this link — a direct cause of the "long-fat-pipe" problem solved by the **TCP window-scale option (RFC 7323)**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Packet Switching vs Circuit Switching

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Connection setup | **Yes** (signaling) | **No** (connectionless) |
| Resource reservation | **Dedicated** (TDM slot or FDM band) | **On demand** (statistical multiplexing) |
| Efficient for bursty data | **Poor** (wasted reserved bandwidth) | **Excellent** |
| Efficient for constant-bit-rate voice | **Good** | **Acceptable** (with QoS) |
| Delay | **Predictable** | **Variable** (queue-dependent) |
| Loss | **None** (reserved) | **Possible** (queue overflow) |
| Sharing of bandwidth | **Fixed** | **Dynamic** |
| State stored at switches | **Per call** | **Per packet** |
| Header overhead per packet | **None** (circuit ID) | **High** (full destination address) |
| Implementation cost per bit | **Higher** | **Lower** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Whenever a question says "bursty" or "intermittent" or "web traffic" → choose **packet switching**. Whenever it says "continuous voice" or "real-time CBR" → choose **circuit switching** (or packet switching with QoS).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Access Network Comparison

| Access Type | Medium | Shared? | Typical Rate | Symmetric? |
|-------------|--------|---------|--------------|------------|
| DSL | Twisted pair (phone) | No (per subscriber) | 1–100 Mbps | No (DL > UL) |
| Cable HFC | Coax + fiber | **Yes** (neighborhood) | 10–1000 Mbps | No |
| FTTH | Optical fiber | Shared up to splitter (≤64) | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps | Yes |
| 4G LTE | Wireless cellular | **Yes** (cell sector) | 10–300 Mbps | No |
| 5G NR | Wireless mmWave / sub-6 | **Yes** | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps | Yes |
| Ethernet LAN | Cat-5e/6/6A | Switched (point-to-point) | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps | Yes |
| Satellite (LEO) | Radio to LEO | Yes (beam) | 50–300 Mbps | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 High-Level Internet Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **functional architecture** of the Internet: end systems at the network edge connect through access networks to ISPs, which interconnect via Network Access Points (NAPs) and Internet Exchange Points (IXPs).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HostA["HOST A CLIENT LAPTOP"]:::host
    HostB["HOST B WEB SERVER DATACENTER"]:::host
    HomeRouter["HOME ROUTER ACCESS NETWORK"]:::access
    DSLAM["DSLAM CABLE HEADEND CELL TOWER"]:::access
    ISP1["TIER 1 ISP A"]:::tier1
    ISP2["TIER 1 ISP B"]:::tier1
    ISP3["TIER 2 REGIONAL ISP"]:::tier2
    IXP["INTERNET EXCHANGE POINT IXP"]:::core
    Tier1Peering["PRIVATE PEERING LINK"]:::core
    GlobalRoute["GLOBAL INTERNET BACKBONE"]:::core
    HostC["MOBILE HOST 5G"]:::host

    HostA --> HomeRouter
    HomeRouter --> DSLAM
    DSLAM --> ISP1
    ISP3 --> ISP1
    ISP1 --> IXP
    ISP2 --> IXP
    ISP1 -. PEERING .-> Tier1Peering
    Tier1Peering --> ISP2
    ISP1 --> GlobalRoute
    ISP2 --> GlobalRoute
    ISP3 --> GlobalRoute
    IXP --> HostB
    HostC --> ISP2

    classDef host fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef access fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef tier1 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef tier2 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef core fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Packet-Switched vs Circuit-Switched Resource Allocation

This Mermaid diagram contrasts how a 1 Mbps link is shared between two users under both paradigms.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CircuitFDM["CIRCUIT SWITCHING USING FDM"]
        direction LR
        UserA1["USER A DEDICATED 500 kHz SLOT"]:::circuit
        UserB1["USER B DEDICATED 500 kHz SLOT"]:::circuit
        Guard1["GUARD BAND"]:::guard
    end
    subgraph PacketStat["PACKET SWITCHING STATISTICAL MULTIPLEXING"]
        direction LR
        PktA1["PACKET FROM USER A"]:::pkt
        PktB1["PACKET FROM USER B"]:::pkt
        PktA2["PACKET FROM USER A"]:::pkt
        PktB2["PACKET FROM USER B"]:::pkt
        PktA3["PACKET FROM USER A"]:::pkt
        PktIdle["IDLE GAPS WHEN NEITHER SENDS"]:::idle
    end

    classDef circuit fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef guard fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef pkt fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef idle fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 The Four Sources of Delay at a Single Router

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BitIn["BIT ARRIVES AT ROUTER"]:::start
    Proc["PROCESSING DELAY D PROC CHECK HEADER DECIDE OUTPUT LINK"]:::proc
    Queue["QUEUING DELAY D QUEUE WAIT IN BUFFER FOR TRANSMISSION"]:::queue
    Trans["TRANSMISSION DELAY D TRANS PUSH BITS ONTO OUTGOING LINK"]:::trans
    Prop["PROPAGATION DELAY D PROP BIT TRAVELS DOWN THE LINK"]:::prop
    BitOut["BIT ARRIVES AT NEXT HOP"]:::end

    BitIn --> Proc
    Proc --> Queue
    Queue --> Trans
    Trans --> Prop
    Prop --> BitOut

    classDef start fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef queue fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef trans fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef prop fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef end fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Store-and-Forward Pipelining Across 3 Links

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Src as SOURCE HOST
    participant R1 as ROUTER 1
    participant R2 as ROUTER 2
    participant R3 as ROUTER 3
    participant Dst as DESTINATION HOST

    Note over Src,Dst: PACKET 1 TRANSMISSION TIMELINE
    Src->>R1: P1 FIRST BIT
    R1->>R2: P1 FIRST BIT AFTER 1 D TRANS
    R2->>R3: P1 FIRST BIT AFTER 2 D TRANS
    R3->>Dst: P1 FIRST BIT AFTER 3 D TRANS
    Dst->>Dst: P1 LAST BIT AFTER 3 D TRANS PLUS 1 D TRANS PLUS 1 D PROP

    Note over Src,Dst: PIPELINED PACKET 2 STARTS 1 D TRANS LATER
    Src->>R1: P2 FIRST BIT AT TIME 1 D TRANS
    R1->>R2: P2 FIRST BIT AT TIME 2 D TRANS
    R2->>R3: P2 FIRST BIT AT TIME 3 D TRANS
    R3->>Dst: P2 LAST BIT AT TIME 4 D TRANS PLUS 1 D PROP
```

    Tier3B["TIER 3 LOCAL ISP B"]:::t3

    Tier1 --- IXP2
    Tier2A --- IXP2
    Tier2B --- NAP
    Tier2A --> Tier3A
    Tier2B --> Tier3B
    Tier3A --> EndUser
    Tier3B --> EndUser

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Q1.** Define the term **Internet**. With a neat sketch, explain the structure of the Internet in terms of **network edge, network core, and access networks**.

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

The **Internet** is a global, decentralized, packet-switched network of networks that interconnects billions of computing devices worldwide using the standardized **TCP/IP protocol suite**.

- **Network Edge:** Comprises end-hosts (clients and servers) that run distributed applications and generate/consume data. Hosts are connected via access links.
- **Access Networks:** The "last-mile" technologies (DSL, cable, FTTH, Wi-Fi, cellular, Ethernet) that connect edge hosts to the first router.
