- **Network Core:** A mesh of high-speed routers and links (owned by Tier-1, Tier-2, and Tier-3 ISPs, interconnected through IXPs) that forward packets from source to destination using store-and-forward packet switching.

*(Diagrammatic sketch as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes may be reproduced for full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Q2.** Differentiate between **packet switching** and **circuit switching**.

**Model Answer (Comparative Table — 8–10 lines):**

| Feature | Packet Switching | Circuit Switching |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| Resource allocation | On-demand (statistical) | Pre-reserved (FDM or TDM) |
| Suitability for bursty data | Excellent | Poor |
| Delay | Variable (queue-dependent) | Fixed and predictable |
| Connection setup | Not required | Required (signaling) |
| Loss | Possible (queue overflow) | No loss |
| Bandwidth sharing | Dynamic | Static |
| Header per data unit | Full destination address | Only circuit ID |
| Example technology | Internet (TCP/IP) | PSTN (legacy telephony) |

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE rules: Module 1 carries **two 14-mark questions** (internal choice). Each sub-part is **7 marks**. The model answers below are written to the **exact length expected by KTU examiners**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Q(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the various types of **access networks** used to connect an end user to the Internet. (7 Marks)
>
> **Q(b)** A packet of length **L = 2000 bits** travels from source to destination over **3 links**, each of transmission rate **R = 1 Mbps**. Each link has a propagation delay of **d_prop = 10 ms**. The processing delay at each intermediate router is **d_proc = 1 ms**. Queuing delay is negligible. Calculate the **total end-to-end delay** and the **time at which the last bit arrives at the destination** if **4 such packets** are sent in pipeline. (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer for Q(a) — Access Networks (7 Marks)

An **access network** is the physical link that connects an end host to the first router (the "edge router") on the path toward the Internet core. The major categories are:

1. **Residential Access Networks:**
   - **DSL (Digital Subscriber Line):** Uses the existing telephone twisted-pair. Splits the line into three frequency bands — voice (0–4 kHz), upstream (25–160 kHz), and downstream (240 kHz–1.5 MHz). Asymmetric: downstream ≫ upstream. The DSLAM at the telephone office aggregates many DSL lines.
   - **Cable Internet (HFC):** Uses the cable-TV infrastructure. Coaxial cable is shared among all homes in a neighborhood → all users share the access link's capacity, leading to contention during peak hours. DOCSIS 3.1 supports up to 1 Gbps downstream.
   - **FTTH (Fiber-to-the-Home):** Optical fiber from the central office to the home. PON (Passive Optical Network) splits one fiber among up to 64 homes. Speeds: 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps symmetric. Lowest latency and highest reliability.

2. **Wireless Access Networks:**
   - **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11):** Short-range (~30 m indoor, ~200 m outdoor), unlicensed 2.4/5/6 GHz spectrum, shared medium. Speeds up to 9.6 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6, 802.11ax).
   - **4G LTE / 5G NR:** Wide-area cellular. 5G mmWave can deliver 1–10 Gbps in dense urban areas; sub-6 GHz 5G offers 100–900 Mbps. Latency as low as 1 ms in URLLC mode.

3. **Enterprise / Institutional Access Networks:**
   - **Ethernet LAN:** Twisted-pair copper (Cat-5e / Cat-6 / Cat-6A) with switched full-duplex connections. Speeds of 100 Mbps, 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps. Each host gets a dedicated point-to-point link to the switch.

> **Conclusion:** Modern FTTH and 5G access networks are rapidly displacing legacy DSL/copper, but the shared-medium contention of cable/Wi-Fi/cellular means that real-world throughput is always lower than the advertised peak rate.

#### Model Answer for Q(b) — Numerical Delay Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Packet length: $L = 2000$ bits
- Number of links: $N = 3$
- Link rate: $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^{6}$ bps
- Propagation delay per link: $d_{prop} = 10$ ms $= 10 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- Processing delay per router: $d_{proc} = 1$ ms (applies to $N-1 = 2$ intermediate routers)
- Queuing delay: $d_{queue} \approx 0$

**Step 1: Compute the transmission delay.**

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2000}{10^{6}} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the total end-to-end delay for a single packet.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{total} &= (N-1) \cdot d_{proc} + N \cdot d_{trans} + N \cdot d_{prop} \\
&= 2 \cdot 1 \text{ ms} + 3 \cdot 2 \text{ ms} + 3 \cdot 10 \text{ ms} \\
&= 2 + 6 + 30 \\
&= 38 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the time for the last bit of the 4th packet to arrive (store-and-forward pipeline).**

For $P$ packets over $N$ links:

$$
T_{last} = (P + N - 1) \cdot d_{trans} + N \cdot d_{prop} + (N-1) \cdot d_{proc}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{last} &= (4 + 3 - 1) \cdot 2 \text{ ms} + 3 \cdot 10 \text{ ms} + 2 \cdot 1 \text{ ms} \\
&= 6 \cdot 2 \text{ ms} + 30 \text{ ms} + 2 \text{ ms} \\
&= 12 + 30 + 2 \\
&= 44 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Total end-to-end delay for a single packet: **$d_{total} = 38$ ms**
- Time for the last bit of the 4th packet to arrive: **$T_{last} = 44$ ms**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Q(a)** Explain the concept of **packet switching** in detail. Discuss the role of **store-and-forward transmission** and **statistical multiplexing**. (7 Marks)
>
> **Q(b)** Consider a link with a transmission rate of **R = 4 kbps** that is currently being **circuit-switched** among **10 users** using **FDM**. Each user uses the link **10% of the time**.
>   **(i)** With circuit switching, what is the maximum number of users that can be supported if each requires **400 bps**?
>   **(ii)** With packet switching, what is the probability that a given user is generating data, and what is the average throughput per user if all 10 users generate data simultaneously? (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer for Q(a) — Packet Switching (7 Marks)

**Packet switching** is a paradigm in which data is broken into discrete units called **packets**, each containing a header (with source and destination addresses) and a payload. Packets are routed independently through the network using **store-and-forward** behavior at each intermediate router.

**Store-and-Forward Transmission:**
At every router, the *entire* packet must arrive before the router can begin transmitting the first bit of that packet onto the next link. This introduces a **transmission delay of $L/R$** at every hop, in addition to the propagation delay of the physical medium. The advantage is that intermediate nodes can buffer (store) the packet if the outgoing link is busy, providing resilience and enabling statistical multiplexing.

**Statistical Multiplexing:**
Unlike circuit switching, which reserves a fixed slice of bandwidth per user (FDM frequency slot or TDM time slot), packet switching shares the link on demand. When a user has data to send, it transmits at the full link rate. If multiple users send simultaneously, their packets are **interleaved** at the router's output buffer. The link bandwidth is allocated on a *first-come, first-served* basis, hence the name "statistical."

**Consequences:**
- **Variable queuing delay:** Packets may have to wait behind other packets in the buffer.
- **Possible packet loss:** If the buffer overflows, packets are dropped.
- **Higher link utilization:** Idle users do not waste reserved capacity.
- **No connection setup:** Packets can be sent immediately, ideal for bursty web/app traffic.

**Comparison Snapshot:** Packet switching is the foundation of IP, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, 4G/5G core, and all modern data networks.

#### Model Answer for Q(b) — Circuit vs Packet Switching Capacity (7 Marks)

**(i) Circuit Switching — Maximum Number of Users:**

Under **circuit switching (FDM)**, the link bandwidth $R = 4$ kbps is divided into fixed frequency slots. Each user is allocated a slot of 400 bps.

Maximum number of users:

$$
N_{max} = \frac{R}{\text{per-user rate}} = \frac{4000 \text{ bps}}{400 \text{ bps}} = 10 \text{ users}
$$

**Answer:** Exactly 10 users can be supported under circuit switching. (In fact, all 10 slots are already in use by the given scenario.)

**(ii) Packet Switching — Probability and Throughput:**

Each user generates data with probability $p = 0.10$ (uses the link 10% of the time). All 10 users are independent. The probability that **exactly $k$** out of 10 users are simultaneously active follows a **binomial distribution**:

$$
P(X = k) = \binom{10}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{10-k}
$$

The **probability that a specific user is generating data** is simply $p = 0.10$.

**Probability that all 10 users generate data simultaneously:**

$$
P(X = 10) = (0.10)^{10} = 10^{-10}
$$

This is **negligibly small** (one in 10 billion). In a packet-switched system, we can statistically allow more than 10 users because the chance of all 10 needing the link at the same instant is vanishingly small.

**Average throughput per user (assuming all 10 users are active simultaneously):**

If 10 users share 4 kbps equally:

$$
\text{Per-user throughput} = \frac{4000}{10} = 400 \text{ bps}
$$

So in the worst case, each user still gets the same 400 bps that circuit switching would have guaranteed — but **without wasting bandwidth** when fewer than 10 users are active.

**Conclusion:** Packet switching supports the same 10 users with statistically equivalent performance, but uses the link **far more efficiently** when traffic is bursty. This is the canonical justification for the Internet's adoption of packet switching.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in Module 1 ("The Internet"):**
> 1. **Confusing $d_{trans}$ with $d_{prop}$:** Transmission delay ($L/R$) is the time to push bits onto the link; propagation delay ($d/s$) is the time for a bit to travel through the medium. Examiners report 30% of all numerical errors trace to this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting $(N-1) \cdot d_{proc}$ in end-to-end delay:** There are $N-1$ intermediate routers, not $N$. Writing $N \cdot d_{proc}$ costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up "Internet" with "WWW":** The Internet is the network infrastructure; the World Wide Web is an *application* that runs on the Internet. Examiners will deduct for conflating them.
> 4. **In FDM vs TDM comparison, forgetting to mention guard bands and the "no idle time" penalty of circuit switching:** This is the most common reason a 7-mark answer gets only 4.
> 5. **Omitting units in the final numerical answer:** Always write the unit (ms, s, μs). KTU's automated answer-key script will not award full credit for "67" without "ms."

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "The Internet" (Module 1, KTU PEITT412):**

- ✅ **Internet = "network of networks"** using the **TCP/IP** protocol suite. Operated by thousands of independent ISPs.
- ✅ **Structure:** Network Edge (hosts) → Access Networks (last mile) → Network Core (routers, ISPs, IXPs).
- ✅ **Client-Server** vs **Peer-to-Peer (P2P)** are the two communication paradigms. P2P is self-scalable; Client-Server is centrally managed.
- ✅ **Access Network Types:** DSL, Cable (HFC), FTTH, Wi-Fi, Cellular (4G/5G), Ethernet. Each has distinct bandwidth, symmetry, and shared-medium properties.
- ✅ **Packet Switching** uses **store-and-forward** transmission and **statistical multiplexing**. Suited for **bursty** data.
- ✅ **Circuit Switching** reserves a fixed circuit using **FDM** or **TDM**. Suited for **constant-bit-rate** real-time streams.
- ✅ **Four Delays:** $d_{proc}$ + $d_{queue}$ + $d_{trans}$ + $d_{prop}$. **Mnemonic: Please Queue To Pass (P-Q-T-P).**
- ✅ **Transmission delay** = $L / R$. **Propagation delay** = $d / s$. These are different and additive.
- ✅ **End-to-end delay for one packet over $N$ links:** $d_{total} = (N-1) \cdot d_{proc} + N \cdot d_{trans} + N \cdot d_{prop}$.
- ✅ **Last-bit arrival for $P$ packets in pipeline:** $T_{last} = (P + N - 1) \cdot d_{trans} + N \cdot d_{prop} + (N-1) \cdot d_{proc}$.
- ✅ **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP):** $R \cdot d_{prop}$ = number of bits "in flight." Determines the minimum TCP receive window for full link utilization.
- ✅ **Throughput** = $\min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N)$ along the path. The bottleneck link limits throughput.
- ✅ **Tiers of ISPs:** Tier 1 (global, peers only) → Tier 2 (regional) → Tier 3 (local). They interconnect at **IXPs (Internet Exchange Points)**.
- ✅ **Internet's core functions:** **forwarding** (local, per-packet, data-plane) and **routing** (global, end-to-end, control-plane).
- ✅ **Key standard bodies:** **IETF** (protocol standards — RFCs), **ICANN** (IP & DNS allocation), **IEEE** (LAN/MAN — 802.x), **3GPP** (cellular).
- ✅ **Always carry units** in numerical answers. Always draw a diagram for any "explain with sketch" question.
- ✅ **High-frequency KTU keywords to memorize verbatim:** *store-and-forward, statistical multiplexing, best-effort, FDM, TDM, IXP, access network, bottleneck, queuing delay, propagation delay, transmission delay, bandwidth-delay product*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Transmission: Concepts and Terminology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Transmission: Concepts and Terminology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Transmission** is the process of transferring digital or analog data between two or more devices over a communication channel, which may be a physical medium (copper wire, optical fiber) or a wireless medium (radio, microwave, infrared). In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Code: PEITT412), this topic establishes the foundational vocabulary and quantitative metrics (frequency, bandwidth, data rate, signal-to-noise ratio) upon which all subsequent modules — encoding schemes, multiplexing, switching, and protocol design — are constructed.

According to KTU Module 1 specifications, *Data Transmission: Concepts and Terminology* introduces the learner to the **characterization of signals**, the **capacity limits of channels**, the **modes and methods** of moving bits across a link, and the **impairments** that degrade those bits in transit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The student must master the *concepts* (terminology, definitions, classification) and the *quantitative limits* (Nyquist and Shannon theorems) before progressing to encoding techniques in Module 1's later sections.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **water pipeline system** connecting two cities. The water flowing inside is your *data*. The pipe's diameter is your *bandwidth* — a wider pipe carries more water per second. The pressure pushing the water is your *signal strength*. Obstacles, rust, leaks, and turbulence inside the pipe represent *attenuation, distortion, and noise*.

- **Bandwidth** $\rightarrow$ pipe diameter (how much can flow at once)
- **Data rate (bps)** $\rightarrow$ liters per second (how much actually arrives)
- **Latency** $\rightarrow$ travel time of a single droplet
- **Noise** $\rightarrow$ dirt and air bubbles mixing with the water
- **Throughput** $\rightarrow$ clean water actually delivered at the destination

If you make the pump too strong (excessive signal amplitude), the pipe may burst (**clipping/distortion**). If the pipe is too long, the water slows down and weakens (**attenuation**). The *maximum clean water flow* is determined by both pipe diameter **and** water purity — this is the essence of the **Shannon-Hartley Theorem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy works:** Communication engineers literally model channels as fluid systems using **transfer functions**, and the math of flow (Poiseuille's law) is dimensionally similar to **Shannon's channel capacity equation**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Glossary (KTU Board-Favourite Definitions)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Data** | A representation of facts, concepts, or instructions in a formalized manner suitable for communication, interpretation, or processing. |
| **Signal** | The physical quantity (electrical voltage, light pulse, radio wave) that carries data across the medium. |
| **Analog Signal** | A continuous, time-varying signal whose amplitude can take **infinitely many values** within its range. |
| **Digital Signal** | A discrete-time, discrete-amplitude signal that represents data as a sequence of **binary bits** (0s and 1s). |
| **Bit Rate ($R_b$)** | The number of **bits transmitted per second** (bps, kbps, Mbps, Gbps). |
| **Baud Rate ($S$)** | The number of **signal units (symbols) transmitted per second**. |
| **Bandwidth ($BW$)** | The width of the range of frequencies that a channel can carry, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. |
| **Period ($T$)** | The time required to complete one full cycle of a periodic signal (seconds). |
| **Frequency ($f$)** | The number of cycles per second of a periodic signal, $f = 1/T$ (Hertz). |
| **Wavelength ($\lambda$)** | The spatial distance of one full cycle, $\lambda = v/f$, where $v$ is propagation speed. |
| **Throughput** | The actual rate of *useful* data successfully delivered (lower than bit rate due to overhead). |
| **Latency (Delay)** | The time taken for an entire message to travel from source to destination. |
| **Jitter** | Variation in packet arrival times due to network congestion or path changes. |
| **SNR** | Signal-to-Noise Ratio — the ratio of signal power to noise power. |
| **Attenuation** | Loss of signal strength as it propagates through the medium. |
| **Distortion** | Alteration of signal shape due to non-ideal channel response. |
| **Noise** | Unwanted random electrical energy added to the signal (thermal, crosstalk, impulse). |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Periodic vs. Non-Periodic Signals

A **periodic signal** completes a pattern within a measurable time frame called the **period ($T$)**, repeating indefinitely. A **non-periodic (aperiodic) signal** changes constantly without repetition. In digital communication, all real signals are non-periodic because data is random; however, periodic signals are used as **carriers** and **test signals**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Fourier Series** of a periodic signal decomposes it into a fundamental sine wave plus harmonics, while the **Fourier Transform** does the same for non-periodic signals, producing a continuous **frequency spectrum**.

## 2.2 Frequency Domain vs. Time Domain

The same signal can be viewed in two ways:

- **Time domain:** Amplitude plotted against time $s(t)$.
- **Frequency domain:** Amplitude (or phase) plotted against frequency $S(f)$.

The frequency domain reveals the **bandwidth** of a signal — the band of frequencies the signal occupies. This is the central idea used to determine whether a channel can carry the signal.

## 2.3 The Three Components of Any Signal

1. **Amplitude (A)** — vertical magnitude; measured in volts, watts, or dBm.
2. **Frequency ($f$)** — rate of oscillation; measured in Hertz.
3. **Phase ($\phi$)** — horizontal position relative to a reference; measured in degrees or radians.

A general sinusoid is: $s(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$.

## 2.4 Transmission Modes (Directional)

| Mode | Symbol | Direction | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | SX | One-way only | TV broadcast, radio |
| **Half-Duplex** | HDX | Both ways, **not simultaneously** | Walkie-talkie, CB radio |
| **Full-Duplex** | FDX | Both ways, **simultaneously** | Telephone, Ethernet (full-duplex) |

## 2.5 Serial vs. Parallel Transmission

- **Serial:** Bits sent one after another on a **single channel** (cheaper, long-distance, USB, Ethernet).
- **Parallel:** Bits sent simultaneously on **multiple channels** (faster over short distances, e.g., older printer ports, internal CPU bus).

## 2.6 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Transmission

- **Asynchronous:** Each byte is framed with **start and stop bits**; no shared clock; simple but ~20% overhead.
- **Synchronous:** Bits are sent as a **continuous stream**; a shared clock or clock-recovery mechanism aligns sender and receiver; high efficiency.

## 2.7 Baseband vs. Broadband Transmission

- **Baseband:** Digital signal sent directly on the medium (e.g., Ethernet on twisted pair).
- **Broadband (Passband):** Signal is **modulated** onto a carrier to shift its frequency band (e.g., radio, TV, DSL).

## 2.8 Transmission Impairments (KTU 3-Mark Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three impairments limit any real channel:

1. **Attenuation** — Loss of energy: $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{(-A/10)}$
   - Compensated by **amplifiers** (analog) or **repeaters** (digital).
   - Measured in **decibels (dB)**: $\text{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_2 / P_1)$.
2. **Distortion** — Different frequency components travel at different speeds; the signal shape changes.
3. **Noise** — Random unwanted energy; four types:
   - **Thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise:** $N = kTB$, where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
   - **Induced noise** from nearby sources (motors, fluorescent lights).
   - **Crosstalk** from adjacent wires.
   - **Impulse noise** — sharp spikes (lightning, switching).

## 2.9 Data Rate Limits — Nyquist & Shannon (THE KTU FAVOURITES)

### A. Nyquist Theorem (Noiseless Channel)

For a **noiseless** channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with $L$ discrete signal levels:

$$C = 2B \log_2 L \quad \text{(bps)}$$

Doubling bandwidth doubles the capacity; doubling signal levels adds only a logarithmic increase.

### B. Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Noisy Channel)

For a **noisy** channel with bandwidth $B$ and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$:

$$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \quad \text{(bps)}$$

This is the **absolute upper limit** — no encoding can exceed it. It is independent of the number of signal levels.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the *Nyquist* formula (uses $L$, signal levels) with the *Shannon* formula (uses SNR). **Remember:** Nyquist = noiseless + levels; Shannon = noisy + SNR. They are **not interchangeable**.

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f = 1/T$ | Frequency from period | Hz, s |
| 2 | $\lambda = v/f$ | Wavelength | m |
| 3 | $C = 2B \log_2 L$ | Nyquist capacity (noiseless) | bps |
| 4 | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon capacity (noisy) | bps |
| 5 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | SNR in decibels | dB |
| 6 | $\text{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_2/P_1)$ | Attenuation / Gain in dB | dB |
| 7 | $N = kTB$ | Thermal noise power | W |
| 8 | $L = 2^V$ or $V = \log_2 L$ | Bits per symbol / levels | bits, levels |
| 9 | $S = R_b / \log_2 L$ | Baud rate from bit rate | baud |
| 10 | $\text{Throughput} = \text{Data delivered} / \text{Time}$ | Effective useful data rate | bps |
| 11 | $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{queue}}$ | Total delay | s |
| 12 | $T_{\text{prop}} = d / v$ | Propagation delay | s |

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical fiber design** uses Shannon's limit to determine how far a signal can travel before regeneration.
- **5G/6G cellular** engineers set modulation order ($L$) and bandwidth ($B$) so $R_b$ matches Shannon's capacity.
- **DSL broadband** is engineered with knowledge that as distance increases, SNR drops, forcing a lower data rate.
- **Audio CD (44.1 kHz sampling)** is based on Nyquist's theorem: human hearing tops at 20 kHz, so sampling at $>40$ kHz captures everything.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless Channel)

### Setup
A noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz is used. The signal uses $L$ discrete amplitude levels. We want the maximum bit rate $C$.

### Step 1 — Maximum Symbol Rate (Signaling Rate)
According to **Nyquist's sampling criterion**, a band-limited channel of bandwidth $B$ can support at most $2B$ independent signal changes per second. Each change is one **symbol**. Hence the maximum baud rate is:

$$
S_{\max} = 2B \quad \text{(symbols/sec)}
$$

### Step 2 — Bits per Symbol
If each symbol carries one of $L$ discrete levels, the number of bits encoded per symbol is given by the base-2 logarithm:

$$
\text{bits per symbol} = \log_2 L
$$

### Step 3 — Combine Symbol Rate and Bits per Symbol
Bit rate is symbol rate multiplied by bits per symbol:

$$
C = S_{\max} \times \log_2 L
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 3
Replacing $S_{\max}$ with $2B$:

$$
C = 2B \log_2 L \quad \text{(bits per second)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Doubling the bandwidth doubles the capacity. Doubling the signal levels adds only $\log_2 2 = 1$ extra bit per symbol — a **logarithmic**, not linear, gain. This is why engineers prefer wider channels over higher signal levels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Noisy Channel)

### Step 1 — Channel Capacity Concept
A noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ with signal power $S$ and additive white Gaussian noise power $N$ has a capacity $C$ (in bps) that is the **maximum achievable error-free information rate**.

### Step 2 — Information per Symbol
Shannon proved that the **maximum information per symbol** (in bits) is $\log_2(1 + S/N)$, because the noise limits the number of distinguishable signal levels to approximately $\sqrt{1 + S/N}$ in each dimension.

### Step 3 — Maximum Symbol Rate
The Nyquist limit still applies: a bandwidth-$B$ channel can change at most $2B$ times per second.

### Step 4 — Combine
Multiplying the maximum symbol rate by the maximum bits per symbol:

$$
C = 2B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)
$$

For the **one-sided bandwidth** convention used in KTU textbooks (B from DC to $B$ Hz, *not* $B$ from $-B$ to $+B$), the factor of 2 is omitted:

$$
\boxed{C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \quad \text{(bps)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Indian textbooks (Forouzan, Stallings) use the one-sided bandwidth form. **Always omit the factor 2** unless the question explicitly states "two-sided bandwidth".

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (Board-Exam Style)

### Example 1 — Nyquist Capacity
**Q:** A noiseless 4-kHz channel uses 16 signal levels. Find the maximum bit rate.

**Solution:**

$$
C = 2B \log_2 L = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 16
$$

$$
= 8000 \times 4 = 32{,}000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}
$$

### Example 2 — Shannon Capacity
**Q:** A 3-kHz channel has an SNR of 30 dB. Find the maximum theoretical bit rate.

**Step 1:** Convert SNR from dB to linear ratio.

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 30 = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(S/N) = 3
$$

$$
S/N = 10^3 = 1000
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Shannon's formula.

$$
C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) = 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000)
$$

$$
= 3000 \times \log_2(1001)
$$

$$
\approx 3000 \times 9.967 = 29{,}901 \text{ bps} \approx 29.9 \text{ kbps}
$$

### Example 3 — Combined Nyquist + Shannon
**Q:** A channel of 4 kHz with SNR = 24 dB uses 8 levels. (a) Maximum bit rate from Nyquist. (b) Maximum bit rate from Shannon. (c) Is the channel over- or under-utilized?

**(a) Nyquist:**

$$
C_{\text{Nyq}} = 2B \log_2 L = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 8 = 8000 \times 3 = 24{,}000 \text{ bps}
$$

**(b) Shannon:** Convert SNR: $S/N = 10^{24/10} = 10^{2.4} \approx 251.19$.

$$
C_{\text{Sh}} = 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 251.19) = 4000 \times \log_2(252.19) \approx 4000 \times 7.978 = 31{,}912 \text{ bps}
$$

**(c)** Since Nyquist gives 24 kbps and Shannon allows up to 31.9 kbps, the channel is **under-utilized** at 24 kbps — more levels or more bandwidth would be safe.

### Example 4 — Latency Calculation
**Q:** A packet of 10,000 bits is sent over a 2000-km link at propagation speed $2 \times 10^8$ m/s, with a transmission rate of 1 Mbps. Find total latency.

**Step 1 — Propagation delay:**

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission delay:**

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{\text{Size}}{\text{Rate}} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bps}} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Total (ignoring queueing & processing):**

$$
T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} = 10 + 10 = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: At-a-Glance Comparison

| Property | Nyquist | Shannon-Hartley |
|---|---|---|
| Channel assumed | Noiseless | Noisy |
| Key parameter | $L$ (levels) | SNR (linear) |
| Effect of bandwidth $B$ | Linear | Linear |
| Effect of $L$ / SNR | Logarithmic | Logarithmic |
| Defines | **Maximum possible** bit rate at chosen $L$ | **Absolute theoretical** limit |
| Used for | Encoding design | Capacity planning, link budget |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Communication System — Block Architecture (Mermaid)

The complete **block-level functional architecture** of a data transmission system, showing how data flows from source to destination, the impairments introduced, and the role of protocols:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SOURCE - Application] --> B[Source Encoder - PGP Compression]
    B --> C[Channel Encoder - Error Correction]
    C --> D[Modulator - Carrier Signal]
    D --> E[Channel - Physical Medium]
    F[Noise Attenuation Distortion] -.->|impairs| E
    E --> G[Demodulator - Carrier Recovery]
    G --> H[Channel Decoder - Error Detection]
    H --> I[Source Decoder - Decompression]
    I --> J[DESTINATION - Application]
    K[Protocol Layer - Control] --> D
    K --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **solid arrows** trace the forward data path. The **dashed arrow** from the "impairments" block represents the **unwanted disturbances** that the channel injects. The bottom control path (K) represents protocol-level synchronization, framing, and clock recovery.

---

## 4.2 Transmission Mode Comparison (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Simplex["SIMPLEX - One Way"]
        SA[Source A] -->|only direction| SB[Destination B]
    end
    subgraph HalfDuplex["HALF DUPLEX - Both Ways Alternately"]
        HC1[Device X] <-->|one at a time| HC2[Device Y]
    end
    subgraph FullDuplex["FULL DUPLEX - Both Ways Simultaneously"]
        FC1[Device P] <-->|simultaneous two channels| FC2[Device Q]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" modes, always draw **two arrows** for full-duplex (one each direction, simultaneous), **one arrow with a back-and-forth indicator** for half-duplex, and **one arrow only** for simplex.

---

## 4.3 Data Transmission Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Data Transmission] --> mode[Directional Mode]
    root --> sync[Synchronization Method]
    root --> encoding[Signal Encoding]
    mode --> m1[Simplex]
    mode --> m2[Half Duplex]
    mode --> m3[Full Duplex]
    sync --> s1[Asynchronous - start stop bits]
    sync --> s2[Synchronous - shared clock]
    encoding --> e1[Baseband - no carrier]
    encoding --> e2[Broadband - modulated carrier]
```

---

## 4.4 Channel Capacity Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Given Channel] --> q1{Is noise significant?}
    q1 -->|No| n[Nyquist Theorem]
    q1 -->|Yes| s[Shannon Hartley Theorem]
    n --> nf[Use 2B log2 L]
    s --> sf[Use B log2 1 plus SNR]
    nf --> compare{Is Nyquist result within Shannon limit?}
    compare -->|Yes| ok[Feasible Design]
    compare -->|No| rej[Reduce L or B]
    sf --> ok2[Absolute Upper Bound]
```

---

## 4.5 Impairment Flow on a Channel (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Transmitted Signal Power] --> B[Channel - Attenuation Loss]
    B --> C[Amplifier or Repeater]
    C --> D[Received Signal]
    N1[Thermal Noise] --> D
    N2[Crosstalk] --> D
    N3[Impulse Noise] --> D
    D --> E[SNR Measurement]
    E --> F[Capacity C equals B log2 1 plus SNR]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** mix Nyquist and Shannon formulas. Pick the right one based on whether the problem specifies a *noisy* or *noiseless* channel.
> - When converting SNR from dB to linear, **always** show the intermediate step $S/N = 10^{(\text{SNR}_{dB}/10)}$.
> - Bandwidth units: **Hz** for analog channels; **bits per second** for digital links. Mixing them is a common 2-mark deduction.
> - In latency problems, **propagation delay** uses distance/speed; **transmission delay** uses size/rate. Do not interchange them.
> - For "compare" questions, draw a labelled diagram — partial marks are awarded for diagrams alone.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Explain any **three** transmission impairments that degrade a signal as it propagates through a communication channel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three principal transmission impairments are:

1. **Attenuation** — Loss of signal energy as the signal travels through the medium. It is measured in **decibels (dB)** and compensated by **amplifiers** (analog) or **repeaters** (digital). Formula: $\text{Attenuation(dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})$.

2. **Distortion** — Change in the shape of the signal caused by the channel treating different frequency components differently. A composite signal's harmonics travel at different phase velocities, so the received waveform is a distorted version of the transmitted one. Compensated by **equalizers**.

3. **Noise** — Random, unwanted electrical energy added to the signal. Major types are **thermal noise** (proportional to $kTB$), **crosstalk** (coupling from adjacent wires), **induced noise** (from motors/lights), and **impulse noise** (spikes from lightning/switching).

> Each point carries **1 mark**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** Define **bandwidth**, **bit rate**, and **baud rate**. How are bit rate and baud rate related?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Bandwidth** is the width of the range of frequencies that a channel can pass without significant attenuation, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Bit Rate** is the number of **bits transmitted per second**, measured in **bits per second (bps)**.
- **Baud Rate** (signaling rate) is the number of **signal units (symbols) transmitted per second**, measured in **baud**.

The relationship is:

$$
R_b = S \times \log_2 L \quad \text{or} \quad S = \frac{R_b}{\log_2 L}
$$

where $L$ is the number of discrete signal levels per symbol. When $L = 2$ (binary), bit rate equals baud rate.

---

## Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model, Adapted]**  
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the components of a data communication system. (**7 marks**)  
**(b)** A communication channel has a bandwidth of **5 kHz** and an SNR of **40 dB**.  
    (i) Calculate the maximum theoretical data rate using the Shannon-Hartley theorem.  
    (ii) If the channel is to use **16 signal levels**, calculate the Nyquist bit rate.  
    (iii) Comment on whether the chosen encoding is feasible. (**7 marks**)

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

A data communication system has **five** essential components:

1. **Message / Source** — the data to be transmitted (text, audio, video).
2. **Sender / Transmitter** — the device that converts and encodes the message into a transmittable signal (e.g., a modem, a network interface card).
3. **Transmission Medium** — the physical path (twisted pair, coaxial, fiber, wireless) carrying the signal.
4. **Receiver** — the device that decodes the incoming signal back into usable data.
5. **Destination** — the final recipient of the data (computer, server, phone).

Supporting components include the **protocol** (rules for exchange), **encoder/decoder**, and the **channel** with its **noise source**.

> **[5 marks for the five components + 2 marks for the neat labelled diagram.]**

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Shannon capacity — 3 marks**

Convert SNR from dB to linear:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 40 = 10 \log_{10}(S/N) \Rightarrow S/N = 10^4 = 10{,}000
$$

Apply Shannon's formula:

$$
C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 5000 \times \log_2(1 + 10{,}000)
$$

$$
= 5000 \times \log_2(10{,}001) \approx 5000 \times 13.288 = 66{,}440 \text{ bps} \approx 66.44 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(ii) Nyquist bit rate — 3 marks**

With $L = 16$ levels, $\log_2 16 = 4$ bits per symbol.

$$
C_{\text{Nyq}} = 2B \log_2 L = 2 \times 5000 \times 4 = 40{,}000 \text{ bps} = 40 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(iii) Feasibility comment — 1 mark**

Since the Nyquist bit rate (40 kbps) is **less than** the Shannon limit (~66.44 kbps), the chosen encoding with 16 levels is **feasible**. In fact, the design has some **headroom** — one could try $L = 32$ to push closer to the Shannon limit.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, Adapted]**  
**(a)** Compare the **Nyquist** and **Shannon-Hartley** theorems. State the formulas and explain the underlying assumptions. (**7 marks**)  
**(b)** A **10,000-bit** file is transmitted over a link of length **2,500 km** at a propagation speed of $2.5 \times 10^8$ m/s. The transmission rate is **2 Mbps**.  
    (i) Calculate the **propagation delay** and **transmission delay**.  
    (ii) Calculate the **total latency** (ignore queueing & processing).  
    (iii) If a **10 repeaters** are placed equally along the link, what is the new total latency? (**7 marks**)

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

| Aspect | Nyquist Theorem | Shannon-Hartley Theorem |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $C = 2B \log_2 L$ | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ |
| **Channel** | Noiseless | Noisy |
| **Key parameter** | $L$ (signal levels) | SNR (linear) |
| **Effect of $B$** | Linear | Linear |
| **Effect of $L$ / SNR** | Logarithmic | Logarithmic |
| **Interpretation** | Maximum bit rate at a chosen encoding | Absolute upper bound of any encoding |
| **Use case** | Design target | Capacity ceiling |

**Assumptions:**
- *Nyquist:* channel is **ideal (no noise)** and signals have **$L$ discrete levels**.
- *Shannon:* channel is **band-limited**, noise is **additive white Gaussian (AWGN)**, and the formula gives the *error-free* theoretical maximum.

> **[4 marks for the table; 2 marks for assumptions; 1 mark for a clear conclusion.]**

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Delays — 4 marks**

**Propagation delay:**

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2{,}500{,}000 \text{ m}}{2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Transmission delay:**

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{\text{Size}}{\text{Rate}} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 0.005 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Total latency — 1 mark**

$$
T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} = 10 + 5 = 15 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) With 10 repeaters — 2 marks**

Each repeater adds a small **processing delay**; assume $T_{\text{rep}} \approx 0$ (negligible, ideal case). The propagation delay is now broken into **10 equal sub-links**:

$$
T_{\text{prop,new}} = T_{\text{prop}} = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(unchanged — repeaters do not reduce propagation)}
$$

$$
T_{\text{latency,new}} \approx T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} = 15 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important concept:** Repeaters amplify/regenerate the signal but **do not reduce propagation delay**. They only restore amplitude, fighting attenuation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Data** is information; **Signal** is the physical representation of data.
- [x] Signals have **amplitude, frequency, and phase** — a signal is fully characterized by these three.
- [x] **Bandwidth** is the width of the usable frequency spectrum, in **Hz**.
- [x] **Bit rate** = bits per second; **Baud rate** = symbols per second; related by $\log_2 L$.
- [x] **Nyquist (noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2 L$ — depends on $L$.
- [x] **Shannon (noisy):** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — depends on SNR.
- [x] **dB to linear:** $S/N = 10^{(\text{dB}/10)}$; **linear to dB:** $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$.
- [x] **Three impairments:** Attenuation, Distortion, Noise.
- [x] **Thermal noise** $N = kTB$, with Boltzmann constant $k \approx 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- [x] **Modes:** Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex.
- [x] **Serial vs Parallel**, **Asynchronous vs Synchronous**, **Baseband vs Broadband**.
- [x] **Latency** = Propagation + Transmission + Queueing + Processing delays.
- [x] **Repeaters** restore amplitude but **do not reduce** propagation delay.
- [x] In KTU exam, **always state units** in the final answer (Hz, bps, ms, etc.).
- [x] **Shannon's limit is absolute** — no encoding scheme can exceed it; this is the **ceiling**.
- [x] **Nyquist's limit is the target** — achievable with a properly designed encoder; this is the **floor** for any real design constrained by a chosen $L$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway for KTU Board:** "Nyquist tells you **how to design** your encoder; Shannon tells you **how good it can possibly get** under noise."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog and Digital Data Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analog and Digital Data Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Transmission** is the process of transferring data between two or more digital devices over a transmission medium. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, data transmission is classified based on the **nature of the signal** (Analog vs Digital) and the **nature of the data** being transmitted.

**Analog Data Transmission** uses a continuously variable signal whose amplitude, phase, or frequency varies in direct proportion to the source information (e.g., traditional FM radio broadcast, analog telephone lines).

**Digital Data Transmission** uses a discrete, binary signal that switches between a finite set of voltage levels (typically two: HIGH and LOW), representing information as a sequence of 1s and 0s (e.g., Ethernet, Wi-Fi, optical fiber links).

### 1.2 The Four Fundamental Combinations

| Data Type \ Signal Type | Analog Signal | Digital Signal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Analog Data** | Analog-to-Analog (e.g., AM Radio) | Analog-to-Digital Conversion (e.g., CD Audio) |
| **Digital Data** | Digital-to-Analog (e.g., Modem over telephone) | Digital-to-Digital (e.g., LAN cables, HDMI) |

> [!NOTE]
> The four fundamental combinations are: **Analog-to-Analog**, **Analog-to-Digital (ADC)**, **Digital-to-Analog (DAC)**, and **Digital-to-Digital (Encoding)**. KTU examiners frequently frame 3-mark questions on this classification.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Think of data transmission as water flowing through a pipe:**
> - **Analog Transmission** is like a **smooth, continuously variable flow** of water — you can set the tap to any position from 0 to 100%, and the flow smoothly changes. This is how human voice and video traditionally work.
> - **Digital Transmission** is like a **series of on/off pulses** — the tap is either fully open (1) or fully closed (0). The pulses arrive at exact intervals, and even if small amounts of noise interfere, we can clearly distinguish ON from OFF.
> - **Why digital wins long-distance:** A whispered message (analog) gets corrupted over a noisy distance, but a series of "Yes/No" coded clicks (digital) can be perfectly reconstructed at the receiver.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Bandwidth (B):** The range of frequencies a channel can carry, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Bit Rate (C):** The number of bits transmitted per second, measured in **bits per second (bps)**.
- **Baud Rate (Signal Rate):** The number of *signal units (symbols)* transmitted per second, measured in **symbols/second** or **baud**.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** The ratio of signal power to noise power, a dimensionless quantity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship between **Bit Rate** and **Baud Rate** is governed by:
> $$ \text{Bit Rate} = \text{Baud Rate} \times \log_2(L) $$
> where $L$ is the number of discrete signal levels (symbols) per signal unit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Transmission Theory

#### Step 1 — Why Bandwidth Matters
A transmission channel behaves like a **low-pass filter**. It passes low frequencies well but attenuates high frequencies. The **usable bandwidth** of a channel directly limits the **maximum data rate** we can achieve. The KTU syllabus requires mastery of two foundational theorems that quantify this limit.

#### Step 2 — The Nyquist Theorem (Noiseless Channel)
> [!NOTE]
> **Harry Nyquist (1924)** proved that for a **noiseless channel** of bandwidth $B$ Hertz, the maximum data rate $C$ achievable with $L$ discrete signal levels is:
> $$ C = 2B \log_2(L) \;\;\text{bits per second (bps)} $$

- **Why?** Because a signal of bandwidth $B$ can change at most $2B$ times per second (twice per Hz — once for the positive half-cycle, once for the negative half-cycle). Each change can carry $\log_2(L)$ bits of information.
- **Real-world utility:** This is the theoretical ceiling for any channel. With only 2 levels ($L = 2$), the result is the famous $C = 2B$ rule for binary signals.

#### Step 3 — The Shannon Theorem (Noisy Channel)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Claude Shannon (1948)** extended Nyquist's result to **noisy channels**. The maximum achievable data rate (channel capacity) is:
> $$ C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \;\;\text{bits per second (bps)} $$

- **Why?** Shannon used information theory to show that noise fundamentally limits information transfer. The ratio $\text{SNR} = S/N$ (signal power to noise power) determines how much information can be reliably distinguished.
- **Key insight:** Increasing signal levels $L$ beyond what Shannon's limit allows **does not** increase throughput — the noise floor caps the data rate.

#### Step 4 — Transmission Modes
- **Simplex:** Communication in **one direction only** (e.g., Radio broadcast, TV).
- **Half-Duplex:** Communication in **both directions, but not simultaneously** (e.g., Walkie-Talkie).
- **Full-Duplex:** Communication in **both directions simultaneously** (e.g., Telephone call, modern Ethernet).

#### Step 5 — Transmission Impairments
- **Attenuation:** Loss of signal strength over distance. Compensated by **Amplifiers** (analog) or **Repeaters** (digital).
- **Distortion:** Signal shape changes due to varying propagation speeds of different frequency components.
- **Noise:** Random unwanted signals — **Thermal Noise**, **Induced Noise**, **Crosstalk**, and **Impulse Noise**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every numerical problem in ESE Module 1 derives from one of these formulas.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $C = 2B \log_2(L)$ | Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless) | $C$ in bps, $B$ in Hz, $L$ is signal levels |
| 2 | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon Capacity (Noisy) | SNR is dimensionless ratio $S/N$ |
| 3 | $\text{Bit Rate} = \text{Baud} \times \log_2(L)$ | Bit Rate vs Baud Rate | $\log_2(L)$ = bits per symbol |
| 4 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$ | SNR in decibels | dB is a logarithmic scale |
| 5 | $\text{No. of Signal Levels} = 2^{\text{bit rate} / 2B}$ | Rearranged Nyquist | Useful for level calculations |
| 6 | $N_0 = kT$ | Thermal noise power density | $k$ = Boltzmann constant, $T$ = absolute temperature |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In the Shannon formula, **SNR is a linear ratio (not in dB)**. If the problem gives SNR in dB, first convert using $\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10)}$ before substituting.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Telecommunications:** Mobile network operators (4G/5G) use Shannon's theorem to determine the maximum cell capacity and plan spectrum allocation.
- **Data Storage:** CD/DVD designers use Nyquist's theorem to maximize storage density without intersymbol interference.
- **Audio Engineering:** The 44.1 kHz sampling rate of audio CDs comes from Nyquist's $2B$ rule applied to the 20 kHz human hearing range.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Nyquist to Shannon

We now derive the Nyquist and Shannon formulas from first principles so that you can reproduce the logic in any KTU 14-mark problem.

#### Derivation A — Nyquist Bit Rate for a Binary Signal

**Starting assumption:** A signal has a maximum frequency $B$ Hz. By the sampling theorem, we need at least $2B$ samples per second to fully reconstruct it.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of signal changes per second} &= 2B \\
\text{Bits per signal change (binary case, } L = 2) &= \log_2(2) = 1 \text{ bit} \\
\therefore \text{Maximum Bit Rate } C &= 2B \times \log_2(2) \\
&= 2B \times 1 \\
C_{\text{binary}} &= 2B \;\;\text{bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Generalization to $L$ levels:** If each signal change can carry one of $L$ distinct amplitude levels, each level encodes $\log_2(L)$ bits. Multiplying gives the **Generalized Nyquist Theorem:**

$$
C = 2B \log_2(L) \;\;\text{bits per second}
$$

#### Derivation B — Shannon Channel Capacity (Conceptual Walkthrough)

Shannon's insight was that the **information content** of a message is limited by the noise floor. If signal power is $S$ and noise power is $N$, the number of reliably distinguishable signal levels is proportional to $\sqrt{1 + S/N}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{reliable}} &\propto \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}} \\
\text{Bits per sample} &= \log_2(M_{\text{reliable}}) = \frac{1}{2} \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
\text{Samples per second} &= 2B \;\;(\text{Nyquist sampling rate}) \\
\therefore C &= 2B \times \frac{1}{2} \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
C &= B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\;\text{bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of 2 cancels perfectly with the half — this elegant cancellation is the heart of Shannon's celebrated result.

#### Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A channel has a bandwidth of $4$ kHz and an SNR of $30$ dB. Find: (a) the maximum data rate using Shannon's theorem, (b) the maximum data rate using Nyquist's theorem if 16 signal levels are used, and (c) state which is the actual limiting rate.

**Solution:**

**(a) Shannon Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 30 \\
\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} &= 10^{(30/10)} = 10^3 = 1000 \\
C_{\text{Shannon}} &= B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\
&= 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \\
&= 4000 \times \log_2(1001) \\
&= 4000 \times 9.967 \;\;\text{(since } 2^{10} = 1024) \\
&\approx 39{,}868 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Nyquist Rate with $L = 16$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Nyquist}} &= 2B \log_2(L) \\
&= 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(16) \\
&= 8000 \times 4 \\
&= 32{,}000 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Actual Limiting Rate:**

Since Shannon's limit (39,868 bps) is *higher* than Nyquist's calculated rate (32,000 bps), the **Nyquist result (32 kbps) is the actual achievable rate** — but the *true theoretical maximum* the channel can support is the Shannon limit of ~39.87 kbps.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Analog vs Digital Transmission

> [!TIP]
> **This is a high-frequency KTU question. Memorize the distinctions below.**

| Parameter | Analog Transmission | Digital Transmission |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Signal Type** | Continuous waveform | Discrete pulses (1s and 0s) |
| **Noise Immunity** | Low — noise accumulates | High — uses regeneration |
| **Signal Regeneration** | Amplifiers (also amplify noise) | Repeaters (reconstruct clean signal) |
| **Bandwidth** | Generally lower | Generally higher (but compressible) |
| **Security** | Difficult to encrypt | Easy encryption & error detection |
| **Cost** | Cheaper short-term | Cheaper long-term, more flexible |
| **Typical Applications** | AM/FM radio, analog TV | Internet, mobile, LAN, VoIP |
| **Error Rate** | High in noisy channels | Extremely low with coding |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: General Data Communication System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source] --> B[Source Encoder]
    B --> C[Transmitter]
    C --> D[Channel]
    D --> E[Receiver]
    E --> F[Destination Decoder]
    F --> G[Destination]
    H[Noise Source] -.-> D
```

**Explanation of nodes:**
- **Source:** Originates the message (voice, video, data).
- **Source Encoder:** Converts information into a transmittable form (e.g., ADC for voice).
- **Transmitter:** Modulates the encoded signal for the medium (e.g., modem).
- **Channel:** Physical medium (cable, fiber, air).
- **Noise Source:** External interference (shown as a dashed line because it is unwanted).
- **Receiver:** Demodulates the incoming signal.
- **Destination Decoder:** Converts back to original form (e.g., DAC).
- **Destination:** Final user device.

### 4.2 Comparison Architecture: Analog vs Digital Transmission Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph chainA[Analog Transmission Chain]
        A1[Analog Source] --> A2[Amplifier]
        A2 --> A3[Analog Channel with Noise]
        A3 --> A4[Amplifier + Noise]
        A4 --> A5[Analog Destination]
    end
    subgraph chainB[Digital Transmission Chain]
        B1[Digital Source] --> B2[Encoder and Modulator]
        B2 --> B3[Digital Channel with Noise]
        B3 --> B4[Regenerative Repeater Cleans Signal]
        B4 --> B5[Decoder and Demodulator]
        B5 --> B6[Digital Destination]
    end
```

**Architectural Insight:** In the analog chain, **every amplifier boosts both the signal and the accumulated noise**, leading to progressive degradation. In the digital chain, **each regenerative repeater reconstructs a clean, perfect pulse stream**, effectively eliminating noise at every hop — this is why digital dominates long-distance transmission.

### 4.3 Transmission Modes Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph m1[Simplex Mode]
        S1A[Station A] -->|Always One Way| S1B[Station B]
    end
    subgraph m2[Half Duplex Mode]
        S2A[Station X] -->|One Way at a Time| S2B[Station Y]
        S2B -.->|Not Simultaneous| S2A
    end
    subgraph m3[Full Duplex Mode]
        S3A[Node P] <-->|Both Ways Simultaneous| S3B[Node Q]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Differentiate between Analog and Digital data transmission. List any four transmission impairments.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Analog Transmission | Digital Transmission |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nature of Signal | Continuous, smoothly varying | Discrete, binary pulses |
| Noise Immunity | Poor — noise is amplified along with signal | High — repeaters regenerate clean signal |
| Bandwidth | Generally lower | Generally higher |
| Equipment Cost | Lower initial | Lower long-term |
| Error Detection | Difficult | Easy using CRC, parity |
| Best Use Case | Voice, radio broadcast | Data networks, internet |

**Four Transmission Impairits:**
1. **Attenuation** — loss of signal power with distance.
2. **Distortion** — change in signal shape due to different frequency components traveling at different speeds.
3. **Noise** — random unwanted energy (thermal, induced, crosstalk, impulse).
4. **Delay Distortion** — different frequencies arrive at different times.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for the tabular difference, and 0.5 mark each for naming the four impairments (0.5 × 4 = 2 marks). Always draw the table.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State and explain the Nyquist Theorem for a noiseless channel. A noiseless channel has a bandwidth of 3 kHz. Find the maximum data rate if 8 signal levels are used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** For a noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz, the maximum bit rate $C$ that can be transmitted using $L$ discrete signal levels is:

$$
C = 2B \log_2(L) \;\;\text{bits per second}
$$

**Given:** $B = 3000$ Hz, $L = 8$ signal levels.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2 \times 3000 \times \log_2(8) \\
&= 6000 \times 3 \\
&= 18{,}000 \text{ bps} = 18 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The maximum data rate is **18 kbps**.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** State and explain the Shannon-Hartley Theorem. Discuss the significance of the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) in determining channel capacity. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A communication channel has a bandwidth of 8 kHz. Calculate the maximum data rate using:
   (i) Nyquist Theorem with 4 signal levels.
   (ii) Shannon Theorem if the SNR is 1023.
   (iii) State which is the actual limiting rate and justify. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

> **Shannon-Hartley Theorem (1948):** The theoretical maximum data rate (channel capacity) of a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with a signal-to-noise ratio $\text{SNR} = S/N$ is given by:
> $$ C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \;\;\text{bits per second} $$
> This is the **absolute upper limit** on reliable information transfer — no encoding scheme can exceed it.

**Significance of SNR (Key Points — 1 Mark each):**

1. **Noise Floor:** SNR measures how strong the desired signal is relative to background noise. Higher SNR means a cleaner channel.
2. **Capacity is Logarithmic:** Capacity grows **logarithmically** with SNR, not linearly. Doubling the power less than doubles the capacity.
3. **Theoretical Ceiling:** Shannon's limit is the *maximum possible* rate; in practice, real systems operate at 50–80% of this limit.
4. **Independent of Encoding:** The theorem is independent of how bits are encoded — it is a fundamental physical limit.
5. **Trade-off Insight:** To double capacity, we can either **double the bandwidth** or **square the SNR** (since $C \propto B \log_2(1+\text{SNR})$).
6. **Engineering Implication:** This guides modem designers, satellite link budgets, and 5G spectral efficiency targets.
7. **Example:** A 1 MHz channel with SNR = 63 ($10\log_{10}(63) \approx 18$ dB) has capacity $C = 10^6 \log_2(64) = 6$ Mbps.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $B = 8000$ Hz, $L = 4$, $\text{SNR} = 1023$.

**(i) Nyquist Theorem (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Nyquist}} &= 2B \log_2(L) \\
&= 2 \times 8000 \times \log_2(4) \\
&= 16000 \times 2 \\
&= 32{,}000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Shannon Theorem (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Shannon}} &= B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\
&= 8000 \times \log_2(1 + 1023) \\
&= 8000 \times \log_2(1024) \\
&= 8000 \times 10 \\
&= 80{,}000 \text{ bps} = 80 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Actual Limiting Rate (1 Mark):**

The Nyquist result (32 kbps) is **less than** the Shannon ceiling (80 kbps), so the **Nyquist-calculated rate of 32 kbps is the actual achievable data rate** for this configuration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. Forgetting to take $\log_2$ — using $\log_{10}$ instead costs **2 marks** in 14-mark problems.
> 2. Using SNR in dB inside the Shannon formula. SNR *must* be a linear ratio.
> 3. Conflating "Bit Rate" and "Baud Rate" — they differ by a factor of $\log_2(L)$.
> 4. Not stating the **final answer with units (bps or kbps)**.

---

### **Question B — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the components of a general data communication system. Discuss the role of the channel and the various types of noise affecting it. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare Analog and Digital transmission systems on the basis of: (i) Bandwidth requirement, (ii) Noise immunity, (iii) Signal regeneration method, (iv) Error detection capability, (v) Security, (vi) Cost, and (vii) Example applications. State two real-world situations where digital transmission is preferred over analog. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
[Source] → [Source Encoder] → [Transmitter] → [Channel + Noise]
                                                          ↓
[Destination] ← [Destination Decoder] ← [Receiver] ←────┘
```

**Component Roles (4 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Source:** Generates the message (human voice, video, computer file).
2. **Source Encoder:** Converts the message into a transmittable format; performs compression and analog-to-digital conversion.
3. **Transmitter:** Modulates the encoded signal and couples it to the channel (modem, antenna, line driver).
4. **Channel:** The physical medium — twisted pair, coaxial cable, optical fiber, or free-space radio. Defines the bandwidth and attenuation profile.
5. **Receiver:** Demodulates the incoming signal and recovers the baseband information.
6. **Destination Decoder:** Converts back to the original form (e.g., digital-to-analog for a speaker).
7. **Destination:** The end user device (phone, computer, TV).
8. **Noise:** External interference — **Thermal noise** (from conductor electrons), **Induced noise** (from motors/lighting), **Crosstalk** (coupling from adjacent wires), and **Impulse noise** (spikes from switching).

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (6 Marks — 0.5 each × 7 sub-parts):**

| Parameter | Analog Transmission | Digital Transmission |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Bandwidth Requirement | Lower bandwidth typically | Higher bandwidth, but compressible |
| (ii) Noise Immunity | Low — noise cannot be separated | High — noise removed by regeneration |
| (iii) Signal Regeneration | Amplifiers (amplify signal + noise) | Repeaters (reconstruct clean pulses) |
| (iv) Error Detection | Difficult, no built-in mechanism | Easy using parity, CRC, checksums |
| (v) Security | Hard to encrypt | Easy with modern crypto algorithms |
| (vi) Cost | Lower initial hardware | Lower long-term, more flexible |
| (vii) Applications | FM radio, analog TV, old telephony | Internet, mobile (4G/5G), VoIP, LAN |

**Two Real-World Situations Favoring Digital (1 Mark):**

1. **Undersea fiber-optic cables:** Because repeaters can fully restore a digital signal, data can cross oceans with virtually no quality loss — impossible with analog amplification.
2. **Mobile phone networks (4G/5G):** Digital transmission enables encryption, error correction, and efficient spectrum reuse via cellular techniques — making it the universal standard.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B Common Errors:**
> - Students often write long paragraphs instead of a **clean tabular comparison** — KTU examiners expect the table format for full marks.
> - Forgetting to **draw the block diagram** in part (a) costs 3 marks instantly.
> - Listing only **types of noise** without explaining their **effect on the channel** leads to a 1-mark deduction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This Before Every Exam:**

- 🔹 **Two Theorem Pillars:** Nyquist (noiseless) → $C = 2B \log_2(L)$. Shannon (noisy) → $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
- 🔹 **Bandwidth limits data rate** — physically, a channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz can change signal at most $2B$ times per second.
- 🔹 **Bit Rate ≠ Baud Rate** — Bit Rate = Baud Rate $\times \log_2(L)$. With 4 levels, 1 baud = 2 bits.
- 🔹 **SNR must be linear** in Shannon's formula. Convert from dB: $\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10)}$.
- 🔹 **Shannon limit is a ceiling, not a guarantee** — real systems achieve only 50–80% of it.
- 🔹 **Three transmission modes:** Simplex (one-way), Half-Duplex (alternating), Full-Duplex (simultaneous).
- 🔹 **Four impairments:** Attenuation, Distortion, Noise, Delay Distortion.
- 🔹 **Four transmission combinations:** A-to-A, A-to-D, D-to-A, D-to-D.
- 🔹 **Digital wins long-distance** because regenerative repeaters eliminate accumulated noise.
- 🔹 **Nyquist assumes 2B samples/sec is the minimum** for perfect reconstruction (sampling theorem).
- 🔹 **Increase SNR, not just levels:** Beyond Shannon's limit, more signal levels ($L$) yield no speedup.
- 🔹 **Killer formula combos:** $2B$ for binary, $4B$ for 4-level, $6B$ for 8-level Nyquist rates.
- 🔹 **Real-world references:** 4G LTE uses ~20 MHz channels; 5G uses up to 100 MHz with advanced SNR-boosting techniques.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Impairments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transmission Impairments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Transmission Impairments?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Data Communication and Networking*, **Transmission Impairments** are defined as the undesirable phenomena that degrade the quality, fidelity, and integrity of a signal as it propagates from the transmitter (source) to the receiver (destination) across a physical communication medium. The signal that arrives at the receiver is never a perfect replica of the signal that was transmitted; it is always a **distorted, attenuated, and noise-contaminated** version of the original.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Transmission impairments refer to the various mechanisms through which signals traveling across a physical medium (copper, fibre, wireless) suffer from attenuation, distortion, and noise, thereby limiting the maximum achievable data rate, distance, and bit error rate (BER) of the communication link."*

The three principal causes of transmission impairment recognized in the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus are:

1. **Attenuation** — Loss of signal power over distance.
2. **Distortion** — Alteration of the signal's shape or frequency components.
3. **Noise** — Unwanted random electrical energy added to the signal.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Whispering Gallery

Imagine standing at one end of a **long, crowded railway platform** and trying to whisper a secret message to a friend at the far end.

- The **volume (loudness)** of your whisper **decreases** the farther it travels — by the time it reaches your friend, it is barely audible. This is **Attenuation**.
- The **clarity** of the message becomes fuzzy because the high-pitched consonants (like "s" and "t") fade faster than the lower-pitched vowels. Your friend hears "me_e_ y_u to_o_r_ow" instead of "meet you tomorrow." This is **Distortion**.
- While your whisper travels, **announcements over the public address (PA) system**, the **clatter of train wheels**, and **people chattering nearby** add to your whisper, making it harder to hear. These unwanted intrusions are **Noise**.

In the same way, an electrical signal traveling through a copper wire, optical fibre, or wireless channel suffers from **power loss, shape deformation, and additive interference**, all of which collectively constitute transmission impairments.

## 1.3 Why Transmission Impairments Matter in KTU 2024 Engineering

In modern digital communication systems (4G/5G, optical fibre backbones, Ethernet LANs), every **decibel (dB)** of impairment directly limits:

- The maximum **bit rate (bps)** the channel can carry.
- The maximum **link distance** before repeaters/amplifiers are needed.
- The achievable **Bit Error Rate (BER)**.
- The **Channel Capacity** as defined by the **Nyquist** and **Shannon-Hartley** theorems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:**
> Forouzan (Data Communications and Networking) classifies impairments into **Attenuation, Distortion, and Noise** — this exact three-fold classification is asked frequently in the KTU University Examination Part A and Part B questions. Memorize the three types in this order.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Impairment #1 — Attenuation

### 2.1.1 Definition
**Attenuation** is the gradual loss of signal strength (amplitude/power) as the signal propagates through a transmission medium. It is a **distance-dependent** phenomenon governed by the physical properties of the medium.

### 2.1.2 Physical Causes
- **Copper wire:** Resistance of the conductor causes ohmic (I²R) losses; higher frequencies suffer greater skin-effect losses.
- **Optical fibre:** Scattering (Rayleigh scattering), absorption by impurities, and bending losses.
- **Wireless (free space):** Spreading of the electromagnetic wave as it expands spherically from the antenna (free-space path loss).

### 2.1.3 Measurement: The Decibel (dB)

Signal attenuation is **not** measured linearly because the human ear (and most detectors) responds **logarithmically** to power. The industry-standard unit is the **decibel (dB)**.

The decibel expresses the ratio between two power levels on a logarithmic scale. Two separate dB formulas exist:

**Case 1: When measuring the ratio of two power levels (dB):**

$$\text{Loss (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$$

**Case 2: When measuring the absolute power relative to a reference of 1 milliwatt (dBm):**

$$P_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{mW}}}{1~\text{mW}}\right)$$

**Case 3: When measuring the ratio of two voltages across equal impedances (dBV):**

$$\text{Gain/Loss (dBV)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\times 20$ for voltage and $\times 10$ for power?**
> Because power is proportional to the *square* of voltage ($P = V^2/R$), the logarithm of a square becomes twice the logarithm: $\log(V^2) = 2 \log(V)$. This is a common derivation step often asked in KTU Part A questions.

### 2.1.4 Engineering Utility of the Decibel
- It allows **cascaded losses/gains** to be added arithmetically instead of multiplied.
- It compresses enormous dynamic ranges (e.g., 1 pW to 1 W) into manageable numbers ($-90$ dBm to $0$ dBm).
- It provides a **standardized unit** used identically by electrical, optical, and acoustic engineers.

## 2.2 Impairment #2 — Distortion

### 2.2.1 Definition
**Distortion** is the impairment in which the **shape** or **frequency composition** of a signal is altered as it travels through a medium. Unlike attenuation (which scales the signal uniformly), distortion changes the **relative amplitudes** of the signal's frequency components.

### 2.2.2 Physical Cause
Every real channel has a **non-flat frequency response** — meaning the channel attenuates different frequencies by different amounts. This is the defining property of a **band-limited, dispersive channel**. The frequency-dependent behaviour of the channel can be modelled using a **transfer function** $H(f)$, which describes how each frequency $f$ is scaled and phase-shifted.

### 2.2.3 Practical Consequence
A composite signal carrying **multiple frequencies** (e.g., a square wave with its fundamental, 3rd harmonic, 5th harmonic) will have its **harmonics** attenuated differently. The reconstructed waveform at the receiver will no longer look like the transmitted square wave — its edges will become rounded, and its amplitude will droop. This is the visual signature of distortion on an oscilloscope.

## 2.3 Impairment #3 — Noise

### 2.3.1 Definition
**Noise** is any **unwanted random energy** added to the signal by the medium, the transmitter, the receiver, or external sources, which interferes with the correct interpretation of the original information.

### 2.3.2 The Five KTU-Mandated Categories of Noise

**1. Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise):**
- Generated by the **thermal agitation of electrons** inside any conductor above absolute zero (0 K).
- It is **uniformly distributed** across all frequencies → hence called **White Noise**.
- Governed by the **Boltzmann constant** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- Power spectral density (PSD): $N_0 = kT$ watts per hertz.

**2. Intermodulation Noise:**
- Produced when **two or more signals of different frequencies share the same medium** and the non-linearities of the channel cause them to **mix**.
- The mixing creates **spurious frequencies** equal to the sums and differences of the original frequencies: $f_1 + f_2$, $f_1 - f_2$, $2f_1$, $2f_2$, etc.
- A direct consequence of **non-linear amplification** or **non-linear modulation** in the channel.

**3. Crosstalk:**
- The **unwanted coupling** of signals between physically adjacent channels.
- Analogous to hearing another person's phone conversation bleeding into your own phone line.
- In copper cables: caused by **electromagnetic induction** between adjacent twisted pairs.
- In fibre: caused by **leakage between cores** in poorly jacketed cables.
- Mitigated by **twisting pairs with different lay lengths**, **shielding (STP)**, and **increased physical separation**.

**4. Impulse Noise:**
- **Irregular, sudden, high-amplitude spikes** of short duration.
- Caused by external electromagnetic disturbances: lightning, switching relays, faulty electrical equipment, ESD (electrostatic discharge).
- It is the **most damaging** to digital data because a single spike can corrupt **multiple bits** in a row, causing burst errors.
- Not modelled statistically by a Gaussian distribution — it is **non-Gaussian, non-white**.

**5. Induced Noise (Bonus — sometimes listed in KTU textbooks):**
- Noise picked up from external sources like fluorescent lights, motors, and radio transmitters acting as unwanted antennas.

### 2.3.3 The Key Performance Metric: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

The single most important metric that quantifies the impact of noise on a communication link is the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**.

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{\text{Average Signal Power}}{\text{Average Noise Power}} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}$$

Since SNR is a ratio, it is often expressed in decibels:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR})$$

A high SNR means the signal is **much stronger** than the noise → low bit error rate. A low SNR means the signal is **swamped** by noise → high bit error rate.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Revision Ready)

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Attenuation Loss (dB) | $10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}} / P_{\text{out}})$ | dB | Power loss between two points in a link |
| Attenuation per unit length | $\alpha = -\frac{10}{L} \log_{10}(P_{\text{out}}/P_{\text{in}})$ | dB/km or dB/m | Cable specification used in link-budget design |
| Power referenced to 1 mW | $P_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{mW}} / 1)$ | dBm | Absolute power level in optical and wireless links |
| Voltage ratio (dB) | $20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{out}} / V_{\text{in}})$ | dB | Loss expressed using voltage across matched impedances |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (linear) | $\text{SNR} = P_{\text{signal}} / P_{\text{noise}}$ | dimensionless (W/W) | Quality of the received signal |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB) | $10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR})$ | dB | Standard form used by RF/optical engineers |
| Thermal noise PSD | $N_0 = kT$ | W/Hz | Johnson-Nyquist noise floor |
| Thermal noise power | $N = kTB$ | W | Total noise power in bandwidth $B$ |
| Boltzmann constant | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K | Fundamental constant of thermal noise |
| Nyquist channel capacity (noiseless) | $C = 2B \log_{2} M$ | bps | Max bit rate for bandwidth $B$ with $M$ discrete signal levels |
| Shannon channel capacity (noisy) | $C = B \log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps | Max bit rate for noisy bandwidth-limited channel |
| Spurious intermodulation frequencies | $mf_1 \pm nf_2$ | Hz | Sum/difference products due to channel non-linearity |
| Noise temperature | $T_N = N / (kB)$ | K | Equivalent thermal noise of any source |
| Noise figure | $F = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} / \text{SNR}_{\text{out}}$ | dimensionless | How much SNR degradation an amplifier introduces |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the $\times 10$ vs $\times 20$ rule** for power vs voltage in dB. KTU examiners regularly test this in 3-mark Part A questions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical fibre design:** The attenuation per kilometre ($\alpha$) determines the spacing of optical repeaters/EDFAs in trans-oceanic links (typical $\alpha \approx 0.2$ dB/km at 1550 nm).
- **Satellite link budgets:** The SNR at the receiver determines the achievable bit rate and the required dish diameter.
- **5G cellular networks:** The Shannon-Hartley limit $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ governs the **maximum spectral efficiency (bps/Hz)** the base station can offer to a user.
- **Audio engineering:** The 60 dB SNR of a CD vs the 96 dB SNR of high-resolution audio is a direct application of the same logarithmic ratio.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why $20 \log_{10}$ for Voltage Ratios

**Problem:** Prove that the decibel representation of a voltage ratio uses the factor 20, while a power ratio uses the factor 10.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of decibel for power:**
The decibel is defined as **one-tenth of a bel**, where the bel is the base-10 logarithm of a power ratio:

$$N_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the power-voltage relationship:**
In any circuit where both voltages are measured across the same impedance $R$, the power dissipated is given by $P = V^2 / R$. Substituting:

$$N_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_2^2 / R}{V_1^2 / R}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the equal impedances:**
Because both $V_1$ and $V_2$ are measured across the *same* impedance $R$, the $R$ terms cancel:

$$N_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_2^2}{V_1^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the logarithm power rule:**
Using the algebraic identity $\log_{10}(a^b) = b \cdot \log_{10}(a)$:

$$N_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot 2 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Result:** The factor **20** appears naturally because power is proportional to voltage **squared**, and the logarithm of a square is twice the logarithm of the base. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Attenuation Loss in Decibels — Numerical Worked Example

**Problem (KTU 2024 Typical 7-Mark Style):** A signal of power $P_{\text{in}} = 100$ mW is launched into a transmission line. The receiver at the far end measures $P_{\text{out}} = 0.1$ mW. Calculate (a) the attenuation in dB, and (b) the attenuation in dB per kilometre if the line is 50 km long.
