**Part (a) — Total attenuation in dB:**

$$\text{Loss}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$$

Substitute the values (note: mW units cancel, leaving a dimensionless ratio):

$$\text{Loss}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{100}{0.1}\right)$$

$$\text{Loss}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1000)$$

$$\text{Loss}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \times 3 = 30~\text{dB}$$

**Part (b) — Attenuation per kilometre:**

$$\alpha = \frac{\text{Total Loss in dB}}{\text{Length}} = \frac{30~\text{dB}}{50~\text{km}} = 0.6~\text{dB/km}$$

**Result:** The cable loses **30 dB of signal power** over the 50 km link, which is a 1000-fold reduction in power. This is a typical specification for legacy twisted-pair telecom cables. ∎

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SNR in dB from Linear SNR

**Problem:** A Wi-Fi receiver measures a signal power of $4 \times 10^{-8}$ W and a noise power of $1.6 \times 10^{-9}$ W. Compute the SNR in linear units and in dB.

**Step 1 — Compute the linear SNR:**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-8}}{1.6 \times 10^{-9}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the ratio:**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{4}{1.6} \times 10^{-8-(-9)} = 2.5 \times 10^{1} = 25$$

**Step 3 — Convert to decibels:**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(25) = 10 \times 1.3979 = 13.98~\text{dB}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** A linear SNR of 25 means the signal is **25 times stronger** than the noise. In dB, this is about **14 dB** — sufficient for reliable digital communication (typical Wi-Fi systems require at least 10 dB SNR to maintain a 10⁻⁵ BER). ∎

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Thermal Noise Power in a Resistor

**Problem:** Derive the formula for the thermal noise power $N$ available in a resistor of bandwidth $B$ at absolute temperature $T$.

**Step 1 — Start from the Johnson-Nyquist theorem for noise voltage across a resistor:**

$$\overline{V_n^2} = 4 k T B R$$

where $k$ is the Boltzmann constant, $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin, $B$ is the noise bandwidth in Hz, and $R$ is the resistance in ohms.

**Step 2 — Use the maximum power transfer theorem:**
The maximum noise power that can be delivered by the resistor to a matched load (also of resistance $R$) is:

$$N = \frac{\overline{V_n^2}}{4R}$$

(The factor of 4 arises because the load is matched in series.)

**Step 3 — Substitute the noise voltage squared expression:**

$$N = \frac{4 k T B R}{4 R}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the $4R$ terms:**

$$N = k T B$$

**Result:** The thermal noise power is **independent of the resistance value**! It depends only on temperature, bandwidth, and the Boltzmann constant. For a typical 1 MHz receiver bandwidth at room temperature ($T = 300$ K):

$$N = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times (10^6) = 4.14 \times 10^{-15}~\text{W} \approx -114~\text{dBm}$$

This is the famous **thermal noise floor** that limits the sensitivity of every radio receiver on Earth. ∎

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of the Three Impairments

| **Property** | **Attenuation** | **Distortion** | **Noise** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What it affects** | Signal amplitude/power | Signal shape/frequency content | Adds unwanted energy to the signal |
| **Depends on distance?** | Yes (linearly with length) | No (depends on frequency response) | Generally no (localized at the source of noise) |
| **Depends on frequency?** | Often yes (higher freq → more loss) | Yes (different freq components attenuated differently) | Thermal: uniform; Impulse: broadband spikes |
| **Reversible?** | Yes — by amplifiers or repeaters | No — shape information is lost | No — random by nature |
| **Measured in** | dB (loss) | Bit Error Rate (BER) | SNR (dB) |
| **Primary cause** | Resistance, scattering, path loss | Non-flat channel frequency response $H(f)$ | Thermal agitation, interference, crosstalk |
| **Worst case scenario** | Long undersea cables without repeaters | Wideband signals over band-limited media | Industrial environments with heavy EMI |
| **KTU question weightage** | Very high (definitions + numerics) | Moderate (conceptual + diagram-based) | High (all five types + Shannon application) |
| **Mitigation strategy** | Repeaters, amplifiers, EDFAs (fibre) | Equalizers, adaptive filters, OFDM | Shielding, filtering, error-correction coding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU often frames a Part B question asking *"Compare attenuation, distortion, and noise with examples."* Use the matrix above as a memory anchor — pick **4–5 rows** and write a paragraph for each to comfortably score 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The End-to-End Communication Link with Impairments

This Mermaid block diagram shows how a signal is affected by all three impairments as it travels from the source to the destination, including the locations where each impairment is typically introduced.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["SOURCE: Transmitter and Modulator"]
    amp["Power Amplifier"]
    chan["TRANSMISSION MEDIUM: Copper / Fibre / Wireless"]
    attn["Attenuation Block: Power loss with distance"]
    dist["Distortion Block: Non flat frequency response Hf"]
    nse["Noise Injection Block: Thermal Intermodulation Crosstalk Impulse"]
    recv["RECEIVER: Amplifier Detector Demodulator"]
    dst["DESTINATION: Original Information Recovered"]

    src -->|Clean Signal S t| amp
    amp -->|Boosted Signal| chan
    chan --> attn
    attn --> dist
    dist --> nse
    nse -->|Degraded Signal S r| recv
    recv --> dst

    style nse fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style recv fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style dst fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of the Five Noise Types

This Mermaid tree diagram organizes the five KTU-mandated categories of noise into a clear hierarchy based on their origin.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["NOISE in Communication Systems"]
    B["INTERNAL NOISE: Generated inside the system"]
    C["EXTERNAL NOISE: Originates outside the system"]
    D["Thermal Noise: kTB, white, due to electron agitation"]
    E["Intermodulation Noise: Sum and difference frequencies from non linearities"]
    F["Shot Noise: Discrete charge carriers in semiconductors"]
    G["Crosstalk: Coupling between adjacent channels"]
    H["Impulse Noise: Lightning, switching spikes, ESD"]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    B --> E
    B --> F
    C --> G
    C --> H

    style A fill:#312e81,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff

    style E fill:#047857,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#047857,color:#ffffff

    style H fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 The Functional Architecture of Impairment Compensation

A professional-grade receiver does not simply accept the degraded signal — it actively compensates for each impairment. The block diagram below shows the **architecture of impairment compensation** in a modern digital receiver.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Received Signal Sr: Attenuated, Distorted, and Noisy"]
    AGC["AGC Block: Compensates attenuation by automatic gain control"]
    EQ["Adaptive Equalizer: Reverses distortion by inverse filter Hf inverse"]
    FIL["Bandpass Filter: Rejects out of band noise"]
    ECC["Error Correction Decoder: Detects and corrects impulse noise errors"]
    O["Recovered Bit Stream: Approximation of original Source"]

    R --> AGC
    AGC --> EQ
    EQ --> FIL
    FIL --> ECC
    ECC --> O

    style R fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style AGC fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style EQ fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style FIL fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style ECC fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Tip for Examiners:** When KTU asks *"How does a digital receiver overcome transmission impairments?"*, the correct answer must list **AGC → Equalizer → Filter → Error Correction** in this exact order. Writing them in random order loses 1–2 marks.

## 4.4 Signal Power Degradation vs Distance (Attenuation Profile)

This Mermaid line chart visualizes how the signal power (in dBm) decreases linearly as a function of distance when expressed in dB units, which is why dB is the preferred scale for link-budget analysis.

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Attenuation Profile: Signal Power vs Distance"
    x-axis "Distance in km" [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
    y-axis "Received Power in dBm" 0, -10, -20, -30, -40
    line [0, -6, -12, -18, -24, -30]
```

**Interpretation of the graph:** With an attenuation coefficient $\alpha = 0.6$ dB/km, the received power drops by 6 dB for every 10 km. After 50 km, the signal has lost 30 dB (i.e., it is now 1000× weaker in linear power). This is the standard attenuation pattern used by KTU in numerical problems.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**Q: Define transmission impairment. List and briefly explain the three main causes of transmission impairment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Transmission impairment is the degradation in the quality, strength, or fidelity of a signal as it travels from the transmitter to the receiver through a physical communication medium. The received signal is never a perfect replica of the transmitted signal.

**Three Main Causes (2 marks — 1 for naming, 1 for explanation):**

1. **Attenuation:** The loss of signal power as the signal propagates over distance. It is measured in decibels (dB) and is compensated using amplifiers or repeaters.
2. **Distortion:** The alteration in the shape of the signal caused by the channel's non-flat frequency response. Different frequency components of the signal are attenuated and phase-shifted by different amounts, changing the signal's waveform.
3. **Noise:** The unwanted random energy added to the signal from internal sources (thermal agitation, intermodulation) and external sources (crosstalk, impulse noise), reducing the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: What is Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)? How is it expressed in decibels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) is the ratio of the average signal power to the average noise power at the receiver. It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies how much the desired signal stands out above the background noise. Mathematically:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}$$

**In Decibels (1 mark):** Because SNR is often a very large or very small number, it is conventionally expressed on a logarithmic decibel scale:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$$

A high SNR (e.g., 40 dB or more) indicates a clean, reliable link. A low SNR (e.g., less than 10 dB) indicates the signal is corrupted by noise and the BER will be unacceptably high.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Explain the three main types of transmission impairments in detail. For each impairment, state its physical cause, its effect on the signal, and the standard engineering technique used to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal of power $500$ mW is transmitted into a 60 km cable. The received power is measured as $0.05$ mW. Calculate: (i) the total attenuation in dB, (ii) the attenuation per kilometre in dB/km, and (iii) the loss as a power ratio in linear units. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part A(a) — Three Transmission Impairments (7 marks)

**1. Attenuation (2 marks):**
- **Physical cause:** Ohmic resistance in copper cables, Rayleigh scattering and absorption in optical fibre, and free-space path loss in wireless channels.
- **Effect on signal:** Signal power decreases exponentially with distance; a 30 dB loss means the signal is 1000× weaker.
- **Mitigation:** Use of **repeaters** (for digital signals — regenerate the bit pattern) and **amplifiers** (for analog signals — boost the analog waveform), or **EDFA** (Erbium-Doped Fibre Amplifiers) in long-haul optical links.

**2. Distortion (2 marks):**
- **Physical cause:** The transmission medium has a non-flat **frequency response** $H(f)$. Different frequency components of the signal are attenuated by different amounts and delayed by different phase shifts.
- **Effect on signal:** The shape of the waveform changes — for example, square waves become rounded, harmonics are lost, and composite signals become smeared in time (inter-symbol interference).
- **Mitigation:** Use of **equalizers** (filters with the inverse frequency response, $H(f)^{-1}$), **adaptive filters** that track the channel, and **OFDM modulation** which is robust to frequency-selective fading.

**3. Noise (2 marks):**
- **Physical cause:** Unwanted random electrical energy from thermal agitation of electrons (Johnson-Nyquist noise), intermodulation between co-propagating signals, crosstalk from adjacent channels, and impulse spikes from external EMI.
- **Effect on signal:** Random fluctuations are superimposed on the signal, reducing the SNR and increasing the Bit Error Rate (BER).
- **Mitigation:** **Shielding** of cables (STP), **bandpass filtering** at the receiver, **error-correction coding** (Hamming, Reed-Solomon, LDPC), and **spread-spectrum techniques** to spread the noise.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** All three impairments collectively limit the channel capacity as described by the Shannon-Hartley theorem $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ and the Nyquist criterion $C = 2B \log_2 M$.

#### Solution to Part A(b) — Numerical Attenuation Problem (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Transmitted power: $P_{\text{in}} = 500$ mW
- Received power: $P_{\text{out}} = 0.05$ mW
- Cable length: $L = 60$ km

**(i) Total attenuation in dB (3 marks):**

$$\text{Loss}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$$

$$\text{Loss}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{500}{0.05}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(10000)$$

$$\text{Loss}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \times 4 = 40~\text{dB}$$

**(ii) Attenuation per kilometre (2 marks):**

$$\alpha = \frac{\text{Total Loss}}{L} = \frac{40~\text{dB}}{60~\text{km}} = 0.667~\text{dB/km}$$

**(iii) Linear power loss ratio (2 marks):**

$$\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}} = 10^{(\text{Loss in dB}/10)} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10000$$

**Verification:** This means the received signal is **10,000 times weaker** than the transmitted signal — only 0.01% of the original power survives the 60 km link, confirming the 40 dB loss.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the five major categories of noise that affect a communication channel. For each category, identify its physical origin and state one practical mitigation technique. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital receiver operating at room temperature ($T = 300$ K) has an effective noise bandwidth of $B = 10$ MHz. Calculate: (i) the thermal noise power in watts, (ii) the thermal noise power in dBm, and (iii) the SNR in dB if the received signal power is $P_s = -80$ dBm. Use $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part B(a) — Five Categories of Noise (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** A clear hierarchy showing "Noise" branching into "Internal" and "External" categories, with the five sub-types listed below.

**1. Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise) (1.5 marks):**
- **Physical origin:** Random thermal agitation of electrons inside any conductor at a temperature $T > 0$ K.
- **Characteristics:** White (uniform PSD), Gaussian-distributed, unavoidable.
- **Mitigation:** Cooling the receiver front-end (e.g., cryogenic LNAs in radio telescopes), reducing the bandwidth, using low-noise components.

**2. Intermodulation Noise (1.5 marks):**
- **Physical origin:** Non-linearities in the channel or amplifiers cause two frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ to mix, producing sum and difference frequencies $mf_1 \pm nf_2$.
- **Characteristics:** Appears as spurious sidebands near the carrier.
- **Mitigation:** Use of **linear amplifiers**, proper **input back-off**, and **bandpass filters** to remove the out-of-band products.

**3. Crosstalk (1.5 marks):**
- **Physical origin:** Electromagnetic coupling between physically adjacent channels (e.g., adjacent twisted pairs in a cable).
- **Characteristics:** Bandwidth-limited, often near-end (NEXT) or far-end (FEXT).
- **Mitigation:** Use of **shielded cables (STP)**, **twisted pairs with different lay lengths**, and **increased physical separation** between cables.

**4. Impulse Noise (1.5 marks):**
- **Physical origin:** Sudden, high-amplitude electromagnetic spikes from lightning, relay switching, ESD, or faulty industrial equipment.
- **Characteristics:** Irregular, short-duration, non-Gaussian; the most damaging for digital data (causes burst errors).
- **Mitigation:** **Error-correction coding** (Reed-Solomon, convolutional codes), **interleaving** to spread the burst errors, and **optical isolation**.

#### Solution to Part B(b) — Thermal Noise Power Numerical (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Temperature: $T = 300$ K
- Bandwidth: $B = 10~\text{MHz} = 10^7$ Hz
- Boltzmann constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- Signal power: $P_s = -80$ dBm

**(i) Thermal noise power in watts (3 marks):**

$$N = k T B = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times (10^7)$$

Compute step by step:
- $1.38 \times 300 = 414$
- $414 \times 10^{-23} = 4.14 \times 10^{-21}$
- $4.14 \times 10^{-21} \times 10^7 = 4.14 \times 10^{-14}$ W

$$N = 4.14 \times 10^{-14}~\text{W}$$

**(ii) Thermal noise power in dBm (2 marks):**

Convert to milliwatts first: $N = 4.14 \times 10^{-14}$ W $= 4.14 \times 10^{-11}$ mW.

$$N_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}(4.14 \times 10^{-11}) = 10 \times [\log_{10}(4.14) + (-11)]$$

$$N_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \times [0.617 - 11] = 10 \times (-10.383) = -103.83~\text{dBm}$$

**(iii) SNR in dB (2 marks):**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = P_{s,\text{dBm}} - N_{\text{dBm}} = -80 - (-103.83) = 23.83~\text{dB}$$

**Interpretation:** An SNR of 23.83 dB is sufficient for reliable digital communication (linear SNR ≈ 242). This is a typical figure for a well-designed 10 MHz bandwidth receiver.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #1 — Confusing dB with dBm:**
> Students frequently interchange **dB** (a ratio, dimensionless) with **dBm** (absolute power referenced to 1 mW). Remember: dB is used when comparing two powers; dBm is used when stating an absolute power level. Using dBm where dB is required (and vice versa) costs 1–2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #2 — Forgetting the $\times 20$ factor for voltage:**
> If the question gives voltages (not powers), the formula changes to $20 \log_{10}(V_2 / V_1)$. Many students use $\times 10$ for voltage ratios, leading to a factor-of-2 error in dB.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #3 — Writing only the formula and no explanation for SNR:**
> KTU examiners allocate marks for *both* the formula *and* its interpretation. Always conclude with: *"A higher SNR indicates a cleaner link with lower BER."*

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #4 — Conflating Thermal Noise with Shot Noise:**
> Thermal noise arises in **resistors** (passive elements) due to electron motion, while shot noise arises in **semiconductor junctions** (active devices) due to discrete carrier injection. Do not use these terms interchangeably.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #5 — Forgetting to convert units before applying the dB formula:**
> Watts and milliwatts are *not* equal in the dB formula. Either convert all powers to the same unit (W or mW) before substituting, or use the dBm formula which automatically handles the conversion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #6 — Confusing "frequency" with "bandwidth":**
> A signal has a center frequency; it occupies a **bandwidth** $B$ (range of frequencies). Thermal noise depends on **bandwidth $B$**, not on the carrier frequency. This distinction appears in KTU numericals where students incorrectly use $f$ instead of $B$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Transmission Impairments** are unavoidable degradations that affect every signal as it travels through any physical medium.
- **The three principal impairments** are: **Attenuation** (loss of power), **Distortion** (change in shape), and **Noise** (added unwanted energy).
- **Attenuation** is measured in **decibels (dB)** using $10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}} / P_{\text{out}})$; voltage ratios use the factor **20** instead of 10.
- **Attenuation per unit length** is the cable specification $\alpha$ in dB/km, used in link-budget design.
- **Distortion** is caused by the **non-flat frequency response** $H(f)$ of the channel; it changes the relative amplitudes of frequency components.
- **Noise** has **five KTU-mandated types**: Thermal (white, $kTB$), Intermodulation ($mf_1 \pm nf_2$), Crosstalk (coupling between channels), Impulse (spikes, most damaging to digital data), and Shot (in semiconductors).
- **SNR** is the most important link-quality metric; expressed in dB as $10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$; high SNR means a clean link.
- **Thermal noise power** $N = kTB$ is **independent of resistance** and depends only on temperature, bandwidth, and the Boltzmann constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- **The Shannon-Hartley theorem** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ sets the absolute upper limit on the bit rate over a noisy channel.
- **The Nyquist formula** $C = 2B \log_2 M$ sets the limit for a **noiseless** channel with $M$ discrete signal levels.
- **The decibel is logarithmic** → cascading losses/gains **add** instead of multiply.
- **Inter-symbol interference (ISI)** is a direct consequence of distortion in band-limited channels and is mitigated by equalizers.
- **Impulse noise** is the most damaging to digital data because a single spike can corrupt multiple consecutive bits (burst errors).
- **Crosstalk** can be near-end (NEXT) or far-end (FEXT), depending on the direction of the offending signal relative to the receiver.
- **Intermodulation products** are mathematically $mf_1 \pm nf_2$ for two carriers $f_1$ and $f_2$; the order is $m + n$.
- **For KTU 2024 Part B questions**, always structure answers as: **Definition → Cause → Effect → Mitigation → Example → Numerical (if applicable).**
- **A link budget** in dB is the sum of all transmitter gains, antenna gains, and all losses (attenuation, free-space path loss, etc.) — it must end with a positive **margin** above the receiver sensitivity.
- **Remember the unit conversions:** 0 dBm = 1 mW; 30 dBm = 1 W; $-30$ dBm = 1 $\mu$W.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** *"Know the three impairments by name, the five noise types by category, the dB formulas by heart, and the Shannon limit by application — and you will comfortably score full marks in any Module 1 transmission impairment question."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Channel Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Channel Capacity # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Channel Capacity > [!IMPORTANT] > **Channel Capacity (C)** is the *maximum rate at which information (data bits) can be reliably transmitted over a communication channel* of a given bandwidth, subject to specific noise and signal-power constraints. It represents the theoretical upper bound of the data rate (in **bits per second, bps**) that a noiseless or noisy physical medium can support with an arbitrarily low probability of bit error. Mathematically, it defines the highest achievable bit rate $C$ such that there exists an encoding scheme capable of transmitting information at rate $R \le C$ with negligible decoding error probability. This is the central tenet of the **Noisy Channel Coding Theorem** formalized by **Claude Shannon** in 1948. ### Two Fundamental Formulations (KTU Syllabus Core): | Variant | Scenario | Author | |---|---|---| | **Nyquist Bit Rate** | Noiseless channel (only bandwidth limit) | Harry Nyquist (1928) | | **Shannon Capacity** | Noisy channel (bandwidth + noise) | Claude Shannon (1948) | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Analogy 🚗 Imagine a multi-lane highway as a **communication channel**: - **Bandwidth ($B$)** = Number of lanes → wider road = more lanes = more cars per second. - **Signal Power ($S$)** = Quality/clarity of vehicles (good headlights, clear paint) → easy to identify. - **Noise ($N$)** = Fog, rain, and potholes → makes cars harder to see. - **Channel Capacity ($C$)** = Maximum number of *identifiable* cars passing a checkpoint per second. Even with infinite lanes (infinite bandwidth), thick fog (high noise) limits how many cars you can *reliably count*. Conversely, on a clear day, even a single narrow lane can carry traffic — but only at the speed the lane allows. **Capacity** is the maximum *identifiable* flow under *both* constraints simultaneously. --- ## 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Metrics > [!NOTE] > The following constants/metrics are central to all KTU numerical problems on Channel Capacity: > - **Bandwidth ($B$)** → measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, typical voice channel = **3 kHz**, typical telephone = **4 kHz**. > - **Number of discrete signal levels ($M$)** → dimensionless integer, $M = 2^n$ where $n$ = bits per symbol. > - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio ($\text{SNR}$)** → unitless ratio, often expressed in **decibels (dB)**: $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$. > - **Shannon's absolute reference** → $k_B T \approx 4 \times 10^{-21} \text{ W/Hz}$ at room temperature ($T = 290 \text{ K}$). > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Memory Hook:** Nyquist is about *signal levels* (M); Shannon is about *noise* (S/N). If the question says "noisy," use Shannon. If it says "noiseless," use Nyquist. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Nyquist's Noiseless Channel Theorem For a **noiseless** channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz that can carry $M$ discrete signal levels per symbol, the maximum data rate is: $$C = 2B \log_2(M) \;\;\; \text{bits/second}$$ **Operational logic, step by step:** 1. **Sampling Theorem Basis**: A band-limited signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz can be perfectly reconstructed from **$2B$ samples per second** (Nyquist sampling rate). 2. **Each Sample Carries Information**: If each sample can take $M$ distinct amplitude levels, the number of bits conveyed per sample is $\log_2(M)$. 3. **Multiply**: Samples per second $\times$ Bits per sample = $2B \cdot \log_2(M)$ bits/second. 4. **Binary Special Case**: When $M = 2$ (only two levels, e.g., NRZ), then $C = 2B$ bps. > [!IMPORTANT] > Nyquist's formula is **independent of noise**. It treats the channel as mathematically perfect and only accounts for the *physical bandwidth* constraint imposed by the medium. --- ## 2.2 Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Noisy Channel) For a **noisy** channel, the absolute maximum data rate (Shannon Capacity) is: $$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\;\; \text{bits/second}$$ **Operational logic, step by step:** 1. **Bottleneck Identification**: Real channels are corrupted by thermal noise, crosstalk, and interference. Bandwidth alone is insufficient. 2. **Information-Theoretic Insight**: Shannon showed that, given sufficient coding complexity, the *channel* behaves as a "staircase" of distinguishable voltage levels whose count is $\sqrt{1 + S/N}$. 3. **Bits per Hz**: The number of bits per Hz of bandwidth is $\log_2(1 + S/N)$ — this is the **bandwidth efficiency** $\eta$. 4. **Asymptotic Behavior**: As $S/N \to \infty$, the noise becomes negligible and the formula gracefully degrades toward Nyquist's regime (for sufficiently large $M$). > [!WARNING] > Shannon's capacity is an **achievable upper bound** — it is theoretically *never exceeded* regardless of coding. The formula is dimensionless-safe: S and N must be in the same power units (both in Watts, for example). --- ## 2.3 Conversion Between Linear Ratio and Decibels The SNR in decibels is: $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$ To convert back to a linear ratio: $$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{\frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}}{10}}$$ **Quick reference table:** | SNR (dB) | S/N (linear) | |---|---| | **0 dB** | 1 | | **10 dB** | 10 | | **20 dB** | 100 | | **30 dB** | 1000 | | **40 dB** | 10000 | | **3 dB** | $\approx 2$ (half-power point) | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Key Constraint | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C = 2B \log_2 M$ | Nyquist bit rate (noiseless) | $M$ is an integer $\ge 2$ | | 2 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Shannon capacity (noisy) | $S/N$ must be in linear units | | 3 | $\eta = C/B = \log_2(1+S/N)$ | Spectral efficiency (bits/s/Hz) | $0 \le \eta \le \infty$ | | 4 | $E_b/N_0 = S/(C \cdot N)$ | Energy per bit to noise density | Used in power-limited design | | 5 | $E_b/N_0 \, (\text{dB}) = (S/N)_\text{dB} - 10\log_{10}(C/B)$ | dB-conversion of bit-energy SNR | Compare with QPSK/BPSK requirements | | 6 | $M = 2^{C/(2B)}$ | Levels required to *attain* Nyquist rate | $M$ must be an integer $\ge 2$ | | 7 | $N = k_B T B$ | Thermal noise power (Johnson–Nyquist) | $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Modem Design (Telephone Lines):** Shannon's theorem dictates that a 4 kHz voice channel with ~30 dB SNR cannot exceed ~40 kbps without advanced coding. This drove the evolution from V.32 (9.6 kbps) to V.90 (56 kbps). - **Optical Fiber Links:** Extremely high $S/N$ (~40–50 dB) plus enormous bandwidth ($\sim$ THz) gives Shannon capacities in the **Tbps** range. - **Wireless (4G/5G/6G):** Shannon's bound defines *peak spectral efficiency*. 5G targets ~30 bps/Hz using MIMO-OFDM; 6G aims for 50+ bps/Hz. - **Satellite Links:** Power-limited (very low $E_b/N_0$); trade-off between bandwidth and power is governed by Shannon's flat region. - **Data Storage:** Shannon's concepts directly apply to magnetic and SSD channel modeling. --- ## 2.6 The Capacity Triangle — Three Operating Regimes ``` Bandwidth-Limited Power-Limited (S/N large) (S/N small) | | C = B log₂(1+S/N) ────┼──────────────────────────┤ | | S/N > ~10 | S/N < ~10 | | | "Many levels, "Few levels, wide spectrum" narrow spectrum" ``` The two extreme regimes (purely bandwidth- and purely power-limited) define the design space of **all** practical communication systems. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Sampling Theorem to Nyquist Bit Rate **Goal:** Derive $C = 2B \log_2 M$ from first principles. **Step 1 — Nyquist Sampling Constraint.** A real-valued, baseband signal with maximum frequency component $B$ Hz is uniquely described by its samples taken at a rate $f_s$ where: $$f_s \ge 2B \;\;\; \text{samples/second}$$ The minimum rate $f_s = 2B$ is the **Nyquist rate**; it guarantees zero loss of information due to sampling. **Step 2 — Information per Sample.** If each sample is quantized into one of $M$ discrete amplitude levels, the number of distinct symbols the sample can represent is exactly $M$. Since each of the $M$ levels is equally likely at the *capacity* bound, the information content of one sample is: $$I_{\text{sample}} = \log_2(M) \;\;\; \text{bits}$$ **Step 3 — Combine Sample Rate and Information per Sample.** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= f_s \cdot I_{\text{sample}} \\ &= (2B) \cdot \log_2(M) \\ &= 2B \log_2(M) \;\;\; \text{bits/second} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Special Case Verification (Binary, $M=2$).** $$C = 2B \log_2(2) = 2B \cdot 1 = 2B \;\;\; \text{bps}$$ *Engineering interpretation:* A 3 kHz voice channel using 2-level signaling (e.g., on-off keying) can carry at most **6000 bps** — a foundational result of baseband digital transmission. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon-Hartley Capacity **Goal:** Show $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$ from information-theoretic reasoning. **Step 1 — Channel Model.** A continuous-time AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise) channel with bandwidth $B$ Hz and two-sided noise power spectral density $N_0/2$ has total noise power: $$N = N_0 B$$ **Step 2 — Distinguishable Signal Levels.** Shannon proved that the number of *distinguishable* signal levels (in voltage) over one symbol period is proportional to the square root of the SNR: $$L = \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}$$ (Proof: this follows from the sphere-packing argument in high-dimensional signal space; a complete proof requires geometric measure theory and is beyond this course's scope.) **Step 3 — Information per Symbol.** $$I_{\text{sym}} = \log_2(L^2) = \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\;\; \text{bits/symbol}$$ **Step 4 — Symbol Rate.** The maximum symbol rate is constrained by bandwidth to $2B$ symbols per second (Nyquist), therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} C &= f_s \cdot I_{\text{sym}} \\ &= 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\ &= B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\;\; \text{bits/second} \end{aligned} $$ The factor of $1/2$ arises because $L^2$ levels can encode twice the information per *two* symbol periods (interleaving real and imaginary parts in baseband). For real passband channels at baseband, the standard form is as above. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SNR Conversion Between Linear and dB **Step 1 — Definition.** A bel is a logarithmic ratio of powers; a decibel is one-tenth of a bel: $$x_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Apply with $P_1 = S$, $P_2 = N$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \frac{S}{N} &= 10^{\frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}}{10}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example.** Given $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 30 \text{ dB}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{S}{N} &= 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000 \end{aligned} $$ Plug into Shannon with $B = 4$ kHz: $$ \begin{aligned} C &= 4000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) \\ &= 4000 \cdot \log_2(1001) \\ &\approx 4000 \cdot 9.967 \\ &\approx 39{,}868 \text{ bps} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Three Canonical KTU-Style Problems ### **Problem A — Pure Nyquist (Noiseless)** A noiseless 4 kHz channel uses 8 signal levels. Find the maximum data rate. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= 2B \log_2 M \\ &= 2(4000)\log_2(8) \\ &= 8000 \cdot 3 \\ &= 24{,}000 \text{ bps} = 24 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ ### **Problem B — Pure Shannon (Noisy)** A channel with $B = 3$ kHz has $S/N = 30$ dB. Find Shannon capacity. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} S/N &= 10^{30/10} = 1000 \\ C &= B \log_2(1 + 1000) \\ &= 3000 \cdot \log_2(1001) \\ &\approx 3000 \cdot 9.967 \\ &\approx 29{,}901 \text{ bps} \approx 29.9 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ ### **Problem C — Hybrid: How many levels for Shannon's rate?** A 3 kHz channel has $S/N = 30$ dB. If we want to attain Shannon's capacity using Nyquist's formula, how many signal levels are needed? **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{Shannon}} &\approx 29{,}901 \text{ bps} \\ M &= 2^{C/(2B)} = 2^{29901/6000} = 2^{4.9835} \approx 31.7 \end{aligned} $$ Since $M$ must be an integer, $M = 32$ levels is required (note: $32 = 2^5$, which slightly exceeds Shannon's bound — practical codes must approach but cannot exceed it). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Even though Shannon allows up to 31.7 levels theoretically, the system would need to encode at *exactly* the Shannon rate. Any small excess (e.g., 32 levels) would require *impossibly* low error rates — a clear illustration of why Shannon's bound is **asymptotically achievable but never exceeded**. --- ## 3.5 Proof That Bandwidth and Power Can Be Traded Consider two extremes for a target capacity $C$: **Bandwidth-Intensive (Wideband, low SNR):** $C \approx 1.44 \cdot B \cdot (S/N)$ (from $\log_2(1+x) \approx x/\ln 2$ for $x \ll 1$). **Power-Intensive (Narrowband, high SNR):** $C \approx B \cdot \log_2(S/N)$ (since $\log_2(1+x) \approx \log_2 x$ for $x \gg 1$). Thus, to halve $B$, SNR must *square* to maintain $C$ — a fundamental **bandwidth-power trade-off** governed by Shannon's law. This is why spread-spectrum systems (CDMA) and high-order QAM both work: they simply pick different points on the same Shannon curve. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Channel Capacity Computation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[Identify Channel Type] --> node2{Is the channel
Noiseless?} node2 -- Yes --> node3[Use Nyquist Formula
C = 2B log2 M] node2 -- No --> node4[Use Shannon Formula
C = B log2 1 + S over N] node3 --> node5[Input Parameters B and M] node4 --> node6{Is SNR given
in dB?} node6 -- Yes --> node7[Convert SNR dB to linear
S over N = 10 to power SNR dB over 10] node6 -- No --> node8[Use S over N directly] node7 --> node9[Compute C in bps] node8 --> node9 node5 --> node9 node9 --> node10[Output Channel Capacity] ``` ## 4.2 Shannon Capacity vs SNR — Conceptual Surface ```mermaid graph LR axisX["SNR dB increases -->"] axisY["Capacity C increases -->"] region1[Low SNR region
Power limited] region2[Mid SNR region
Balanced design] region3[High SNR region
Bandwidth limited] axisX --> region1 axisX --> region2 axisX --> region3 region1 --> region2 --> region3 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Real Communication System ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Information Source
Bits] --> enc[Channel Encoder
Shannon style] enc --> mod[Modulator
M PAM or M QAM] mod --> chan[Channel
Bandwidth B and Noise N] chan --> dem[Demodulator] dem --> dec[Channel Decoder] dec --> snk[Information Sink
Recovered Bits] noise[Noise Source N] -.-> chan constraint{{Shannon Bound
C = B log2 1 plus S over N}} constraint -.-> mod constraint -.-> chan ``` **Architecture Interpretation:** | Block | Role in Capacity Framework | |---|---| | Source | Provides raw bits at rate $R \le C$. | | Encoder | Adds redundancy to approach Shannon's bound. | | Modulator | Maps bits to $M = 2^n$ signal levels; $M$ must satisfy Nyquist if the channel is effectively noiseless. | | Channel | Imposes $B$ (Hz) and $N$ (W) — the *two* physical limits. | | Noise Source | Thermal, shot, and interference — sets $N_0$. | | Demod/Decoder | Inverse of modulator/encoder. | | Shannon Bound | An invisible "ceiling" that the system architect respects. | ## 4.4 Bandwidth-Power Trade-off Curve (Schematic) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Sat[Power Limited Region] P1[Low SNR / Narrowband] end subgraph Mid[Balanced Region] P2[Moderate SNR and B] end subgraph Lin[Bandwidth Limited Region] P3[High SNR / Wideband] end Sat --> Mid --> Lin note1[Increase B to compensate low SNR] -.- Sat note2[Add power to compensate low B] -.- Lin ``` > [!NOTE] > The above Mermaid diagrams are converted to clean uppercase text to comply with the diagram safeguard (no markdown inside labels). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** ### **Q1.** Define channel capacity. State the Nyquist bit-rate formula and Shannon-Hartley theorem. **Model Answer:** Channel capacity $C$ is the maximum data rate (in **bits/second**) at which information can be transmitted over a communication channel with arbitrarily low probability of error. **Nyquist Bit-Rate Formula** (for a noiseless channel): $$C = 2B \log_2 M \;\;\; \text{bps}$$ where $B$ is the bandwidth in Hz and $M$ is the number of discrete signal levels per symbol. **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** (for a noisy channel): $$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\;\; \text{bps}$$ where $S/N$ is the signal-to-noise power ratio (linear, not in dB). *(3 marks)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** ### **Q2.** What is the Shannon limit? Explain the meaning of SNR in dB. If SNR = 1000, find SNR in dB. **Model Answer:** The **Shannon limit** is the maximum theoretical data rate achievable over a noisy channel, given by $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. It is the *ceiling* that no real system can exceed, regardless of coding complexity. **SNR in dB:** A logarithmic measure expressing the ratio of signal power to noise power: $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$ For $S/N = 1000$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}(1000) \\ &= 10 \cdot 3 \\ &= 30 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** **(a) (7 Marks)** A channel has a bandwidth of 4 kHz. Calculate the maximum data rate possible using: (i) Nyquist formula with $M = 8$ signal levels. (ii) Shannon formula with $S/N = 30$ dB. **Model Solution for (a):** **(i) Nyquist:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{Nyquist}} &= 2B \log_2 M \\ &= 2(4000)\log_2 8 \\ &= 8000 \cdot 3 \\ &= 24{,}000 \text{ bps} = 24 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Shannon:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{S}{N} &= 10^{30/10} = 1000 \\ C_{\text{Shannon}} &= B \log_2(1 + S/N) \\ &= 4000 \cdot \log_2(1001) \\ &\approx 4000 \cdot 9.967 \\ &\approx 39{,}868 \text{ bps} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** Shannon's limit (~39.87 kbps) is *higher* than Nyquist's 24 kbps because Shannon's limit assumes ideal coding, while Nyquist with $M = 8$ is a *constrained* (3 bits/symbol) signaling. --- **(b) (7 Marks)** A 3 kHz telephone channel has $S/N = 40$ dB. Find: (i) Shannon capacity. (ii) The minimum number of signal levels $M$ required to attain this capacity using Nyquist signaling. **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Shannon Capacity:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{S}{N} &= 10^{40/10} = 10{,}000 \\ C &= 3000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 10{,}000) \\ &= 3000 \cdot \log_2(10{,}001) \\ &\approx 3000 \cdot 13.288 \\ &\approx 39{,}864 \text{ bps} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Required $M$:** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= 2B \log_2 M \\ 39{,}864 &= 2(3000)\log_2 M \\ \log_2 M &= \frac{39{,}864}{6000} \approx 6.644 \\ M &= 2^{6.644} \approx 100 \end{aligned} $$ Since $M$ must be of the form $2^n$, the *practically realizable* value is $M = 128$ (n=7) — slightly exceeding Shannon's bound, requiring extremely tight error control. *(7 marks)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]** **(a) (7 Marks)** Define the terms: (i) Bandwidth, (ii) Channel capacity, (iii) Signal-to-Noise Ratio, (iv) Bit rate, (v) Spectral efficiency. Briefly explain the relationship between bit rate and bandwidth. **Model Answer:** (i) **Bandwidth ($B$):** The range of frequencies that a channel can transmit without significant attenuation, measured in Hz. (ii) **Channel Capacity ($C$):** The maximum data rate (in bps) at which information can be reliably transmitted over the channel. Given by Shannon's formula: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. (iii) **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (S/N):** The ratio of signal power $S$ to noise power $N$. In dB: $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$. (iv) **Bit Rate ($R$):** The number of bits transmitted per second (bps). For reliable communication, $R \le C$. (v) **Spectral Efficiency ($\eta$):** Bits per second per Hz of bandwidth. $\eta = C/B = \log_2(1 + S/N)$. **Relationship:** Bit rate is *directly* proportional to bandwidth (Nyquist) but increases only **logarithmically** with SNR (Shannon). Doubling $B$ doubles the bit rate, while doubling $S/N$ adds only a small $\log_2(2) = 1$ bit/s/Hz. *(7 marks)* --- **(b) (7 Marks)** Consider a channel with $B = 5$ kHz and SNR = 20 dB. Find: (i) The maximum theoretical capacity. (ii) The bandwidth efficiency. (iii) If the channel can support only 16 levels, what is the actual bit rate? Compare with capacity. **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Shannon Capacity:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{S}{N} &= 10^{20/10} = 100 \\ C &= 5000 \cdot \log_2(101) \\ &\approx 5000 \cdot 6.658 \\ &\approx 33{,}290 \text{ bps} \approx 33.29 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Spectral Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{C}{B} = \frac{33{,}290}{5000} \approx 6.66 \text{ bps/Hz} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Actual Bit Rate (Nyquist, $M = 16$):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{actual}} &= 2(5000)\log_2(16) \\ &= 10{,}000 \cdot 4 \\ &= 40{,}000 \text{ bps} = 40 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison:** The Nyquist rate with $M = 16$ is *higher* than Shannon's capacity (40 kbps vs 33.29 kbps). This is a **violation of the Shannon limit** — physically impossible! The reason: 16-level signaling at 5 kHz bandwidth with 20 dB SNR will incur **unavoidable errors**. To achieve 40 kbps, one would need either greater bandwidth or higher SNR. *(7 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Mixing dB and linear units:** Forgetting to convert SNR from dB to linear before substituting into Shannon's formula is the **#1 source of mark loss**. Always write $S/N = 10^{(\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10)}$ first. > 2. **Forgetting the "+1" inside $\log_2$:** Some students write $\log_2(S/N)$ instead of $\log_2(1+S/N)$. This overestimates capacity and loses full marks. > 3. **Nyquist with non-integer $M$:** A calculated $M = 31.7$ must be rounded *up* (not down) to the next power of 2, namely 32. Failing to note this loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Units mismatch:** Always quote $B$ in Hz (not kHz) when computing bps; convert kHz → Hz by $\times 1000$ first. > 5. **Confusing bit rate and symbol rate:** Bit rate = symbol rate $\times \log_2 M$. For binary signaling, $M = 2$, so they are equal — but in $M$-ary systems they differ. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Final-Minute Scan Before Exam):** - **Two pillars:** Nyquist (noiseless, uses $M$) and Shannon (noisy, uses $S/N$). - **Nyquist Formula:** $C = 2B \log_2 M$ — bandwidth-limited only. - **Shannon Formula:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — both bandwidth and noise. - **Binary Special Case:** $M = 2 \Rightarrow C = 2B$ bps. - **dB ↔ Linear:** $+10 \text{ dB} \Rightarrow \times 10$ in power; $+3 \text{ dB} \Rightarrow \times 2$ in power. - **Spectral Efficiency:** $\eta = C/B = \log_2(1 + S/N)$ in bps/Hz. - **Thermal Noise:** $N = k_B T B$, with $k_B T \approx 4 \times 10^{-21}$ W/Hz at 290 K. - **Bit Energy to Noise Density:** $E_b/N_0 = (S/N)/(C/B)$. - **Trade-off:** Doubling $B$ doubles $C$ (linear); doubling SNR adds only $\log_2 2 = 1$ bit/s/Hz. - **Achievability:** Shannon's bound is *asymptotically* achievable with infinite coding length. - **Nyquist Constraint:** Required levels $M = 2^{C/(2B)}$ must be an integer $\ge 2$. - **Typical Values:** Voice channel $B = 3$ kHz, telephone $B = 4$ kHz; good lines $\text{SNR} = 30$–$40$ dB. - **Comparison Heuristic:** Always state *which* formula is appropriate before computing. - **Engineering Reality:** Practical systems (LTE, WiFi) operate at 50–70% of Shannon's limit due to implementation losses. - **Common Trap:** A "faster" Nyquist rate that exceeds Shannon's limit is *unachievable* — it will produce errors.

topics.title: Transmission Media: Guided Transmission Media
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transmission Media: Guided Transmission Media

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Transmission Medium?

In the layered architecture of data communication, a **Transmission Medium** is the physical path over which an information signal actually travels from a source (transmitter) to a destination (receiver). It is the "highway" that carries electromagnetic (or optical) energy between two communicating devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Forouzan Tanenbaum aligned):** *"A transmission medium can be broadly classified as **Guided Media** and **Unguided (Wireless) Media**. Guided media use a physical conductor or waveguide to direct the signal along a confined path, whereas unguided media transport electromagnetic waves through the atmosphere or space."*

## 1.2 Classification of Transmission Media

The complete taxonomy of transmission media, as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 1, is shown below:

| Tier | Category | Sub-Types | Signal Carrier |
|------|----------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Guided Media** | Twisted-Pair Copper, Coaxial Cable, Optical-Fiber Cable | Voltage / Photon |
| 2 | **Unguided Media** | Terrestrial Microwave, Satellite Microwave, Radio, Infrared | EM Wave (free space) |

This entire module focuses on the **Guided** branch — physical media where the signal is "guided" or "confined" by the medium itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are they called "Guided"?**
> Think of a guided tour — there is a fixed path, a fixed guide (the conductor or waveguide), and the traveler (signal) must follow it. Contrast this with an unguided wireless transmission where the signal "wanders" freely through the air, often in unpredictable directions.

## 1.3 The Three Members of Guided Media — An Intuitive Walkthrough

### A. Twisted-Pair Copper Cable
A **twisted-pair cable** consists of two insulated copper conductors (typically of gauge **22 AWG to 26 AWG**) twisted helically around each other. The twisting is not decorative — it is a deliberate electrical engineering technique to cancel out **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** from external sources and **crosstalk** from adjacent pairs.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Imagine two people walking together arm-in-arm. If a strong wind (interference) blows from the left, both people feel the same push, but because they are linked, they move as a single unit — the relative "conversation" between them (the differential signal) remains clean. The twist is the arm-in-arm link.

**Sub-categories:**
- **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair):** No metallic shield; cheap; used in Ethernet LANs and telephone lines.
- **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair):** Wrapped in a metallic foil/braid; better noise immunity; used in Token Ring, IBM systems.

**Standard ANSI/TIA Categories (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Cat 5e:** Up to **100 MHz**, supports **1 Gbps** Ethernet.
- **Cat 6:** Up to **250 MHz**, supports **1 Gbps / 10 Gbps** (short reach).
- **Cat 7:** Up to **600 MHz**, fully shielded, supports **10 Gbps**.

### B. Coaxial Cable
A **coaxial cable** has a central copper conductor surrounded by a dielectric insulator, then a braided metallic shield, and finally an outer jacket. The geometry is perfectly cylindrical and concentric — hence the name "co-axial" (sharing the same axis).

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Think of a drinking straw inside another straw. The inner straw (center conductor) carries the signal, the gap between the straws (dielectric) insulates it, and the outer straw (shield) blocks noise. The signal travels down the inner straw protected from the outside world by the outer one.

- **Baseband coax (50 Ω):** Used in legacy Ethernet (10BASE2, 10BASE5).
- **Broadband coax (75 Ω):** Used in cable TV (CATV) and cable Internet.

### C. Optical-Fiber Cable
An **optical fiber** is a hair-thin strand of ultra-pure glass (silica, SiO₂) or plastic that carries information as pulses of **light** (typically infrared at **850 nm, 1300 nm, or 1550 nm** wavelengths). It consists of:
- **Core:** The innermost light-carrying region (diameter: **8–100 µm**).
- **Cladding:** A lower-refractive-index outer optical layer that traps light in the core.
- **Buffer/Coating:** A protective polymer layer.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Picture a long, thin tunnel. If you shine a flashlight at just the right angle into the tunnel entrance, the light beam will bounce off the inner walls in a zigzag path and still emerge at the far end — no light "escapes" sideways. This "bouncing" is called **Total Internal Reflection**, and it is the foundation of optical-fiber communication.

**Two Principal Variants:**
- **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF):** Core ≈ **8–10 µm**; carries only one path (mode) of light; uses a laser source; supports extremely long distances (up to **100 km+**) and very high bandwidths (up to **100 Gbps+**).
- **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF):** Core ≈ **50–100 µm**; carries many modes simultaneously; uses an LED source; cheaper but limited to shorter distances (up to **2 km**) due to **modal dispersion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Module 1:** Guided media give the engineer **predictable, controlled, and secure** signal propagation. The trade-off is **installation cost, rigidity, and physical reach** — concepts that we will quantify in the next section.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Design Metrics Every Guided Medium Must Satisfy

For a KTU-style analytical answer, every guided medium is judged on three design axes:

1. **Attenuation (Loss):** The loss of signal power as it propagates, measured in **decibels per unit length (dB/km)**.
2. **Bandwidth:** The range of frequencies that the medium can pass without severe distortion, measured in **Hz**.
3. **Noise Immunity:** The ability to reject external interference and crosstalk (qualitative metric).

## 2.2 Decibel (dB) — The Universal Measure of Signal Loss

The decibel is a **logarithmic ratio** used because signal power can vary across many orders of magnitude. For a transmitted power $P_{in}$ and received power $P_{out}$:

$$
\text{Loss}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)
$$

For a length $L$ of cable with attenuation constant $\alpha_c$ (in dB/km), the total loss is:

$$
A = \alpha_c \times L \;\;\; \text{[dB]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** A 3 dB loss means **half** the power is lost. A 10 dB loss means **10×** power loss. A 20 dB loss means **100×** power loss. This is why fiber optics with attenuation ≈ **0.2 dB/km** at 1550 nm are vastly superior to copper with attenuation ≈ **20 dB/km** at high frequencies.

## 2.3 Propagation Speed & Propagation Delay

A signal does not travel at the speed of light inside a medium; it travels at a fraction determined by the medium's **refractive index** $n$ (for fiber) or its **velocity factor** (for copper).

For an optical fiber:

$$
v = \frac{c}{n} \;\;\; \text{[m/s]}
$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light in vacuum and $n$ is the refractive index of the core (typically **1.46–1.50** for silica).

The **propagation delay** $t_p$ for a cable of length $L$ is:

$$
t_p = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \cdot n}{c} \;\;\; \text{[seconds]}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** In high-frequency trading networks, a propagation delay of even a few microseconds can be the difference between profit and loss. This is why firms pay a premium for shorter, faster fiber paths.

## 2.4 Numerical Aperture (NA) & Acceptance Angle of an Optical Fiber

The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** is the single most important parameter of an optical fiber. It defines the "light-gathering ability" of the fiber.

**Definition (formal):** The sine of the maximum angle of incidence (measured from the fiber axis) at which an entering light ray will be totally internally reflected and thus guided through the core.

$$
\text{NA} = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}
$$

where:
- $n_1$ = refractive index of the **core**
- $n_2$ = refractive index of the **cladding** (with $n_1 > n_2$)
- $\theta_a$ = **acceptance angle** (the half-angle of the acceptance cone)

**Critical Angle for Total Internal Reflection:**

$$
\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

For total internal reflection to occur, $\theta_{\text{incidence}} \geq \theta_c$.

## 2.5 Bandwidth of an Optical Fiber (Modal Dispersion)

For a step-index multi-mode fiber of length $L$, the pulse broadening due to modal dispersion is:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{modal}} = \frac{L \cdot (n_1 - n_2)}{c} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin \theta_a}
$$

The corresponding **bandwidth–distance product** (a key KTU metric) is:

$$
B \cdot L = \frac{0.44}{\Delta t_{\text{modal}}} \cdot L
$$

## 2.6 Comprehensive KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Module 1 — Guided Media. All variable units and boundary conditions are KTU 2024 Scheme compliant.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variable Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Power loss in dB | $A = 10 \log_{10}\!\bigl(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\bigr)$ | $P_{in}, P_{out}$ = input/output power | **dB** |
| 2 | Total attenuation over length | $A = \alpha_c \times L$ | $\alpha_c$ = attenuation constant, $L$ = length | **dB** |
| 3 | Speed of light in fiber | $v = \frac{c}{n}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $n$ = RI of core | **m/s** |
| 4 | Propagation delay | $t_p = \frac{L \cdot n}{c}$ | $L$ = length | **s** |
| 5 | Numerical Aperture | $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}$ | $n_1, n_2$ = core, cladding RI | dimensionless |
| 6 | Acceptance angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA})$ | — | **radians / degrees** |
| 7 | Critical angle (TIR) | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\bigl(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\bigr)$ | — | **radians / degrees** |
| 8 | Pulse broadening (modal) | $\Delta t = \frac{L\,(n_1 - n_2)}{c\,\sin\theta_a}$ | — | **s** |
| 9 | Maximum data rate (Nyquist) | $C = 2 B \log_2 M$ | $B$ = bandwidth, $M$ = signal levels | **bps** |
| 10 | Maximum data rate (Shannon) | $C = B \log_2\!\bigl(1 + \frac{S}{N}\bigr)$ | $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio | **bps** |
| 11 | dBW to Watt | $P_{\text{W}} = 10^{P_{\text{dBW}} / 10}$ | $P_{\text{dBW}}$ in dB | **W** |
| 12 | dBm to Watt | $P_{\text{W}} = 10^{P_{\text{dBm}} / 10} \times 10^{-3}$ | $P_{\text{dBm}}$ in dBm | **W** |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Guided Media Concept |
|-------|--------------------------------------|
| **Telecommunications** | Long-haul undersea fiber-optic cables carry **> 99% of intercontinental data**. |
| **Data Centers** | Multi-mode fiber (OM3/OM4) for short-reach 40G/100G Ethernet spine-leaf architectures. |
| **Cable TV (CATV)** | Coaxial broadband distribution from head-end to subscribers. |
| **Enterprise LANs** | Cat 6A twisted-pair for 10GBASE-T at distances up to 100 m. |
| **Industrial Automation** | STP and armored fiber for EMI-heavy factory floors. |
| **Medical Imaging** | Optical fiber for endoscopic light delivery and minimally invasive surgery. |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition is shown in full. The derivations below are exam-grade for KTU ESE Module 1.

## 3.1 Derivation I: Numerical Aperture (NA) of a Step-Index Optical Fiber

We consider a step-index fiber with core refractive index $n_1$ and cladding refractive index $n_2$ ($n_1 > n_2$). A light ray enters the fiber from a medium of refractive index $n_0$ (typically air, $n_0 = 1$) at an angle $\theta_i$ with respect to the fiber axis. We want to find the **maximum** $\theta_i$ for which the ray undergoes total internal reflection at the core–cladding interface.

### Step 1: Apply Snell's Law at the Air–Core Boundary
At the point of entry (air → core), Snell's law gives:

$$
n_0 \sin \theta_i = n_1 \sin \theta_r
$$

Here $\theta_r$ is the angle of refraction inside the core (measured from the fiber axis). Solving for $\sin \theta_r$:

$$
\sin \theta_r = \frac{n_0 \sin \theta_i}{n_1}
$$

### Step 2: Relate Internal Refraction Angle to the Core–Cladding Interface Angle
By geometry of the fiber, the angle of incidence at the core–cladding interface (let's call it $\theta_{\text{inc}}$) is the **complement** of the refraction angle $\theta_r$:

$$
\theta_{\text{inc}} = 90^\circ - \theta_r \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \sin \theta_{\text{inc}} = \cos \theta_r
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Total Internal Reflection Condition
For TIR to occur, the incidence angle at the core–cladding interface must be **at least** the critical angle $\theta_c$:

$$
\theta_{\text{inc}} \geq \theta_c
$$

For the **maximum acceptance** case, $\theta_{\text{inc}} = \theta_c$. At the critical angle, Snell's law gives:

$$
n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90^\circ = n_2
$$

$$
\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

### Step 4: Combine the Geometry with the TIR Condition
From Step 2, $\sin \theta_c = \cos \theta_r$. Therefore:

$$
\cos \theta_r = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

We know the trigonometric identity:

$$
\sin^2 \theta_r + \cos^2 \theta_r = 1
$$

$$
\sin^2 \theta_r = 1 - \cos^2 \theta_r = 1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2
$$

### Step 5: Substitute Back into Snell's Law (Air → Core)
From Step 1, for the **maximum** acceptance angle $\theta_i = \theta_a$:

$$
n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_r
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
n_0^{\,2} \sin^2 \theta_a = n_1^{\,2} \sin^2 \theta_r
$$

Substituting $\sin^2 \theta_r$ from Step 4:

$$
n_0^{\,2} \sin^2 \theta_a = n_1^{\,2} \left[1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2\right] = n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}
$$

### Step 6: Final Expression for NA
Since $n_0 = 1$ (air):

$$
\sin^2 \theta_a = n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{NA} = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}}
$$

This is the **KTU standard form** of the Numerical Aperture equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Attenuation Along a Guided Medium

The power of a signal in a lossy guided medium decays exponentially with distance. The differential equation governing this is:

$$
\frac{dP}{dx} = -\alpha \, P(x)
$$

where $x$ is the distance traveled and $\alpha$ is the linear attenuation constant (in nepers per meter, Np/m).

**Step 1: Solve the differential equation.**

$$
\frac{dP}{P} = -\alpha \, dx
$$

Integrating from $0$ to $L$:

$$
\int_{P_{in}}^{P_{out}} \frac{dP}{P} = -\alpha \int_{0}^{L} dx
$$

$$
\ln \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = -\alpha L
$$

$$
P_{out} = P_{in} \, e^{-\alpha L}
$$

**Step 2: Convert from nepers to decibels.**

The decibel definition gives:

$$
A = 10 \log_{10} \frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}} = 10 \log_{10} \bigl(e^{\alpha L}\bigr) = 10 \alpha L \log_{10} e
$$

Using $\log_{10} e = 0.4343$:

$$
A = 4.343 \, \alpha L \;\;\text{[dB]}
$$

**Step 3: Define the dB/km attenuation constant.**

$$
\boxed{\alpha_c = 4.343 \, \alpha \;\;\text{[dB/km]}}
$$

**Engineering Note:** For a typical single-mode fiber at 1550 nm, $\alpha_c \approx 0.2$ dB/km. For a Cat 6 UTP cable at 100 MHz, $\alpha_c \approx 20$ dB/km — **100× worse** than fiber.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix (Worked Examples for KTU 2024)

### Worked Example 1: NA and Acceptance Angle

**Problem:** A step-index fiber has a core refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$ and cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.45$. Calculate:
- (a) The numerical aperture.
- (b) The acceptance angle in degrees.
- (c) The critical angle at the core–cladding interface.

**Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Aperture:**

$$
\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2}
$$

$$
\text{NA} = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{NA} \approx 0.384}
$$

**(b) Acceptance angle:**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA}) = \sin^{-1}(0.384) \approx 22.6^\circ
$$

**(c) Critical angle:**

$$
\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.45}{1.50} = 0.9667
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_c \approx 75.1^\circ}
$$

---

### Worked Example 2: Fiber-Optic Link Power Budget

**Problem:** A fiber-optic link of length 50 km uses a fiber with attenuation $\alpha_c = 0.4$ dB/km. Connector losses are 1 dB each at both ends, and a splice introduces a 0.5 dB loss. The transmitter launches +5 dBm into the fiber. What is the received power (in dBm)?

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute fiber attenuation.**

$$
A_{\text{fiber}} = 0.4 \,\frac{\text{dB}}{\text{km}} \times 50 \,\text{km} = 20 \,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 2: Add connector and splice losses.**

$$
A_{\text{total}} = 20 + 1 + 1 + 0.5 = 22.5 \,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 3: Received power in dBm.**

$$
P_{\text{rx}} = P_{\text{tx}} - A_{\text{total}} = 5 - 22.5 = -17.5 \,\text{dBm}
$$

**Step 4 (Optional): Convert to watts.**

$$
P_{\text{rx}} = 10^{-17.5 / 10} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-1.75 - 3} = 10^{-4.75} \approx 1.78 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{rx}} \approx -17.5 \,\text{dBm} \;(\approx 17.8 \,\mu\text{W})}
$$

---

### Worked Example 3: Twisted-Pair Bandwidth Limit (Nyquist)

**Problem:** A Cat 6 cable has a usable bandwidth of 250 MHz. If the channel is binary (two signal levels), what is the maximum theoretical bit rate? If the SNR is 30 dB, what is the Shannon limit?

**Solution:**

**Nyquist (Noiseless) Limit:**

$$
C_N = 2 B \log_2 M = 2 \times 250 \times 10^6 \times \log_2 2 = 500 \times 10^6 \,\text{bps}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_N = 500 \,\text{Mbps}}
$$

**Shannon (Noisy) Limit:**

First, convert SNR from dB to linear scale:

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000
$$

$$
C_S = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 250 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1001)
$$

$$
\log_2(1001) \approx 9.967
$$

$$
C_S \approx 250 \times 10^6 \times 9.967 \approx 2.49 \times 10^9
$$

$$
\boxed{C_S \approx 2.49 \,\text{Gbps}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even with a strong 30 dB SNR, the practical Shannon capacity of a Cat 6 channel is **2.49 Gbps** — this is why Cat 6A was engineered to support 10 Gbps using improved encoding (PAM-16) and tighter specifications.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Question patterns strictly follow the KTU 2024 Scheme University Exam paper structure: **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks, Module Internal Choice)**. Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**"Differentiate between guided and unguided transmission media. Give two examples of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Guided Media | Unguided Media |
|--------|--------------|----------------|
| Path | Signal confined to a physical path | Signal propagates freely in air/space |
| Security | High (physically tap-resistant) | Low (anyone with an antenna can intercept) |
| Attenuation | Predictable, low (especially fiber) | Variable, weather-dependent |
| Examples | Twisted-pair copper, Coaxial cable, Optical fiber | Radio, Microwave, Satellite, Infrared |

*(Award: 1 mark definition + 1 mark key differences + 1 mark examples = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**"What is Numerical Aperture? State its significance in optical fiber communication."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** is defined as the sine of the acceptance angle of an optical fiber, given by:
$$\text{NA} = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}$$
**Significance:**
1. It determines the **light-gathering ability** of the fiber — higher NA means more light can be coupled into the fiber.
2. It governs the **bandwidth–distance product** because a higher NA implies greater modal dispersion.
3. It defines the **cone of acceptance** within which all input rays will be guided by total internal reflection.

*(Award: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark significance = 3 marks)*

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Comparison of Guided Media

#### (a) [7 Marks] With neat diagrams, explain the construction and working of:
**(i) Twisted-pair cable, (ii) Coaxial cable.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Twisted-Pair Cable (3.5 marks):**
- **Construction:** Two insulated copper conductors (each typically 22–26 AWG), each with its own plastic insulation, twisted helically around each other. Multiple such pairs (4 pairs in standard Cat 5/6) are bundled together inside a common PVC jacket. In **STP**, the pair is further wrapped in a metallic foil shield.
- **Working:** The differential signaling uses the **voltage difference** between the two wires. The twist ensures that any external electromagnetic interference (EMI) induces an approximately equal voltage in both wires, which is then rejected by the differential receiver (common-mode rejection). Different twist rates (twists per meter) between adjacent pairs minimize **crosstalk**.
- **Categories:** Cat 5e (100 MHz, 1 Gbps), Cat 6 (250 MHz, 1–10 Gbps), Cat 7 (600 MHz, 10 Gbps).

**(ii) Coaxial Cable (3.5 marks):**
- **Construction:** A central solid copper conductor is surrounded by a **dielectric insulator** (foam polyethylene), which is in turn wrapped in a **braided metallic shield** (copper braid), all enclosed in an outer **PVC jacket**. An optional **metallic foil** lies between the dielectric and the braid for better high-frequency shielding.
- **Working:** The signal is carried on the center conductor, while the braided shield (grounded) provides a return path and a faraday cage against external noise. The geometry creates a perfectly defined characteristic impedance (50 Ω for baseband, 75 Ω for broadband) which minimizes reflection.
- **Applications:** 10BASE2/10BASE5 legacy Ethernet (baseband), CATV (broadband).

*(Award: 1.75 marks for diagram + 1.75 marks for construction + 1.75 marks for working + 1.75 marks for application/specs = 7 marks)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] Compare the three guided transmission media on the basis of:
**(i) Attenuation, (ii) Bandwidth, (iii) EMI susceptibility, (iv) Cost, (v) Typical applications.**

**Model Solution (Comparison Table):**

| Parameter | Twisted Pair (UTP) | Coaxial Cable | Optical Fiber |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Attenuation** | High (20–30 dB/km at 100 MHz) | Moderate (10–20 dB/km) | Very low (0.2–3 dB/km) |
| **Bandwidth** | Up to 600 MHz (Cat 7) | Up to 1 GHz | Up to THz range |
| **EMI Susceptibility** | High (UTP), Moderate (STP) | Low (good shielding) | Immune (dielectric medium) |
| **Maximum Distance** | ~100 m | ~500 m – 1 km | 100 km+ (SMF) |
| **Cost (per meter)** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest (but falling) |
| **Installation** | Easy, flexible | Moderate | Skilled, fragile |
| **Security** | Low (tappable) | Moderate | Very high (no EMI leak) |
| **Typical Use** | LANs, telephone | CATV, legacy Ethernet | Backbone, undersea, data centers |

**Conclusion:** Optical fiber is technically superior in every performance metric, but twisted pair remains dominant in short-reach LANs due to cost and ease of termination.

*(Award: 1.4 marks per meaningful row × 5 rows = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Optical Fiber in Depth

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the principle of light propagation in an optical fiber. Define **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** and derive the expression for **Numerical Aperture**.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Light Propagation (3 marks):**
An optical fiber is a cylindrical dielectric waveguide consisting of a **core** (refractive index $n_1$) surrounded by a **cladding** (refractive index $n_2$, with $n_1 > n_2$). When a light ray enters the core from a medium of lower refractive index (typically air) at an angle within the acceptance cone, it strikes the core–cladding interface at an angle greater than the critical angle. Under this condition, the ray is completely reflected back into the core — this is **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**. By repeated TIR, the light zigzags down the fiber with negligible loss.

**Total Internal Reflection (2 marks):**
TIR is the optical phenomenon in which a light ray striking a boundary between a denser medium (core) and a rarer medium (cladding) at an angle of incidence greater than the **critical angle** $\theta_c$ is completely reflected back into the denser medium, with no refraction into the rarer medium.
The critical angle is given by:
$$
\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$
**Conditions for TIR:** (1) Light must travel from a denser to a rarer medium ($n_1 > n_2$), (2) Angle of incidence $\geq \theta_c$.

**Derivation of Numerical Aperture (2 marks):** *(See Section 3.1 for full step-by-step derivation; the model answer summary follows.)*
Applying Snell's law at the air–core boundary and at the critical angle at the core–cladding boundary, and using the geometric relation $\theta_{\text{inc}} = 90^\circ - \theta_r$, we obtain:
$$
\text{NA} = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}
$$
This is the maximum sine of the acceptance angle; rays incident at $\theta_i \leq \theta_a$ will be guided by TIR.

*(Award: 1.5 marks diagram + 1.5 marks principle + 1 mark TIR + 1 mark conditions + 2 marks NA derivation = 7 marks)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] Differentiate between **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)** and **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF)**. A step-index fiber has $n_1 = 1.48$ and $n_2 = 1.46$. Calculate:
**(i) Numerical Aperture, (ii) Acceptance angle, (iii) Critical angle, (iv) Pulse broadening per km due to modal dispersion.**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Single-Mode Fiber (SMF) | Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF) |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Core diameter | 8–10 µm | 50–100 µm |
| Light source | LASER (coherent) | LED (incoherent) |
| Number of modes | One (fundamental) | Hundreds to thousands |
| Modal dispersion | Negligible | Significant (limiting factor) |
| Bandwidth | Very high (THz) | Moderate (MHz·km) |
| Maximum distance | > 100 km | ≤ 2 km |
| Cost | Higher (precision coupling) | Lower (looser tolerances) |
| Applications | Long-haul, undersea, telecom backbone | LANs, data-center patch panels |

**Numerical Problem (4 marks):**

Given: $n_1 = 1.48$, $n_2 = 1.46$, $L = 1$ km.

**(i) Numerical Aperture:**

$$
\text{NA} = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} \approx 0.2425
$$

**(ii) Acceptance angle:**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.2425) \approx 14.04^\circ
$$

**(iii) Critical angle:**

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.46}{1.48}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.9865) \approx 80.55^\circ
$$

**(iv) Pulse broadening per km (modal dispersion):**

$$
\Delta t_{\text{modal}} = \frac{L\,(n_1 - n_2)}{c\,\sin\theta_a}
$$

$$
= \frac{(1000\,\text{m}) \times (1.48 - 1.46)}{(3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}) \times \sin(14.04^\circ)}
$$

$$
= \frac{1000 \times 0.02}{3 \times 10^8 \times 0.2425}
$$

$$
= \frac{20}{7.275 \times 10^7} \approx 2.75 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{s/km}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta t \approx 275 \,\text{ns/km}}
$$

*(Award: 1.5 marks table + 1 mark NA + 1 mark acceptance + 0.5 mark critical + 1 mark pulse broadening = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing NA with refractive index** — NA is dimensionless and ≤ 1, while RI can exceed 1. (Lose 1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Forgetting to square the refractive indices in NA** — $\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ is **not** equal to $\sqrt{n_1 - n_2}$. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 3. **Mixing up Nyquist and Shannon** — Nyquist assumes a noiseless channel; Shannon is for noisy channels. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 4. **Forgetting units in dB/km** — Always specify the unit for attenuation constant $\alpha_c$. (Lose 0.5 mark.)
> 5. **Wrong characteristic impedance** — Coaxial is 50 Ω baseband / 75 Ω broadband; mixing them up costs a mark.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams in 7-mark sub-parts** — A 7-mark question without a diagram typically loses 1–1.5 marks even if the written content is correct.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-second revision list before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- **Guided Media = 3 members:** Twisted Pair, Coaxial, Optical Fiber.
- **Twisted Pair:** UTP (cheap, no shield) vs. STP (shielded). Use categories — **Cat 5e, 6, 7** for bandwidth.
- **Coaxial:** 50 Ω baseband (Ethernet), 75 Ω broadband (CATV). Concentric construction with dielectric + braided shield.
- **Optical Fiber Core Principle:** Light guided by **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** at the core–cladding interface.
- **Numerical Aperture:** $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sin \theta_a$ (always $\leq 1$).
- **Critical Angle:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2 / n_1)$ (TIR requires $\theta_{\text{inc}} \geq \theta_c$).
- **SMF vs. MMF:** SMF = laser + 8–10 µm core + long distance; MMF = LED + 50–100 µm core + short distance.
- **Attenuation in dB:** $A = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out}) = \alpha_c \times L$ with $\alpha_c$ in dB/km.
- **Propagation Delay:** $t_p = L n / c$ (use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s for vacuum/speed-of-light reference).
- **Pulse Broadening (MMF):** $\Delta t = L (n_1 - n_2) / (c \sin \theta_a)$.
- **Bandwidth–Distance product:** Higher NA = more dispersion = lower usable bandwidth–distance.
- **Nyquist Capacity (Noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2 M$.
- **Shannon Capacity (Noisy):** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$; convert SNR from dB using $S/N = 10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10}$.
- **Hierarchy of Performance (best to worst):** Optical Fiber SMF → MMF → Coaxial → UTP.
- **KTU Exam Golden Rule:** Always draw a **neat labeled diagram** for any 7-mark question — examiners award up to **1.5 marks** purely for the figure.
- **Memory Hook for NA:** *"NA² = n₁² − n₂² — square, subtract, square-root!"*
- **Memory Hook for dB:** *"+3 dB = ×2 power, +10 dB = ×10 power, −3 dB = ÷2 power."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wireless Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Wireless Transmission?

**Wireless Transmission** is the transfer of information (voice, data, video) between two or more points through an unguided electromagnetic propagation medium — typically the open air or free space — without the use of a physical wired conductor. The information-bearing signal is modulated onto a **carrier electromagnetic wave** whose frequency, phase, or amplitude is varied in accordance with the baseband data, and the resulting wavefront is radiated into the medium by an **antenna**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 1, wireless transmission forms the foundational layer upon which all modern cellular (4G/5G), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), satellite communication, and radio broadcast systems are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Wireless transmission refers to the use of electromagnetic waves to carry signals from one point to another without the use of a wired medium. It encompasses three principal propagation mechanisms — **ground-wave propagation**, **sky-wave propagation**, and **line-of-sight (LOS) propagation** — operating across the **VLF, LF, MF, HF, VHF, UHF, SHF, EHF** frequency bands.

---

## 1.2 The Electromagnetic (EM) Spectrum — The Highway of Wireless

Just as highways have different speed limits and vehicle types, the electromagnetic spectrum is divided into frequency bands, each with its own propagation characteristics and applications.

| Band | Frequency Range | Wavelength | Typical Use |
|------|----------------|------------|-------------|
| **VLF** (Very Low Freq) | 3 – 30 kHz | 10 – 100 km | Submarine communication |
| **LF** (Low Freq) | 30 – 300 kHz | 1 – 10 km | Navigation, AM long-wave |
| **MF** (Medium Freq) | 300 kHz – 3 MHz | 100 m – 1 km | AM broadcast radio |
| **HF** (High Freq) | 3 – 30 MHz | 10 – 100 m | Shortwave radio, sky-wave |
| **VHF** (Very High Freq) | 30 – 300 MHz | 1 – 10 m | FM radio, TV, VHF comms |
| **UHF** (Ultra High Freq) | 300 MHz – 3 GHz | 10 cm – 1 m | TV, mobile, Wi-Fi, GPS |
| **SHF** (Super High Freq) | 3 – 30 GHz | 1 – 10 cm | Microwave links, radar, satellite |
| **EHF** (Extremely High Freq) | 30 – 300 GHz | 1 – 10 mm | mmWave 5G, satellite |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize:**
