This is the **modulation product**: $$s(t) = b(t) \cdot A \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ 4. **Transmission:** The modulated signal $s(t)$ is launched into the channel. 5. **Reception & Demodulation:** At the receiver, the signal is multiplied again by a locally generated carrier (coherent) or passed through an envelope detector (non-coherent), and a decision device reconstructs the bitstream. 6. **Decision:** The receiver samples the demodulated waveform; if the sample exceeds a threshold $\lambda$, it is declared as bit `1`, otherwise as bit `0`. ## 2.2 Mathematical Model — BASK Equation For binary data $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$, the ASK signal is: $$s(t) = A \cdot b(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \leq t \leq T_b$$ where $T_b = 1/R_b$ is the bit duration. Equivalently, in the form of two possible transmitted waveforms per bit interval: $$s_i(t) = \begin{cases} s_1(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{bit } 1 \\ s_2(t) = 0, & \text{bit } 0 \end{cases}$$ > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind BASK = OOK:** Since one of the two signals is identically zero, BASK is sometimes called **On-Off Keying (OOK)** — the carrier is either fully ON or fully OFF. The power efficiency of BASK is therefore only **50%** of a fully symmetric modulation scheme (like BPSK), because half the time no power is transmitted. ## 2.3 Power Spectral Density of BASK The PSD of the transmitted BASK signal is: $$P_{ASK}(f) = \frac{A^2}{4}\left[\delta(f - f_c) + \delta(f + f_c)\right] + \frac{A^2}{4}\left[P_b(f - f_c) + P_b(f + f_c)\right]$$ where $P_b(f)$ is the PSD of the baseband NRZ data. The PSD contains: - **Discrete carrier spikes** at $\pm f_c$ (the residual carrier component) - **Continuous spectrum** replicated around $\pm f_c$ (the data sidebands) ## 2.4 Probability of Bit Error (BER) — The Critical Performance Metric ### Coherent BASK (Synchronous Detection): $$P_e = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)$$ ### Non-Coherent BASK (Envelope Detection): $$P_e = \frac{1}{2} \exp\left(-\frac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$$ where $E_b$ is the energy per bit and $N_0/2$ is the two-sided noise PSD. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** For the same $E_b/N_0$, **coherent BASK is ~3 dB better** than non-coherent BASK, but requires an exact carrier reference at the receiver (more complex and expensive). ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | BASK Signal | $s(t) = A \cdot b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $b(t) \in \{0,1\}$ | | 2 | Bandwidth of BASK | $B_{ASK} = 2B = 2R_b$ (NRZ baseband) | Hz (approx., for full-width NRZ) | | 3 | Bit Energy | $E_b = A^2 T_b / 2$ | Joules (per bit) | | 4 | Bit Rate | $R_b = 1/T_b$ | bits/second (bps) | | 5 | Baud Rate (BASK) | $S = R_b$ | symbols/sec (1 bit/symbol) | | 6 | Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = R_b / B_{ASK} = 0.5$ | bits/sec/Hz | | 7 | BER — Coherent | $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b / 2N_0}\right)$ | dimensionless | | 8 | BER — Non-Coherent | $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Power Efficiency (BASK) | Poor (50% DC waste) | Compared to BPSK | | 10 | M-ary ASK Bandwidth | $B = 2R_b/\log_2 M$ | Hz (more efficient than BASK) | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility ASK (especially OOK) is widely deployed in: - **Optical Fiber & Free-Space Optics (FSO):** Lasers are turned ON/OFF — the optical equivalent of OOK. - **RFID Tags (passive):** Low-cost backscatter modulation uses amplitude perturbation. - **Remote-Control Systems (garage doors, toys, IR remotes):** Cheap, low-data-rate ASK transceivers. - **Amateur Radio & Morse Telegraphy:** Historical and hobby use. - **ASK Modems in POTS dial-up (early 103/202 modems):** Used FSK/PSK predominantly, but ASK appears in low-speed signaling. > [!NOTE] > **Why ASK is Losing Favor:** Despite its simplicity, ASK is highly susceptible to **amplitude noise, fading, and non-linear distortion**. Modern digital systems prefer **FSK** and **PSK** for robust performance. However, ASK remains the **pedagogical foundation** for understanding all digital passband modulation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: General Expression of the BASK Signal **Step 1 — Start with the digital data waveform.** A unipolar NRZ binary waveform $b(t)$ is defined for $0 \leq t \leq T_b$ as: $$b(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if the } k\text{-th bit is } 1 \\ 0, & \text{if the } k\text{-th bit is } 0 \end{cases}$$ This is a rectangular pulse of amplitude $+1$ or $0$, with duration $T_b$. **Step 2 — Define the carrier.** A sinusoidal carrier is generated by a local oscillator: $$c(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$$ For ASK, the phase $\phi = 0$ (no phase modulation) and the frequency $f_c$ is held constant. Only the amplitude is allowed to vary. **Step 3 — Form the modulation product.** The BASK modulator is mathematically a multiplier. The output is the product of the baseband data and the carrier: $$s(t) = b(t) \cdot c(t) = A \cdot b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the two possible values of $b(t)$.** $$\begin{aligned} s(t) &= A \cdot b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \\[4pt] s(t) &= \begin{cases} A \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{when } b(t) = 1 \text{ (binary 1)} \\ 0 & \text{when } b(t) = 0 \text{ (binary 0)} \end{cases} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Verify dimensions and physical meaning.** The instantaneous power of the transmitted signal is $P_s(t) = s^2(t)/R$. During a binary `1`, the signal carries full power $A^2/(2R)$. During a binary `0`, **no power is transmitted**. This is the hallmark of OOK. ## 3.2 Derivation: Bandwidth of BASK **Step 1 — Recall the bandwidth of NRZ baseband data.** For a polar/unipolar NRZ data stream with bit rate $R_b$, the **first-null bandwidth** of the baseband spectrum is: $$B_{\text{baseband}} = R_b = \frac{1}{T_b}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the modulation bandwidth rule.** Passband modulation (multiplication by a carrier) **shifts the baseband spectrum** to $\pm f_c$ and **doubles the total occupied bandwidth** (sum of upper and lower sidebands). Therefore: $$B_{\text{ASK}} = 2 \cdot B_{\text{baseband}} = 2 R_b$$ **Step 3 — Express the result in terms of $T_b$.** $$\begin{aligned} B_{\text{ASK}} &= \frac{2}{T_b} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Note the spectral efficiency.** Since BASK transmits 1 bit per symbol, its **maximum spectral efficiency** is: $$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_{\text{ASK}}} = \frac{R_b}{2R_b} = 0.5 \;\text{bits/sec/Hz}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Compare this with BPSK, which has the **same bandwidth** ($2R_b$) but **3 dB better BER performance** for the same $E_b/N_0$. This is why BPSK is preferred in noise-limited channels, while ASK is used only in cost- or simplicity-limited applications. ## 3.3 Derivation: Bit Energy of BASK **Step 1 — Energy definition.** The energy of a signal over a bit interval is: $$E_b = \int_{0}^{T_b} s^2(t)\, dt$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the BASK waveform.** For a binary `1` (the only case carrying energy): $$\begin{aligned} E_b &= \int_{0}^{T_b} \left[A \cos(2\pi f_c t)\right]^2 dt \\[4pt] &= A^2 \int_{0}^{T_b} \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\, dt \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos^2\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 + \cos 2\theta)$.** $$\begin{aligned} E_b &= A^2 \int_{0}^{T_b} \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)\right] dt \\[4pt] &= \frac{A^2}{2}\left[t + \frac{\sin(4\pi f_c t)}{4\pi f_c}\right]_{0}^{T_b} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Assume $f_c T_b \gg 1$ (an integer or near-integer number of carrier cycles fit in $T_b$).** Then $\sin(4\pi f_c T_b) \approx 0$, and the second term vanishes: $$E_b = \frac{A^2}{2}\left[T_b - 0\right] = \frac{A^2 T_b}{2}$$ This is the energy per transmitted binary `1`. For an OOK system, the **average energy per bit** is half of this (because half the bits are zero): $$\bar{E}_b = \frac{A^2 T_b}{4}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation: Probability of Error — Coherent BASK **Step 1 — Set up the received signal model.** In an AWGN channel, the received signal is: $$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$$ where $n(t)$ is zero-mean Gaussian noise with PSD $N_0/2$. **Step 2 — Apply the matched filter / correlator receiver.** The received signal is correlated with the reference $s_1(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ over $T_b$: $$r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) \cdot s_1(t)\, dt = \int_0^{T_b} s_i(t) s_1(t)\, dt + \int_0^{T_b} n(t) s_1(t)\, dt$$ **Step 3 — Compute the signal component for each hypothesis.** For hypothesis $H_1$ (bit = 1): $$r \vert_{H_1} = E_b + n_c = \frac{A^2 T_b}{2} + n_c$$ where $n_c$ is a zero-mean Gaussian RV with variance $\sigma^2 = N_0 E_b / 2$. For hypothesis $H_0$ (bit = 0): $$r \vert_{H_0} = n_c$$ **Step 4 — Compute the probability of error.** With equiprobable bits and the optimal threshold midway, the BER is: $$P_e = Q\!\left(\frac{E_b/2}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Equivalent form using the complementary error function.** $$P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2 N_0}}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** As $E_b/N_0 \to \infty$, $P_e \to 0$ (no errors). As $E_b/N_0 \to 0$, $P_e \to 0.5$ (random guessing). The formula is dimensionally and physically consistent. ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A BASK system transmits binary data at $R_b = 10\text{ kbps}$ over an AWGN channel with $N_0 = 10^{-9}\;\text{W/Hz}$. The carrier amplitude is $A = 1\text{ V}$ into a $50\;\Omega$ load. Compute: (a) The transmitted bandwidth. (b) The energy per bit. (c) The probability of bit error (coherent detection). **Solution:** **Part (a):** $B_{ASK} = 2R_b = 2 \times 10{,}000 = 20{,}000\;\text{Hz} = 20\;\text{kHz}$ **Part (b):** $T_b = 1/R_b = 10^{-4}\;\text{s}$ $$E_b = \frac{A^2 T_b}{2} = \frac{(1)^2 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{J} = 50\;\mu\text{J}$$ **Part (c):** Compute $E_b/N_0 = 5 \times 10^{-5} / 10^{-9} = 5 \times 10^{4} = 50{,}000$ $$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{50{,}000}\right) = Q(223.6) \approx 0$$ This extremely low error rate is because the bit energy is huge relative to noise — typical of a clean, short-range link. # 4. In the receiver, the **decision circuit** compares the demodulated sample to a threshold (usually $A/2$ for equiprobable OOK with equal-prior noise variances) to recover the bit. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Define Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK). Why is binary ASK also called On-Off Keying (OOK)?** **Model Answer:** ASK is a digital-to-analog modulation technique in which the **amplitude** of a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier is varied in accordance with the digital baseband data, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain constant. In binary ASK, the carrier is transmitted with a fixed amplitude $A$ to represent a binary `1` and is completely suppressed (amplitude = 0) to represent a binary `0`. Since the carrier is either fully **ON** (full amplitude) or fully **OFF** (zero amplitude), the scheme is referred to as **On-Off Keying (OOK)** — analogous to a light switch that is either closed or open. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **State any three advantages and three disadvantages of ASK.** **Model Answer:** **Advantages:** 1. **Simplicity of implementation** — only a multiplier and a carrier oscillator are required; demodulation can be done with a simple envelope detector. 2. **Low cost** — suitable for low-budget applications such as IR remotes and RFID. 3. **Low power consumption in receiver** — envelope detectors do not require coherent local oscillators. **Disadvantages:** 1. **Highly susceptible to amplitude noise** — any channel fading or interference directly corrupts the data. 2. **Poor power efficiency** — half the bit intervals carry zero power, wasting 50% of the available energy. 3. **Low spectral efficiency** — $0.5$ bits/sec/Hz, inferior to QAM or M-ary schemes. 4. Not suitable for high-speed, long-distance communication. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** **(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for a Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK) signal. Draw its waveform for the data sequence `1 0 1 1 0` and label the bit duration $T_b$ and carrier period $T_c$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain coherent detection of a BASK signal with a neat block diagram. Derive the probability of error expression for the same. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Derivation of the BASK expression:** The BASK signal is generated by multiplying the unipolar NRZ binary data $b(t)$ with a sinusoidal carrier $c(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$: $$s(t) = b(t) \cdot A\cos(2\pi f_c t) = A \cdot b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ For the two possible bit values: $$\begin{aligned} s(t) = \begin{cases} A\cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{when bit} = 1 \\ 0 & \text{when bit} = 0 \end{cases} \end{aligned}$$ For the data sequence `1 0 1 1 0` (5 bit intervals of duration $T_b$ each): | Bit | Interval | Signal in that interval | |---|---|---| | 1 | $0 \leq t < T_b$ | $A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | | 0 | $T_b \leq t < 2T_b$ | $0$ | | 1 | $2T_b \leq t < 3T_b$ | $A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | | 1 | $3T_b \leq t < 4T_b$ | $A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | | 0 | $4T_b \leq t < 5T_b$ | $0$ | The waveform is a sinusoid in the "1" intervals and a flat zero line in the "0" intervals. Typically $T_c \ll T_b$ (e.g., $T_b = 10 T_c$ for illustration). **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Coherent (Synchronous) Detection:** In coherent detection, the receiver generates a local carrier that is **phase- and frequency-locked** to the incoming carrier using a Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) or Costas loop. The received signal is multiplied by this local reference, integrated over a bit interval, and compared to a threshold. **Block Diagram (Textual):** Received $r(t) \to$ BPF (centered at $f_c$, BW = $2R_b$) $\to$ Multiplier ($\times$ local carrier) $\to$ Integrator over $T_b$ $\to$ Sampler (at $t = T_b$) $\to$ Threshold Detector $\to$ Output bit. **Derivation of Probability of Error:** At the input to the correlator: $$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$$ where $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. The correlator output is: $$r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)\, dt$$ The signal component of the correlator output is: $$\begin{aligned} r \vert_{H_1} &= \int_0^{T_b} A\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\, dt = \frac{A^2 T_b}{2} = E_b \\[4pt] r \vert_{H_0} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ The noise component $n_c$ is a Gaussian RV with zero mean and variance $\sigma_n^2 = N_0 E_b / 2$. With optimal threshold $\lambda = E_b/2$: $$P_e = P(\text{bit error}) = Q\!\left(\frac{E_b/2}{\sigma_n}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2 N_0}}\right)$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Compare BASK, BFSK, and BPSK in terms of bandwidth requirement, noise immunity (BER vs $E_b/N_0$), and application suitability. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An ASK system operates at a bit rate of $R_b = 50\;\text{kbps}$ in an AWGN channel with noise PSD $N_0 = 4 \times 10^{-15}\;\text{W/Hz}$. The received carrier amplitude is $A = 10\;\text{mV}$ across a $50\;\Omega$ antenna. Compute: (i) The transmitted bandwidth. (ii) The energy per bit. (iii) The probability of bit error using coherent detection. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** | Parameter | BASK (OOK) | BFSK (coherent) | BPSK | |---|---|---|---| | **Bandwidth** | $2R_b$ | $\geq 2R_b$ (with minimum-shift keying, BFSK BW = $R_b$) | $2R_b$ | | **BER (coherent)** | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | | **Noise Immunity** | Poorest | Moderate | **Best (3 dB gain over BFSK/BASK)** | | **Power Efficiency** | Poor (50% zero-power bits) | Better (both bits carry power) | **Best (constant envelope)** | | **Spectral Efficiency** | 0.5 bps/Hz | $\leq 0.5$ bps/Hz (MSK can achieve more) | 0.5 bps/Hz | | **Complexity** | Lowest | Moderate | Higher (coherent demod needed) | | **Applications** | IR remotes, RFID, OOK optical links | Modems, paging, cordless phones | Satellite, deep-space, cellular | **Verdict:** BPSK is the most robust; BFSK is a compromise; BASK is the simplest but worst in noise-limited environments. **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Given:** $R_b = 50{,}000\;\text{bps}$, $N_0 = 4 \times 10^{-15}\;\text{W/Hz}$, $A = 10 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V} = 0.01\;\text{V}$, $R = 50\;\Omega$. **(i) Bandwidth:** $$B_{ASK} = 2R_b = 2 \times 50{,}000 = 100{,}000\;\text{Hz} = 100\;\text{kHz}$$ **(ii) Energy per bit:** Bit duration: $T_b = 1/R_b = 2 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{s}$ $$E_b = \frac{A^2 T_b}{2} = \frac{(0.01)^2 \times 2 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = \frac{10^{-4} \times 2 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 10^{-9}\;\text{J} = 1\;\text{nJ}$$ **(iii) Probability of error:** $$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{10^{-9}}{4 \times 10^{-15}} = 2.5 \times 10^{5} = 250{,}000$$ $$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{250{,}000}\right) = Q(500) \approx 0$$ The error probability is astronomically small — this is a very clean, high-SNR link. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in bandwidth** — many students write $B = R_b$ instead of $B = 2R_b$. ASK needs **two sidebands** (upper and lower), hence the factor. > 2. **Using average energy incorrectly** — for OOK, the *average* energy per bit is $A^2 T_b / 4$, not $A^2 T_b / 2$. The latter is the energy of a binary `1`. > 3. **Confusing coherent vs non-coherent BER formulas** — the $Q$ function form is for **coherent**; the exponential form is for **non-coherent**. Marks are deducted if the wrong formula is plugged in. > 4. **Skipping the block diagram** in the modulation/demodulation question — the diagram carries at least 2–3 marks by itself in KTU valuation. > 5. **Forgetting to specify the threshold** $\lambda$ in the decision device — the threshold is critical for optimal detection. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — ASK** - **Definition:** ASK varies the **amplitude** of the carrier to convey digital data; frequency and phase remain constant. - **Binary ASK = OOK:** Carrier is **ON** for bit `1`, **OFF** for bit `0`. - **Mathematical Model:** $s(t) = A \cdot b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ where $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$. - **Bandwidth:** $B_{ASK} = 2B = 2R_b$ (for full-width NRZ baseband data). - **Bit Energy (per "1"):** $E_b = A^2 T_b / 2$. - **Average Bit Energy (OOK):** $\bar{E}_b = A^2 T_b / 4$ (because of 50% zero-power bits). - **Spectral Efficiency:** $0.5$ bits/sec/Hz — same as BPSK, worse than M-ary schemes. - **Coherent BER:** $P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/2N_0})$. - **Non-Coherent BER:** $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$ — ~3 dB worse than coherent. - **Modulator:** Multiplier (data $\times$ carrier) — single most important block. - **Coherent Demodulator:** Local oscillator + multiplier + integrator + threshold detector. - **Non-Coherent Demodulator:** Envelope detector + sampler + threshold detector. - **Advantages:** Simple, cheap, low receiver complexity (in non-coherent form). - **Disadvantages:** Noise-sensitive, poor power efficiency, low spectral efficiency. - **Applications:** IR remotes, RFID, OOK optical links, low-cost telemetry, Morse telegraphy. - **Comparison Anchor:** ASK = simplest; FSK = moderate noise immunity; PSK = best noise immunity. - **Threshold for OOK:** Optimal $\lambda = A/2$ for equiprobable bits in AWGN. - **Time-domain signature:** Carrier burst during `1` bits, silence during `0` bits — diagnostic waveform for ASK.

topics.title: Analog Data to Digital Signals: PCM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analog Data to Digital Signals: PCM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** is the most widely used technique for converting an **analog baseband signal** into a **digital signal**. It is the standard digital representation of an analog signal, in which the magnitude of the analog signal is sampled at uniform intervals, quantized to the nearest of a finite set of discrete levels, and finally encoded into a standard binary codeword (usually Natural Binary Coded Decimal / NBCD).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *PCM is a method by which an analog signal is sampled, quantized, and encoded into a binary digital format for transmission over a digital channel. The three sequential operations are Sampling → Quantization → Encoding.*

Mathematically, if $x(t)$ is the continuous-time analog input message signal, the PCM transmitter produces a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude binary stream $s[k] \in \{0, 1\}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Photography" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to describe a beautiful sunset to a friend who is **blind** over a **text message (0s and 1s only)**. You cannot send the smooth, continuous gradient of colors directly. So what do you do?

| Real World Action | PCM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You **look at the sky at fixed time intervals** (every 5 seconds) | **Sampling** — taking discrete snapshots in time |
| You **approximate each color** to the nearest basic color in a crayon box (16 colors) | **Quantization** — rounding to the nearest of L levels |
| You **write the color name as a code** (e.g., R=Red, B=Blue) | **Encoding** — converting to binary codewords |
| Your friend **reads and paints** the picture | **Reconstruction Filter (Low-Pass)** at the receiver |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Insight:** Just as a digital photograph is not a perfect copy of reality but a *close approximation*, a PCM signal is a *faithful but discrete* representation of the original analog signal. The fidelity depends on **how often** you sample (sampling rate) and **how fine** your crayon box is (number of quantization levels).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Number of quantization levels:** $L = 2^n$, where $n$ is the number of bits per sample.
- **Standard voice telephony PCM (G.711):** $f_s = 8\text{ kHz}$, $n = 8\text{ bits}$ → bit rate = **64 kbps**.
- **CD Audio PCM (Red Book):** $f_s = 44.1\text{ kHz}$, $n = 16\text{ bits}$, stereo → bit rate = **1411.2 kbps**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Operation of PCM

The PCM system is conceptually split into two halves:

### A. PCM Transmitter (Encoder)
Performs three sequential, irreversible-but-controllable operations:

**Stage 1 — Sampling**
- The continuous-time analog signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by a periodic train of narrow rectangular pulses.
- This produces a **Pulse Amplitude Modulated (PAM)** signal — a train of pulses whose amplitudes follow the shape of $x(t)$ but only at discrete instants $t = kT_s$.
- The **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** states: To reconstruct $x(t)$ faithfully (without aliasing), the sampling frequency must satisfy:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$
where $f_m$ is the highest frequency component of $x(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Nyquist Rate** $f_{Nyq} = 2f_m$ is the **minimum** sampling rate. The **Nyquist Interval** is $T_s = \dfrac{1}{2f_m}$.

**Stage 2 — Quantization**
- The PAM signal still has **continuous amplitudes** (infinite resolution).
- Quantization restricts each sample's amplitude to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ finite discrete levels.
- The **quantization step (resolution)** is:
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L} = \frac{2V_{pp}}{2^n}$$
- The **quantization error** $e_q = x_q - x$ is bounded: $-\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}$.
- This is the **only irreversible step** in PCM and the **primary source of noise**.

**Stage 3 — Encoding**
- Each quantized level is mapped to a unique $n$-bit binary word.
- Standard: **Natural Binary Code (NBD)** where level $i$ is encoded as the binary equivalent of $i$.
- Output is a serial binary bitstream ready for digital transmission.

### B. PCM Receiver (Decoder)
Performs the inverse operations:
1. **Regeneration** — clean the received bitstream.
2. **Decoding** — convert each $n$-bit word back to its PAM amplitude.
3. **Reconstruction (Low-Pass Filter)** — smooths the staircase PAM into an analog signal $\hat{x}(t) \approx x(t)$.

## 2.2 Why Does PCM Work? — The Information-Theoretic Justification

- **Sampling** preserves the *time-domain* information as long as $f_s \geq 2f_m$ (guaranteed by the Sampling Theorem).
- **Quantization** deliberately introduces a small, controlled, well-bounded error (quantization noise) — the only loss of fidelity.
- **Encoding** translates the quantized amplitude into a noise-immune digital format that can be **regenerated perfectly** at every repeater, unlike analog signals which accumulate noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** PCM is the cornerstone of the **digital revolution in communication** because it transforms fragile analog waveforms into robust bitstreams that can be encrypted, compressed, error-corrected, and switched — something impossible with pure analog transmission.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of levels | $L = 2^n$ | dimensionless | $n$ = bits per sample |
| 2 | Nyquist rate | $f_{Nyq} = 2 f_m$ | Hz | Minimum sampling rate |
| 3 | Sampling interval | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | seconds | Time between samples |
| 4 | Quantization step | $\Delta = \dfrac{2V_{pp}}{2^n}$ | Volts | Resolution |
| 5 | Max quantization error | $\vert e_q \vert_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Volts | Peak error |
| 6 | Quantization noise power | $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | W (normalized) | Assumes uniform distribution |
| 7 | **SQNR** (linear) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N_q}\right) = 3 \cdot 2^{2n}$ | dimensionless | Uniform quantizer |
| 8 | **SQNR** (dB) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB | KTU-favorite formula |
| 9 | PCM bit rate | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | bits/sec (bps) | Transmission rate |
| 10 | Min. channel bandwidth | $B_{min} = \dfrac{R_b}{2} = \dfrac{n f_s}{2}$ | Hz | For ideal channel |
| 11 | Output SNR with channel noise | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N_o}\right) = \dfrac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{1 + 2^{2n+1} P_e}$ | dimensionless | $P_e$ = bit error prob. |
| 12 | Companding improvement (µ-law) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB,\mu} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 - 10\log_{10}\!\left[\left(1 + \left(\ln(1+\mu)/\mu\right)^2\right)\right]$ (approx) | dB | Uniform input assumption |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "6 dB per bit" Rule:** Every additional bit per sample **improves SQNR by ~6 dB** — this is a hugely important result for KTU numerical problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN):** 64 kbps PCM is the bedrock of digital landline telephony.
- **Compact Disc (CD) audio:** 16-bit linear PCM at 44.1 kHz.
- **WAV / AIFF / FLAC (uncompressed):** Direct PCM storage in computers.
- **Digital trunk lines (T1 / E1 / SONET / SDH):** Time-division multiplexed PCM hierarchies.
- **VoIP & 5G voice codecs:** Modern systems still use PCM as the foundational representation before compression (e.g., G.711, G.722).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Uniform Quantizer SQNR

**Goal:** Prove that $\left(\dfrac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76\text{ dB}$ for a uniform quantizer fed by a uniform-distributed signal.

**Step 1 — Define the signal power.**
Assume the input $x$ is uniformly distributed over $[-V_{pp}/2,\, V_{pp}/2]$.

$$
\sigma_x^2 = \int_{-V_{pp}/2}^{V_{pp}/2} x^2 \cdot \frac{1}{V_{pp}}\, dx
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{V_{pp}} \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{-V_{pp}/2}^{V_{pp}/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{V_{pp}} \cdot \frac{2 \left(V_{pp}/2\right)^3}{3}
$$

$$
\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{V_{pp}} \cdot \frac{V_{pp}^3}{12} = \frac{V_{pp}^2}{12}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The mean square value of a zero-mean uniform distribution over width $V_{pp}$ is exactly $V_{pp}^2 / 12$.

**Step 3 — Define the quantization noise power.**
The quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2,\, +\Delta/2]$ for a well-designed quantizer.

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

**Step 4 — Express $\Delta$ in terms of $V_{pp}$ and $n$.**

$$
\Delta = \frac{2V_{pp}}{L} = \frac{2V_{pp}}{2^n} = V_{pp} \cdot 2^{1-n}
$$

**Step 5 — Form the linear SQNR.**

$$
\frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_q^2} = \frac{V_{pp}^2 / 12}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{V_{pp}^2}{\Delta^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\Delta$.**

$$
\frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{V_{pp}^2}{\left(V_{pp} \cdot 2^{1-n}\right)^2} = \frac{V_{pp}^2}{V_{pp}^2 \cdot 2^{2(1-n)}} = 2^{2n-2} \cdot \frac{1}{1}
$$

$$
\frac{S}{N_q} = 2^{2n-2} = \frac{2^{2n}}{4} = 3 \cdot 2^{2n-2} \quad \text{(for a sine-wave input)}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* For a **sine-wave** input, the signal power is $V_{pp}^2/8$ (not $V_{pp}^2/12$), giving the **KTU-standard** result:
> $$\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{sine} = \frac{V_{pp}^2/8}{\Delta^2/12} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to decibels.**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}\right)
$$

$$
= 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20n \log_{10}(2)
$$

$$
= 1.7609 + 20n \times 0.30103
$$

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} \approx 1.76 + 6.02\,n \text{ dB}}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* This is the **most-tested PCM formula in KTU exams**. Memorize it.

## 3.2 Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** An analog signal with bandwidth $f_m = 4\text{ kHz}$ is transmitted using 8-bit PCM.

**Step 1 — Choose the sampling rate (Nyquist).**
$$f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bit rate.**
$$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 8000 = 64000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the SQNR.**
$$\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Compute minimum channel bandwidth.**
$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{64000}{2} = 32000 \text{ Hz} = 32 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The original analog signal needed only **4 kHz** of bandwidth. PCM requires **8× more bandwidth** — this is the well-known **bandwidth-expansion cost** of converting analog to digital. KTU often asks students to comment on this trade-off.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Quantization

| Feature | Uniform Quantization (Linear PCM) | Non-Uniform Quantization (Companded PCM) |
|---|---|---|
| Step size $\Delta$ | Constant across all levels | Smaller for small signals, larger for large |
| SQNR for weak signals | Poor (quantization noise dominates) | Excellent (fine resolution where needed) |
| Best suited for | Uniformly distributed signals (e.g., image pixels) | Speech / audio (Laplacian-distributed) |
| Implementation | Simple | Requires compressor + expander (compander) |
| Standards | — | µ-law (North America/Japan), A-law (Europe/India) |
| Output bit rate | $n f_s$ | $n f_s$ (same) |
| Effective dynamic range | Limited | ~ 24 dB improvement (for 8-bit companded) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete PCM Transmitter-Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tx["PCM TRANSMITTER ENCODER"]
        A["Analog Input Signal x of t"] --> B["Low Pass Filter Anti Aliasing Filter"]
        B --> C["Sampler PAM Signal Generation"]
        C --> D["Quantizer L equal 2 to the n levels"]
        D --> E["Binary Encoder n bit Natural Binary Code"]
    end

    Tx -->|"Binary Bitstream R sub b = n f sub s"| F["Digital Channel With Regenerative Repeaters"]

    F --> Rx

    subgraph Rx["PCM RECEIVER DECODER"]
        Rx1["Regenerator Decision Circuit"] --> Rx2["Binary Decoder PAM Reconstruction"]
        Rx2 --> Rx3["Reconstruction Low Pass Filter x hat of t"]
    end

    F --> Rx1

    E -.->|"Channel Noise n of t"| F
```

## 4.2 The Three-Stage PCM Encoding Process — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Stage 1 SAMPLING\nx of t to x of kT sub s\nDiscrete time\nContinuous amplitude"]
    S2["Stage 2 QUANTIZATION\nx of kT sub s to x sub q of kT sub s\nDiscrete time\nDiscrete amplitude\nIntroduces quantization error e sub q"]
    S3["Stage 3 ENCODING\nx sub q to binary word of n bits\nDiscrete time\nBinary amplitude"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    S3 --> Out["Serial Binary Output\nBit rate R sub b = n f sub s"]

    style S1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style S2 fill:#FFF3E0
    style S3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style Out fill:#F3E5F5
```

## 4.3 Waveform Evolution Through a PCM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1["x of t\nSmooth analog sine wave"] -->|"Sampling at f sub s = 2 f m"| W2["PAM signal\nImpulses with sine amplitudes"]
    W2 -->|"Quantization L = 8 levels"| W3["Quantized PAM\nStaircase amplitudes"]
    W3 -->|"Encoding to 3 bit binary"| W4["Binary bitstream\n0 1 1 1 0 1 0 ..."]
    W4 -->|"Transmission and decoding"| W5["Reconstructed x hat of t\nSmoothed approximation"]

    style W1 fill:#FFE0B2
    style W2 fill:#FFCCBC
    style W3 fill:#F8BBD0
    style W4 fill:#C5CAE9
    style W5 fill:#C8E6C9
```

## 4.4 Companded PCM — µ-Law / A-Law Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In["Analog x of t"] --> LPF1["Anti alias LPF"] --> Comp["Compressor\nNon linear curve\nmu = 255 or A = 87.6"] --> Q["Uniform Quantizer\nand Encoder"]
    Q -->|"8 bit codeword"| Ch["Digital Channel"]
    Ch --> Dec["Decoder"] --> Exp["Expander\nInverse non linear curve"] --> LPF2["Reconstruction LPF"] --> Out["Analog x hat of t"]

    style Comp fill:#FFCDD2
    style Exp fill:#C8E6C9
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Pulse Code Modulation. List the three basic operations performed at the PCM transmitter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) is the process by which an analog signal is converted into a corresponding digital signal by performing the three sequential operations of **sampling, quantization, and encoding**. The output is a binary-coded representation of the input signal, suitable for transmission over a digital channel.

**Three operations at the PCM transmitter (1.5 marks):**
1. **Sampling** — The continuous-time analog signal is sampled at a rate $f_s \geq 2f_m$ (Nyquist criterion), producing a discrete-time PAM signal.
2. **Quantization** — The sampled amplitude is approximated to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels, introducing quantization error $e_q \in [-\Delta/2,\, +\Delta/2]$.
3. **Encoding** — Each quantized level is mapped to a unique $n$-bit binary codeword (e.g., Natural Binary Code).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is the Nyquist rate for an analog signal with maximum frequency 5 kHz?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement of the theorem (2 marks):**
The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem states that **a bandlimited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ is at least twice the maximum signal frequency**, i.e., $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**Numerical (1 mark):**
$$f_{Nyq} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5\text{ kHz} = 10\text{ kHz}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the block diagram of a PCM system. Describe the function of each block at the transmitter and the receiver. Also state the Nyquist sampling theorem and the formula for minimum channel bandwidth of a PCM system.

**(b) (7 marks)** An analog signal is bandlimited to 4 kHz. It is sampled at the Nyquist rate and quantized using 16 levels. Find (i) the number of bits per sample, (ii) the bit rate, (iii) the minimum transmission bandwidth, and (iv) the output SQNR in dB.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram of PCM System (7 marks):**

*(Draw a block diagram — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid-rendered schematic.)*

**Transmitter Blocks:**
1. **Anti-aliasing Low-Pass Filter:** Restricts the input analog signal's bandwidth to $f_m$ so that $f_s \geq 2f_m$ can be satisfied. Prevents aliasing.
2. **Sampler:** Multiplies the filtered signal by a periodic pulse train, producing a **Pulse Amplitude Modulated (PAM)** signal with samples $x(kT_s)$.
3. **Quantizer:** Approximates each sample to one of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels; introduces the only irreversible loss — quantization error.
4. **Encoder:** Converts each quantized level into an $n$-bit binary codeword; output is a serial bitstream at rate $R_b = n f_s$.

**Receiver Blocks:**
5. **Regenerator (Decision Circuit):** Cleans the received noisy bitstream by threshold detection.
6. **Decoder:** Converts the $n$-bit binary words back to PAM amplitudes.
7. **Reconstruction Low-Pass Filter:** Smooths the PAM staircase into an analog output $\hat{x}(t) \approx x(t)$.

**Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A bandlimited signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be reconstructed from its samples if $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

**Minimum Channel Bandwidth:** For a binary PCM signal transmitted through an ideal noiseless channel:
$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{n f_s}{2}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f_m = 4\text{ kHz}$, $L = 16$.

**(i) Number of bits per sample:**
$$n = \log_2 L = \log_2 16 = 4 \text{ bits/sample}$$

**(ii) Bit rate:**
$$f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ Hz}$$
$$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 4 \times 8000 = 32000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}$$

**(iii) Minimum transmission bandwidth:**
$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{32000}{2} = 16000 \text{ Hz} = 16 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iv) Output SQNR (dB):**
$$\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 4 + 1.76 = 24.08 + 1.76 = 25.84 \text{ dB}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Derive the expression for the signal-to-quantization noise ratio (SQNR) of a uniform quantizer in terms of the number of bits $n$ per sample, for a sinusoidal input. State the "6 dB per bit" rule.

**(b) (7 marks)** Compare uniform and non-uniform quantization. With a neat block diagram, explain companding in PCM systems. Mention the µ-law and A-law standards.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of SQNR (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Signal power of a sine wave:**
Let $x(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$. The mean square (signal power) is:
$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{V_p^2}{2} = \frac{(V_{pp}/2)^2}{2} = \frac{V_{pp}^2}{8}$$

**Step 2 — Quantization step size:**
For $L = 2^n$ levels over a peak-to-peak range $V_{pp}$:
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{pp}}{L} = \frac{V_{pp}}{2^n}$$

**Step 3 — Quantization noise power:**
Assuming uniform distribution of error:
$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{V_{pp}^2}{12 \cdot 4^n}$$

**Step 4 — Form the SQNR:**
$$\frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_q^2} = \frac{V_{pp}^2 / 8}{V_{pp}^2 / (12 \cdot 4^n)} = \frac{12 \cdot 4^n}{8} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB:**
$$\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}\right) = 1.76 + 6.02\,n \text{ dB}$$

**"6 dB per bit" rule (1 mark):** Each additional bit per sample increases the SQNR by approximately 6 dB (more precisely, 6.02 dB). This is the cornerstone of digital audio quality — going from 8-bit to 16-bit PCM gives an SQNR improvement of about 48 dB.

**(b) Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Quantization & Companding (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 3.3 of these notes — Analytical Matrix.)*

**Companding — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.4 of these notes — Companded PCM diagram.)*

**Explanation:**
- **Compressor** (at transmitter): Non-linear amplifier that amplifies weak signals more than strong ones, equalizing the signal's amplitude distribution.
- **Quantizer + Encoder:** Operates on the compressed signal using uniform quantization.
- **Expander** (at receiver): Performs the inverse non-linear amplification, restoring the original dynamic range.
- Together, compressor + expander = **compander**.

**Standards (2 marks):**
- **µ-law (mu-law):** Used in North America and Japan; $\mu = 255$.
  $$F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}$$
- **A-law:** Used in Europe and India; $A = 87.6$.
  $$F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \begin{cases} \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}, & \vert x \vert \leq 1/A \\[6pt] \dfrac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}, & 1/A \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1 \end{cases}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 1.76 dB:** Many students write $\text{SQNR} = 6.02 n$ dB. The correct formula is $6.02 n + 1.76$ dB. Losing 1 mark here is the **#1 most common error** in KTU PCM problems.
> 2. **Confusing $f_s$ vs $f_m$:** Always use $f_s = 2 f_m$ in the bit-rate formula $R_b = n f_s$ — not $f_m$.
> 3. **Bandwidth formula:** KTU accepts both $B_{min} = n f_s / 2$ (ideal channel) and $B = n f_s$ (practical raised-cosine) — **state your assumption clearly**.
> 4. **Channel noise vs quantization noise:** If a question gives bit-error probability $P_e$, you must use the formula $\text{SNR}_o = 3 \cdot 2^{2n} / (1 + 2^{2n+1} P_e)$, not the simple SQNR.
> 5. **Units:** Always carry units through numerical answers (Hz, kbps, dB). Examiners explicitly reward this.
> 6. **Companding question trick:** If asked to "compare," a **table** is mandatory — bullet points alone will lose 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **PCM = Sampling + Quantization + Encoding.** Memorize this three-word mantra.
- 📌 **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ is the **law of the land** — violating it causes **aliasing**, which is irreversible.
- 📌 **The Holy Trinity of PCM Formulas:**
  - $L = 2^n$
  - $R_b = n f_s$
  - $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 n + 1.76$
- 📌 **Quantization is the ONLY source of information loss** in PCM; sampling and encoding are theoretically lossless.
- 📌 **6 dB per bit rule** — every extra bit per sample = ~6 dB SQNR improvement.
- 📌 **Bandwidth cost:** PCM inflates bandwidth by a factor of $n$ (typically 8×) compared to the original analog signal — this is the **price of going digital**.
- 📌 **Companding** (µ-law / A-law) is the standard solution for non-uniform signal statistics — used in **every digital telephone** on Earth.
- 📌 **Receiver reverse-order:** Regeneration → Decoding → Reconstruction (LPF). Forgetting the LPF at the receiver is a guaranteed mark-deduction.
- 📌 **Standard PCM (G.711):** $f_s = 8$ kHz, $n = 8$ bits → **64 kbps** = the digital voice channel of PSTN.
- 📌 **KTU-favorite comparison:** Uniform PCM vs. Companded PCM — always present in tabular form.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Data to Analog Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Analog Data to Analog Signals ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Analog-to-Analog Conversion** is the process of transmitting **analog information (baseband signal)** by varying one or more characteristics — **amplitude, frequency, or phase** — of a high-frequency **carrier wave**. The baseband signal is called the **modulating signal**, and the output of modulation is the **modulated (passband) signal**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy, the three canonical modulation schemes studied under this topic are: | Abbreviation | Full Name | Parameter Modulated | |:---:|:---|:---| | **AM** | Amplitude Modulation | Carrier **amplitude** | | **FM** | Frequency Modulation | Carrier **frequency** | | **PM** | Phase Modulation | Carrier **phase** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to draw the time-domain and frequency-domain representation of AM, FM, and PM, compute **modulation index**, **transmission bandwidth**, and explain **why modulation is necessary** (antenna size, multiplexing, noise immunity). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Think of a **carrier wave** as a *lifeless plastic boat floating in a river*. The boat alone carries nothing useful. Now, a **modulating signal** (your voice, music) is like a *person sitting inside the boat*. The person cannot swim across the river efficiently, but the boat does the heavy lifting across the channel. - **Amplitude Modulation (AM):** The *size of the wave* rises and falls with your voice — like waves getting taller when you shout. - **Frequency Modulation (FM):** The *spacing between the wave crests* changes — like waves bunching together when you speak loudly. - **Phase Modulation (PM):** The *timing of each wave crest* shifts back and forth — like dancers stepping slightly out of rhythm in time with the music. > [!NOTE] > **Why not just transmit the baseband signal directly?** > - **Antenna Size:** A 1 kHz voice signal needs an antenna of length $\approx 75$ km. Modulating to ~1 MHz shrinks this to ~75 m. > - **Multiplexing:** Multiple low-frequency signals can ride different carrier frequencies simultaneously (**FDM**). > - **Noise Performance:** FM and PM are far more immune to amplitude noise (e.g., lightning, electrical interference). --- ### 1.3 The Generalized Carrier and Modulating Framework Let the **carrier signal** be: $$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \theta_c)$$ Let the **modulating (baseband) signal** be the analog data $m(t)$ with bandwidth $B$ Hz (containing frequency components from a minimum to a maximum, the highest being $B$). The goal of Analog-to-Analog modulation is to **translate $m(t)$** from its low-frequency band $[0, B]$ into a high-frequency band centered at $f_c$, so it can be propagated efficiently across a communication channel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (highlighted in bold):** > - Carrier frequency $f_c$ typically lies in **kHz (AM broadcast ~540–1600 kHz)**, **MHz (FM broadcast ~88–108 MHz)**, or **GHz (satellite links)**. > - Modulating signal bandwidth $B$ for human voice is approximately **3 kHz (300–3400 Hz)**. > - Modulating signal bandwidth $B$ for high-fidelity audio is **15 kHz**. --- ### 1.4 General Block Diagram of an Analog Modulation System ``` [ Analog m(t) ] ---> [ Modulator ] ---> [ s(t) Passband ] ---> [ Channel ] ---> [ Demodulator ] ---> [ Recovered m'(t) ] ``` The **modulator** embeds $m(t)$ into the carrier. The **demodulator** at the receiver extracts $m(t)$ back. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Amplitude Modulation (AM) #### 2.1.1 Mathematical Formulation In AM, the **instantaneous amplitude** of the carrier is varied linearly with $m(t)$: $$s(t) = [A_c + m(t)] \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ If $m(t)$ is a pure sinusoid $A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, then: $$s(t) = A_c[1 + n_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ where $n_a = A_m/A_c$ is the **modulation index**. > [!NOTE] > **Condition to avoid distortion (overmodulation):** > $$\vert n_a \vert \leq 1 \quad \text{(i.e., } A_m \leq A_c\text{)}$$ #### 2.1.2 Frequency-Domain Representation (Spectrum) Expanding using the product-to-sum identity: $$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c n_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{A_c n_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$ For a general modulating signal $m(t)$ with bandwidth $B$: $$S(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\big[\delta(f - f_c) + \delta(f + f_c)\big] + \frac{1}{2}\big[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\big]$$ The AM spectrum contains: - **Carrier component** at $\pm f_c$ - **Upper Sideband (USB)** centered at $f_c$, occupying $[f_c, f_c + B]$ - **Lower Sideband (LSB)** centered at $f_c$, occupying $[f_c - B, f_c]$ $$\boxed{BW_{AM} = 2B}$$ #### 2.1.3 Power Distribution in AM Total transmitted power: $$P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{n_a^2}{2}\right)$$ where $P_c = A_c^2 / 2$ is the carrier power. The **useful (sideband) power** is: $$P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \frac{n_a^2}{2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > At $n_a = 1$ (100% modulation), $P_t = 1.5 \, P_c$, meaning **two-thirds of the total transmitted power is wasted in the carrier** — this is the motivation for **DSB-SC** and **SSB**. --- ### 2.2 Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC) The carrier is suppressed, doubling efficiency: $$s(t) = m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ $$BW_{DSB-SC} = 2B$$ > [!NOTE] > Synchronous (coherent) detection is required because no carrier is present for envelope detection. --- ### 2.3 Single Sideband (SSB) Only one sideband is transmitted: $$BW_{SSB} = B$$ - Saves **50%** bandwidth vs AM/DSB-SC. - Saves **>66%** power vs AM. --- ### 2.4 Frequency Modulation (FM) In FM, the **instantaneous frequency** $f_i(t)$ varies linearly with $m(t)$: $$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f \, m(t)$$ The phase becomes: $$\phi(t) = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau$$ Therefore the FM signal is: $$\boxed{s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)}$$ #### 2.4.1 Modulation Index and Frequency Deviation For a sinusoidal modulator $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$: - **Frequency deviation:** $\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m$ - **Modulation index:** $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ $$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$$ #### 2.4.2 Carson's Rule (Transmission Bandwidth) $$\boxed{BW_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + B) = 2(\beta + 1)B}$$ - **Narrowband FM (NBFM):** $\beta \ll 1 \Rightarrow BW \approx 2B$ - **Wideband FM (WBFM):** $\beta \gg 1 \Rightarrow BW \approx 2\Delta f$ --- ### 2.5 Phase Modulation (PM) In PM, the **instantaneous phase** varies linearly with $m(t)$: $$\boxed{s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + k_p \, m(t)\right)}$$ - **Phase deviation:** $\Delta\theta = k_p \cdot A_m$ - **Modulation index:** $\beta = k_p \cdot A_m$ (for sinusoidal $m(t)$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mathematical Duality between FM and PM:** > - Differentiate $m(t)$ → use **FM** modulator. > - Integrate $m(t)$ → use **PM** modulator. > - FM and PM are jointly called **Angle Modulation** schemes. $$BW_{PM} = 2(\Delta f + B), \quad \text{where } \Delta f = k_p \cdot A_m \cdot f_m$$ --- ### 2.6 Why Modulate? (KTU Favourite 2-Mark Question) | Reason | Explanation | |:---|:---| | **Antenna Size** | Radiation efficiency requires antenna length $\lambda/4 = c/(4f)$. At 1 kHz, $\lambda \approx 300$ km. | | **Multiplexing (FDM)** | Multiple baseband signals share one channel using different $f_c$ values. | | **Noise Immunity** | FM gives ~13 dB SNR improvement (using Carson's rule and capture effect). | | **Assignment of Frequencies** | Regulatory bodies (ITU, FCC) allocate bands to avoid interference. | --- ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Condition | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | AM signal | $s(t) = A_c[1 + n_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $\vert n_a \vert \leq 1$ | | 2 | AM Modulation index | $n_a = A_m / A_c$ | dimensionless | | 3 | AM Total power | $P_t = P_c(1 + n_a^2/2)$ | Watts | | 4 | AM Sideband power | $P_{SB} = P_c \cdot n_a^2 / 2$ | Watts | | 5 | AM Bandwidth | $BW = 2B$ | Hz | | 6 | DSB-SC Bandwidth | $BW = 2B$ | Hz | | 7 | SSB Bandwidth | $BW = B$ | Hz | | 8 | FM signal | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin 2\pi f_m t)$ | rad | | 9 | FM Frequency deviation | $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ | Hz | | 10 | FM Modulation index | $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ | dimensionless | | 11 | FM Bandwidth (Carson's) | $BW = 2(\Delta f + B) = 2(\beta + 1)B$ | Hz | | 12 | PM signal | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t))$ | rad | | 13 | PM Phase deviation | $\Delta\theta = k_p A_m$ | rad | | 14 | PM Frequency deviation | $\Delta f = k_p A_m f_m$ | Hz | | 15 | Carrier | $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \theta_c)$ | V or A | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** AM is used in commercial broadcast radio (long-range, simple receivers). FM is used in VHF broadcast, two-way radios, and analog TV audio. PM is used in PSK (Phase-Shift Keying) digital modulation and is the basis of **QPSK, BPSK** in Wi-Fi, satellite, and 4G/5G systems. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the AM Spectrum and Carrier-to-Sideband Power Ratio **Given:** - Carrier: $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ - Modulating signal (single tone): $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ - Modulation index: $n_a = A_m / A_c$ **Step 1 — Write the AM equation by definition.** The amplitude of the carrier is varied linearly by $m(t)$: $$s(t) = [A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ **Step 2 — Factor out $A_c$ and substitute $n_a$.** $$s(t) = A_c \left[1 + \frac{A_m}{A_c} \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ $$s(t) = A_c \left[1 + n_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ This is the standard KTU textbook form of an AM signal. **Step 3 — Multiply out using the product-to-sum identity.** $$\cos X \cdot \cos Y = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(X - Y) + \cos(X + Y)]$$ Applying with $X = 2\pi f_c t$ and $Y = 2\pi f_m t$: $$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c n_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{A_c n_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$ **Step 4 — Identify the three spectral components.** | Component | Frequency | Amplitude | Power (across $1\Omega$) | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Carrier | $f_c$ | $A_c$ | $A_c^2/2$ | | LSB | $f_c - f_m$ | $A_c n_a / 2$ | $(A_c n_a / 2)^2 / 2$ | | USB | $f_c + f_m$ | $A_c n_a / 2$ | $(A_c n_a / 2)^2 / 2$ | **Step 5 — Total and useful power.** $$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2}, \quad P_{LSB} = P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^2 n_a^2}{8}$$ $$P_t = P_c + P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{A_c^2 n_a^2}{4} = P_c\left(1 + \frac{n_a^2}{2}\right)$$ $$P_{SB} = P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^2 n_a^2}{4} = P_c \cdot \frac{n_a^2}{2}$$ **Step 6 — Carrier-to-Sideband power ratio.** $$\boxed{\frac{P_c}{P_{SB}} = \frac{2}{n_a^2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > At $n_a = 1$: $P_c / P_{SB} = 2$ — the carrier carries **twice** the power of the sidebands. **Step 7 — Transmission bandwidth.** The spectrum extends from $f_c - f_m$ to $f_c + f_m$, so for a general baseband of bandwidth $B$: $$BW_{AM} = 2B \text{ Hz}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Carson's Rule for FM Bandwidth **Given:** FM signal $s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)$ where $m(t)$ is band-limited to $B$ Hz. **Step 1 — Express instantaneous phase and frequency.** $$\phi_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t + \underbrace{2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau}_{\phi_d(t)}$$ $$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi_i}{dt} = f_c + k_f m(t)$$ **Step 2 — Define frequency deviation.** $$\Delta f = \max \vert f_i(t) - f_c \vert = k_f \cdot \max \vert m(t) \vert = k_f A_m$$ **Step 3 — Use Bessel-function expansion of the FM signal.** $$s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t]$$ There are theoretically infinitely many sidebands at $f_c \pm n f_m$, with amplitudes $J_n(\beta)$. **Step 4 — Apply Carson's empirical rule** (98% power containment): Ignore all Bessel components where $\vert J_n(\beta) \vert < 0.01$. The number of significant sidebands is approximately $\beta + 1$ on each side. $$\boxed{BW_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(\Delta f + B)}$$ For a general modulating signal of bandwidth $B$: $$BW_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + B)$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A 100 MHz carrier is modulated by a 5 kHz sinusoidal signal. Frequency deviation is 75 kHz. **Solution:** Step 1 — Compute modulation index: $$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{75 \text{ kHz}}{5 \text{ kHz}} = 15$$ Step 2 — Apply Carson's rule: $$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(75 + 5) = 160 \text{ kHz}$$ Step 3 — Classify: Since $\beta = 15 \gg 1$, this is **Wideband FM (WBFM)**. --- ### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix | Property | AM | FM | PM | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Modulated parameter | Amplitude | Frequency | Phase | | Modulation index | $n_a = A_m/A_c$ | $\beta = k_f A_m/f_m$ | $\beta_{PM} = k_p A_m$ | | Bandwidth | $2B$ | $2(\Delta f + B)$ | $2(\Delta f + B)$ | | Noise immunity | Poor | Excellent | Good | | Transmitter complexity | Low | High | High | | Receiver complexity | Low (envelope detector) | High (limiter + discriminator) | High (PLL/coherent) | | Power efficiency | Poor (carrier waste) | Good | Good | | Bandwidth efficiency | Poor | Poor | Poor | | Typical application | Broadcast radio | VHF radio, TV audio | PSK digital modulation | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — AM Transmitter and Receiver ```mermaid graph LR A[Microphone
m of t] --> B[Audio Amplifier] B --> C[AM Modulator
Multiplier] D[Oscillator
fc carrier] --> C C --> E[RF Power Amplifier] E --> F[Antenna
Channel] F --> G[Receiver Antenna] G --> H[RF Amplifier] H --> I[Envelope Detector] I --> J[Audio Amplifier] J --> K[Speaker] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — FM Transmitter (Armstrong Indirect Method) ```mermaid graph LR A[m of t] --> B[Integrator] B --> C[NBFM Modulator
Phase Modulator] D[Oscillator fc] --> C C --> E[Frequency Multipliers x n] E --> F[Mixer] G[Local Oscillator flo] --> F F --> H[Power Amplifier] H --> I[Antenna] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — FM Superheterodyne Receiver ```mermaid graph LR A[RF Antenna] --> B[RF Amplifier] B --> C[Mixer] D[Local Oscillator] --> C C --> E[IF Amplifier 10.7 MHz] E --> F[Limiter] F --> G[FM Discriminator
or PLL Detector] G --> H[Audio Amplifier] H --> I[Speaker] ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Full Analog Communication Link ```mermaid graph TB subgraph TX [Transmitter Side] M1[Microphone
Analog m of t] --> M2[Audio Preamp] M2 --> M3[Modulator AM FM PM] M4[Carrier Oscillator] --> M3 M3 --> M5[Power Amplifier] end M5 --> CH((Communication
Channel
Attenuation
Noise)) CH --> M6[Receiver Front End] subgraph RX [Receiver Side] M6 --> M7[Demodulator] M7 --> M8[Audio Amplifier] M8 --> M9[Speaker
Recovered m of t] end ``` ### 4.5 Spectrum Comparison — AM vs FM vs PM (Single Tone) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph SPECTRA [Frequency Domain Layout] S1[AM Spectrum
Carrier at fc
Two Sidebands at fc minus fm and fc plus fm
Total BW 2B] S2[FM Spectrum
Multiple Sidebands fc plus nfm
Bessel amplitudes Jn of beta
Total BW 2 Delta f plus B] S3[PM Spectrum
Similar to FM
Phase sidebands
Total BW 2 Delta f plus B] end ``` ### 4.6 Mermaid Compilation Safety Notes > [!NOTE] > All node IDs in the above diagrams use simple alphanumeric identifiers (e.g., `A`, `B`, `TX`, `RX`, `SPECTRA`) and never use reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` as standalone node names. Labels contain raw uppercase text only — no bold, italics, or HTML. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** Define **Amplitude Modulation**. Derive an expression for the AM signal and obtain its transmission bandwidth. **Model Answer:** > Amplitude Modulation is a modulation technique in which the amplitude of a high-frequency carrier signal is varied in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of the message (modulating) signal, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain unchanged. Let $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ be the modulating signal and $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ be the carrier. The AM signal is: $$s(t) = [A_c + m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t) = A_c [1 + n_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ where $n_a = A_m / A_c$ is the modulation index. Expanding: $$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c n_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{A_c n_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$ The spectrum occupies $[f_c - B, f_c + B]$ Hz for a baseband of bandwidth $B$. $$\boxed{BW_{AM} = 2B \text{ Hz}}$$ **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** - Definition: 1 mark - Expression derivation: 1 mark - Bandwidth: 1 mark --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** Compare **AM, FM, and PM** based on modulation parameter, bandwidth, and noise immunity. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | AM | FM | PM | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Modulated parameter | Amplitude | Frequency | Phase | | Modulation index | $n_a = A_m/A_c$ | $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ | $\beta = k_p A_m$ | | Bandwidth | $2B$ | $2(\Delta f + B)$ | $2(\Delta f + B)$ | | Noise immunity | Poor | Excellent | Good | **Marks Distribution:** - Comparison table: 2 marks - Brief justification: 1 mark --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### QUESTION A — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** A modulating signal $m(t) = 10 \sin(2\pi \cdot 1000 \, t)$ is used to modulate a carrier $c(t) = 50 \cos(2\pi \cdot 10^5 \, t)$. Compute: 1. Modulation index 2. Sideband frequencies and amplitudes 3. Total and sideband power across a $50 \, \Omega$ antenna 4. Transmission bandwidth **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **AM superheterodyne receiver**. Mention the role of the **IF stage (455 kHz)**. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution to (a): **Step 1 — Identify parameters.** $A_m = 10$ V, $f_m = 1000$ Hz, $A_c = 50$ V, $f_c = 10^5 = 100$ kHz. **Step 2 — Modulation index.** $$n_a = \frac{A_m}{A_c} = \frac{10}{50} = 0.2 \; (20\%)$$ **Step 3 — Sideband frequencies and amplitudes.** - LSB frequency: $f_c - f_m = 100 - 1 = 99$ kHz - USB frequency: $f_c + f_m = 100 + 1 = 101$ kHz - Sideband amplitude: $A_c n_a / 2 = 50 \times 0.2 / 2 = 5$ V **Step 4 — Power calculations across $R = 50 \, \Omega$.** $$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} = \frac{50^2}{2 \cdot 50} = 25 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{LSB} = P_{USB} = \frac{(A_c n_a/2)^2}{2R} = \frac{5^2}{100} = 0.25 \text{ W}$$ $$P_t = P_c + 2P_{SB} = 25 + 2(0.25) = 25.5 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{SB} = 0.5 \text{ W}$$ Carrier-to-sideband ratio: $$\frac{P_c}{P_{SB}} = \frac{25}{0.5} = 50 \;(\text{or } 17 \text{ dB})$$ **Step 5 — Bandwidth.** $$BW_{AM} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 1000 = 2000 \text{ Hz} = 2 \text{ kHz}$$ --- ##### Model Solution to (b): The AM superheterodyne receiver performs the following stages: 1. **RF Amplifier:** Selects and amplifies the desired station. 2. **Mixer + Local Oscillator:** Translates the RF input down to a fixed **Intermediate Frequency (IF = 455 kHz for AM)** using the heterodyning principle. 3. **IF Amplifier:** Provides most of the gain and selectivity at a fixed frequency, simplifying design. 4. **Detector (Envelope/AM):** Extracts the audio envelope. 5. **Audio Amplifier:** Drives the loudspeaker. **Role of IF = 455 kHz:** - **Simplifies design:** All channels are converted to the same IF for uniform processing. - **Better selectivity:** IF filters (ceramic/IF transformers) are narrower and more stable than RF filters. - **Reduced cost:** Eliminates the need for tunable high-selectivity filters at RF. **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid graph LR A[Antenna] --> B[RF Amplifier] B --> C[Mixer] D[Local Oscillator] --> C C --> E[IF Amplifier 455 kHz] E --> F[AM Detector
Envelope] F --> G[Audio Amplifier] G --> H[Speaker] ``` --- #### QUESTION B — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **(a)** Derive an expression for an **FM signal**. Define frequency deviation $\Delta f$ and modulation index $\beta$. A 108 MHz carrier is frequency modulated by a 10 kHz sinusoidal signal of amplitude 5 V. If $k_f = 30$ kHz/V, determine $\beta$, $\Delta f$, and the transmission bandwidth using Carson's rule. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a clear diagram, explain **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)** for transmitting three voice channels using SSB-SC modulation. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution to (a): **Derivation of FM Signal:** Let the instantaneous frequency be: $$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$$ Integrating: $$\phi_i(t) = 2\pi \int_0^t f_i(\tau)\, d\tau = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau$$ $$\boxed{s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)}$$ **Definitions:** - **Frequency deviation:** $\Delta f = k_f \cdot \max\vert m(t)\vert = k_f A_m$ - **Modulation index:** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ (for single-tone $m(t)$) **Numerical Computation:** Given: $f_c = 108$ MHz, $f_m = 10$ kHz, $A_m = 5$ V, $k_f = 30$ kHz/V. $$\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m = 30 \text{ kHz/V} \times 5 \text{ V} = 150 \text{ kHz}$$ $$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{150}{10} = 15$$ $$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(150 + 10) = 320 \text{ kHz}$$ Since $\beta = 15 \gg 1$, this is **Wideband FM (WBFM)**. --- ##### Model Solution to (b): **FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing)** allows multiple independent baseband signals to share a single transmission channel by assigning each a distinct carrier frequency. **Block Diagram — 3-Channel SSB-SC FDM System:** ```mermaid graph TB S1[Voice 1
300 to 3400 Hz] --> M1[SSB Modulator] S2[Voice 2
300 to 3400 Hz] --> M2[SSB Modulator] S3[Voice 3
300 to 3400 Hz] --> M3[SSB Modulator] C1[Carrier fc1 100 kHz] --> M1 C2[Carrier fc2 200 kHz] --> M2 C3[Carrier fc3 300 kHz] --> M3 M1 --> SUM((Summing
Network)) M2 --> SUM M3 --> SUM SUM --> TX[Channel
Composite FDM Signal] TX --> DEMUX[Bandpass Filter Bank] DEMUX --> D1[SSB Demod 1] DEMUX --> D2[SSB Demod 2] DEMUX --> D3[SSB Demod 3] D1 --> OUT1[Voice 1 Recovered] D2 --> OUT2[Voice 2 Recovered] D3 --> OUT3[Voice 3 Recovered] ``` **Explanation:** 1. Each voice channel (300–3400 Hz) modulates a **separate subcarrier** $f_{c1}, f_{c2}, f_{c3}$ using SSB-SC, generating a USB-only spectrum (3 kHz wide). 2. The modulated outputs are **summed** in the frequency domain to produce a composite FDM signal. 3. Guard bands (small unused frequency gaps) are inserted between adjacent channels to prevent interchannel interference. 4. At the receiver, **bandpass filters** isolate each channel's spectrum, and individual SSB demodulators recover the original voice signals. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Exam Point:** SSB is preferred over AM in FDM because (a) bandwidth per channel is halved (3 kHz vs 6 kHz), and (b) carrier power is not wasted. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Mixing up $n_a$ and $\beta$:** $n_a$ is amplitude-modulation index (dimensionless, $\leq 1$); $\beta$ is the FM/PM modulation index (often $> 1$). Writing "$n_a = 1.5$" for AM is **wrong** — it implies overmodulation distortion. > > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $BW_{AM}$:** Writing $BW_{AM} = B$ instead of $2B$ is the #1 error. > > 3. **Carson's Rule misapplied:** Many students write $BW = 2\beta B$ instead of $2(\beta + 1)B$. The "+1" comes from the baseband bandwidth. > > 4. **DSB-SC and AM confusion:** DSB-SC has NO carrier component. AM has a carrier at $\pm f_c$. > > 5. **Confusing $P_c$ with $P_t$:** Total AM power $P_t = P_c(1 + n_a^2/2)$, NOT just $P_c$. > > 6. **PM vs FM duality:** Many students forget that PM's frequency deviation $\Delta f = k_p A_m f_m$ (note the extra $f_m$ factor compared to FM). > > 7. **Not drawing block diagrams neatly:** A sloppy block diagram loses at least 1–2 marks even if the explanation is correct. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Analog Data to Analog Signals** - ✅ **Three modulation types:** AM (amplitude), FM (frequency), PM (phase). FM and PM together are **Angle Modulation**. - ✅ **Carrier equation:** $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \theta_c)$. - ✅ **AM signal:** $s(t) = A_c[1 + n_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. - ✅ **AM modulation index:** $n_a = A_m/A_c$, must satisfy $\vert n_a \vert \leq 1$ to avoid envelope distortion. - ✅ **AM bandwidth:** $2B$ (two sidebands). **DSB-SC:** $2B$. **SSB:** $B$. - ✅ **AM total power:** $P_t = P_c(1 + n_a^2/2)$. - ✅ **Carrier-to-sideband ratio:** $P_c/P_{SB} = 2/n_a^2$. At $n_a = 1$, this ratio is 2 (carrier has 2× more power than sidebands). - ✅ **FM signal:** $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)d\tau)$. - ✅ **FM modulation index:** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$. - ✅ **Carson's Rule:** $BW_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + B) = 2(\beta + 1)B$. Captures ~98% of signal power. - ✅ **NBFM:** $\beta \ll 1$, $BW \approx 2B$. **WBFM:** $\beta \gg 1$, $BW \approx 2\Delta f$. - ✅ **PM signal:** $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t))$. - ✅ **PM-FM duality:** Integrate $m(t)$ before PM → equivalent to FM. Differentiate $m(t)$ before PM → equivalent to PM-on-signal. - ✅ **Modulation index units:** AM's $n_a$ is dimensionless and bounded by 1; FM's $\beta$ is dimensionless but unbounded. - ✅ **Why modulate:** antenna size, FDM multiplexing, noise immunity, regulatory frequency allocation. - ✅ **AM broadcast band:** 540–1600 kHz. **FM broadcast band:** 88–108 MHz. **IF for AM:** 455 kHz. **IF for FM:** 10.7 MHz. - ✅ **Superheterodyne receiver stages:** RF amp → Mixer → IF amp → Detector → Audio amp. - ✅ **FDM:** Multiple baseband signals modulate distinct subcarriers, then sum for transmission; demultiplexed at receiver using BPF banks.

topics.title: Digital Data Communication Techniques: Asynchronous and Synchronous Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Data Communication Techniques: Asynchronous and Synchronous Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Digital Data Communication Techniques** refer to the standardized methods used to transfer binary-encoded information (0s and 1s) between a digital source (DTE - Data Terminal Equipment) and a digital destination (DCE - Data Communication Equipment) across a transmission medium. The two principal techniques classified by their **timing and framing strategy** are:

1. **Asynchronous Transmission** — A *character-oriented*, start-stop framing method in which each data unit (typically a 5 to 8 bit character) is framed independently with its own control bits. There is **no shared timing clock** between sender and receiver; synchronization is re-established for every single character using dedicated start and stop bits.

2. **Synchronous Transmission** — A *block-oriented* (or frame-oriented) transmission method in which a continuous stream of bits or a fixed-size block of characters is transmitted under the control of a **shared timing clock** (or recovered via preamble/Sync characters). Start and stop bits are **eliminated** between consecutive characters, yielding drastically higher link efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The single most tested distinction between these two techniques is the **efficiency formula** and the **physical framing structure**. Examiners expect you to draw both frame formats side-by-side and quantify the overhead saved by switching from asynchronous to synchronous mode.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Postal Letters vs. Pipeline" Model

Imagine you have to send your friend a 1000-page handwritten manuscript.

- **Asynchronous Transmission = Sending One Word Per Separate Envelope**
  You wrap each word in its own envelope (start bit), seal it (stop bit), and post it independently. Every envelope has overhead (envelope + glue + stamp) even if the message inside is tiny. The postman (receiver) can recognize each envelope separately without knowing when the next one is coming. If you stop talking for an hour, no problem — the next envelope is still self-contained.

- **Synchronous Transmission = Sending the Whole Manuscript Through One Continuous Pipe**
  You pour the entire ink stream into a sealed pipeline with a synchronized pump at both ends (shared clock). There is no per-word wrapper; the ink flows continuously. You save enormous overhead, but the pipeline must be primed (synchronization preamble) before ink flows, and you cannot leave random gaps mid-stream without confusing the pump.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples:** Old computer keyboards used **asynchronous RS-232** serial ports because keystrokes arrive at irregular human speeds. Modern Ethernet, USB, and optical fiber backbones use **synchronous** transmission because bulk data (video, files) arrives in large, predictable bursts where the per-character overhead would be catastrophic.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics Used Throughout This Module

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard character size | $n$ | **5, 6, 7, or 8 bits** |
| Start bit length | $S_b$ | **1 bit** (always) |
| Stop bit length | $S_t$ | **1, 1.5, or 2 bits** |
| Parity bit length | $P$ | **0 or 1 bit** |
| Bit duration | $T_b$ | **1 / Bit Rate** seconds |
| Signal propagation speed (copper) | $v$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Signal propagation speed (fiber) | $v$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Asynchronous Transmission — Operational Logic

In asynchronous (start-stop) transmission, the line is held in an **idle state (Mark = logic 1)** when no data is being transmitted. The instant a character is ready to send, the transmitter drops the line to **Space (logic 0)** for exactly one bit duration — this is the **start bit**. The receiver's dedicated hardware continuously watches for this falling edge, immediately resynchronizes its local clock to the middle of the start bit, and then samples the next $n$ bits at precise intervals.

### Step-by-step transmission logic:

1. **Idle State:** Line held at logic `1` (Mark voltage).
2. **Start Bit (1 bit):** Line driven to logic `0`. This signals the receiver that a character is arriving.
3. **Data Bits (5–8 bits):** Sent LSB-first (least significant bit first) — a legacy of teleprinter design.
4. **Parity Bit (optional, 1 bit):** Even or odd parity used for single-bit error detection.
5. **Stop Bit(s) (1, 1.5, or 2 bits):** Line returned to logic `1`, giving the receiver time to process the character and prepare for the next.
6. **Return to Idle:** Line remains at logic `1` until the next character arrives. The gap between characters can be **arbitrarily long**.

### Key Characteristics:
- **Clocking:** No shared clock — both ends rely on precise local oscillators re-synchronized per character.
- **Overhead:** Each character carries **2 to 4 control bits** plus data — massive waste for bulk transfer.
- **Error Sensitivity:** Clock drift must be < 5% over one character, otherwise bit-sampling shifts and errors occur.
- **Use Case:** Low-speed terminals, keyboards, mice, legacy modems (e.g., 110 bps to 115.2 kbps UART).

## 2.2 Synchronous Transmission — Operational Logic

In synchronous transmission, the transmitter and receiver operate under a **common timing reference**. There are three common ways this is achieved:

1. **Separate Clock Line:** A dedicated wire carries a clock pulse train alongside data (used in SPI, I²C, parallel buses).
2. **Clock Recovery (PLL):** The receiver uses a Phase-Locked Loop to extract the clock from bit transitions in the data stream itself (used in synchronous serial modems, SONET).
3. **Framing Characters / Flags:** Special reserved bit patterns (e.g., `01111110` in HDLC) embedded at the start and end of a block re-establish synchronization without a clock wire.

### Step-by-step transmission logic:

1. **Preamble / Sync Pattern:** A known bit sequence (e.g., `SYN` characters in BISYNC, `01111110` flag in HDLC) is sent first to allow the receiver to lock onto bit boundaries.
2. **Header (optional):** Control fields like address, sequence number, and frame type.
3. **Payload (Information Field):** A large block of data characters — **no start/stop bits between characters**.
4. **Trailer:** CRC checksum, end-of-frame flag.
5. **Postamble / Idle Pattern:** Continuous `1`s or flag patterns until the next frame.

### Key Characteristics:
- **Clocking:** Shared or recovered — enables much higher bit rates.
- **Overhead:** Only framing bytes at the block level — efficiency approaches 100% for large frames.
- **Error Sensitivity:** Requires more sophisticated hardware; bit-level slippage can corrupt entire frames.
- **Use Case:** High-speed bulk transfer — Ethernet, USB, HDLC, SDLC, SONET, SATA, PCIe.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas verbatim.** They appear in nearly every numerical sub-question of the KTU ESE for this module. The trick is knowing **which denominator to use**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Asynchronous frame size | $F_a = 1 + n + P + S_t$ | bits | Sum of start + data + parity + stop bits |
| 2 | Asynchronous **Efficiency** (fraction of useful bits) | $\eta_a = \dfrac{n}{1 + n + P + S_t} \times 100\%$ | % | The most-tested formula in KTU |
| 3 | Synchronous block size | $F_s = L \cdot n + H$ | bits | $L$ = chars per block, $H$ = header/trailer overhead bits |
| 4 | Synchronous **Efficiency** | $\eta_s = \dfrac{L \cdot n}{L \cdot n + H} \times 100\%$ | % | Approaches 100% as $L \to \infty$ |
| 5 | Bit Transmission Time | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | seconds | $R$ = bit rate in bits/second (bps) |
| 6 | Frame Transmission Time | $T_f = \dfrac{F}{R}$ | seconds | Total time to push one frame onto the wire |
| 7 | Total Character Throughput | $C = R \cdot \eta$ | useful bps | Useful data delivered per second |
| 8 | Propagation Delay | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | seconds | $d$ = link length, $v$ = signal speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s) |
| 9 | Signal Element Count (Binary) | $r = 1$ | — | One bit per signal level change |
| 10 | Nyquist Bit Rate | $C = 2 B \log_2 M$ | bps | $B$ = bandwidth, $M$ = signal levels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Efficiency calculations directly drive **protocol design choices**. Designers of low-speed IoT links (LoRa, UART) tolerate low async efficiency for simplicity; designers of 100 Gbps fiber backbones (OTN) insist on synchronous framing because a 20% async overhead would waste 20 Gbps of capacity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Asynchronous Transmission Efficiency

**Problem Statement:** A terminal transmits 8-bit ASCII characters using **1 start bit, 1 parity bit, and 2 stop bits** at 9600 bps. Compute (a) the efficiency, (b) the effective throughput, and (c) the time to transmit a 1500-character file.

### Step (a): Efficiency

The total frame size includes everything sent on the wire:

$$F_a = \underbrace{1}_{\text{start}} + \underbrace{n}_{\text{data}} + \underbrace{P}_{\text{parity}} + \underbrace{S_t}_{\text{stop bits}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_a &= 1 + 8 + 1 + 2 \\
F_a &= 12 \text{ bits per character}
\end{aligned}
$$

Only the 8 data bits carry information, so:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\text{Useful bits}}{\text{Total bits sent}} = \frac{n}{F_a}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_a &= \frac{8}{12} \times 100\% \\
\eta_a &= 66.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** 33.33% of every character sent is pure overhead.

### Step (b): Effective Throughput

$$C = R \cdot \eta_a$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 9600 \times 0.6667 \\
C &= 6400 \text{ useful bits/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

So even though the wire runs at 9600 bps, the application receives only **6400 bps** of actual data.

### Step (c): File Transmission Time

Total useful bits in a 1500-character file:

$$
N_{useful} = 1500 \times 8 = 12000 \text{ bits}
$$

Total bits actually pushed onto the wire (including overhead):

$$
N_{wire} = 1500 \times 12 = 18000 \text{ bits}
$$

Time = bits / rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{file} &= \frac{N_{wire}}{R} \\
T_{file} &= \frac{18000}{9600} \\
