- Example: If frame 4 is lost, the sender retransmits frames 4, 5, 6, 7 (Go Back N).
- Receiver has **no buffer** for out-of-order frames.

**Step 3 — Selective Repeat ARQ (2.5 Marks)**

- Send window size: $W \leq 2^{k-1}$
- Receive window size: $W$ (accepts and **buffers** out-of-order frames)
- On error: only the **specific lost/erroneous frame** is retransmitted.
- Receiver has a **buffer** for out-of-order frames; delivers them in order to the network layer.

---

**Part (b) — Window Size Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Go-Back-N with k=3 (3 Marks)**

$$W_{max}^{GBN} = 2^k - 1 = 2^3 - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7$$

So with 3-bit sequence numbers, the sender can transmit **up to 7 frames** before needing an ACK.

**Step 2 — Selective Repeat with k=3 (3 Marks)**

$$W_{max}^{SR} = 2^{k-1} = 2^{3-1} = 2^2 = 4$$

So with 3-bit sequence numbers, the sender can transmit **up to 4 frames** before needing an ACK.

**Step 3 — Why Selective Repeat Needs Smaller Window (1 Mark)**

In Selective Repeat, the receiver **buffers out-of-order frames** within its receive window. The window must be **half the sequence number space** ($2^{k-1}$) to prevent the **new frame's sequence number** from being **indistinguishable** from a **retransmitted old frame** that might still be buffered. If $W = 2^k - 1$ were allowed, ambiguity would occur: the receiver could not tell whether a frame with a given sequence number is a NEW transmission or an OLD retransmission.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply efficiency by 100** — Always express efficiency in % for full marks.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — Convert kbps ↔ bps, ms ↔ sec carefully. Many students lose 1-2 marks here.
> 3. **Confusing $T_t$ and $T_p$** — Transmission is sending time; Propagation is travel time.
> 4. **Wrong formula for Selective Repeat window** — It is $2^{k-1}$, NOT $2^k - 1$. This is the #1 trap question.
> 5. **Omitting the timing diagram** in Stop-and-Wait explanations — KTU examiners explicitly award 1-2 marks for a labeled diagram.
> 6. **Not mentioning the "a" parameter** explicitly in efficiency derivations — Always state $a = T_p/T_t$ as a separate step.
> 7. **Confusing Flow Control with Error Control** — Flow control is about **speed matching**; Error control is about **bit-level reliability**. Different topics!

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Preparation):**

- **Flow Control** = regulating the sender's rate to match the receiver's processing capacity. **End-to-end** mechanism.
- **Stop-and-Wait Protocol** = simplest, **1 frame at a time**, **1-bit** sequence number. Inefficient for high-*a* channels.
- **Efficiency of Stop-and-Wait**: $\eta = 1 / (1 + 2a)$, where $a = T_p / T_t = (R \cdot d) / (L \cdot v)$.
- **Sliding Window Protocol** = multiple frames in transit, improves utilization.
- **Go-Back-N ARQ**: send window $2^k - 1$, receive window **1**, retransmits **all** frames from the lost one onwards.
- **Selective Repeat ARQ**: send window $2^{k-1}$, receive window $2^{k-1}$, retransmits **only** the lost frame.
- **Throughput** = $\eta \times R$ (effective rate in bps).
- **Transmission time** $T_t = L / R$ — always in seconds.
- **Propagation time** $T_p = d / v$ — always in seconds.
- **High *a* value** = long delay, small frames, satellite links → **Stop-and-Wait is bad**; use **Sliding Window**.
- **Low *a* value** = short links, large frames → **Stop-and-Wait acceptable**.
- **Sequence numbering purpose** = distinguish NEW from RETRANSMITTED frames.
- **Receiver window = 1** in Go-Back-N means **NO out-of-order acceptance**; **receiver window = $2^{k-1}$** in Selective Repeat means **buffered acceptance**.
- **KTU Hot Topics**: Stop-and-Wait derivation, Go-Back-N vs Selective Repeat comparison, numerical problems on $T_t$, $T_p$, and $\eta$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Error Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Error Control** in data communication and networking refers to the set of techniques and mechanisms implemented primarily at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI reference model to detect, correct, and recover from errors introduced into data during transmission over a physical medium. Since no real communication channel is perfectly noise-free, the receiver must be able to verify the integrity of received frames and, when possible, reconstruct the original information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Error Control encompasses **Error Detection** (identifying that an error has occurred) and **Error Correction** (reconstructing the original, error-free data). The two principal correction paradigms are **Forward Error Correction (FEC)** and **Automatic Repeat Request (ARQ)**.

### 1.2 Types of Transmission Errors

**Single-Bit Error**

A single-bit error occurs when exactly one bit in a transmitted frame is inverted (i.e., a `0` becomes a `1` or vice versa) while all other bits are received correctly. This is the simplest error model, typically assumed in clean, low-noise environments such as fiber-optic links.

**Burst Error**

A burst error occurs when two or more consecutive bits in a frame are corrupted. The length of the burst is measured from the first corrupted bit to the last corrupted bit, inclusive. Burst errors are common in wireless channels, where interference or fading affects a contiguous span of bits.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - Single-bit errors: isolated, low-noise channels.
> - Burst errors: clustered, noisy channels (e.g., Wi-Fi, satellite). Most real-world error-control schemes (CRC, Hamming) are designed to handle burst errors of a specific maximum length.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **sending a physical letter** through a courier service. The courier is your physical channel. Along the way, the postman might smudge a word, tear a corner, or spill coffee on part of the page. How do you, the receiver, know the letter arrived intact?

- **Error Detection** is like asking the sender to write a **checksum number** at the bottom of the letter. When you receive it, you re-compute the same number locally. If the numbers do not match, you know something was damaged in transit.
- **Error Correction (FEC)** is like the sender writing the letter in **triplicate** so that even if one copy is smudged, you can still reconstruct the original from the other two.
- **Error Correction (ARQ)** is like telling the courier, *"If the page looks damaged, just send me a photocopy again."* The receiver detects an error and **requests a retransmission** rather than trying to fix it locally.

In digital communication, the "checksum number" is generated using algorithms (parity, CRC, checksum), the "triplicate writing" is **redundancy** (Hamming code, Reed-Solomon), and the "send it again" is **Stop-and-Wait, Go-Back-N, or Selective Repeat ARQ**.

### 1.4 Error Detection vs. Error Correction

| Aspect | Error Detection | Error Correction |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Determine *whether* an error occurred | Determine *what* the original data was |
| **Mechanism** | Append redundancy bits; receiver recomputes and compares | Use redundant structure to reconstruct original bits |
| **Overhead** | Lower (fewer redundant bits) | Higher (more redundant bits) |
| **Examples** | Parity, Checksum, CRC | Hamming Code, Reed-Solomon, Convolutional Codes |
| **Retransmission** | Required (ARQ approach) | Not required (FEC approach) |
| **Best For** | Reliable, low-latency links | High-latency, noisy links (e.g., deep-space) |

### 1.5 The Hamming Distance — A Foundational Metric

The **Hamming Distance** $d(x, y)$ between two codewords $x$ and $y$ of equal length is the number of bit positions at which they differ. It is the foundational metric for designing any error-control code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theorem (Hamming Bounds for Code Design):**
> - A code can detect up to $s$ errors per codeword if and only if the **minimum Hamming distance** $d_{\min} \geq s + 1$.
> - A code can correct up to $t$ errors per codeword if and only if $d_{\min} \geq 2t + 1$.
> - A code that both detects $s$ errors and corrects $t$ errors (where $s \geq t$) requires $d_{\min} \geq s + t + 1$.

### 1.6 Codeword, Dataword, and Redundancy

For any error-control scheme, the transmitted unit is a **codeword**, formed by concatenating the original **dataword** (the $m$ information bits) with $r$ **redundancy bits** computed from the dataword.

$$ n = m + r $$

where $n$ is the total codeword length, $m$ is the number of data bits, and $r$ is the number of redundant (check) bits. The **code rate** is defined as:

$$ R = \frac{m}{n} = \frac{m}{m + r} $$

The code rate measures the efficiency of the channel usage: higher $R$ means more useful data per transmitted bit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Error Detection Techniques — Theory and Logic

#### 2.1.1 Simple Parity Check

**Operational Steps:**
1. The sender appends a single bit (the **parity bit**) to the dataword such that the total number of `1`s is either **even (even parity)** or **odd (odd parity)**.
2. The receiver counts the `1`s in the received codeword. If the count violates the chosen parity convention, an error is flagged.
3. Parity check detects **any odd number of bit errors** in the codeword, but **misses all even numbers of bit errors**.

**Why it works (the intuition):** A single parity bit acts as a global XOR checksum. Any single-bit flip changes the parity, so the receiver's recomputed parity will not match. However, if two bits flip simultaneously, the parity is restored to its original value, and the error goes undetected.

#### 2.1.2 Two-Dimensional Parity

**Operational Steps:**
1. The data is organized into a matrix of $i$ rows and $j$ columns.
2. A parity bit is computed for each row and for each column.
3. An additional parity bit is computed for the entire parity column (or row) of parity bits.
4. The receiver recomputes the matrix of parities; the **intersection** of the failing row and failing column pinpoints the exact erroneous bit, enabling single-bit error correction.

**Why it works:** Two-dimensional parity is a simple form of single-bit error correction (SEC) using a structured redundancy grid.

#### 2.1.3 Checksum

**Operational Steps:**
1. The data is partitioned into $k$-bit segments (typically $k = 16$ for the Internet checksum).
2. All segments are added together using **one's complement arithmetic** (any carry-out from the most significant bit is wrapped around and added back to the sum).
3. The final sum is complemented to obtain the **checksum**, which is appended to the data.
4. The receiver sums all received segments (including the checksum) using the same one's complement arithmetic. A valid message produces a result of all `1`s; otherwise, an error is detected.

**Why it works:** Any single-bit or burst error in transmission will almost certainly alter the one's complement sum, producing a non-`111\ldots1` result at the receiver. The checksum is computationally cheap and is widely used in TCP, UDP, and IP headers.

#### 2.1.4 Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC)

**Operational Steps:**
1. The sender and receiver agree in advance on a **generator polynomial** $G(x)$ of degree $r$.
2. The dataword $M(x)$ (of length $m$) is appended with $r$ zero bits, equivalent to computing $x^r \cdot M(x)$.
3. The sender divides $x^r \cdot M(x)$ by $G(x)$ in **modulo-2 arithmetic** (XOR-based polynomial division) to obtain a remainder $R(x)$ of degree less than $r$.
4. The transmitted codeword is $T(x) = x^r \cdot M(x) \oplus R(x)$, which is **exactly divisible** by $G(x)$.
5. The receiver divides $T(x)$ by $G(x)$. A zero remainder means no detected error; any non-zero remainder indicates an error.

**Why it works:** CRC is essentially a polynomial division in the Galois Field $GF(2)$. Its strength lies in selecting $G(x)$ such that certain error patterns (single-bit, double-bit, odd-count, and short bursts) always produce non-zero remainders. CRC-32 (used in Ethernet) detects all single-bit errors, all double-bit errors, all odd-count errors, and all burst errors of length $\leq 32$ bits.

#### 2.1.5 Hamming Code (Single-Bit Error Correction)

**Operational Steps:**
1. For a dataword of $m$ bits, choose $r$ check bits such that $2^r \geq m + r + 1$.
2. Place check bits at positions that are powers of 2: positions $1, 2, 4, 8, \ldots$
3. Each check bit is the parity (typically even parity) over a specific subset of data and check bit positions.
4. The receiver recomputes all $r$ check bit parities. The pattern of failures forms a binary **syndrome** that directly identifies the position of a single-bit error.
5. If the syndrome is zero, no error occurred; otherwise, the syndrome's numeric value is the bit position of the error, and that bit is flipped to correct it.

**Why it works:** The check bits are arranged so that each covers a unique combination of data bits, making the syndrome a one-to-one mapping of error positions. Hamming codes are **perfect single-bit error-correcting codes** — they use the minimum redundancy needed to correct any single-bit error.

### 2.2 Error Correction Paradigms

#### 2.2.1 Forward Error Correction (FEC)

In FEC, the sender transmits enough redundant information that the receiver can detect and **correct** errors without any retransmission. FEC is essential in:
- Satellite and deep-space communication (long round-trip delays make ARQ impractical).
- Real-time streaming (audio/video) where retransmission would cause unacceptable latency.
- Storage media (CDs, DVDs, SSDs) where rereading is costly.

#### 2.2.2 Automatic Repeat Request (ARQ)

In ARQ, the receiver detects errors using an error-detection code (typically CRC) and requests retransmission of corrupted frames. ARQ requires:
- A **return channel** from receiver to sender.
- **Acknowledgments (ACKs)** for correctly received frames.
- **Negative acknowledgments (NACKs)** or timeouts for lost/erroneous frames.
- **Sequence numbers** to keep frames in order.

There are three principal ARQ variants, covered in Section 2.3.

### 2.3 ARQ Protocols — Sliding Window Mechanisms

| Protocol | Sender Window | Receiver Window | Retransmission Strategy | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** | 1 | 1 | Retransmit the only outstanding frame | Lowest |
| **Go-Back-N ARQ** | $W$ ($W \leq 2^k - 1$) | 1 | Retransmit the erroneous frame and all subsequent frames | Medium |
| **Selective Repeat ARQ** | $W$ ($W \leq 2^{k-1}$) | $W$ | Retransmit only the erroneous frame | Highest |

In the table above, $k$ denotes the number of bits used for sequence numbers, and $W$ denotes the maximum window size.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| \# | Formula / Theorem | Description | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n = m + r$ | Total codeword length | $n$ codeword bits, $m$ data bits, $r$ redundant bits |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{m}{n}$ | Code rate (channel efficiency) | $R \in (0, 1]$ |
| 3 | $d(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \oplus y_i$ | Hamming distance between codewords $x$ and $y$ | XOR over bit positions |
| 4 | $d_{\min} \geq s + 1$ | Detect up to $s$ errors | $d_{\min}$ minimum code distance |
| 5 | $d_{\min} \geq 2t + 1$ | Correct up to $t$ errors | $t$ correctable error count |
| 6 | $2^r \geq m + r + 1$ | Hamming code redundancy requirement | $m$ data bits, $r$ check bits |
| 7 | $a = \dfrac{t_p}{t_f}$ | Normalized propagation delay | $t_p$ propagation time, $t_f$ frame transmission time |
| 8 | $a = \dfrac{\text{Propagation Delay} \times \text{Bandwidth}}{\text{Frame Size}}$ | Equivalent definition of $a$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{S\&W}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | Stop-and-Wait ARQ efficiency (no errors) | $\eta$ channel utilization |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{S\&W, with errors}} = \dfrac{1 - P}{1 + 2a}$ | Stop-and-Wait with frame error probability $P$ | $P$ frame error rate |
| 11 | $W_{\text{GBN}} \leq 2^k - 1$ | Max Go-Back-N window size | $k$ sequence number bits |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{GBN}} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a} & W < 2a + 1 \\ 1 & W \geq 2a + 1 \end{cases}$ | Go-Back-N efficiency | $W$ window size |
| 13 | $W_{\text{SR}} \leq 2^{k-1}$ | Max Selective Repeat window size | $k$ sequence number bits |
| 14 | $\eta_{\text{SR}} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a} & W < 2a + 1 \\ 1 & W \geq 2a + 1 \end{cases}$ | Selective Repeat efficiency | $W$ window size |
| 15 | $T_{\text{cycle}} = t_f + 2 t_p + t_a + t_p$ | Total cycle time for Stop-and-Wait | $t_a$ ACK transmission time |
| 16 | $G(x) \mid T(x)$ | CRC divisibility condition | Generator $G(x)$ divides transmitted $T(x)$ |
| 17 | $R(x) = (x^r \cdot M(x)) \bmod G(x)$ | CRC remainder computation | Modulo-2 polynomial division |
| 18 | $L_{\text{burst}} \leq r$ | All burst errors up to length $r$ detected by CRC | $r$ degree of generator polynomial |
| 19 | $P_{\text{undetected}} \approx \dfrac{1}{2^r}$ | Approx. undetected-error probability for random CRC | $r$ CRC width |
| 20 | $d_{\min,\text{Hamming}} = 3$ | Hamming code minimum distance | Single-bit error correction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **CRC-32** (Ethernet, ZIP, PNG): generator polynomial $0x04C11DB7$.
> - **CRC-16-CCITT** (Bluetooth, HDLC): generator polynomial $0x1021$.
> - **Hamming(7,4)** (early memory ECC): encodes 4 data bits into a 7-bit codeword, correcting any single-bit error.
> - **Reed-Solomon** (QR codes, CDs, DVDs, DVB): powerful burst-error correction using Galois Field arithmetic.

### 2.5 CRC Error Detection Capabilities — What $G(x)$ Achieves

The choice of generator polynomial determines which error patterns are guaranteed to be detected. Standard CRC polynomials detect:
- **All single-bit errors** (if $G(x)$ has at least two non-zero terms).
- **All double-bit errors** (if $G(x)$ does not divide $x^k + 1$ for any $k \leq n$).
- **All odd-count errors** (if $G(x)$ contains the factor $(x + 1)$).
- **All burst errors of length $\leq r$**, where $r = \deg G(x)$.
- **Most burst errors of length $> r$** with probability $1 - 2^{-r}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hamming Code Check-Bit Count Requirement

We derive the fundamental relationship $2^r \geq m + r + 1$ for a Hamming code that corrects all single-bit errors.

**Step 1: Identify the requirements.**
A codeword of $n = m + r$ bits has $n$ possible single-bit error positions, plus one "no error" state. So the receiver must be able to distinguish among $n + 1$ distinct outcomes.

**Step 2: Count the information in the syndrome.**
The syndrome is the result of $r$ parity checks. Each parity check is binary, so the syndrome is an $r$-bit binary word with $2^r$ possible values.

**Step 3: Require a one-to-one mapping.**
To distinguish all $n + 1$ outcomes, the syndrome space must be at least as large as the outcome space:

$$ 2^r \geq n + 1 = (m + r) + 1 = m + r + 1 $$

**Step 4: Conclude.**
This is the Hamming inequality. The smallest $r$ satisfying this for a given $m$ is the **minimum number of check bits** needed for a perfect single-bit error-correcting code.

**Example:** For $m = 4$ data bits, try $r = 3$: $2^3 = 8 \geq 4 + 3 + 1 = 8$. ✓ This gives the famous **Hamming(7,4) code**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Stop-and-Wait ARQ Channel Utilization

We derive the channel efficiency (utilization) $\eta$ for Stop-and-Wait ARQ in an error-free channel.

**Step 1: Define the cycle time.**
In one complete cycle, the sender:
- Transmits one data frame: time $t_f$.
- Waits for the ACK to arrive: time $2 t_p$ (propagation to receiver + propagation of ACK back to sender).

Ignoring ACK transmission time for simplicity, the total cycle is:

$$ T_{\text{cycle}} = t_f + 2 t_p $$

**Step 2: Define the channel utilization.**
Channel utilization $\eta$ is the fraction of total cycle time spent transmitting useful data:

$$ \eta = \frac{\text{Data transmission time}}{\text{Total cycle time}} = \frac{t_f}{t_f + 2 t_p} $$

**Step 3: Introduce the parameter $a$.**
Define $a = t_p / t_f$ (the ratio of propagation to transmission time). Factor $t_f$ from the denominator:

$$ \eta = \frac{t_f}{t_f (1 + 2 t_p / t_f)} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} $$

**Step 4: Include frame error probability.**
If the frame error probability is $P$, the expected number of transmissions per successful frame is $1 / (1 - P)$. The efficiency becomes:

$$ \eta_{\text{err}} = \frac{1 - P}{1 + 2a} $$

**Example (KTU-style):** A channel has bandwidth 1 Mbps, one-way propagation delay 20 ms, and a frame size of 1000 bits. Compute $a$ and $\eta$ (error-free case).

- Transmission time: $t_f = 1000 \text{ bits} / 10^6 \text{ bps} = 1 \text{ ms}$.
- Propagation time: $t_p = 20 \text{ ms}$.
- $a = t_p / t_f = 20 / 1 = 20$.
- $\eta = 1 / (1 + 2 \times 20) = 1 / 41 \approx 0.0244$ or **2.44%**.

This very low utilization is precisely why Stop-and-Wait is inefficient on long-fat networks and why Sliding Window protocols are needed.

### 3.3 Derivation: CRC Polynomial Division (Worked Example)

**Problem:** Compute the CRC checksum for a dataword $M(x) = x^7 + x^5 + x^3 + x^2 + 1$ (binary: `10101101`) using generator polynomial $G(x) = x^4 + x^3 + 1$ (binary: `11001`).

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**
$M(x)$ has degree 7, so $m = 7$ in this single-dataword example. $G(x)$ has degree $r = 4$.

**Step 2: Append zeros.**
Append $r = 4$ zero bits to the dataword: $x^4 \cdot M(x)$ in binary is `101011010000`.

**Step 3: Perform modulo-2 division.**
Divide `101011010000` by `11001` using XOR subtraction:

```
              101011010000
            ⊕ 11001
            ─────────
              011001010000
              ⊕ 11001
              ────────
              000000010000
              ⊕ 11001
              ─────────
                       0101
```

The long division produces a remainder $R(x) = x^2 + 1$ (binary: `0101`), confirmed at the end of the division.

**Step 4: Form the transmitted codeword.**
Subtract (XOR) the remainder from the appended data:

$$ T(x) = x^4 M(x) \oplus R(x) = 101011010000 \oplus 000000000101 = 101011010101 $$

**Step 5: Verify at the receiver.**
The receiver divides `101011010101` by `11001`. A zero remainder confirms an error-free transmission.

### 3.4 Derivation: Hamming Code — Encoding and Decoding a Dataword

**Problem:** Encode the 4-bit dataword `1011` using a Hamming(7,4) code with even parity, and show error correction at the receiver.

**Step 1: Set up bit positions.**
Number the bit positions 1 through 7. Check bits go to positions that are powers of 2: positions **1, 2, 4**. Data bits fill the remaining positions: 3, 5, 6, 7.

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Type | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | $d_1$ | $c_3$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ | $d_4$ |
| Value | ? | ? | 1 | ? | 0 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 2: Compute check bits (even parity).**
- $c_1$ covers positions with bit 0 of position number set: 1, 3, 5, 7. Values: ?, 1, 0, 1. To make even: $? + 1 + 0 + 1 = \text{even} \Rightarrow ? = 0$. So $c_1 = 0$.
- $c_2$ covers positions with bit 1 set: 2, 3, 6, 7. Values: ?, 1, 1, 1. $? + 1 + 1 + 1 = \text{even} \Rightarrow ? = 1$. So $c_2 = 1$.
- $c_3$ covers positions with bit 2 set: 4, 5, 6, 7. Values: ?, 0, 1, 1. $? + 0 + 1 + 1 = \text{even} \Rightarrow ? = 0$. So $c_3 = 0$.

**Step 3: Form the codeword.**

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Value | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |

**Codeword:** `0110011`.

**Step 4: Introduce a single-bit error.**
Suppose the receiver obtains `0110111` (bit 6 flipped from 1 to 0).

**Step 5: Compute the syndrome at the receiver.**
- $s_1$ (parity over positions 1, 3, 5, 7): $0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 0$ (even, OK, $s_1 = 0$).
- $s_2$ (parity over positions 2, 3, 6, 7): $1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 1$ (odd, $s_2 = 1$).
- $s_3$ (parity over positions 4, 5, 6, 7): $0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 1$ (odd, $s_3 = 1$).

**Syndrome:** $s_3 s_2 s_1 = 110_2 = 6$.

**Step 6: Correct the error.**
The syndrome value 6 means the error is at bit position 6. Flip bit 6 back from 0 to 1, restoring the original codeword `0110011`. The dataword `1011` is recovered.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of ARQ Protocols

| Metric | Stop-and-Wait | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sequence number bits $k$ | 1 | $k$ (typically 3-8) | $k$ (typically 4-8) |
| Sender window $W_s$ | 1 | $\leq 2^k - 1$ | $\leq 2^{k-1}$ |
| Receiver window $W_r$ | 1 | 1 | $\leq 2^{k-1}$ |
| Out-of-order buffer | No | No | Yes |
| Retransmission on error | All un-ACKed | All from error onward | Only the erroneous |
| Bandwidth-delay product (BDP) usage | Poor (1 frame in flight) | Good (up to $W$ frames in flight) | Excellent |
| Receiver complexity | Lowest | Medium | Highest (needs buffer + reorder) |
| Best use case | Low-BDP, simple links | Moderate BDP | High-BDP, noisy links |
| Duplicate-ACK handling | N/A | May resend ahead of timer | Avoids unnecessary resends |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the asymmetric window limits?**
> Go-Back-N uses $W \leq 2^k - 1$ because the receiver discards out-of-order frames, so the sender and receiver must never confuse a new frame with a retransmission. Selective Repeat uses $W \leq 2^{k-1}$ to avoid the "window overlap ambiguity" that arises when ACKs are lost.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an Error-Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer Data] --> B[Data Link Layer - Sender]
    B --> C[Error Detection Encoder]
    C -->|Append CRC or Parity| D[Frame Buffer]
    D --> E[Physical Layer - Sender]
    E -->|Transmit over channel| F[Noisy Communication Channel]
    F --> G[Physical Layer - Receiver]
    G --> H[Error Detection Decoder]
    H -->|Check remainder| I{Error Detected?}
    I -->|No| J[Deliver Data to Upper Layer]
    I -->|Yes - ARQ| K[Send NACK or Timeout]
    K -->|Trigger Retransmission| B
    I -->|Yes - FEC| L[Forward Error Correction Decoder]
    L --> M[Reconstruct Original Data]
    M --> J
    J --> N[Application Layer - Receiver]
```

### 4.2 Stop-and-Wait ARQ — Sequence Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant C as Channel
    participant R as Receiver
    S->>C: Frame 0
    C->>R: Frame 0 (delivered)
    R->>C: ACK 0
    C->>S: ACK 0 (delivered)
    S->>C: Frame 1
    C->>R: Frame 1 (corrupted)
    Note over R: CRC fails
    R->>C: NACK 1
    C->>S: NACK 1 (delivered)
    S->>C: Frame 1 (retransmitted)
    C->>R: Frame 1 (delivered correctly)
    R->>C: ACK 1
    C->>S: ACK 1 (delivered)
```

### 4.3 Go-Back-N ARQ — Window and Retransmission Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Frame 0 - Sent ACK received] --> W2[Frame 1 - Sent ACK received]
    W2 --> W3[Frame 2 - Sent ERROR detected]
    W3 --> W4[Frame 3 - Sent]
    W4 --> W5[Frame 4 - Sent]
    W5 --> EN{Error in Frame 2?}
    EN -->|Yes NACK or timeout| RE[Retransmit Frame 2, 3, 4]
    RE --> R2[Resend 2]
    R2 --> R3[Resend 3]
    R3 --> R4[Resend 4]
    EN -->|No| NEXT[Proceed to next window]
```

### 4.4 Selective Repeat ARQ — Independent Retransmission

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sender transmits frames 0 to 4] --> B[Receiver accepts 0, 1, 3, 4 correctly]
    B --> C[Frame 2 corrupted - NACK 2 sent]
    C --> D[Frames 5, 6, 7 sent within window]
    D --> E[Receiver buffers 5, 6, 7 out of order]
    E --> F{Frame 2 NACK or timeout?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Sender retransmits ONLY Frame 2]
    G --> H[Receiver receives Frame 2]
    H --> I[All frames 0 to 7 delivered in order]
    F -->|No| J[Continue normal flow]
```

### 4.5 CRC Encoding-Decoding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sender
        M[Dataword M of m bits] --> Z[Append r zero bits]
        Z --> DIV[Modulo-2 division by G]
        DIV --> R[Remainder R of r bits]
        R --> TX[Transmit M concatenated with R]
    end
    TX -->|Over channel| RX
    subgraph Receiver
        RX[Received T of n bits] --> CDIV[Modulo-2 division by G]
        CDIV --> CK{Remainder is 0?}
        CK -->|Yes| OK[Accept frame - no error detected]
        CK -->|No| ER[Discard frame - request retransmission]
    end
```

### 4.6 Hamming Code — Bit Position and Parity Coverage Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Position 1 - Check bit c1]:::check
    P2[Position 2 - Check bit c2]:::check
    P3[Position 3 - Data bit d1]:::data
    P4[Position 4 - Check bit c3]:::check
    P5[Position 5 - Data bit d2]:::data
    P6[Position 6 - Data bit d3]:::data
    P7[Position 7 - Data bit d4]:::data
    P1 --- COV1[c1 covers 1, 3, 5, 7]
    P2 --- COV2[c2 covers 2, 3, 6, 7]
    P4 --- COV3[c3 covers 4, 5, 6, 7]
    classDef check fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

### 4.7 ARQ Protocol Comparison — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose Error Control Strategy] --> Q1{Channel has high latency or is unidirectional?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| FEC[Use FEC - Hamming, Reed-Solomon]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Real-time or non-real-time?}
    Q2 -->|Real-time, low error rate| FEC2[FEC preferred - VoIP, video]
    Q2 -->|Non-real-time, reliable delivery needed| ARQ[Use ARQ]
    ARQ --> Q3{Bandwidth-delay product?}
    Q3 -->|Small, simple link| SAW[Stop-and-Wait ARQ]
    Q3 -->|Moderate| GBN[Go-Back-N ARQ]
    Q3 -->|Large, noisy link| SR[Selective Repeat ARQ]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Distinguish between Single-Bit Error and Burst Error with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Single-Bit Error:** Exactly one bit in a transmitted frame is inverted (e.g., `00110` becomes `00100`). Most common in fiber-optic and high-quality wired channels where noise affects bits independently. *(1 mark)*
- **Burst Error:** Two or more consecutive bits in a frame are corrupted. The burst length is measured from the first to the last corrupted bit (e.g., `00110` becomes `11001` — a burst of length 3). Common in wireless channels affected by fading or impulse noise. *(1 mark)*
- **Example:** A lightning strike near a Wi-Fi antenna may corrupt 8 consecutive bits in a frame — a burst error. In contrast, a single cosmic-ray particle flip on a satellite link causes a single-bit error. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*Explain the role of Hamming Distance in error detection and correction. State the relationship between minimum Hamming distance and the number of errors detected/corrected.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role of Hamming Distance:** It quantifies the dissimilarity between two codewords as the number of bit positions in which they differ. It is the fundamental design parameter for any error-control code. *(1 mark)*
- **Detection rule:** A code with minimum distance $d_{\min}$ can detect up to $s$ errors where $d_{\min} \geq s + 1$. *(1 mark)*
- **Correction rule:** The same code can correct up to $t$ errors where $d_{\min} \geq 2t + 1$. For example, a code with $d_{\min} = 3$ can correct any single-bit error ($t = 1$) and detect up to 2-bit errors ($s = 2$). *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / 2023 Pattern]**
*For a data communication system, a 4-bit dataword is to be transmitted using a Hamming code. Answer the following:*

**(a)** Determine the number of check bits required and draw the structure of the Hamming(7,4) code, clearly indicating which positions hold check bits and which hold data bits. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Encode the dataword `1100` using even-parity Hamming code. Then, simulate a single-bit error at position 5 and show, with full computation, how the receiver detects and corrects it. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The number of check bits $r$ must satisfy $2^r \geq m + r + 1$ where $m = 4$.

- Try $r = 2$: $2^2 = 4 < 4 + 2 + 1 = 7$. Not sufficient.
- Try $r = 3$: $2^3 = 8 \geq 4 + 3 + 1 = 8$. Sufficient. ✓

So $r = 3$ check bits are required, giving $n = m + r = 7$ total bits. *(1 mark)*

Structure: Check bits occupy positions $2^0, 2^1, 2^2 = 1, 2, 4$. Data bits occupy the remaining positions $3, 5, 6, 7$. *(1 mark)*

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Type | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | $d_1$ | $c_3$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ | $d_4$ |
| Role | Check (parity 0,1,3,5,7) | Check (parity 1,2,3,6,7) | Data | Check (parity 2,4,5,6,7) | Data | Data | Data |

The diagram below shows the coverage:

- $c_1$ (position 1) covers positions $\{1, 3, 5, 7\}$
- $c_2$ (position 2) covers positions $\{2, 3, 6, 7\}$
- $c_3$ (position 4) covers positions $\{4, 5, 6, 7\}$ *(2 marks)*

**Justification of coverage:**
- $c_1$ checks all positions whose 3-bit binary representation has the LSB (bit 0) set to 1.
- $c_2$ checks all positions whose middle bit (bit 1) is set to 1.
- $c_3$ checks all positions whose MSB (bit 2) is set to 1. *(3 marks)*

**(b)** **Encoding dataword `1100`:**

Place data bits $d_1 d_2 d_3 d_4 = 1100$ at positions 3, 5, 6, 7:

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Value | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | 1 | $c_3$ | 1 | 0 | 0 |

Compute check bits (even parity):
- $c_1$: parity over $\{1, 3, 5, 7\}$: $c_1 + 1 + 1 + 0 = \text{even} \Rightarrow c_1 = 0$.
- $c_2$: parity over $\{2, 3, 6, 7\}$: $c_2 + 1 + 0 + 0 = \text{even} \Rightarrow c_2 = 1$.
- $c_3$: parity over $\{4, 5, 6, 7\}$: $c_3 + 1 + 0 + 0 = \text{even} \Rightarrow c_3 = 1$.

**Transmitted codeword:** `0 1 1 1 1 0 0` = `0111100`. *(3 marks)*

**Simulate error at position 5:**
Bit 5 flips from 1 to 0. Received codeword: `0111000`. *(1 mark)*

**Receiver syndrome calculation:**
- $s_1$ over $\{1, 3, 5, 7\}$: $0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 1$ (odd → $s_1 = 1$).
- $s_2$ over $\{2, 3, 6, 7\}$: $1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 0$ (even → $s_2 = 0$).
- $s_3$ over $\{4, 5, 6, 7\}$: $1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 1$ (odd → $s_3 = 1$).

Syndrome = $s_3 s_2 s_1 = 101_2 = 5$. *(2 marks)*

**Correction:** Syndrome 5 indicates error at position 5. Flip bit 5 back from 0 to 1, restoring the original codeword `0111100`. Dataword `1100` is recovered. *(1 mark)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*In a Stop-and-Wait ARQ system, the channel has the following parameters: bandwidth = 4 kbps, one-way propagation delay = 20 ms, frame size = 800 bits, ACK transmission time is negligible, and the probability of a single frame being in error is $P = 0.1$.*

**(a)** Compute the channel utilization (efficiency) of Stop-and-Wait ARQ. State whether the protocol is efficient on this link and justify. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain Go-Back-N ARQ and Selective Repeat ARQ with sender/receiver window diagrams. For $k = 3$ sequence number bits, compute the maximum sender window sizes for both protocols. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Compute $a$ (normalized propagation delay):**

$$ a = \frac{t_p}{t_f} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{800 \text{ bits} / 4000 \text{ bps}} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{200 \text{ ms}} = 0.1 $$

*(2 marks)*

**Channel utilization (error-free):**

$$ \eta_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2 \times 0.1} = \frac{1}{1.2} \approx 0.8333 $$

So ideal efficiency is **83.33%**. *(2 marks)*

**Channel utilization with errors:**

$$ \eta_{\text{err}} = \frac{1 - P}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1 - 0.1}{1.2} = \frac{0.9}{1.2} = 0.75 $$

So actual efficiency is **75%**. *(2 marks)*

**Justification:** The efficiency is reasonably good (75%–83%) because the propagation delay is much smaller than the frame transmission time ($a = 0.1 < 1$). For this link, Stop-and-Wait is acceptable. If the propagation delay were larger (e.g., satellite link with $a \gg 1$), Stop-and-Wait would be highly inefficient and a sliding window protocol would be preferred. *(1 mark)*

**(b)** **Go-Back-N ARQ:**
- The sender maintains a window of $W$ outstanding (unacknowledged) frames. The receiver accepts frames only in order; out-of-order frames are discarded.
- On detecting an error (or timeout), the sender retransmits the erroneous frame **and all subsequent** frames in the window.
- Receiver window size = 1.
- Maximum sender window: $W_{\text{GBN}} \leq 2^k - 1$. For $k = 3$: $W_{\text{GBN}} \leq 2^3 - 1 = 7$. *(3 marks)*

**Selective Repeat ARQ:**
- Both sender and receiver maintain windows of size $W$. The receiver accepts out-of-order frames and buffers them.
- On detecting an error, the sender retransmits **only the specific erroneous** frame(s).
- Maximum sender window: $W_{\text{SR}} \leq 2^{k-1}$. For $k = 3$: $W_{\text{SR}} \leq 2^{3-1} = 4$. *(3 marks)*

**Summary table:**

| Protocol | Max Window ($k = 3$) | Retransmission Scope |
|---|---|---|
| Go-Back-N | 7 | All frames from error onward |
| Selective Repeat | 4 | Only the erroneous frame(s) |

*(1 mark)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Hamming code parity coverage:** Forgetting that each check bit covers positions based on the binary representation of the position number (bit 0, bit 1, bit 2) — not based on consecutive positions.
> 2. **Stop-and-Wait efficiency:** Confusing $t_f$ (frame transmission time) with $t_p$ (propagation time). Always use $a = t_p / t_f$. Mixing units (e.g., $t_f$ in ms and bandwidth in kbps without conversion) is a frequent error.
> 3. **Window size limits:** Writing $W \leq 2^k$ for Go-Back-N or Selective Repeat. The correct limits are $2^k - 1$ and $2^{k-1}$ respectively. The "off-by-one" error here is heavily penalized.
> 4. **CRC polynomial division:** Performing ordinary arithmetic division instead of **modulo-2 (XOR)** division. CRC uses XOR subtraction, not carry propagation.
> 5. **Syndrome interpretation:** Computing the syndrome bits correctly but interpreting them in the wrong order (LSB first vs. MSB first). Convention: syndrome = $s_r s_{r-1} \ldots s_1$ read as a binary number, with $s_1$ as LSB.
> 6. **Confusing code rate with channel utilization:** Code rate $R = m/n$ measures redundancy in the code. Channel utilization $\eta$ measures time spent transmitting useful data. They are different quantities.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Error Control** has two pillars: **Error Detection** (cheaper, requires retransmission) and **Error Correction** (expensive, no retransmission needed).
- **Hamming Distance** $d(x, y)$ is the foundational metric: $d_{\min} \geq s+1$ for detection of $s$ errors, $d_{\min} \geq 2t+1$ for correction of $t$ errors.
- **Parity Check** is the simplest detector but misses even-numbered errors. **2D Parity** extends it to single-bit correction.
- **Checksum** uses one's complement addition; widely used in TCP/UDP/IP. Cheap but weaker than CRC.
- **CRC** is the workhorse of modern networks (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, storage). Polynomial selection determines detection capability. All single-bit, double-bit, odd-count, and short bursts ($\leq r$) are detected.
- **Hamming Code** is a perfect single-bit error-correcting code. Use $2^r \geq m + r + 1$ to find $r$. The syndrome is the bit position of the error.
- **FEC** (Hamming, Reed-Solomon) is preferred for high-latency or one-way channels; **ARQ** is preferred for reliable two-way channels.
- **ARQ Variants:**
  - **Stop-and-Wait:** Window = 1, simple, inefficient for $a \gg 1$.
  - **Go-Back-N:** Window $\leq 2^k - 1$, no receiver buffer, simple retransmission.
  - **Selective Repeat:** Window $\leq 2^{k-1}$, receiver buffer required, most efficient.
- **Channel utilization key formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a}$ (capped at 1) for sliding-window ARQ.
- **Normalize carefully:** $a = t_p / t_f$ — a dimensionless ratio that decides whether Stop-and-Wait is viable.
- **Code rate** $R = m / n$ tells you the fraction of useful bits in the codeword; lower $R$ means more redundancy (and stronger protection).
- **Real-world CRC polynomials** are standardized: CRC-32 (Ethernet), CRC-16-CCITT (HDLC, Bluetooth), CRC-8 (embedded systems).
- **KTU favorite questions** typically test: (1) Hamming code encoding/decoding with syndrome calculation, (2) ARQ efficiency computation given $a$ and $P$, (3) CRC polynomial division with a worked example, and (4) conceptual comparison of error detection and correction techniques.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:**
> Memorize the three window-size limits ($1$ for S&W, $2^k - 1$ for GBN, $2^{k-1}$ for SR), the Hamming code relationship $2^r \geq m + r + 1$, and the efficiency formula $\eta = 1 / (1 + 2a)$. These three facts alone will let you solve 80% of the Part B questions on Error Control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplexing: Frequency-Division Multiplexing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Multiplexing: Frequency-Division Multiplexing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Multiplexing? **Multiplexing** is the process of combining multiple analog or digital signals into one signal over a shared transmission medium, with the goal of maximizing the utilization of expensive bandwidth resources. The inverse process, performed at the receiving end, is called **demultiplexing**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Multiplexing is a foundational concept that enables cost-effective communication. It is the *primary reason* a single physical cable can carry hundreds of phone calls or TV channels simultaneously. There are three principal multiplexing families: 1. **Frequency-Division Multiplexing (FDM)** — separates signals by **frequency**. 2. **Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM)** — separates signals by **time slot**. 3. **Wavelength-Division Multiplexing (WDM)** — separates optical signals by **wavelength** (light frequency). ## 1.2 Frequency-Division Multiplexing (FDM) — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Board Standard):** > *Frequency-Division Multiplexing (FDM) is an analog multiplexing technique in which the available bandwidth of a transmission channel is divided into several non-overlapping frequency sub-bands, each carrying a separate information signal simultaneously, by translating each signal to a distinct carrier frequency.* In simpler terms, FDM puts many separate "radio stations" on a single wire or wireless channel by giving each one its own private slice of the frequency spectrum. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway of Sound Imagine a 6-lane highway where each lane is reserved for a specific type of vehicle — buses in lane 1, cars in lane 2, trucks in lane 3. **FDM does the same thing with frequencies**: - The **highway** = the transmission medium (copper wire, optical fiber, air). - The **lanes** = distinct frequency bands. - The **vehicles** = information signals (voice, video, data). - **No lane is shared**, so signals never collide, much like radio stations broadcasting on 90.1 MHz vs 95.5 MHz do not interfere with each other. > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Shortcut:** If TDM is *taking turns speaking in a meeting*, then FDM is *everyone speaking at the same time but in different languages that don't overlap*. The receiver (ear) tunes in to only one language (frequency) at a time. ## 1.4 Where is FDM Used? (KTU 2024 Real-World Context) - **FM/AM Radio Broadcasting** — Each station is assigned a fixed carrier frequency. - **Analog Television** — Video, audio, and color subcarriers occupy different frequency slots. - **First-Generation Cellular Telephony (1G)** — Used FDMA (Frequency-Division Multiple Access). - **Telephone Trunk Lines (E1/T1 Hierarchies)** — The North American FDM hierarchy stacked 12 voice channels (4 kHz each) into a 48 kHz group, 5 groups into a 240 kHz supergroup, and 10 supergroups into a mastergroup. - **Cable TV (CATV)** — Hundreds of channels modulated onto a single coaxial cable. ## 1.5 Core Terminology | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Baseband Signal** | The original information signal (e.g., voice: 0–4 kHz) before modulation. | | **Carrier Signal** | A high-frequency sinusoid that "carries" the baseband signal. | | **Modulation** | Translating the baseband spectrum to the carrier frequency band. | | **Guard Band** | A small unused frequency band placed between adjacent channels to prevent interference. | | **Sub-channel** | One frequency band dedicated to one input signal inside the composite. | | **Multiplexer (MUX)** | Hardware device that combines the modulated sub-channels. | | **Demultiplexer (DEMUX)** | Hardware device that extracts and demodulates individual sub-channels. | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of FDM The complete FDM process is best understood as a five-stage pipeline: ### Stage 1 — Signal Conditioning & Limiting Each input signal is first passed through a **low-pass filter (LPF)** to limit its bandwidth to a known maximum (e.g., voice limited to **0–4 kHz**). This prevents spectral leakage into adjacent channels. ### Stage 2 — Modulation (Frequency Translation) Each filtered baseband signal modulates its own dedicated **carrier frequency** ($f_{c_i}$). The most common modulation schemes used in FDM are: - **AM (Amplitude Modulation)** — used in classic telephone FDM hierarchies. - **FM (Frequency Modulation)** — used in radio broadcasting and TV audio. - **SSB (Single-Sideband Modulation)** — preferred in long-distance telephony because it conserves bandwidth. After modulation, the original baseband spectrum (centered at 0 Hz) is shifted so it sits centered at $f_{c_i}$. ### Stage 3 — Bandpass Filtering A **bandpass filter (BPF)** removes unwanted sidebands and harmonics generated during modulation. For example, in SSB-USB (Upper Sideband), the carrier and lower sideband are filtered out, leaving only the upper sideband. ### Stage 4 — Summation (Multiplexing) All filtered, modulated signals are fed into a **linear summing amplifier (adder)**. The output is a composite signal whose spectrum contains all $n$ sub-channels placed side-by-side, separated by guard bands. ### Stage 5 — Demultiplexing (Receiver Side) At the receiver, the inverse operations occur: 1. **Bandpass filters** isolate each sub-channel from the composite. 2. **Coherent demodulators** translate the spectrum back to baseband. 3. **Low-pass filters** recover the original information. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Why" Matters in KTU Answers:** Examiners at KTU consistently reward answers that explain the *purpose* of each block, not just name them. Always state *why* a guard band is needed (to prevent inter-channel crosstalk) and *why* SSB is preferred (to halve the required bandwidth per channel). ## 2.2 The "How" — Mathematical Foundation Let each input signal have a baseband bandwidth $B_s$ (Hz). For each of the $n$ channels, the modulation process creates a passband of width $2 \cdot B_s$ (for DSB) or $B_s$ (for SSB). Let $G$ (Hz) be the width of each guard band. The **total composite bandwidth** $B_{total}$ required by the FDM system is: $$B_{total} \;=\; n \cdot B_{channel} \;+\; (n - 1) \cdot G$$ where $B_{channel}$ is the bandwidth occupied by *one* modulated sub-channel (which equals $B_s$ for SSB or $2B_s$ for DSB). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas — they are the highest-frequency formulas tested in KTU 2024 ESE for FDM.** | # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $B_{total} = n \cdot B_{channel} + (n-1) \cdot G$ | Total composite bandwidth | Hz | Capacity planning of FDM link | | 2 | $f_{i,upper} = f_{c_i} + B_s$ | Upper edge of channel $i$ | Hz | Spectrum boundary calculation | | 3 | $f_{i,lower} = f_{c_i} - B_s$ | Lower edge of channel $i$ | Hz | Spectrum boundary calculation | | 4 | $f_{c_{i+1}} = f_{c_i} + B_{channel} + G$ | Adjacent carrier spacing | Hz | Carrier frequency assignment | | 5 | $B_{channel} = 2 B_s$ (DSB) | DSB channel width | Hz | AM-FDM bandwidth | | 6 | $B_{channel} = B_s$ (SSB) | SSB channel width | Hz | Telephony-FDM bandwidth | | 7 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon capacity of *one* sub-channel | bps | Theoretical upper limit | | 8 | $N = \lfloor B_{total} \div (B_{channel} + G) \rfloor$ | Maximum number of channels | — | Quick capacity check | | 9 | $\text{Spectral Efficiency} = N \div B_{total}$ | Channels per Hz | channels/Hz | Comparison of FDM schemes | | 10 | $G \geq \Delta f_{drift} + 2 B_{filter}$ | Minimum guard band | Hz | Filter design rule of thumb | > **Convention used in table:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ is written as $\vert$ to avoid breaking markdown rendering. Use $\mid$ when typographic clarity is required. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility FDM is the *backbone* of the global **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)** and analog cable TV. In the **North American FDM Hierarchy (AT&T / Bell System)**: - **Group** = 12 voice channels, occupies 60–108 kHz (bandwidth = **48 kHz**). - **Supergroup** = 5 groups = 60 voice channels, occupies 312–552 kHz. - **Mastergroup** = 10 supergroups = 600 voice channels, occupies 564–3084 kHz. - **Jumbogroup** = 6 mastergroups = 3,600 voice channels. This hierarchical stacking allows the same physical infrastructure to scale seamlessly from a small town PBX to transcontinental trunks. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick for KTU:** *"Group **G**rows **G**radually: G → SG → MG → JG"*. A Group is 12 channels, and every higher level multiplies by 5, then 10, then 6. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Composite Bandwidth of an FDM System **Problem Statement (Standard KTU 3-Mark Pattern):** An FDM system multiplexes $n$ voice signals. Each voice signal has a baseband bandwidth of $B_s = 4$ kHz. The system uses **SSB-SC** modulation, and a guard band of $G = 0.5$ kHz is inserted between adjacent channels. Derive the total composite bandwidth $B_{total}$ occupied by the FDM link. ### Step 1 — Determine the bandwidth of a single modulated channel For SSB (Single-Sideband) modulation, only one sideband is transmitted, so the channel width equals the baseband width: $$B_{channel} = B_s = 4 \text{ kHz}$$ ### Step 2 — Account for guard bands between channels With $n$ channels placed side-by-side, there are exactly $(n-1)$ gaps between them: $$\text{Guard bandwidth total} = (n-1) \cdot G = (n-1) \cdot 0.5 \text{ kHz}$$ ### Step 3 — Sum the channel and guard bandwidths $$B_{total} = n \cdot B_{channel} + (n-1) \cdot G$$ $$B_{total} = n \cdot 4 + (n-1) \cdot 0.5 \;\; \text{kHz}$$ $$B_{total} = 4n + 0.5n - 0.5$$ $$\boxed{B_{total} = 4.5n - 0.5 \;\; \text{kHz}}$$ ### Step 4 — Numerical Example If $n = 12$ (a standard telephony group): $$B_{total} = 4.5(12) - 0.5 = 54 - 0.5 = 53.5 \text{ kHz}$$ > **Engineering Note:** The classical Bell System "Group" actually occupies 48 kHz (60–108 kHz), which corresponds to a slightly different carrier-spacing convention; the formula above is the *general* closed-form derivation. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Carrier Frequency Assignment **Problem Statement:** Given $n = 4$ voice channels, each of bandwidth $B_s = 4$ kHz, modulated as DSB-SC, with guard band $G = 1$ kHz. The first carrier is $f_{c_1} = 20$ kHz. Determine the carrier frequencies $f_{c_2}, f_{c_3}, f_{c_4}$. ### Step 1 — Compute channel bandwidth For DSB-SC, both sidebands are retained: $$B_{channel} = 2 B_s = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ kHz}$$ ### Step 2 — Derive the carrier-spacing rule The next carrier must start after the previous channel **plus** one guard band: $$f_{c_{i+1}} = f_{c_i} + B_{channel} + G = f_{c_i} + 8 + 1 = f_{c_i} + 9 \text{ kHz}$$ ### Step 3 — Recursive computation $$f_{c_1} = 20 \text{ kHz}$$ $$f_{c_2} = 20 + 9 = 29 \text{ kHz}$$ $$f_{c_3} = 29 + 9 = 38 \text{ kHz}$$ $$f_{c_4} = 38 + 9 = 47 \text{ kHz}$$ ### Step 4 — Verify total bandwidth $$B_{total} = f_{c_4} + B_{channel}/2 - (f_{c_1} - B_{channel}/2) = 47 + 4 - (20 - 4) = 51 - 16 = 35 \text{ kHz}$$ Cross-check with the master formula: $B_{total} = n B_{channel} + (n-1)G = 4(8) + 3(1) = 35$ kHz ✓ ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (FDM vs TDM) | Parameter | FDM | TDM | |---|---|---| | **Signal Type** | Primarily analog | Primarily digital | | **Domain** | Frequency | Time | | **Channel Separation** | Guard band | Guard time / synchronization | | **Bandwidth Use** | Continuous spectrum | Discretized time slots | | **Synchronization** | Not strictly required | Strictly required (frame sync) | | **Crosstalk Susceptibility** | High (needs sharp filters) | Low | | **Hardware Cost** | Filters + modulators | Logic gates + clock recovery | | **Scalability** | Limited by spectrum | Limited by clock speed | | **Power Efficiency** | Lower (carriers consume power) | Higher | | **Typical Use** | Radio, TV, analog trunk | Digital telephony, PCM | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Part) **Problem:** A 60-channel FDM system uses DSB-SC modulation. Each voice channel occupies 0–4 kHz. A guard band of 500 Hz is placed between channels. The first carrier is at 100 kHz. **(a)** Calculate the highest carrier frequency used. **(b)** Calculate the total composite bandwidth. ### Solution (a) Step 1 — DSB-SC channel width: $B_{channel} = 2 \times 4 \text{ kHz} = 8 \text{ kHz}$. Step 2 — Carrier spacing rule: $\Delta f_c = B_{channel} + G = 8 + 0.5 = 8.5$ kHz. Step 3 — Highest carrier (the 60th channel): $f_{c_{60}} = f_{c_1} + (60 - 1) \cdot \Delta f_c = 100 + 59 \times 8.5$. $$ f_{c_{60}} = 100 + 501.5 = 601.5 \text{ kHz} $$ ### Solution (b) Step 1 — Apply total bandwidth formula: $$ B_{total} = n \cdot B_{channel} + (n-1) \cdot G = 60 \times 8 + 59 \times 0.5 $$ $$ B_{total} = 480 + 29.5 = 509.5 \text{ kHz} $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 FDM Transmitter — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of an $n$-channel FDM multiplexer (transmitter side). It avoids physically impossible drawings and instead focuses on the **logical signal-flow architecture** as required by KTU answer scripts. ```mermaid graph TD src1[Input Signal S1
0 to 4 kHz Voice] --> lpf1[LPF 1
Band Limit] src2[Input Signal S2
0 to 4 kHz Voice] --> lpf2[LPF 2
Band Limit] src3[Input Signal Sn
0 to 4 kHz Data] --> lpfN[LPF N
Band Limit] lpf1 --> mod1[Modulator 1
Carrier fc1] lpf2 --> mod2[Modulator 2
Carrier fc2] lpfN --> modN[Modulator N
Carrier fcN] mod1 --> bpf1[BPF 1
Sideband Select] mod2 --> bpf2[BPF 2
Sideband Select] modN --> bpfN[BPF N
Sideband Select] bpf1 --> adder[Linear Summing
Amplifier] bpf2 --> adder bpfN --> adder adder --> tx[Composite Output
FDM Signal] tx --> chan[Transmission
Medium] ``` ## 4.2 FDM Receiver — Block-Level Demultiplexer Architecture ```mermaid graph TD rx[Composite FDM
Signal Input] --> bpfa[BPF A
fc1 sub channel] rx --> bpfb[BPF B
fc2 sub channel] rx --> bpfn[BPF N
fcN sub channel] bpfa --> dema[Coherent
Demodulator A] bpfb --> demb[Coherent
Demodulator B] bpfn --> demn[Coherent
Demodulator N] dema --> lpfa[LPF A
Recover Baseband] demb --> lpfb[LPF B
Recover Baseband] demn --> lpfn[LPF N
Recover Baseband] lpfa --> outa[Output Signal S1
0 to 4 kHz] lpfb --> outb[Output Signal S2
0 to 4 kHz] lpfn --> outn[Output Signal Sn
0 to 4 kHz] ``` ## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Spectrum Allocation The following diagram shows how three baseband signals ($S_1$, $S_2$, $S_3$) of 4 kHz each are translated by SSB modulation and stacked in the frequency domain, with **G** denoting guard bands. ```mermaid graph LR axis[Spectrum Axis: f in kHz] --> ch1[Channel 1
fc1 - 4 to fc1 kHz] ch1 --> g1[Guard Band G
fc1 + 1 to fc1 + 1 kHz] g1 --> ch2[Channel 2
fc2 - 4 to fc2 kHz] ch2 --> g2[Guard Band G
1 kHz wide] g2 --> ch3[Channel 3
fc3 - 4 to fc3 kHz] style ch1 fill:#cce5ff style ch2 fill:#cce5ff style ch3 fill:#cce5ff style g1 fill:#ffe0b3 style g2 fill:#ffe0b3 ``` ## 4.4 Hierarchy Diagram — Bell System FDM Stacking ```mermaid graph TD v12[12 Voice Channels
60 to 108 kHz] --> grp[Group
48 kHz bandwidth] grp --> sg[Supergroup
5 Groups equals 60 Channels
312 to 552 kHz] sg --> mg[Mastergroup
10 Supergroups equals 600 Channels
564 to 3084 kHz] mg --> jg[Jumbogroup
6 Mastergroups equals 3600 Channels] jg --> coax[Transmitted on Coaxial
or Microwave Link] style v12 fill:#d4edda style grp fill:#cce5ff style sg fill:#fff3cd style mg fill:#f8d7da style jg fill:#e2d4f0 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark Each, 3-Mark with Explanation) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students write "FDM is a digital technique" — this is **wrong** for the classical FDM. Classical FDM is **analog**; only when you use OFDM does it become digital. Read the question carefully! ### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Q: Define Frequency Division Multiplexing. List any two of its applications.** **Model Answer:** Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) is a multiplexing technique in which multiple signals are transmitted simultaneously over a single communication channel by allocating a distinct non-overlapping frequency band to each signal, with non-utilized **guard bands** inserted between adjacent bands to minimize inter-channel interference. **Applications (any two):** 1. **Radio and TV broadcasting** — each station operates on a different carrier frequency. 2. **Analog telephone trunk lines** — multiple voice calls stacked in frequency (e.g., Bell System group/supergroup hierarchy). 3. **First-generation (1G) cellular networks** — using FDMA. 4. **Cable TV distribution systems (CATV)**. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Q: What is a guard band? Why is it necessary in FDM?** **Model Answer:** A **guard band** is a small, unused portion of the frequency spectrum placed between two adjacent sub-channels in an FDM system. **Necessity:** - Filters are non-ideal and cannot produce infinitely sharp cutoff edges. - Carrier frequency drift and modulation imperfections cause spectral spreading beyond the ideal channel width. - The guard band acts as a **safety margin** to prevent adjacent-channel **crosstalk** (interference). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Marking Insight:** A 3-mark answer that *only* states the definition gets 1 mark. The "why" explanation is worth 2 marks. Always include the *reason*. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (14-Mark Questions):** > - Do **not** skip the derivation steps. Partial marks are awarded per logical step, not per final answer. > - Always **state the formula first**, then substitute values, then compute. > - **Unit conversion** mistakes (Hz vs kHz vs MHz) are the most common cause of full-mark deductions. Show unit cancellation explicitly. > - A common trap: students write $B_{total} = nB + (n-1)G$ but then plug $n$ instead of $n-1$ for guard bands — re-verify the count! ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an FDM system. List its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)** **Q: (b)** An FDM system multiplexes 12 voice channels using SSB-SC modulation. Each voice channel has a baseband bandwidth of 4 kHz. A guard band of 500 Hz is provided between adjacent channels. If the first carrier frequency is 60 kHz, calculate: (i) the total composite bandwidth, (ii) the highest carrier frequency. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution — A(a) **Block Diagram:** Refer to the transmitter block diagram in Section 4.1. **Working Explanation (Step-by-Step, 4 Marks):** 1. **Input signals** ($S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n$) are first passed through **low-pass filters** to limit each one to a known maximum baseband bandwidth (4 kHz for voice). 2. Each filtered signal modulates a **separate carrier frequency** ($f_{c_1}, f_{c_2}, \dots, f_{c_n}$) using SSB-SC modulation. This **shifts** the signal spectrum to the carrier band. 3. **Bandpass filters** select only the desired sideband (e.g., USB) and reject the carrier and opposite sideband. 4. The modulated sub-channels are **summed linearly** to form the composite FDM signal, which is then transmitted. 5. At the **receiver**, bandpass filters separate each sub-channel, coherent demodulators shift them back to baseband, and low-pass filters recover the original signals. **Advantages (1½ Marks):** - Allows simultaneous analog signal transmission. - No synchronization required between channels. - Conceptually simple and well-suited for radio broadcasting. - Existing analog infrastructure (telephone hierarchy) leverages it. **Disadvantages (1½ Marks):** - Guard bands **waste spectrum**. - Susceptible to **intermodulation distortion** and **crosstalk**. - Requires **precise filters and stable carrier oscillators**. - Inefficient power usage (carriers carry no information). - Not easily scalable to very large channel counts. --- ### Model Solution — A(b) **Given:** $n = 12$, $B_s = 4$ kHz, $G = 0.5$ kHz, $f_{c_1} = 60$ kHz, SSB-SC modulation. **Step 1 — Channel bandwidth (SSB):** $$B_{channel} = B_s = 4 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 2 — Carrier spacing rule:** $$\Delta f_c = B_{channel} + G = 4 + 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ kHz}$$ **(i) Total composite bandwidth (4 Marks):** $$B_{total} = n \cdot B_{channel} + (n-1) \cdot G = 12(4) + 11(0.5)$$ $$ B_{total} = 48 + 5.5 = 53.5 \text{ kHz} $$ **(ii) Highest carrier frequency (3 Marks):** The highest carrier is for the 12th channel: $$f_{c_{12}} = f_{c_1} + (12 - 1) \cdot \Delta f_c = 60 + 11 \times 4.5$$ $$ f_{c_{12}} = 60 + 49.5 = 109.5 \text{ kHz} $$ **Final Answer:** $B_{total} = 53.5$ kHz and $f_{c_{12}} = 109.5$ kHz. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]` **Q: (a)** Compare FDM and TDM. Explain the concept of a "group" in the Bell System FDM hierarchy. **(7 Marks)** **Q: (b)** A 24-channel FDM system uses DSB-SC modulation. Each channel has a baseband bandwidth of 3.5 kHz. A guard band of 1 kHz is placed between channels. The first carrier is at 40 kHz. Determine: (i) the bandwidth of each modulated channel, (ii) the total composite bandwidth, (iii) the highest carrier frequency. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution — B(a) **Comparison FDM vs TDM (4 Marks):** Refer to the analytical comparison matrix in Section 3.3. Key points to include: - FDM = frequency domain, TDM = time domain. - FDM uses guard bands; TDM uses guard times. - FDM is for analog; TDM is for digital. - FDM needs filters; TDM needs clocks. - FDM is continuous; TDM is sampled. **Bell System "Group" Concept (3 Marks):** In the Bell System FDM hierarchy, a **Group** is the fundamental building block formed by multiplexing **12 voice channels** (each 4 kHz wide) using SSB modulation. The group occupies the frequency range **60 kHz to 108 kHz** (total bandwidth **48 kHz**). Five such groups are stacked to form a **Supergroup** (60 voice channels, 240 kHz wide). This hierarchical stacking is what enabled the analog PSTN to carry thousands of calls over coaxial and microwave links. --- ### Model Solution — B(b) **Given:** $n = 24$, $B_s = 3.5$ kHz, $G = 1$ kHz, $f_{c_1} = 40$ kHz, DSB-SC. **(i) Bandwidth of each modulated channel (2 Marks):** For DSB-SC, both sidebands are transmitted: $$B_{channel} = 2 B_s = 2 \times 3.5 = 7 \text{ kHz}$$ **(ii) Total composite bandwidth (3 Marks):** $$B_{total} = n \cdot B_{channel} + (n-1) \cdot G = 24(7) + 23(1)$$ $$ B_{total} = 168 + 23 = 191 \text{ kHz} $$ **(iii) Highest carrier frequency (2 Marks):** Carrier spacing: $$\Delta f_c = B_{channel} + G = 7 + 1 = 8 \text{ kHz}$$ $$f_{c_{24}} = 40 + (24-1)(8) = 40 + 184 = 224 \text{ kHz}$$ **Final Answer:** $B_{channel} = 7$ kHz, $B_{total} = 191$ kHz, $f_{c_{24}} = 224$ kHz. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Read twice before the exam.)** - ☐ FDM = **analog** multiplexing in the **frequency domain**. - ☐ Each signal gets a **private frequency band**; **guard bands** prevent crosstalk. - ☐ Master formula: $B_{total} = n B_{channel} + (n-1) G$. - ☐ SSB channel width = $B_s$; DSB channel width = $2 B_s$. - ☐ Carrier spacing rule: $\Delta f_c = B_{channel} + G$. - ☐ Highest carrier: $f_{c_n} = f_{c_1} + (n-1)\Delta f_c$. - ☐ Guard band is *not* allocated before the first channel or after the last — only **between** channels. - ☐ FDM is used in: **radio, TV, 1G cellular, cable TV, analog PSTN trunk lines**. - ☐ Bell System hierarchy: **12 = Group, 60 = Supergroup, 600 = Mastergroup, 3600 = Jumbogroup**. - ☐ FDM is **continuous in time** but **discrete in frequency**; TDM is the exact opposite. - ☐ Key drawback: **spectrum inefficiency** due to guard bands and carrier power. - ☐ Always **state units** (Hz / kHz / MHz) in every numerical answer. - ☐ Always show **substitution step-by-step** in derivations — KTU awards partial marks per step.

topics.title: Synchronous Time-Division Multiplexing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synchronous Time-Division Multiplexing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Synchronous Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **Synchronous Time-Division Multiplexing (Synchronous TDM)** is a digital multiplexing technique in which multiple low-speed digital data streams (from independent sources) are combined into a single high-speed digital transmission channel by assigning each source a **fixed, pre-allocated, recurring time slot** within every transmission frame, regardless of whether the source has data to transmit at that instant. The multiplexer and demultiplexer are kept in precise bit-level (or byte-level) synchronization, hence the term *synchronous*.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Synchronous TDM is classified under **Digital Multiplexing / Channelization** techniques, alongside **Statistical TDM (STDM)**, **Frequency-Division Multiplexing (FDM)**, and **Wavelength-Division Multiplexing (WDM)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Toll-Booth Conveyor Belt"

Imagine a **conveyor belt** carrying parcels from 4 different warehouses. The belt completes one full revolution every minute. The system **reserves exactly 15 seconds** in that minute for Warehouse A, 15 seconds for Warehouse B, 15 seconds for Warehouse C, and 15 seconds for Warehouse D — in that exact order.

- Even if Warehouse B is closed today, **its 15-second slot is still kept empty** and the belt keeps moving at the same speed.
- The receiving end knows that the first 15 seconds always belong to A, the next to B, and so on — so it can sort the parcels automatically.

**The belt = the high-speed multiplexed link.**
**The 15-second slices = fixed time slots.**
**Empty slots for silent sources = the main inefficiency of Synchronous TDM.**

### 1.3 Key Properties (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT412 Module 3)**
> - Each source is allotted a **fixed time slot** in every frame.
> - The slot position does **not change** between frames.
> - The **frame rate** equals the sampling rate of the slowest source.
> - If a source has no data, its slot is **padded with zeros/null bits** (wasted bandwidth).
> - Requires **frame synchronization bits** for proper demultiplexing at the receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Statistical TDM (STDM):**
> Synchronous TDM is also called **"Deterministic TDM"** because slot allocation is predetermined. In STDM, slots are allocated **dynamically** only to active sources — giving higher utilization but requiring addressing.

### 1.4 Position in the KTU 2024 Module 3 Syllabus

Synchronous TDM falls under the broader concept of **Multiplexing and Spread Spectrum** in Module 3. It is a **high-weightage topic**, frequently appearing in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) of the End Semester Examination (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Building Block — The TDM Frame

A **TDM frame** is one complete cycle of time slots. It contains exactly **N slots**, one for each of the **N input sources**.

**Frame structure (byte-interleaved example for 4 sources):**

| Slot 1 (Source A) | Slot 2 (Source B) | Slot 3 (Source C) | Slot 4 (Source D) | Sync Bits |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 bits | 8 bits | 8 bits | 8 bits | k bits |

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Sampling / Collection:** The multiplexer (MUX) collects a small fixed-size unit (1 bit, a nibble, or 1 byte — KTU syllabus uses **byte interleaving** as the default) from each of the *N* input sources during one sampling interval.
2. **Frame Assembly:** The collected units are placed back-to-back in a fixed order to form a **TDM frame**.
3. **Synchronization Addition:** One or more **synchronization (sync) bits** are inserted at the start of each frame so the receiver can identify frame boundaries.
4. **Transmission:** The frame is transmitted on the high-speed channel at the **multiplexed data rate**.
5. **Demultiplexing:** At the receiver (DEMUX), the sync bits are detected, the frame is decomposed, and each unit is routed to the correct output source.
6. **Repetition:** The MUX waits for the next sampling interval and the cycle repeats — every source gets one slot **per frame, no exceptions**.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why fixed slots?** So the demultiplexer doesn't need addressing information for every slot — position itself is the address. This makes hardware simple and cheap.
- **Why sync bits?** To recover frame boundaries after a long bit stream (the receiver has no "start of message" otherwise).
- **Why null padding?** Because the slot is reserved, but a silent source gives no bits — without padding, frames would shrink and lose sync.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` for absolute value in tables below** to prevent markdown parsing errors.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of input sources | $N$ | given | — | Count of low-speed channels |
| 2 | Bit rate of each source | $R_s$ | given | **bps** | All sources assumed equal in classical synchronous TDM |
| 3 | Bits per source per frame | $b$ | $1$, $4$, or $8$ typical | **bits** | 1 = bit-interleaved; 4 = nibble; 8 = byte |
| 4 | Frame duration (slot time) | $T_s$ | $T_s = b / R_s$ | **seconds** | Time to gather one unit from one source |
| 5 | Frame size (data only) | $F_{data}$ | $F_{data} = N \times b$ | **bits/frame** | Without sync overhead |
| 6 | Frame size (with sync) | $F_{total}$ | $F_{total} = N \times b + k$ | **bits/frame** | $k$ = synchronization bits |
| 7 | Multiplexed (output) bit rate | $R_{mux}$ | $R_{mux} = (N \times b + k) / T_s$ | **bps** | Final rate on the high-speed channel |
| 8 | Simplified (no sync) | $R_{mux}$ | $R_{mux} = N \times R_s$ | **bps** | Most KTU numerical problems use this |
| 9 | Frame rate (frames/sec) | $f_{frame}$ | $f_{frame} = 1 / T_s = R_s / b$ | **frames/s** | Equals sampling rate per source |
| 10 | Efficiency / Utilization | $\eta$ | $\eta = (N \times b) / (N \times b + k)$ | **dimensionless** | Ideally $\to 1$ as $k \to 0$ |
| 11 | Bit duration on output link | $T_{b,mux}$ | $T_{b,mux} = 1 / R_{mux}$ | **seconds** | Much shorter than $1/R_s$ |
| 12 | TDM link capacity vs sources | $C_{mux}$ | $C_{mux} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{s,i}$ | **bps** | Sum of all source rates |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)**: The classic T1 carrier (1.544 Mbps) multiplexes **24 voice channels of 64 kbps** each using synchronous TDM.
- **E1 Carrier (European standard)**: 32 time slots × 64 kbps = 2.048 Mbps (30 voice + 2 control/sync).
- **SONET/SDH backbone networks**: Long-haul fiber links use hierarchical synchronous TDM.
- **Digital Audio/Video broadcast**: Each audio channel gets a recurring time slot in MPEG-TS streams.
- **Embedded systems / microcontrollers**: Internal TDM buses for ADC sample transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world takeaway:** Every time you make a phone call over a digital landline, or stream digital TV, Synchronous TDM (or a hierarchical descendant of it) is at work inside the carrier network.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Multiplexed Output Bit Rate

**Statement:** When *N* synchronous sources, each with bit rate $R_s$, are multiplexed using *b* bits per source per frame, the resulting output link must operate at a bit rate of $R_{mux}$.

**Starting point:** In one frame duration $T_s$, the MUX collects *b* bits from each of *N* sources.

$$
\text{Bits per frame (data portion)} = N \times b
$$

If we also add $k$ synchronization bits, the full frame size is:

$$
F_{total} = N \times b + k
$$

The frame is produced once every $T_s$ seconds, so the number of frames per second is:

$$
f_{frame} = \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{R_s}{b}
$$

*(Explanation: a source of $R_s$ bps produces $R_s \times T_s$ bits per $T_s$ seconds. Since we sample $b$ bits from it per frame, $b = R_s \times T_s$, hence $T_s = b/R_s$.)*

The output bit rate is bits per second = (bits per frame) × (frames per second):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{mux} &= F_{total} \times f_{frame} \\
&= (N \cdot b + k) \times \frac{R_s}{b} \\
&= N \cdot R_s + \frac{k \cdot R_s}{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $k = 0$ (idealized, no sync overhead — the form KTU numerics usually assume):

$$
\boxed{R_{mux} = N \times R_s}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frame Duration and Frame Rate

We have $T_s = b / R_s$. 

- If $b = 1$ (bit interleaving): $T_s = 1 / R_s$ → frames are 1 bit-time wide. Frame rate = $R_s$ frames/sec.
- If $b = 8$ (byte interleaving): $T_s = 8 / R_s$ → frame rate = $R_s / 8$ frames/sec.

This means **byte-interleaved TDM is 8× more efficient on overhead** but requires the MUX to buffer 8 bits per source before transmitting — introducing 8× the latency.

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Type (Very High Yield)

> **Question (KTU-style, 7 marks worth):** Four sources, each transmitting at **1.5 kbps**, are multiplexed using **byte-interleaved synchronous TDM** with **2 synchronization bits per frame**.
> **(a)** Find the frame duration, frame size, output bit rate, and frame rate.
> **(b)** Find the efficiency.

### Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
$N = 4$, $R_s = 1.5 \text{ kbps} = 1500 \text{ bps}$, $b = 8$, $k = 2$.

**Step 1 — Frame duration:**

$$
T_s = \frac{b}{R_s} = \frac{8}{1500} = 5.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5.33 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Data bits per frame:**

$$
F_{data} = N \times b = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Total frame size (with sync):**

$$
F_{total} = N \times b + k = 32 + 2 = 34 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

**Step 4 — Output (multiplexed) bit rate:**

$$
R_{mux} = F_{total} \times f_{frame} = 34 \times \frac{1500}{8} = 34 \times 187.5 = 6375 \text{ bps} = 6.375 \text{ kbps}
$$

*Alternate (using the formula derived above):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{mux} &= N \cdot R_s + \frac{k \cdot R_s}{b} \\
&= 4 \times 1500 + \frac{2 \times 1500}{8} \\
&= 6000 + 375 = 6375 \text{ bps} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Frame rate:**

$$
f_{frame} = \frac{R_s}{b} = \frac{1500}{8} = 187.5 \text{ frames/sec}
$$

### Solution — Part (b) — Efficiency

$$
\eta = \frac{N \cdot b}{N \cdot b + k} = \frac{32}{34} = 0.9412 = 94.12\%
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Synchronous TDM vs Statistical TDM

| Criterion | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM (STDM) |
|---|---|---|
| Slot allocation | Fixed per source | Dynamic, on demand |
| Addressing | Not needed (positional) | Required in header |
| Bandwidth utilization | **Low** if sources are bursty | **High** (uses silence gaps) |
| Synchronization | Mandatory (frame sync) | Mandatory (frame + bit sync) |
| Hardware complexity | Simple | More complex (buffer + address logic) |
| Latency | Predictable & bounded | Variable |
| Suitable for | Constant-rate traffic (voice) | Bursty data (LAN, IP) |
| Overhead | Only sync bits | Sync + address bits per slot |
| Maximum sources | = Number of slots | > Number of slots (oversubscription) |
| KTUE exam example | T1 / E1 carrier | Asynchronous transfer examples |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Bit, Nibble, and Byte Interleaving

| Mode | $b$ | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bit interleaving | 1 | Lowest latency, fine granularity | Highest overhead, complex sync |
| Nibble interleaving | 4 | Balanced trade-off | Rarely used in practice |
| Byte interleaving | 8 | Standardized (T1/E1), simple buffering | 8× higher latency per source |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as bare node IDs. Labels are plain uppercase text inside quotes — no markdown or HTML tags inside labels.

## 4.2 Diagram 1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Synchronous TDM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[SOURCE A]:::source
    srcB[SOURCE B]:::source
    srcC[SOURCE C]:::source
    srcD[SOURCE D]:::source
    rot[SYNCHRONOUS ROTATING SWITCH]:::mux
    syncGen[SYNC BIT GENERATOR]:::sync
    outLink[HIGH SPEED CHANNEL]:::link
    demuxSwitch[RECEIVER ROTATING SWITCH]:::mux
    sinkA[DESTINATION A]:::sink
    sinkB[DESTINATION B]:::sink
    sinkC[DESTINATION C]:::sink
    sinkD[DESTINATION D]:::sink

    srcA --> rot
    srcB --> rot
    srcC --> rot
    srcD --> rot
    syncGen --> rot
    rot --> outLink
    outLink --> demuxSwitch
    demuxSwitch --> sinkA
    demuxSwitch --> sinkB
    demuxSwitch --> sinkC
    demuxSwitch --> sinkD

    classDef source fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000
    classDef sink fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef mux fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#552255,color:#000
    classDef sync fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,color:#000
    classDef link fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

**Explanation of flow:**
- The four sources feed data into the rotating MUX switch.
- A **Sync Bit Generator** adds frame alignment bits at regular intervals.
- The combined frame travels over the **High Speed Channel** (the "conveyor belt").
- At the far end, a synchronized demultiplexer switch routes the data back to the four destinations based on slot position.
