## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Decision Flow for Synchronous vs Statistical Choice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ{WHAT IS THE NATURE OF INPUT TRAFFIC}:::qbox
    constantPath[CONSTANT RATE STREAMING VOICE OR VIDEO]:::ansBox
    burstyPath[BURSTY INTERMITTENT DATA TRAFFIC]:::ansBox
    choice1[USE SYNCHRONOUS TDM]:::ansBox
    choice2[USE STATISTICAL TDM]:::ansBox
    reason1[PREDICTABLE LATENCY SIMPLE HARDWARE]:::noteBox
    reason2[HIGHER LINK UTILIZATION]:::noteBox

    startQ --> constantPath
    startQ --> burstyPath
    constantPath --> choice1
    burstyPath --> choice2
    choice1 --> reason1
    choice2 --> reason2

    classDef qbox fill:#ffe599,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef ansBox fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000
    classDef noteBox fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A traffic source is classified first. Constant-rate traffic (e.g., a phone call producing 64 kbps continuously) is best served by Synchronous TDM. Bursty traffic (e.g., a user typing on a terminal — bits arrive in short bursts) wastes the reserved slots of Synchronous TDM and is best served by Statistical TDM.

# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Synchronous Time-Division Multiplexing. List any two of its disadvantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Synchronous TDM is a digital multiplexing technique in which multiple low-rate digital signals are combined onto one high-rate channel by allocating a **fixed time slot** to each source in every frame, irrespective of whether the source has data to send. The MUX and DEMUX operate in lock-step synchronism.

**Two disadvantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Wastage of bandwidth:** The fixed slot for an idle source is transmitted as null bits — bandwidth is wasted.
2. **No addressing flexibility:** Sources cannot dynamically borrow bandwidth from idle sources.
3. **Synchronization overhead:** Frame sync bits are mandatory, reducing effective efficiency.
4. **Poor match for bursty traffic:** Most real data is bursty, not constant rate.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Synchronous TDM and Statistical TDM (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per valid point):**

| Sl. | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slots are **fixed and pre-assigned** to each source. | Slots are **dynamically allocated** to active sources. |
| 2 | No addressing information needed inside the frame. | Each slot must carry the **address of the source**. |
| 3 | Bandwidth utilization is **low** when sources are silent. | Bandwidth utilization is **high** — no idle slots. |
| 4 | Number of simultaneous sources $\le$ number of slots. | Number of simultaneous sources can **exceed** number of slots. |
| 5 | Used in **T1/E1 carrier systems**. | Used in **packet switching / data networks**. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice). Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **4-channel synchronous TDM system**. Discuss how the multiplexer and demultiplexer are kept in synchronization. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Four 8-kbps channels are multiplexed using byte-interleaved synchronous TDM. If **1 synchronization bit** is added per frame, calculate:
- (i) Frame duration
- (ii) Frame size (total bits per frame)
- (iii) Multiplexed output bit rate
- (iv) Frame rate
- (v) Efficiency of the system **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

The block diagram of a 4-channel synchronous TDM system consists of four sources $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ feeding into a **rotating switch (MUX)**. A **synchronization bit generator** injects one bit per frame. The combined output travels over a high-speed channel to a **demultiplexer** at the receiver, which is a synchronously rotating switch that routes the incoming bits back to destinations $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$.

**Synchronization is maintained by:**
1. **Frame sync bits** at the start of every frame, which the DEMUX uses to align its switch.
2. **Identical clock frequency** at both ends (often recovered from the data stream using a PLL).
3. **Fixed, known frame structure** — both ends agree on the slot order in advance.

*(Full marks require a labelled diagram and a one-line statement of each synchronization method.)*

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Given:** $N = 4$, $R_s = 8 \text{ kbps} = 8000 \text{ bps}$, $b = 8$ (byte), $k = 1$ sync bit.

**(i) Frame duration:**

$$
T_s = \frac{b}{R_s} = \frac{8}{8000} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Frame size:**

$$
F_{total} = N \cdot b + k = 4 \times 8 + 1 = 33 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

**(iii) Multiplexed output bit rate:**

$$
R_{mux} = N \cdot R_s + \frac{k \cdot R_s}{b} = 4 \times 8000 + \frac{1 \times 8000}{8} = 32000 + 1000 = 33 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(iv) Frame rate:**

$$
f_{frame} = \frac{R_s}{b} = \frac{8000}{8} = 1000 \text{ frames/sec}
$$

**(v) Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{N \cdot b}{N \cdot b + k} = \frac{32}{33} = 0.9697 = 96.97\%
$$

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare Synchronous TDM and Frequency-Division Multiplexing (FDM) on the basis of: signal type, bandwidth utilization, synchronization requirement, and typical applications. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Six voice channels, each **64 kbps**, are multiplexed using synchronous TDM with **1 bit per source per frame** (bit-interleaving). Calculate the multiplexed bit rate, the bit duration on the output link, and the time taken to transmit one complete frame. Comment on why this scheme is rarely used in practice despite its simplicity. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

| Criterion | Synchronous TDM | FDM |
|---|---|---|
| Signal type | **Digital** only | Can carry analog and digital |
| Bandwidth utilization | Slots unused when source is silent → **waste** | Guard bands wasted → **waste** |
| Synchronization | **Required** (frame sync) | Not required in same sense (filters do the work) |
| Crosstalk / Interference | Negligible (time separation) | Possible if filters are imperfect |
| Typical applications | T1/E1 digital carriers, PCM telephony | AM/FM radio, cable TV, analog telephony |

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Given:** $N = 6$, $R_s = 64 \text{ kbps} = 64000 \text{ bps}$, $b = 1$ (bit-interleaved), $k = 0$ (assumed).

**Multiplexed bit rate:**

$$
R_{mux} = N \times R_s = 6 \times 64000 = 384000 \text{ bps} = 384 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Bit duration on output link:**

$$
T_{b,mux} = \frac{1}{R_{mux}} = \frac{1}{384000} = 2.604 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \approx 2.6 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Time taken to transmit one complete frame:**

Each frame has $N \times b = 6$ bits. So one frame duration equals 6 bit-times on the output link:

$$
T_{frame} = 6 \times T_{b,mux} = 6 \times 2.604 \;\mu\text{s} = 15.625 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Why this scheme is rarely used in practice:**
- The MUX must run the output link at 384 kbps to carry 6×64 kbps channels — fine.
- However, with only 1 bit per source per frame, the **framing/sync overhead per bit** is huge. Every 6 data bits need a sync marker, so effective efficiency drops sharply.
- The DEMUX must switch at the bit rate — making hardware sensitive to jitter and requiring a high-bandwidth PLL.
- The receiver also needs very fast bit-level demultiplexing logic.
- Hence **byte-interleaving** is preferred in real systems like T1/E1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to include sync bits in the frame size** — a 1-mark deduction is standard if the answer is just $N \times b$ without $k$.
> 2. **Mixing up $R_s$ and $R_{mux}$** — the source rate is *per channel*; the output rate is *aggregate*. Many students write $R_{mux} = R_s$ and get 0 for that sub-part.
> 3. **Using $T_s = 1/R_{mux}$** instead of $T_s = b/R_s$ — this is wrong; the slot duration is set by the source rate, not the output rate.
> 4. **Reporting efficiency in dB or as a percentage without the formula** — show the formula $\eta = \frac{N \cdot b}{N \cdot b + k}$ explicitly.
> 5. **Drawing the MUX as a black box without sync-bit generator or rotating switch label** — examiners expect a labelled functional diagram, not just rectangles.
> 6. **In part (b) of long-answer numericals, presenting only the final answer** — always show the substitution step to get full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Synchronous TDM = fixed, pre-allocated, recurring time slots** in every frame.
- ⭐ **No addressing inside the frame** — slot position is the implicit address.
- ⭐ **Formula to memorize:** $R_{mux} = N \cdot R_s + \dfrac{k \cdot R_s}{b}$ (with sync) or $N \cdot R_s$ (without sync).
- ⭐ **Frame duration:** $T_s = b / R_s$ — depends on **source** rate, not output rate.
- ⭐ **Frame rate:** $f_{frame} = R_s / b$.
- ⭐ **Efficiency:** $\eta = (N \cdot b) / (N \cdot b + k)$ — always less than 1 unless $k = 0$.
- ⭐ **Idle sources still consume a slot** — bandwidth is wasted on silence.
- ⭐ **Interleaving types:** bit ($b=1$), nibble ($b=4$), byte ($b=8$); byte-interleaving is the **T1/E1 standard**.
- ⭐ **Real-world mapping:** T1 = 24 × 64 kbps + 1 framing bit = 1.544 Mbps. E1 = 32 × 64 kbps = 2.048 Mbps.
- ⭐ **Sync bits are essential** — the receiver cannot otherwise identify the start of a frame.
- ⭐ **Synchronous ≠ Synchronous (optical)** — in this module "synchronous" means time-slot-locked, not "synchronous digital hierarchy (SDH)".
- ⭐ **Use Statistical TDM** when traffic is bursty; use Synchronous TDM when traffic is constant-rate.
- ⭐ **Internal choice** in Part B means *learn both Question A and Question B variants* — KTU examiners alternate them.
- ⭐ **Always show substitution** in numericals — partial credit is given for correctly substituted formulas even if arithmetic slips.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Time-Division Multiplexing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Statistical Time-Division Multiplexing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview **Statistical Time-Division Multiplexing (STDM)**, also termed **Asynchronous TDM** or **Intelligent TDM**, is a dynamic channel-sharing technique in which time slots on the shared high-speed trunk are allocated *on demand* only to those input devices whose data buffers are currently non-empty. Unlike Synchronous TDM, the multiplexer does not reserve a fixed slot for every input — instead, it appends a short **address tag** (channel identifier) to each transmitted sample so that the distant demultiplexer can correctly route the incoming bit stream to its intended destination buffer. > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** Statistical TDM is a line-sharing technique where the multiplexer scans active input lines, constructs frames containing only the data of currently transmitting devices plus an address field, and transmits them on a common medium whose bit rate may be *less than the sum of individual source peak rates*. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Shared Cab Stand Imagine a busy bus terminal with **20** potential passengers, but on average only **4** of them actually want a ride at the same instant. - A **Synchronous TDM** system behaves like a scheduled public bus: it leaves every 5 minutes with **20 fixed seats**, even when 16 of them are empty. The fuel (bandwidth) is wasted on empty seats. - A **Statistical TDM** system behaves like a pool of on-demand cabs: only when a passenger is actually waiting does a cab arrive, picks them up, and attaches a small *destination tag* on the dashboard. The cab driver (demultiplexer) reads the tag and drops the passenger at the correct stop. The key insight — by serving only the *active* sources, STDM can support **roughly twice** the number of devices compared to synchronous TDM on the same physical link, with the same trunk bit rate. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** STDM is classified under *Module 3 — Multiplexing and Digital Carrier Systems*. The standard metrics students must memorize are the **average output data rate**, the **address overhead per slot**, the **slot duration**, and the **slot efficiency ratio** $d/(d+a)$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Steps of an STDM System 1. **Buffering Stage** — Each of the *n* input sources is connected to a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) buffer that absorbs incoming bits at the source's independent bit rate *r*. 2. **Line Scan** — The statistical multiplexer continuously scans the *n* input lines, in either fixed round-robin order or priority order, checking the buffer-fill status. 3. **Dynamic Slot Allocation** — When a buffer is non-empty, the multiplexer composes a slot consisting of an **address field** (size *a* bits) immediately followed by a **data field** (size *d* bits). Empty buffers are *skipped entirely*. 4. **Frame Assembly** — Slots are concatenated back-to-back into a continuous bit stream; no idle gaps are inserted between active slots. 5. **Trunk Transmission** — The composite bit stream is clocked onto the high-speed output link at rate *R* bps. 6. **Demultiplexing** — At the far end, the demultiplexer reads the leading *a* address bits of each slot, decodes them into an output port number, and clocks the following *d* data bits into the corresponding output buffer. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why buffer?** Because input sources operate at their own independent clocks; without buffering the multiplexer has no place to hold incoming bits while it is busy servicing other sources. - **Why scan?** Scanning turns parallel independent channels into a single serial stream that shares one physical wire or fiber. - **Why skip idle lines?** Skipping is the *statistical* edge — it converts wasted synchronous slots into productive bandwidth. - **Why address?** Because with no fixed slot position, the demultiplexer has no positional clue; the address field is the only way to identify the destination. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units | |---|---------|----------------------|-------| | 1 | $R_{sync} \geq n \cdot r$ | Synchronous TDM worst-case trunk rate | bps | | 2 | $\bar{R}_{in} = n \cdot p \cdot r$ | Average input aggregate rate for STDM | bps | | 3 | $a = \lceil \log_{2} n \rceil$ | Address bits required to identify *n* sources | bits | | 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{d}{d+a}$ | Slot efficiency (fraction carrying useful data) | dimensionless | | 5 | $R_{STDM} = \dfrac{\bar{R}_{in}}{\eta}$ | Minimum trunk rate required by STDM | bps | | 6 | $T_{slot} = \dfrac{d+a}{R}$ | Duration of one statistical slot | seconds | | 7 | $n_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{R}{p \cdot r \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{a}{d}\right)} \right\rfloor$ | Maximum number of sources STDM can multiplex | integer | | 8 | $G_{STDM} = \dfrac{n \cdot r}{R_{STDM}}$ | Gain ratio over synchronous TDM | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constants to remember:** Address bits grow logarithmically — **10 channels → 4 bits**, **100 channels → 7 bits**, **1000 channels → 10 bits**. Slot efficiency $\eta$ therefore improves as the data field *d* grows. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility STDM is the conceptual foundation of modern **packet switching**, **ATM cell relay**, **Statistical Multiplexing Concentrators (STUC)** in telecom, and the bandwidth-allocation logic inside **routers and switches** with output-buffered crossbars. Every DSLAM, every LTE base-station scheduler, and every MPLS router applies STDM principles to safely over-subscribe access links. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Numerical Derivations #### Problem 3.1 — Capacity of an STDM Link **Given Data:** - Number of input sources: $n = 10$ - Bit rate of each source: $r = 1$ kbps - Probability that any source is active at a sampling instant: $p = 0.4$ - Data field size per slot: $d = 4$ bits - Address field size per slot: $a = 4$ bits **To Find:** (i) Minimum trunk rate $R_{STDM}$, (ii) Slot duration $T_{slot}$, (iii) Comparison with Synchronous TDM. **Step 1 — Compute the average aggregate input rate.** Because each source independently transmits with probability $p$, the expected traffic from one source is $p \cdot r$. With $n$ statistically independent sources, the mean aggregate input rate is the sum of expectations: $$ \bar{R}_{in} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} p \cdot r \;=\; n \cdot p \cdot r $$ Substituting the numerical values: $$ \bar{R}_{in} \;=\; 10 \times 0.4 \times 1000 \;=\; 4000 \;\text{bps} \;=\; 4 \;\text{kbps} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the slot efficiency $\eta$.** The slot efficiency is the ratio of useful data bits to total bits transmitted per slot: $$ \eta \;=\; \frac{d}{d+a} \;=\; \frac{4}{4+4} \;=\; \frac{4}{8} \;=\; 0.5 $$ Half of the trunk bandwidth is consumed by address overhead — a significant inefficiency. In a real system the address field would be *smaller* (3 or 4 bits) and the data field *larger* to push $\eta$ upward. **Step 3 — Compute the required trunk rate.** The trunk must carry not only the data bits but also the per-slot addressing overhead. Dividing the average input rate by the efficiency gives the minimum required output rate: $$ R_{STDM} \;=\; \frac{\bar{R}_{in}}{\eta} \;=\; \frac{4 \;\text{kbps}}{0.5} \;=\; 8 \;\text{kbps} $$ **Step 4 — Compute slot duration on the 8 kbps trunk.** $$ T_{slot} \;=\; \frac{d+a}{R_{STDM}} \;=\; \frac{8 \;\text{bits}}{8000 \;\text{bps}} \;=\; 1 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{s} \;=\; 1 \;\text{ms} $$ **Step 5 — Compare with Synchronous TDM.** For synchronous TDM the trunk must be sized to the **peak** aggregate rate: $$ R_{sync} \;=\; n \cdot r \;=\; 10 \times 1 \;\text{kbps} \;=\; 10 \;\text{kbps} $$ Hence the **bandwidth saving** with STDM is: $$ \Delta R \;=\; R_{sync} - R_{STDM} \;=\; 10 - 8 \;=\; 2 \;\text{kbps} $$ which corresponds to a **20 % saving** even at the modest activity factor $p = 0.4$. At lower values of $p$, the saving grows rapidly. --- #### Problem 3.2 — Can STDM Support 20 Bursty Sources? **Given Data:** - Number of input sources: $n = 20$ - Bit rate of each source: $r = 10$ kbps - Activity probability: $p = 0.2$ - Data field: $d = 8$ bits per slot - Address field: $a = 4$ bits per slot - Available trunk rate: $R = 100$ kbps **Step 1 — Average aggregate input rate.** $$ \bar{R}_{in} \;=\; n \cdot p \cdot r \;=\; 20 \times 0.2 \times 10 \;\text{kbps} \;=\; 40 \;\text{kbps} $$ **Step 2 — Verify the address-field width.** $$ a_{req} \;=\; \lceil \log_{2} 20 \rceil \;=\; \lceil 4.322 \rceil \;=\; 5 \;\text{bits} $$ The given $a = 4$ bits is **one bit short** of uniquely addressing all 20 sources — only 16 distinct addresses are possible. For a textbook calculation we accept the given value, but flag this design error in the recap. **Step 3 — Slot efficiency.** $$ \eta \;=\; \frac{8}{8+4} \;=\; \frac{8}{12} \;=\; 0.6667 $$ **Step 4 — Required STDM trunk rate.** $$ R_{req} \;=\; \frac{\bar{R}_{in}}{\eta} \;=\; \frac{40 \;\text{kbps}}{0.6667} \;=\; 60 \;\text{kbps} $$ **Step 5 — Feasibility test.** Because $R = 100 \;\text{kbps} \;\geq\; R_{req} = 60 \;\text{kbps}$, STDM **can** support the 20 bursty sources. A 40 kbps safety margin absorbs momentary traffic bursts where more than the average number of sources happen to be active simultaneously. **Step 6 — Worst-case peak load.** If by coincidence all 20 sources become simultaneously active, the instantaneous demand is: $$ R_{peak} \;=\; n \cdot r \;=\; 20 \times 10 \;\text{kbps} \;=\; 200 \;\text{kbps} $$ This far exceeds the 100 kbps trunk; buffers will *fill up* and **data loss (overflow)** can occur. Designers mitigate this risk by sizing the buffers using the *M/M/1* or *M/D/1* queueing models, which yield the blocking probability $P_{block}$. ### 3.2 Summary Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM | |-----------|-----------------|------------------| | Slot allocation | Fixed, pre-assigned | Dynamic, on demand | | Address bits per slot | **0** | $\log_{2} n$ | | Trunk-rate requirement | $n \cdot r$ (peak) | $\dfrac{n \cdot p \cdot r}{\eta}$ (average) | | Idle-slot wastage | High | Negligible | | Buffering at multiplexer | Not required | **Mandatory** | | Demultiplexer logic | Counter-based | **Address-decoder based** | | Behaviour under burst load | Excellent — never overflows | Risk of buffer overflow | | Best suited for | Continuous, smooth traffic | Bursty, interactive traffic | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an STDM System ```mermaid flowchart LR srcA["Source 1 FIFO Buffer"] --> muxNode srcB["Source 2 FIFO Buffer"] --> muxNode srcC["Source 3 FIFO Buffer"] --> muxNode srcD["Source N FIFO Buffer"] --> muxNode muxNode["Statistical Multiplexer
Address plus Data Composer"] --> trunkNode trunkNode["High Speed Trunk
R bps Serial Link"] --> demuxNode demuxNode["Statistical Demultiplexer
Address Decoder"] --> outA demuxNode --> outB demuxNode --> outC demuxNode --> outN outA["Sink 1 FIFO Buffer"] outB["Sink 2 FIFO Buffer"] outC["Sink 3 FIFO Buffer"] outN["Sink N FIFO Buffer"] ``` ### 4.2 Frame Structure inside the Statistical Multiplexer ```mermaid flowchart LR f1["Slot 1
Addr bits plus Data bits"] --- f2["Slot 2
Addr bits plus Data bits"] f2 --- f3["Slot 3
Addr bits plus Data bits"] f3 --- f4["Slot K
Addr bits plus Data bits"] f4 --- f5["Slot K plus 1
Addr bits plus Data bits"] f5 --- f6["More slots as needed"] ``` ### 4.3 Detailed Functional Flow inside the Multiplexer ```mermaid flowchart TD step1["Read all buffer fill flags"] --> step2{"Any buffer non-empty?"} step2 -- No --> step1 step2 -- Yes --> step3["Pick highest priority active source"] step3 --> step4["Append address bits log2 N"] step4 --> step5["Append d data bits from buffer"] step5 --> step6["Shift composite slot onto trunk"] step6 --> step7["Decrement buffer pointer"] step7 --> step1 ``` > [!NOTE] > The scanner in step 1 runs continuously. If *no* source is active at a given instant, the multiplexer may either send a **fill pattern** (all-1s) for line-clock recovery or simply go silent, depending on the physical-layer standard. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Statistical Time-Division Multiplexing. Mention *any two* advantages it offers over Synchronous TDM. **Model Answer:** Statistical TDM is a dynamic multiplexing technique in which the multiplexer allocates time slots on the shared high-speed trunk *only* to those input sources whose data buffers are currently non-empty, attaching a short address field to each slot so that the demultiplexer can route the data correctly. *Advantages:* 1. **Higher bandwidth efficiency** — idle time slots are skipped, allowing the same trunk to support roughly twice the number of devices for bursty traffic. 2. **Lower trunk bit-rate requirement** — the output rate need only match the *average* aggregate input, not the peak, when sources are bursty. **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the role of the *address field* in a Statistical TDM slot? How many address bits are needed for 32 input channels? **Model Answer:** The address field uniquely identifies which of the *N* input channels produced the data bits that follow it in the same slot. Because slots are *not* fixed in position (unlike synchronous TDM), the demultiplexer would otherwise have no way to know where to deliver the data. For $N = 32$ channels, address bits required: $a = \log_{2} 32 = 5$ bits. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) #### Question A — Option (a) 7 Marks + (b) 7 Marks > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **Q.A.(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Synchronous TDM and Statistical TDM along *any seven* parameters in a tabular form. State which technique is preferred when input sources are **bursty in nature** and justify your answer. **Model Solution:** | Parameter | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM | |-----------|-----------------|------------------| | Slot assignment | Fixed to each input | Dynamic, on demand | | Address bits per slot | Not needed | $\log_2 n$ bits | | Trunk bit-rate | $n \cdot r$ (peak) | $\dfrac{n \cdot p \cdot r}{\eta}$ (average) | | Bandwidth utilisation | Low for bursty traffic | High even for bursty traffic | | Buffer requirement | None at MUX | Mandatory at MUX | | Demultiplexer complexity | Simple counter | Address-decoder logic | | Synchronisation | Frame-level clock recovery | Slot-level clock plus address decoding | **Justification:** When sources are bursty (for example, interactive terminals that transmit only when a key is pressed), most synchronous slots are empty — wasting bandwidth. STDM skips idle slots, doubling or tripling the effective throughput on the same trunk, so STDM is **preferred** for bursty traffic. **Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]** A multiplexer handles 10 input lines, each operating at 1 kbps. The probability that any line is active is **0.4**. Each transmitted sample carries **4 bits of data** and **4 bits of address**. Calculate (i) the average aggregate input rate, (ii) slot efficiency, and (iii) the minimum trunk bit-rate required. Compare the result with synchronous TDM. **Model Solution:** (i) Average aggregate input rate: $$ \bar{R}_{in} = 10 \times 0.4 \times 1 \;\text{kbps} = 4 \;\text{kbps} $$ (ii) Slot efficiency: $$ \eta = \frac{d}{d+a} = \frac{4}{4+4} = 0.5 $$ (iii) Required trunk rate: $$ R_{STDM} = \frac{\bar{R}_{in}}{\eta} = \frac{4}{0.5} = 8 \;\text{kbps} $$ Synchronous TDM comparison: $$ R_{sync} = 10 \times 1 = 10 \;\text{kbps} $$ **Result:** STDM saves $10 - 8 = 2$ kbps (a **20 % bandwidth saving**). At lower activity probabilities ($p < 0.4$), the saving grows rapidly toward the theoretical 50 % mark. --- #### Question B — Option (a) 7 Marks + (b) 7 Marks > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Statistical TDM system. What is the role of (i) buffers and (ii) address bits? **Model Solution:** *(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)* - The **statistical multiplexer** continuously scans the *n* input FIFO buffers; whenever a buffer contains data, the multiplexer constructs a slot containing *a* address bits followed by *d* data bits, and ships it out on the high-speed trunk. - The **statistical demultiplexer** at the far end reads the leading *a* address bits, decodes them into a destination port number, and steers the next *d* data bits into the corresponding output buffer. **Role of buffers (i):** Input sources are asynchronous and may momentarily produce data faster than the multiplexer can service them. The FIFO buffer absorbs these bursts, preventing data loss during moderate peak activity — though if a burst saturates the trunk for too long, buffer overflow can still occur. **Role of address bits (ii):** In STDM, the *position* of a slot in the frame no longer identifies its source (because idle slots are skipped). Hence an explicit *address tag* must accompany every data sample so the demultiplexer knows where to deliver it. The address width is $a = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ bits. **Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]** Twenty data sources, each transmitting at 10 kbps with a 0.2 probability of being active at any sampling instant, are to be multiplexed using Statistical TDM onto a 100 kbps trunk. Each slot carries 8 bits of data and 4 bits of address. Verify whether STDM can support this traffic and comment on overflow risk. **Model Solution:** *Average aggregate input rate:* $$ \bar{R}_{in} = 20 \times 0.2 \times 10 \;\text{kbps} = 40 \;\text{kbps} $$ *Slot efficiency:* $$ \eta = \frac{8}{8+4} = 0.6667 $$ *Required STDM trunk rate:* $$ R_{req} = \frac{40}{0.6667} = 60 \;\text{kbps} $$ *Feasibility check:* Available trunk = 100 kbps $\geq$ required = 60 kbps → **STDM can support** the 20 sources with a 40 kbps safety margin. *Overflow risk:* The peak demand (all 20 sources simultaneously active) is: $$ R_{peak} = 20 \times 10 \;\text{kbps} = 200 \;\text{kbps} $$ which **far exceeds** the 100 kbps trunk. Hence during sustained simultaneous bursts the buffers will fill and **overflow** will occur. Designers must therefore choose buffer depths using *M/M/1* or *M/D/1* queueing theory to keep the blocking probability $P_{block}$ within acceptable limits (for example, $10^{-3}$). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — How Marks Are Lost:** > 1. Forgetting to add address bits when computing the required trunk rate — students often write $R = n p r$ and lose 2 marks. > 2. Computing $R_{sync} = n \cdot r$ as the *minimum* required, when in fact it is the **maximum** required by synchronous TDM (the wording matters in the answer). > 3. Failing to mention **buffer overflow risk** under peak load — KTU expects at least one sentence on queueing. > 4. Writing $a = n$ bits instead of $a = \log_2 n$ — this is a common error that costs a full sub-part mark. > 5. Forgetting the unit **bits per second (bps)** in the final answer — half-mark deduction per omission under the KTU 2024 valuation key. ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - **STDM = Asynchronous TDM = Intelligent TDM** — all three names are interchangeable in KTU examinations. - **Slot structure:** address bits ($a = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$) followed by data bits ($d$). - **Slot efficiency:** $\eta = d/(d+a)$. - **Average aggregate input rate:** $\bar{R}_{in} = n \cdot p \cdot r$. - **Required trunk rate:** $R_{STDM} = \bar{R}_{in}/\eta$. - **Synchronous TDM trunk** must support peak: $R_{sync} = n \cdot r$. - **Buffers are mandatory** at the multiplexer; **address decoder is mandatory** at the demultiplexer. - **Idle slots are skipped entirely** — the single biggest reason STDM beats synchronous TDM for bursty sources. - Under peak concurrent activity, **overflow occurs** because the trunk cannot exceed its design rate; queueing theory governs buffer sizing. - For typical interactive-terminal traffic where $p \approx 0.1$, STDM can support **roughly twice** the number of devices on the same trunk compared to synchronous TDM. - STDM trade-off: **bandwidth efficiency** versus **buffer-induced delay and overflow risk**.

topics.title: Spread Spectrum: Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Spread Spectrum: Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** is a spread-spectrum modulation technique in which the carrier frequency of the transmitted signal is rapidly switched (or "hopped") among many distinct frequency channels, using a **pseudorandom noise (PN) sequence** known to both the transmitter and the receiver. The hopping pattern appears random to any unauthorized listener, but the synchronized receiver follows the same hopping sequence to reconstruct the original data. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** *FHSS is a transmission technique where the data signal modulates a carrier whose frequency is periodically changed (hopped) in accordance with a pseudorandom sequence. The total bandwidth occupied is much larger than the minimum bandwidth required to transmit the information signal.* --- ### 1.2 The Two Sub-Classes of FHSS | Sub-Class | Definition | Relationship | |-----------|-----------|--------------| | **Slow Hopping (SFH/FHSS-Slow)** | Multiple data bits are transmitted using the same carrier frequency before a hop occurs | **Hop rate $R_h$ < Bit rate $R_b$** | | **Fast Hopping (FFH/FHSS-Fast)** | The carrier frequency changes multiple times during the transmission of a single data bit | **Hop rate $R_h$ > Bit rate $R_b$** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Favorite:** Examiners frequently test whether the student can clearly distinguish between "number of bits per hop" and "number of hops per bit." Always state the relationship to the bit period $T_b$ explicitly. --- ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **The "Pirate Radio on the Run" Analogy** 🎙️ > Imagine two spies exchanging coded messages inside a city. If one spy simply talks on a fixed frequency, a hostile interceptor can easily tune in and eavesdrop. Instead, Spy-A and Spy-B agree on a *secret schedule* (the PN sequence): at 8:00 AM they talk on Channel 1, at 8:00:10 on Channel 7, at 8:00:20 on Channel 15, and so on. A listener scanning any single channel hears only a meaningless *brief blip* and misses 99% of the conversation. This is **exactly how FHSS works** — the signal "escapes" by being everywhere, but only briefly. --- ### 1.4 Key Physical Metrics (Bolded Per Protocol) The following standard metrics govern every FHSS system and **must be memorized** for KTU examinations: - **Hop Rate ($R_h$)** — number of frequency hops per second, measured in **hops/second**. - **Dwell Time ($T_d$)** — time the carrier remains on a single frequency channel. - **Chip Duration ($T_c$)** — duration of one bit of the PN sequence. - **Number of Hopping Channels ($N$)** — total distinct frequency slots in the hopping pool. - **Processing Gain ($G_p$)** — ratio of spread bandwidth to information bandwidth (measured in **dB**). - **Total Spread Bandwidth ($W_{ss}$)** — typically of the order of **MHz** (Bluetooth uses **79 MHz**, classic 802.11 used **2.4 GHz ISM band**). --- ### 1.5 Why Spread Spectrum Is Used — The Four Engineering Motivations > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Four Pillars" of Spread Spectrum (KTU 2024 Module 3 Core):** 1. **Resistance to Narrowband Jamming** — A jammer tuned to one frequency can only disrupt a tiny fraction of the total transmission. 2. **Low Probability of Intercept (LPI)** — The signal is hidden below the noise floor for unauthorized listeners. 3. **Robustness Against Multipath Fading** — A frequency-selective fade affects only one hop, not the entire message. 4. **Multiple Access Capability (CDMA-style)** — Multiple users can share the same band using *different* PN sequences. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Steps of an FHSS Transmitter > [!NOTE] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners often award 2 marks for correctly labeling the block diagram and another 2 for explaining the role of the **PN sequence generator**. Master this list. 1. **Source Encoding:** The baseband data bits enter the system at bit rate $R_b = 1/T_b$. 2. **PN Sequence Generation:** A maximal-length or Gold-code generator produces a pseudorandom binary sequence at chip rate $R_c = 1/T_c$. 3. **Frequency Synthesizer Tuning:** The PN sequence is fed into a **frequency synthesizer** that selects one of $N$ carrier frequencies from a predetermined table. 4. **Modulation (FSK/BPSK):** The data modulates the *currently selected* carrier using conventional modulation (most commonly BFSK). 5. **Hopping:** After a dwell time $T_d$, the PN generator advances and the synthesizer retunes to a new frequency. The pattern repeats. 6. **Reception & Synchronization:** The receiver's local PN generator is **bit- and phase-synchronized** to the transmitter, enabling despreading of the hopped signal back to baseband. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Meaning | |---|----------|---------|------|---------| | 1 | **Dwell Time** | $T_d = \dfrac{1}{R_h}$ | seconds | Time on one frequency | | 2 | **Bit Duration** | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b}$ | seconds | Time of one data bit | | 3 | **Hops per Bit (Slow FHSS)** | $k_{slow} = \dfrac{T_b}{T_d} = \dfrac{R_h}{R_b}$ | dimensionless | Must be **integer** | | 4 | **Bits per Hop (Fast FHSS)** | $k_{fast} = \dfrac{T_d}{T_b} = \dfrac{R_b}{R_h}$ | dimensionless | Must be **integer** | | 5 | **Number of Hopping Channels** | $N = 2^{n}$ where $n$ = PN bits per hop | dimensionless | Pool size | | 6 | **Processing Gain** | $G_p = \dfrac{W_{ss}}{R_b}$ | ratio (dB = $10 \log_{10} G_p$) | Jammer resistance | | 7 | **Processing Gain in dB** | $G_p(dB) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{W_{ss}}{R_b}\right)$ | **dB** | Board-standard form | | 8 | **Hopset Bandwidth** | $W_{ss} \approx N \cdot \Delta f$ | Hz | Total occupied spectrum | | 9 | **Hop Channel Spacing** | $\Delta f$ (e.g., 1 MHz for Bluetooth) | Hz | Width of one hop slot | | 10 | **Probability of Hit (Jamming)** | $P_{hit} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ for random jammer | dimensionless | Decreases with $N$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Convention:** The vertical bar `$\vert$` is reserved for table syntax. For absolute value or probability, **always** use $\lvert x \rvert$. Board evaluators will not penalize you for $\lvert x \rvert$ but they will if the markdown table breaks. --- ### 2.3 Mathematical Relationship — Slow vs Fast Hopping (Derivable Form) > [!TIP] > **The Golden Rule of Hopping Classification:** - If the number of bits transmitted in one dwell time $\geq 1$ → **Slow Hopping**. - If the number of bits transmitted in one dwell time $< 1$ (i.e., the hop changes mid-bit) → **Fast Hopping**. This is mathematically: $$ \text{If } \quad T_b \geq T_d \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; R_b \leq R_h \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{FAST HOPPING} $$ $$ \text{If } \quad T_b < T_d \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; R_b > R_h \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{SLOW HOPPING} $$ --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | Why FHSS? | |--------|-------------|-----------| | **Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1)** | Wireless earphones, IoT | 79 hops in 2.4 GHz ISM band, 1600 hops/sec | | **Military Tactical Radio (SINCGARS, Have Quick)** | Battlefield voice/data | Anti-jamming critical | | **Older Wi-Fi (802.11 legacy, 1997)** | 1–2 Mbps WLAN | Coexistence with microwave ovens | | **GSM (Frequency Hopping Overlay)** | Cellular base station option | Combats co-channel interference | | **GPS (Future M-Code)** | Satellite navigation | Resistant to spoofing | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Processing Gain of an FHSS System > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** Processing gain is the *single most-tested* quantity in KTU Module 3. The full derivation is shown below for full marks. **Given:** A data signal of bit rate $R_b$ is transmitted using FHSS with a hopset of $N$ channels, each of width $\Delta f$ Hz. **Step 1 — Identify the Information Bandwidth.** The minimum Nyquist bandwidth required to transmit the data at rate $R_b$ is: $$ B_{info} \approx R_b $$ > **Logic:** By the Nyquist–Shannon criterion, the baseband signal occupies roughly half its bit rate on each side; for board-level calculations, $B_{info} = R_b$ is the accepted approximation. **Step 2 — Identify the Spread (Hopset) Bandwidth.** The transmitter hops across $N$ distinct carrier frequencies, each spaced $\Delta f$ apart. The total occupied bandwidth is: $$ W_{ss} = N \cdot \Delta f $$ > **Logic:** Each hop slot occupies a narrow slice of spectrum, but the *union* of all slots across the entire hopping range forms the wideband spectrum. **Step 3 — Form the Processing Gain Ratio.** Processing gain is defined as the ratio of the spread bandwidth to the information bandwidth: $$ G_p = \frac{W_{ss}}{B_{info}} = \frac{N \cdot \Delta f}{R_b} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to Decibels (KTU Board Requirement).** KTU questions almost always expect the answer in dB. Apply the definition: $$ G_p (dB) = 10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{N \cdot \Delta f}{R_b} \right) $$ **Step 5 — Special Case for Bluetooth.** Bluetooth uses $N = 79$ channels, $\Delta f = 1$ MHz, $R_b = 1$ Mbps. Substituting: $$ G_p = \frac{79 \cdot 10^6}{10^6} = 79 $$ $$ G_p (dB) = 10 \log_{10}(79) \approx 18.97 \text{ dB} $$ > **Result:** Bluetooth achieves approximately **19 dB** of processing gain, which is the figure cited in the 802.15.1 standard. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Classification of Hopping Type from Numerical Data > [!TIP] > **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style, 7-mark sub-part):** **Problem:** A transmitter sends data at $R_b = 50$ kbps using FHSS with hop rate $R_h = 200$ hops/second. Each hop occupies $\Delta f = 500$ kHz, and the PN sequence uses $n = 5$ bits per hop. Classify the system and compute the processing gain. **Step 1 — Compute Bit Duration and Dwell Time.** $$ T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{50{,}000} = 20 \ \mu s $$ $$ T_d = \frac{1}{R_h} = \frac{1}{200} = 5 \ ms $$ **Step 2 — Compare to Classify.** $$ T_b = 20 \ \mu s \quad \text{vs} \quad T_d = 5 \ ms $$ Since $T_b \ll T_d$, multiple bits are transmitted during each dwell period. The number of bits per hop is: $$ k = \frac{T_d}{T_b} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 250 \text{ bits/hop} $$ Because $k \gg 1$, the system is classified as **Slow Frequency Hopping (SFH)**. **Step 3 — Compute Number of Channels.** $$ N = 2^n = 2^5 = 32 \text{ channels} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Total Spread Bandwidth.** $$ W_{ss} = N \cdot \Delta f = 32 \cdot 500 \text{ kHz} = 16 \text{ MHz} $$ **Step 5 — Compute Processing Gain.** $$ G_p = \frac{W_{ss}}{R_b} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{50 \times 10^3} = 320 $$ $$ G_p (dB) = 10 \log_{10}(320) \approx 25.05 \text{ dB} $$ > **Final Answer (Board Format):** > - **Hopping type:** Slow Frequency Hopping > - **Bits per hop:** $k = 250$ > - **Processing Gain:** $G_p = 320$ (or **25.05 dB**) --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Probability of Jammer Interference (Expected Value) **Problem:** A hostile jammer randomly selects **one** of the $N$ hopping channels to jam at any given instant. What is the probability that the jammer hits the *correct* channel during a single hop? **Step 1 — Total Outcomes.** The jammer has $N$ equiprobable choices. The total number of channel choices is $N$. **Step 2 — Favorable Outcomes.** Only **one** channel choice out of $N$ is the correct one being used by the transmitter during any given hop. **Step 3 — Probability of Hit.** $$ P_{hit} = \frac{\text{Favorable Outcomes}}{\text{Total Outcomes}} = \frac{1}{N} $$ **Step 4 — Generalization (Partial-Band Jamming).** If the jammer has enough power to jam $J$ channels simultaneously (with $J < N$): $$ P_{hit} = \frac{J}{N} $$ **Step 5 — Implication.** If $N = 79$ (Bluetooth), the jammer's success probability per hop is: $$ P_{hit} = \frac{1}{79} \approx 1.266\% $$ > **Conclusion:** With appropriate **Forward Error Correction (FEC)** and **interleaving**, the residual 1.27% packet error can be fully recovered — this is why Bluetooth is robust to in-band interference from microwave ovens. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Parameter | Slow Hopping (SFH) | Fast Hopping (FFH) | |-----------|-------------------|--------------------| | $R_h$ vs $R_b$ | $R_h \ll R_b$ | $R_h \gg R_b$ | | Bits per Hop | Many (e.g., 250) | Fractional (e.g., 0.1) | | Implementation Complexity | Low | High (fast synthesizer) | | Anti-Jamming Performance | Moderate | Superior | | Common Use | Bluetooth, GSM | Military ECCM radios | | Synchronization Burden | Lower | Higher | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 FHSS Transmitter–Receiver Block Diagram (Mermaid) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Insight:** A clean, fully-labeled block diagram carries **3 to 4 marks** outright. Always include the **PN generator**, **Frequency Synthesizer**, and **Synchronization Acquisition** block on the receiver side. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Source Data
Bits at Rate Rb] --> nodeB[BFSK or BPSK
Modulator] nodeC[PN Sequence
Generator n bits] --> nodeD[Frequency
Synthesizer] nodeB --> nodeE[Mixer with
Hopping Carrier] nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF[RF Power
Amplifier] nodeF --> nodeG((Antenna
Channel)) nodeG --> nodeH[RF Front End
LNA and Filter] nodeH --> nodeI[Mixer with
Local Hopping Carrier] nodeJ[Receiver PN
Generator Synced] --> nodeK[Local
Synthesizer] nodeK --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeL[BFSK or BPSK
Demodulator] nodeL --> nodeM[Recovered
Data Bits] nodeJ -.Acquisition.-> nodeL nodeH -.Sync Tracking.-> nodeJ ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **two parallel chains** — the data path (top) and the PN-driven hopping path (middle). The receiver's PN generator must be **synchronized** to the transmitter's PN sequence within one chip duration; otherwise, despreading fails. This is why acquisition and tracking loops are critical. --- ### 4.2 Time–Frequency Hopping Pattern (Conceptual Mermaid) > [!TIP] > **Visualizing "What FHSS looks like on a spectrogram."** The X-axis is time (slots $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$), the Y-axis is frequency. Each colored block is one hop. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph T1[Time Slot T1] f1[Frequency f17] end subgraph T2[Time Slot T2] f2[Frequency f03] end subgraph T3[Time Slot T3] f3[Frequency f45] end subgraph T4[Time Slot T4] f4[Frequency f22] end T1 -->|Hop 1| T2 T2 -->|Hop 2| T3 T3 -->|Hop 3| T4 T4 -->|Hop 4| T1 ``` > **Key Observation:** A **narrowband jammer** placed at any single frequency (e.g., $f_{03}$) will only corrupt one of the four slots above. The remaining three hops pass through unaffected. This is the visual essence of **anti-jam resilience**. --- ### 4.3 Slow vs Fast Hopping Visual Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SFH[Slow Hopping One Bit Stream Five Hops] s1[Bit 1-50 on fA] --> s2[Bit 51-100 on fB] --> s3[Bit 101-150 on fC] --> s4[Bit 151-200 on fD] --> s5[Bit 201-250 on fE] end subgraph FFH[Fast Hopping One Bit Span Five Hops] f5[Bit 1 on fP] --> f6[Bit 1 on fQ] --> f7[Bit 1 on fR] --> f8[Bit 1 on fS] --> f9[Bit 1 on fT] end ``` > **Reading the contrast:** In **SFH**, a single frequency carries *many* bits. In **FFH**, a single bit is fragmented across *many* frequencies. The latter is more jam-resistant but requires a **fast-switching synthesizer** (>1 GHz tuning speeds), which is hardware-expensive. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]** **Define Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS). Distinguish clearly between Slow FHSS and Fast FHSS.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** FHSS is a spread-spectrum technique in which the carrier frequency of the transmitted signal is switched (hopped) rapidly among multiple frequency channels using a pseudorandom sequence, spreading the signal energy over a wide bandwidth far larger than the information bandwidth. > > **Slow FHSS (0.75 marks):** The hop rate $R_h$ is much less than the bit rate $R_b$. Each frequency carries multiple data bits during its dwell time. > > **Fast FHSS (0.75 marks):** The hop rate $R_h$ is much greater than the bit rate $R_b$. The frequency changes multiple times during a single data bit. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is meant by Processing Gain in a spread-spectrum system? Why is a higher processing gain desirable?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Processing gain $G_p$ is the ratio of the spread (RF) bandwidth $W_{ss}$ to the information (baseband) bandwidth $R_b$, expressed as $G_p = W_{ss}/R_b$ (in ratio) or $G_p(dB) = 10 \log_{10}(W_{ss}/R_b)$. > > **Higher $G_p$ is desirable (1.5 marks):** A larger processing gain provides greater resistance to narrowband jamming (the jammer's effect is diluted), improves the signal-to-jammer ratio at the receiver, and lowers the probability of interception by unauthorized listeners. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do not** confuse the "bandwidth per hop" $\Delta f$ with the "total spread bandwidth" $W_{ss}$. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** if these are used interchangeably. > - **Always** state the hopping classification (SFH/FFH) *with numerical justification* — a statement like "it is slow hopping" without $T_b$ vs $T_d$ comparison is treated as incomplete. > - **Do not** write $G_p = W_{ss}/R_b$ without first defining both quantities. Definitions carry marks. > - For PN generators, **mention "synchronized"** explicitly. Forgetting synchronization loses 1 mark. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]** **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working principle of an FHSS transmitter and receiver. Discuss the role of the **PN sequence generator** and **frequency synthesizer** in the system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Bluetooth-like FHSS system uses $N = 79$ hopping channels, each of width $\Delta f = 1$ MHz, with a data rate of $R_b = 1$ Mbps.     (i) Compute the total spread bandwidth.     (ii) Calculate the processing gain in dB.     (iii) If the hop rate is 1600 hops/sec, determine the dwell time and classify the hopping type. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** 1. **Transmitter side (2.5 marks):** Source bits enter a **BFSK modulator**. The PN sequence generator (typically an $n$-bit maximal-length LFSR) drives a **frequency synthesizer** that selects one of $N$ carrier frequencies. The modulated signal is amplified and radiated. Block diagram should be drawn and labeled. 2. **Receiver side (2.5 marks):** The received RF is mixed with a **locally generated hopping carrier** produced by an identical PN generator + frequency synthesizer. The local PN generator is **synchronized** to the transmitter's PN code via an acquisition and tracking loop. After down-conversion, a BFSK demodulator recovers the baseband data. 3. **Role of PN generator (1 mark):** It produces the pseudorandom sequence that determines the hopping order. It must be *deterministic* (reproducible at receiver) yet appear *random* to an eavesdropper. 4. **Role of frequency synthesizer (1 mark):** It converts the digital PN code into an analog RF carrier at the selected frequency. The synthesizer's **tuning speed** determines whether the system can support slow or fast hopping. **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Total Spread Bandwidth (2 marks):** $$ W_{ss} = N \cdot \Delta f = 79 \times 1 \text{ MHz} = 79 \text{ MHz} $$ **(ii) Processing Gain in dB (3 marks):** $$ G_p = \frac{W_{ss}}{R_b} = \frac{79 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^6} = 79 $$ $$ G_p (dB) = 10 \log_{10}(79) = 10 \times 1.8976 \approx 18.98 \text{ dB} $$ **(iii) Dwell Time and Classification (2 marks):** $$ T_d = \frac{1}{R_h} = \frac{1}{1600} = 625 \ \mu s $$ $$ T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{10^6} = 1 \ \mu s $$ Since $T_b \ll T_d$, the system is **Slow Frequency Hopping**, with $k = T_d/T_b = 625$ bits per hop. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **Spread Spectrum** communication. List and briefly describe any **three advantages** of using spread-spectrum techniques over narrowband transmission. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An FHSS system uses a 7-bit PN sequence to determine the hopping pattern, with hop rate $R_h = 500$ hops/second and bit rate $R_b = 100$ kbps.     (i) Determine the number of hopping channels.     (ii) Calculate the bits-per-hop ratio and classify the hopping type.     (iii) If a partial-band jammer can jam 4 channels simultaneously, compute the probability that a randomly chosen hop is hit by the jammer. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** 1. **Concept of Spread Spectrum (3 marks):** Spread spectrum is a modulation technique in which the transmitted signal bandwidth $W_{ss}$ is deliberately made much larger than the information bandwidth $R_b$. The spreading is achieved using a pseudorandom code that is independent of the data. Two principal techniques are **FHSS** and **DSSS**. The despreading at the receiver (using a synchronized copy of the code) compresses the desired signal back to baseband while spreading any narrowband interference, thereby improving the Signal-to-Noise Ratio. 2. **Three Advantages (4 marks, ~1.33 each):** - **Anti-jamming:** A jammer must spread its power over the entire hopset to be effective, drastically reducing its spectral power density. - **Low Probability of Intercept (LPI):** The wideband low-power signal is hidden below the thermal noise floor of unauthorized receivers. - **Resistance to Multipath Fading:** A frequency-selective fade affects only a fraction of the hops; combined with FEC, the message is recovered. - **(Optional 4th)** Multiple Access: Different PN codes allow multiple users on the same band (CDMA). **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Number of Hopping Channels (2 marks):** $$ N = 2^7 = 128 \text{ channels} $$ **(ii) Bits-per-Hop and Classification (3 marks):** $$ T_b = \frac{1}{100{,}000} = 10 \ \mu s $$ $$ T_d = \frac{1}{500} = 2 \ ms $$ $$ k = \frac{T_d}{T_b} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 200 \text{ bits/hop} $$ Since $k = 200 \gg 1$, the system is **Slow Frequency Hopping (SFH)**. **(iii) Jammer Hit Probability (2 marks):** $$ P_{hit} = \frac{J}{N} = \frac{4}{128} = \frac{1}{32} = 0.03125 \approx 3.125\% $$ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam):** - ✅ **Definition:** FHSS = rapid switching of carrier frequency using a PN sequence. - ✅ **Two Types:** Slow Hopping (many bits per hop) and Fast Hopping (many hops per bit). - ✅ **Classification Rule:** $R_h \ll R_b$ → SFH; $R_h \gg R_b$ → FFH. - ✅ **Dwell Time:** $T_d = 1/R_h$ — memorize this formula. - ✅ **Processing Gain:** $G_p = W_{ss}/R_b$, in dB is $10 \log_{10}(W_{ss}/R_b)$. - ✅ **Total Bandwidth:** $W_{ss} = N \cdot \Delta f$ — do not confuse with $\Delta f$. - ✅ **Number of Channels:** $N = 2^n$ where $n$ = PN generator length. - ✅ **PN Generator:** Must be *synchronized* at receiver for despreading. - ✅ **Frequency Synthesizer:** Tuning speed dictates SFH vs FFH capability. - ✅ **Bluetooth Numbers to Memorize:** $N = 79$, $\Delta f = 1$ MHz, $R_h = 1600$ hops/sec, $G_p \approx 19$ dB. - ✅ **Jammer Hit Probability:** $P_{hit} = 1/N$ for a single-channel jammer. - ✅ **Four Pillars:** Anti-jamming, LPI, Multipath resistance, Multiple access. - ✅ **Examiner's Pet Trap:** Always state the *justification* (numbers) for SFH/FFH classification. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum**

topics.title: Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)** is a digital modulation and spread spectrum technique in which each information bit of the original baseband signal is mathematically multiplied by a high-rate pseudo-random binary sequence (called the **chipping code** or **PN sequence**). This operation replaces every single data bit with a rapid sequence of smaller-duration pulses known as **chips**, thereby expanding (spreading) the signal energy across a much wider transmission bandwidth than is strictly required by the underlying data rate.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, DSSS is formally defined as:

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> *Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum is a modulation technique where the data signal $d(t)$ is multiplied element-wise by a pseudo-noise (PN) code sequence $c(t)$ having a chip rate $R_c$ significantly greater than the bit rate $R_b$. The resulting transmitted signal $s(t) = d(t) \cdot c(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ occupies a bandwidth $W \approx R_c$, which is $N$ times wider than the original data bandwidth, where $N = R_c / R_b$ is defined as the **processing gain**.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Whispered Conversation in a Loud Room

Imagine two friends trying to share a secret in a noisy, crowded cafeteria. Instead of one friend whispering the entire message in one go (which is easily overheard by eavesdroppers), they agree on a secret codebook in advance:

1. Each letter of the secret message is replaced by a **long sequence of unrelated words** (e.g., the letter "A" becomes the word sequence "banana-tiger-cloud-river-mountain...").
2. The eavesdropper, not knowing the codebook, hears only an apparently meaningless stream of words.
3. The intended receiver, who holds the same codebook, **collapses** every long word sequence back into its single original letter.

In DSSS, the **secret message** is the actual data bit (1 or 0), the **codebook** is the PN sequence, and the **long sequence of unrelated words** is the chip stream. The eavesdropper corresponds to a narrowband jammer or an unauthorized listener — because the signal power has been spread thinly across a huge bandwidth, the jammer's interference appears as just a tiny ripple in the ocean of chips, easily rejected by the receiver.

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Deployment of DSSS**
> - **GPS (Global Positioning System)** — every satellite transmits its data using a unique 1023-chip Gold code (C/A code at 1.023 Mchip/s).
> - **CDMA2000 / IS-95 / W-CDMA cellular networks** — each mobile user shares the same RF band but is uniquely identified by an orthogonal PN code.
> - **IEEE 802.11b Wi-Fi** — uses an 11-chip Barker sequence.
> - **DECT cordless phones**, **3G UMTS**, and **Bluetooth (FHSS variant)** also rely on spread spectrum principles.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Any Spread Spectrum System

DSSS achieves its robustness because it simultaneously satisfies three classic engineering conditions:

| Pillar | Name | Engineering Purpose |
|--------|------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Signal spreading at transmitter** | Hide signal below noise floor of unintended listeners |
| 2 | **Pseudo-random code selection** | Make signal appear noise-like to non-authorized receivers |
| 3 | **Correlation-based despreading at receiver** | Recover original data while rejecting interference |

These pillars yield three measurable benefits that KTU examiners love to test:
- **Anti-jamming capability** (resistance to intentional interference)
- **Low Probability of Intercept (LPI)** (signal is hidden in noise)
- **Multiple Access Capability (CDMA)** (many users on one frequency)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Logic — Transmitter Path

The DSSS transmitter performs the following logical pipeline:

1. **Source Encoding** — Raw information bits $b_k \in \{+1, -1\}$ are produced at bit rate $R_b$ bits/second with bit duration $T_b = 1/R_b$.
2. **PN Sequence Generation** — A pseudo-random binary sequence $c_n \in \{+1, -1\}$ is generated at chip rate $R_c$ chips/second, where chip duration $T_c = 1/R_c$ and $R_c \gg R_b$.
3. **Multiplication (Spreading)** — Each bit is expanded by replacing it with $N$ chips. Mathematically, $s(t) = b(t) \cdot c(t)$, where $b(t)$ is the bipolar NRZ data waveform and $c(t)$ is the bipolar NRZ chip waveform.
4. **Carrier Modulation** — The spread baseband signal modulates a carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ using BPSK, producing $s(t) = b(t) \cdot c(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
5. **Power Amplification and Radiation** — The wideband signal is amplified and transmitted by an antenna. Final RF bandwidth is approximately $W \approx 2 R_c$ (Nyquist equivalent), which is $N$ times wider than the original data bandwidth.

## 2.2 Operational Block Logic — Receiver Path (Despreading)

1. **RF Front-End Reception** — The receiver captures $r(t) = s(t) + n(t) + J(t)$ where $n(t)$ is thermal noise and $J(t)$ is interference/jamming.
2. **Carrier Demodulation (Coherent BPSK Detection)** — The signal is multiplied by a locally generated $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and low-pass filtered.
3. **Despreading via Local PN Replica** — The recovered baseband is multiplied again by a **synchronized** local replica of the PN code $c(t)$ identical to the transmitter's. Since $c(t) \cdot c(t) = 1$ (because $c_i^2 = 1$ for every chip), the data is recovered.
4. **Integrate-and-Dump / Matched Filtering** — A correlator accumulates the chips over each bit period $T_b$, producing a decision variable compared to a threshold.
5. **Bit Decision** — Hard decision yields the recovered bit $\hat{b}_k$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Core "Why" of DSSS**
> Interference and jamming occupy only a **fraction** of the wide RF bandwidth. When the receiver despreads the signal back to its original narrow bandwidth, **all narrowband interference energy is spread by the same factor $N$**, while the desired signal energy is fully collected. Hence the **post-despread Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR)** improves by exactly $N$ times.

## 2.3 The Three Key Ratios — KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **KTU High-Yield Formula Table**

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Bit rate** | $R_b$ | $R_b = 1/T_b$ | bits/s | Information throughput |
| 2 | **Chip rate** | $R_c$ | $R_c = 1/T_c$ | chips/s | Rate of PN sequence |
| 3 | **Processing Gain** | $G_p$ | $G_p = \dfrac{R_c}{R_b} = \dfrac{W}{R_b} = N$ | dimensionless (dB: $10\log_{10}G_p$) | Spreading factor / SNR improvement |
| 4 | **Spread Bandwidth** | $W$ | $W \approx 2R_c$ | Hz | RF transmission bandwidth |
| 5 | **Energy per bit** | $E_b$ | $E_b = P_s T_b$ | Joules | Useful signal energy |
| 6 | **Energy per chip** | $E_c$ | $E_c = P_s T_c$ | Joules | Energy of one chip |
| 7 | **Relation** | — | $E_b = N \cdot E_c$ | — | Total bit energy = sum of chip energies |
| 8 | **Jamming Margin** | $M_j$ | $M_j = \dfrac{G_p}{\left(\dfrac{E_b}{N_J}\right)_{req}}$ | dB | Tolerable jamming power above signal |
| 9 | **Post-despread SIR** | $\left(\dfrac{S}{I}\right)_{out}$ | $\left(\dfrac{S}{I}\right)_{out} = G_p \cdot \left(\dfrac{S}{I}\right)_{in}$ | — | Interference suppression factor |
| 10 | **Probability of Error (BPSK)** | $P_e$ | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2\,\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | — | Unchanged by spreading itself |

**Critical clarification for KTU:** Spreading does **not** change the energy-per-bit-to-noise-spectral-density ratio $E_b/N_0$ in pure AWGN. Spreading *only* helps against **narrowband or partial-band interference**, not against white Gaussian noise whose PSD spans the entire band.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Utility of DSSS |
|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Military Communications** | Anti-jamming for tactical radios (e.g., SINCGARS, HAVE QUICK) |
| **Civilian GPS** | Code Division Multiple Access across 32 satellites on same L1/L2 frequency |
| **3G/4G Cellular (CDMA2000, WCDMA)** | Soft capacity, soft handover, frequency reuse = 1 |
| **Wi-Fi 802.11b** | 11-chip Barker code gives processing gain ≈ **10.4 dB** |
| **Radar and ranging** | Resolution and penetration through clutter |
| **Sensor networks / IoT** | Coexistence of multiple low-power devices on ISM band |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Processing Gain Identity

**Goal:** Prove that $G_p = R_c / R_b = W / R_b = N$.

### Step 1 — Define the bit duration and chip duration
The bit duration is the time occupied by one information bit:

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{R_b}
$$

The chip duration is the time occupied by one PN chip:

$$
T_c = \frac{1}{R_c}
$$

Because the spreading rule assigns exactly $N$ chips to every data bit, the bit duration contains $N$ chip durations:

$$
T_b = N \cdot T_c
$$

### Step 2 — Express $N$ in terms of rates
Taking the reciprocal of both sides of the above:

$$
\frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{1}{N \cdot T_c} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad R_b = \frac{R_c}{N}
$$

Solving for $N$ yields the first identity:

$$
N = \frac{R_c}{R_b}
$$

### Step 3 — Use the Nyquist bandwidth relation
For a baseband signal with symbol/chip rate $R_c$, the minimum Nyquist bandwidth is:

$$
W = \frac{R_c}{2} \cdot 2 = R_c \quad \text{(for full-duty rectangular chips)}
$$

Equivalently, for BPSK modulation the transmitted RF bandwidth equals:

$$
W \approx 2 R_c
$$

The original (unspread) data bandwidth equals $R_b$ (or $2 R_b$ in passband). Hence the bandwidth expansion ratio is:

$$
G_p = \frac{W}{R_b}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine identities
Substituting $W \approx R_c$ into the previous expression:

$$
G_p = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = N
$$

In decibel form:

$$
G_p \,\text{[dB]} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_c}{R_b}\right)
$$

**Conclusion:** All three representations — chips-per-bit, ratio of rates, and ratio of bandwidths — are equivalent measures of the same physical phenomenon: signal energy spreading.

## 3.2 Derivation of Jamming Margin

**Given:** Required minimum $E_b / N_J$ at the receiver to achieve a target BER of $10^{-5}$ (for BPSK this is approximately $9.6$ dB).

**Goal:** Derive the tolerable Jamming-to-Signal power ratio.

### Step 1 — Express jamming spectral density
Total jamming power $P_J$ is concentrated over a bandwidth $W_J$. The equivalent jamming spectral density is:

$$
N_J = \frac{P_J}{W_J}
$$

### Step 2 — Receiver sees only a fraction of jamming after despreading
The despreader narrows the effective bandwidth from $W$ back to $R_b$. Therefore, only the jamming power within the narrow band $R_b$ survives:

$$
P_J^{eff} = N_J \cdot R_b = \frac{P_J}{W_J} \cdot R_b
$$

### Step 3 — Form the ratio $E_b / N_J$
The energy per bit is $E_b = P_s T_b = P_s / R_b$. So:

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_J} = \frac{P_s / R_b}{P_J / W_J} = \frac{P_s}{P_J} \cdot \frac{W_J}{R_b}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for tolerable J/S ratio

$$
\frac{P_J}{P_s} = \frac{W_J / R_b}{E_b / N_J}
$$

### Step 5 — Define jamming margin
The jamming margin (in dB) is defined as the J/S power ratio (in dB) that the system can tolerate while still maintaining the required $E_b / N_J$:

$$
M_j \,\text{[dB]} = G_p \,\text{[dB]} - \left(\frac{E_b}{N_J}\right)_{req} \,\text{[dB]}
$$

where the required $(E_b/N_J)_{req}$ comes from the target BER curve of BPSK.

**Example:** For $G_p = 30$ dB and a required $E_b/N_J = 10$ dB, the jamming margin is $M_j = 20$ dB — meaning the jammer can be up to **100 times** more powerful than the signal before the link fails.

## 3.3 Derivation of Post-Despread SNR Improvement

### Step 1 — Input SIR at the receiver
Let $S$ denote signal power and $I$ denote total in-band interference power before despreading:

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{in}
$$

### Step 2 — Despreading operation
After correlation with the local PN code, the desired signal is collapsed to bandwidth $R_b$. The interference, being uncorrelated with the PN code, is spread over bandwidth $W$. Only a fraction $R_b / W$ of its power enters the decision bandwidth.

### Step 3 — Output SIR

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{out} = \frac{S}{I \cdot (R_b / W)} = \frac{S}{I} \cdot \frac{W}{R_b} = G_p \cdot \left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{in}
$$

This is the central result: **DSSS provides exactly $G_p$ times SIR improvement** against narrowband interference.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A DSSS system uses a PN sequence of length **127 chips per bit**. The data rate is $R_b = 9.6$ kbps. Find (a) the chip rate, (b) the spread bandwidth, (c) the processing gain in dB, and (d) the jamming margin if the required $E_b / N_J$ for $10^{-5}$ BER is 9.6 dB.

### Part (a) — Chip rate
Given $N = 127$ and $R_b = 9.6 \times 10^3$ bits/s:

$$
R_c = N \cdot R_b = 127 \times 9.6 \times 10^{3} = 1.2192 \times 10^{6} \text{ chips/s} \approx 1.22 \text{ Mchip/s}
$$

### Part (b) — Spread bandwidth
Assuming passband BPSK:

$$
W \approx 2 R_c = 2 \times 1.2192 \times 10^{6} = 2.4384 \text{ MHz} \approx 2.44 \text{ MHz}
$$

### Part (c) — Processing gain in dB

$$
G_p \,\text{[dB]} = 10 \log_{10}(127) \approx 21.04 \text{ dB}
$$

### Part (d) — Jamming margin

$$
M_j = G_p \,\text{[dB]} - \left(\frac{E_b}{N_J}\right)_{req} = 21.04 - 9.6 = 11.44 \text{ dB}
$$

The jammer can be **approximately 13.9 times** the signal power while still maintaining $10^{-5}$ BER.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DSSS vs. Narrowband vs. FHSS

| Parameter | Narrowband System | DSSS | FHSS |
|-----------|-------------------|------|------|
| Bandwidth usage | Minimum ($R_b$) | $N R_b$ | $N \times$ hop slots |
| Resistance to jamming | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Resistance to multipath fading | Poor | Excellent (RAKE receiver) | Good |
| Synchronization complexity | Low | High (chip-level) | Medium (hop-level) |
| Power spectral density | High (easily detected) | Low (hidden in noise) | Low |
| Multiple access method | TDMA / FDMA | CDMA | CDMA / TDMA hybrid |
| Example system | AMPS cellular | GPS, CDMA, 802.11b | Bluetooth, older military radios |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DSSS Transmitter Block Diagram

The following Mermaid flow diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a DSSS transmitter. Because DSSS is a continuous-time signal-processing concept rather than a discrete software routine, the block diagram emphasizes mathematical signal-flow stages rather than physical wiring.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Data Source Bits b_k +1 or -1"] --> B["NRZ Encoder Bipolar Waveform b of t"]
    C["PN Code Generator Chip Rate R_c"] --> D["NRZ Encoder Bipolar Chip Waveform c of t"]
    B --> E["Multiplier Spreading Stage"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Product s_baseband equals b of t times c of t"]
    F --> G["BPSK Modulator Multiplier with Carrier"]
    H["Local Oscillator cos 2 pi f_c t"] --> G
    G --> I["Power Amplifier and RF Front End"]
    I --> J["Transmit Antenna Wideband RF Signal s of t"]
```

### Description of Each Block (mapped to mermaid nodes)

1. **node A — Data Source:** Produces raw bits $b_k$ at rate $R_b$. For KTU analysis we assume a binary symmetric source with equiprobable +1 and −1.
2. **node B — NRZ Encoder:** Converts unipolar $\{0,1\}$ bits into bipolar $\{-1,+1\}$ waveform $b(t)$ with rectangular pulse shaping. This is essential because multiplication requires bipolar signalling.
3. **node C — PN Code Generator:** A Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) of degree $m$ produces a maximal-length sequence of period $2^m - 1$ chips. The chips are binary $\{-1,+1\}$ with near-zero autocorrelation for non-zero shifts.
4. **node D — NRZ Encoder:** Same conversion applied to the chip stream so $c(t)$ is bipolar.
5. **node E — Multiplier:** Performs time-domain product $b(t) \cdot c(t)$. Each bit is replaced by $N$ chips; this is the **spreading operation**.
6. **node F — Baseband Spread Signal:** Output $s_{bb}(t)$ has chip rate $R_c$ but still carries one bit's worth of information over $T_b = N T_c$.
7. **node G — BPSK Modulator:** Multiplies the spread baseband with the carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ to shift the spectrum to the RF band.
8. **node H — Local Oscillator:** Generates the carrier reference.
9. **node I — Power Amplifier & RF Front-End:** Amplifies the wideband signal to the required transmit power. Must have bandwidth $\geq 2 R_c$.
10. **node J — Antenna:** Radiates the spread spectrum signal whose power spectral density is approximately uniform over $W$.

## 4.2 DSSS Receiver (Despreader) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Receive Antenna Captured Signal r of t"] --> B["LNA and RF Front End"]
    B --> C["BPSK Demodulator Multiplier with Cosine Carrier"]
    D["Local Oscillator cos 2 pi f_c t"] --> C
    C --> E["Low Pass Filter to Extract Baseband"]
    E --> F["Despreading Multiplier Multiply by Local PN Code"]
    G["Synchronized Local PN Generator c_hat of t"] --> F
    F --> H["Integrate and Dump Correlator over T_b"]
    H --> I["Threshold Detector Decide bit +1 or -1"]
    I --> J["Recovered Data Stream b_hat_k"]
```

### Description of Receiver Stages

1. **node A — Receive Antenna:** Captures $r(t) = s(t) + n(t) + J(t)$ containing the desired spread signal, AWGN, and any narrowband interference.
2. **node B — LNA and RF Front-End:** Amplifies the wideband input.
3. **node C — BPSK Demodulator:** Coherent multiplication with local carrier — requires **carrier synchronization** (PLL or Costas loop).
4. **node D — Local Oscillator:** Must be phase-locked to the incoming carrier.
5. **node E — Low-Pass Filter:** Removes double-frequency components, leaving the spread baseband $b(t) \cdot c(t)$.
6. **node F — Despreading Multiplier:** The cornerstone of the receiver — multiplies the baseband by a synchronized local replica $c_{\text{hat}}(t)$. For the desired signal $c(t) \cdot c(t) = 1$ (perfect correlation), so the data is recovered. For uncorrelated interference $J(t) \cdot c(t)$ remains spread-spectrum noise.
7. **node G — Synchronized Local PN Generator:** The receiver's PN code must be **chip-aligned** with the incoming code; this is achieved by a **code-acquisition** stage (sliding correlator or matched filter) followed by **code-tracking** (delay-locked loop).
8. **node H — Integrate-and-Dump:** Accumulates the despread signal over $T_b = N T_c$. This is mathematically equivalent to a matched filter for rectangular chips.
9. **node I — Threshold Detector:** Compares correlator output to zero; sign yields the bit decision.
10. **node J — Recovered Data:** Bit stream $\hat{b}_k$ identical to the transmitted $b_k$ (with high probability).

## 4.3 Spectral Spreading Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Data Spectrum Narrow Peak around f_c Bandwidth R_b"] --> B["Multiplication by PN Code in Time Domain equals Convolution in Frequency Domain"]
    C["PN Code Spectrum sinc shaped with First Null at R_c"] --> B
    B --> D["Transmitted DSSS Spectrum Wide Sinc Envelope Bandwidth W equals 2 R_c"]
    D --> E["At Receiver Despreading by Local PN collapses Spectrum back to Narrow Peak"]
    E --> F["Recovered Data Spectrum Narrow Peak at f_c"]
```

This diagram captures the central conceptual insight: **multiplication in the time domain equals convolution (spectral spreading) in the frequency domain**. The narrow spectrum of the data is replaced by the much wider sinc-shaped spectrum of the PN code. The reverse process at the receiver collapses it back.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum. What is meant by the term "processing gain" in a DSSS system?**

**Model Answer:**

Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS) is a modulation technique in which each information bit is multiplied by a high-rate pseudo-random binary sequence (the PN code or chipping code), replacing every data bit with $N$ chips of much shorter duration. This causes the transmitted RF bandwidth $W$ to expand by a factor of $N$ relative to the original data bandwidth.

**Processing gain** $G_p$ is defined as the ratio of the spread bandwidth to the original data bandwidth:

$$
G_p = \frac{W}{R_b} = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = N
$$

It expresses the SNR improvement (or interference suppression) provided by the despreading operation at the receiver. For example, a system with 127 chips per bit has $G_p = 127 \approx 21$ dB.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any four advantages of DSSS over conventional narrowband communication.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Resistance to narrowband jamming** — A jammer's power is spread over the wide DSSS bandwidth, reducing its effective in-band impact by the processing gain.
2. **Low Probability of Intercept (LPI)** — The transmitted signal's power spectral density lies below the noise floor, making eavesdropping difficult.
3. **Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)** — Multiple users can share the same RF band using orthogonal or quasi-orthogonal PN codes.
4. **Robustness to multipath fading** — Multipath components separated by more than one chip duration appear as uncorrelated noise and are rejected by the correlator; resolvable multipath can even be combined coherently using a RAKE receiver.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) With the aid of a block diagram, explain the operation of a Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum transmitter and receiver. Briefly describe the role of the PN sequence in the system. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description:**
A DSSS transmitter consists of a data source feeding an NRZ encoder, a PN code generator (typically a maximal-length LFSR) feeding a second NRZ encoder, a multiplier that performs the spreading operation, a BPSK modulator with carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$, and a power amplifier feeding the antenna. The receiver reverses the process with a BPSK demodulator, a synchronized local PN generator, a despreading multiplier, an integrate-and-dump correlator, and a threshold detector.

**Operation:**
At the transmitter, each data bit $b_k$ (duration $T_b$) is multiplied by a PN sequence $c(t)$ with chip duration $T_c = T_b / N$. This replaces every bit by $N$ chips, expanding the spectrum by factor $N$. At the receiver, the same locally generated PN code, synchronized in time, is multiplied with the received baseband. Since $c_i^2 = 1$, the desired signal collapses back to its original bandwidth, while any narrowband interference gets spread by the same factor and is largely rejected by the post-correlation filter.

**Role of the PN Sequence:**
- Provides the secret codebook that allows authorized receivers to despread.
- Determines the chip rate $R_c$ and hence the spread bandwidth $W$.
- Provides orthogonality between users in CDMA.
- Must have excellent autocorrelation properties for synchronization and very low cross-correlation between different users.

**(b) A DSSS system transmits data at $R_b = 19.2$ kbps using a PN sequence of length 255 chips per bit. Calculate the chip rate, the spread bandwidth, the processing gain in dB, and the jamming margin assuming a required $E_b / N_J$ of 10 dB for the target BER. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $R_b = 19.2 \times 10^3$ bits/s, $N = 255$, $(E_b / N_J)_{req} = 10$ dB.

**Step 1 — Chip rate:**

$$
R_c = N \cdot R_b = 255 \times 19.2 \times 10^3 = 4.896 \times 10^6 \text{ chips/s} \approx 4.896 \text{ Mchip/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Spread bandwidth (BPSK):**

$$
W = 2 R_c = 2 \times 4.896 \times 10^6 = 9.792 \text{ MHz} \approx 9.8 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Processing gain in dB:**

$$
G_p \,\text{[dB]} = 10 \log_{10}(255) \approx 24.07 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Jamming margin:**

$$
M_j = G_p - \left(\frac{E_b}{N_J}\right)_{req} = 24.07 - 10 = 14.07 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
A jammer can be up to **approximately 25.5 times** ($10^{14.07/10}$) the power of the desired signal before the link's BER exceeds the target threshold. This demonstrates DSSS's strong anti-jamming capability.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Explain the concept of "spreading" and "despreading" in a DSSS system. How does the despreading operation provide immunity against narrowband interference? Derive the expression for the post-despread signal-to-interference ratio. (7 Marks)**
