## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Define Circuit Switching. Mention two of its disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**

**Circuit Switching** is a switching technique in which a *dedicated, end-to-end physical communication path* is established between the sender and receiver *before* actual data transfer begins, and this path remains reserved exclusively for the entire duration of the communication session. The path is released only after explicit teardown.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Inefficient utilization of bandwidth** — In bursty data traffic (e.g., web browsing, file transfer), the dedicated channel remains idle during gaps, wasting valuable bandwidth.
2. **Long call setup delay** — Establishing a dedicated end-to-end path through multiple switches adds significant initial latency (typically 200 ms to several seconds) before any user data can flow.

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning PSTN as the classic example adds extra marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Differentiate between Datagram Packet Switching and Virtual Circuit Packet Switching.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Datagram Packet Switching | Virtual Circuit Packet Switching |
|---|---|---|
| **Connection Setup** | Not required (connectionless) | Required before data transfer |
| **Routing** | Each packet routed independently | All packets follow the same pre-established logical path |
| **Addressing in Packets** | Full source & destination address in every packet | Only a small Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI) in each packet |
| **Packet Order** | May arrive out of order | Always arrive in order |
| **Failure Recovery** | Easy — router picks another path | Difficult — new VC must be set up |
| **Example** | Internet (IP) | X.25, Frame Relay, ATM |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option for Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the three phases of communication in **Circuit Switching** with a suitable diagram. Compare Circuit Switching with Packet Switching on any **five** parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **3 Mbps** link is used to transmit a **2.4 Mbit** message. The message is split into **60 packets** of equal size. The path consists of **4 hops** (i.e., 5 links). Propagation delay per link is **12 ms**, and switching/queueing delays are negligible. Compute the total transfer time if the network uses:
- (i) Packet Switching
- (ii) Message Switching
- (iii) Circuit Switching (assume T_setup = 0.4 s, T_teardown = 0)

**Compare** the results and comment. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **three phases** of Circuit Switching are:

1. **Circuit Establishment (Setup Phase):** The source sends a connection-request signal to the destination via intermediate switches. Each switch on the path reserves a dedicated physical channel (or timeslot in TDM). Once the destination acknowledges, the circuit is established.

2. **Data Transfer Phase:** Information flows along the dedicated, continuously-connected path. The full bandwidth is reserved exclusively for the session — no contention with other users. No addressing is needed inside data units.

3. **Circuit Disconnect (Teardown Phase):** After communication completes, either end sends a teardown signal. All switches release the reserved channels, making them available for other calls.

**Comparison with Packet Switching (5 parameters):**

| # | Parameter | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Path | Fixed, dedicated | Variable per packet (datagram) or logical fixed (VC) |
| 2 | Resource Reservation | Yes (physical channel) | No (statistical multiplexing) |
| 3 | Delay | Predictable, low jitter | Variable (queueing delays) |
| 4 | Bandwidth Utilization | Poor for bursty data | Excellent (shared resources) |
| 5 | Failure Resilience | Low (call drops) | High (re-routing) |

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- M = 2.4 × 10⁶ bits
- N = 60 packets
- L = M ÷ N = 2.4 × 10⁶ ÷ 60 = 40,000 bits = 4 × 10⁴ bits
- H = 4 hops → 5 links
- B = 3 × 10⁶ bps
- d_p = 12 ms = 0.012 s

**Per-packet transmission time:**

$$
\frac{L}{B} = \frac{4 \times 10^4}{3 \times 10^6} = 0.0133 \text{ s} \approx 13.33 \text{ ms}
$$

**(i) Packet Switching:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ps} &= (N-1) \cdot \frac{L}{B} + (H+1) \cdot \left( \frac{L}{B} + d_p \right) \\
T_{ps} &= (60-1) \cdot 0.0133 + 5 \cdot (0.0133 + 0.012) \\
T_{ps} &= 59 \cdot 0.0133 + 5 \cdot 0.0253 \\
T_{ps} &= 0.7847 + 0.1267 \\
T_{ps} &= 0.9114 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Message Switching:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{M}{B} &= \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{3 \times 10^6} = 0.8 \text{ s} \\
T_{msg} &= (H+1) \cdot \left( \frac{M}{B} + d_p \right) \\
T_{msg} &= 5 \cdot (0.8 + 0.012) \\
T_{msg} &= 5 \cdot 0.812 \\
T_{msg} &= 4.06 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Circuit Switching:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cs} &= T_{setup} + \frac{M}{B} + (H+1) \cdot d_p \\
T_{cs} &= 0.4 + 0.8 + 5 \cdot 0.012 \\
T_{cs} &= 0.4 + 0.8 + 0.06 \\
T_{cs} &= 1.26 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Technique | Total Time |
|---|---|
| Packet Switching | **0.9114 s** ✅ (Fastest) |
| Circuit Switching | 1.26 s |
| Message Switching | 4.06 s ❌ (Slowest) |

**Comment:** Packet Switching outperforms the others due to **pipelining** — while the first packet is being forwarded through intermediate hops, subsequent packets are concurrently being transmitted by the source, achieving a near-optimal flow of data.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice Option**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain **Virtual Circuit (VC) Packet Switching**. How does it differ from a pure **datagram** approach? List any **two real-world protocols/technologies** that use each approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A network uses **packet switching** with the following parameters: total message size **M = 8 Mbits**, packet size **L = 4 kbits**, link bandwidth **B = 2 Mbps**, and the path traverses **6 intermediate switches (H = 6)**. Propagation delay per link is **5 ms**.
- (i) How many packets are generated?
- (ii) Compute the total time to deliver the entire message.
- (iii) If **switching delay (d_sw) = 2 ms per packet per switch** is also considered, what is the new total time?
**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Virtual Circuit (VC) Packet Switching — Diagram Explanation:**

A *Virtual Circuit* is a **logical, pre-established path** between source and destination through a packet-switched network. It is *logical* (not physical) because:
- A dedicated physical channel is **not** reserved
- The path is identified by a small **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** carried in each packet's header
- The actual physical links may be shared with other VCs

**VC has two sub-types:**
- **SVC (Switched Virtual Circuit):** Established dynamically per call (like a phone call)
- **PVC (Permanent Virtual Circuit):** Manually configured by the network administrator (always on)

**Working — Three Phases:**
1. **Setup:** Source sends a CALL REQUEST to destination. Switches along the path reserve a unique VCI for this connection. Destination replies with CALL ACCEPT.
2. **Data Transfer:** All packets carry the same VCI. Each switch uses the VCI to identify the next link (lookup in a VC table).
3. **Teardown:** Either side sends CLEAR REQUEST. Switches delete VCI entries.

**Differences from Datagram:**

| Parameter | Virtual Circuit | Datagram |
|---|---|---|
| Setup needed? | Yes | No |
| Address size in packet | Small VCI (e.g., 12–24 bits) | Full source & destination (e.g., 32-bit IP) |
| Routing decision | Once (at setup) | Per packet |
| Packets follow same route? | Yes | No (independent) |
| State in switches? | Yes (VC tables) | No (stateless forwarding) |
| Out-of-order delivery? | No | Possible |
| Overhead per packet | Lower | Higher |

**Real-World Examples:**
- **Virtual Circuit:** X.25, Frame Relay, ATM, MPLS
- **Datagram:** Internet Protocol (IPv4/IPv6), UDP, ICMP

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- M = 8 × 10⁶ bits
- L = 4 × 10³ bits (4 kbits)
- B = 2 × 10⁶ bps (2 Mbps)
- H = 6 intermediate switches → **7 links** total
- d_p = 5 ms = 0.005 s

**(i) Number of packets generated:**

$$
N = \frac{M}{L} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{4 \times 10^3} = 2000 \text{ packets}
$$

**(ii) Total time (without switching delay):**

Per-packet transmission time:

$$
\frac{L}{B} = \frac{4 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.002 \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ps} &= (N-1) \cdot \frac{L}{B} + (H+1) \cdot \left( \frac{L}{B} + d_p \right) \\
T_{ps} &= (2000-1) \cdot 0.002 + 7 \cdot (0.002 + 0.005) \\
T_{ps} &= 1999 \cdot 0.002 + 7 \cdot 0.007 \\
T_{ps} &= 3.998 + 0.049 \\
T_{ps} &= 4.047 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total time (with switching delay d_sw = 2 ms = 0.002 s per switch):**

Each packet, when passing through all 6 switches, accumulates an additional delay of:

$$
d_{sw\_total} = H \cdot d_{sw} = 6 \cdot 0.002 = 0.012 \text{ s}
$$

The corrected per-packet traversal time becomes:

$$
T_{per\_packet} = (H+1) \cdot \left( \frac{L}{B} + d_p \right) + H \cdot d_{sw}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{per\_packet} &= 7 \cdot (0.002 + 0.005) + 6 \cdot 0.002 \\
&= 7 \cdot 0.007 + 0.012 \\
&= 0.049 + 0.012 \\
&= 0.061 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{ps\_new} = (N-1) \cdot \frac{L}{B} + T_{per\_packet} = 3.998 + 0.061 = 4.059 \text{ seconds}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the (H+1) factor:** KTU students often use *H* instead of *H+1* (since H intermediate switches means H+1 links). This is a **2-mark error** in board papers.
> 2. **Mixing message switching formula with packet switching:** Always use $(N-1) \cdot (L/B) + \text{one-packet delay}$ for packet switching — **never** $(M/B)$.
> 3. **Skipping propagation delay in numericals:** Many KTU problems mention d_p = some value; missing it costs full marks on the "setup-correct" part.
> 4. **Confusing Virtual Circuit with Circuit Switching:** VC still uses **statistical multiplexing** — the link is *not* reserved physically. Write the difference explicitly.
> 5. **Not drawing the store-and-forward pipeline** in diagrams for 7-mark answers — KTU examiners give 1–2 marks for labeled diagrams.
> 6. **Using approximate values without units:** Always write units (s, ms, kbps) — KTU deducts marks for unit-less numericals.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 Board Exams — Last-Minute Revision**

- [x] **Circuit Switching** → dedicated path, 3 phases, best for voice, low efficiency for bursty data
- [x] **Message Switching** → store-and-forward whole message, **no pipelining**, very high delay
- [x] **Packet Switching (Datagram)** → connectionless, independent routing, no reserved path
- [x] **Packet Switching (VC)** → connection-oriented, fixed logical path, VCI in header
- [x] **Master Formula:** $T_{ps} = (N-1) \cdot (L/B) + (H+1) \cdot (L/B + d_p)$
- [x] **Master Formula:** $T_{msg} = (H+1) \cdot (M/B + d_p)$ — note **M, not L**
- [x] **Master Formula:** $T_{cs} = T_{setup} + M/B + (H+1) \cdot d_p + T_{teardown}$
- [x] **Pipelining effect** is what makes packet switching fastest
- [x] **H intermediate switches = H+1 links** (most common KTU trap)
- [x] **N = M/L** (must be integer for clean numericals)
- [x] **Store-and-Forward** applies to Message AND Packet switching
- [x] **PSTN** = canonical Circuit Switching example
- [x] **IP/Internet** = canonical Datagram example
- [x] **X.25, Frame Relay, ATM** = canonical Virtual Circuit examples
- [x] **ATM cell size** = 53 bytes (48 payload + 5 header) — small fixed cells for low jitter
- [x] **Datagram** uses full addressing → larger header overhead per packet
- [x] **VC** uses VCI → smaller header, but switch must maintain VC tables (stateful)
- [x] **Comparison table** with at least 5 parameters is **must-draw** for 7-mark questions
- [x] **SVC vs PVC** — SVC is dynamic; PVC is permanent/manual
- [x] **Throughput η = Useful Data / Total Time** — units in bps
- [x] **Delay-Bandwidth Product (DBP)** — volume of data a link can "hold" in flight

> [!TIP]
> **📘 Recommended Study Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:** Practice at least 3 numerical problems on packet switching delay (with H, N, L, B, d_p given). Examiner 14-mark questions in Module 4 *always* contain a numerical sub-part. Also memorize the comparative table — it is the **single most frequently asked** question in KTU exams for this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit-Switching Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Circuit-Switching Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Circuit Switching** is a network switching methodology in which a dedicated physical communication path (a complete end-to-end circuit) is established between the sender and the receiver **before** any actual data transmission begins. This dedicated path — comprising a sequence of links and switches — remains exclusively reserved for the entire duration of the session, regardless of whether data is actively flowing.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PEITT412), Circuit-Switching is classified as one of the three fundamental **switching techniques** taught in Module 4, alongside **Packet Switching** and **Message Switching**. The defining property is **resource reservation**: the network's transmission capacity (bandwidth) on every link of the established path is locked-in for the two communicating parties until they tear down the connection.

The three sequential phases that govern every circuit-switched call are:
1. **Circuit Establishment (Setup Phase)**
2. **Data Transfer Phase**
3. **Circuit Teardown (Disconnection Phase)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Toll-Gate Model

Imagine you are traveling from **Kochi to Delhi** in your car. You do not want random vehicles cutting in front of you, traffic jams, or unexpected detours. So, you reserve an **entire dedicated toll-road (Greenfield Expressway)** exclusively for yourself before the trip begins.

- You **call the highway authority in advance** → this is the *Setup Phase*.
- The highway authority **locks all the toll-gates** along the route so that no one else can enter those lanes → this is *Bandwidth Reservation*.
- You **drive on the empty, reserved expressway** without interruption → this is the *Data Transfer Phase*.
- When you reach Delhi, you **release the reservation** and the lanes become available for others → this is the *Teardown Phase*.

This is exactly how a traditional **landline telephone call** works. When you dial a number, the telephone exchange reserves a continuous copper (or fiber) line all the way to the other party. Even if there is **silence** in the conversation, the line is held — a property known as **idle reservation overhead**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exams:** In Circuit Switching, the path is **physical and dedicated**. In Packet Switching (Module 4, next topic), no such reservation exists — packets are routed independently and may even arrive **out of order**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following parameters are used throughout the KTU board exam problems on this topic and must be memorized:

- **Propagation Speed in Copper:** $\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- **Propagation Speed in Fiber:** $\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- **Propagation Speed in Wireless / Air:** $\approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- **Standard Voice Sampling Rate (PCM telephony):** **8000 samples/sec**
- **Standard Telephone Bandwidth:** **4 kHz** (300 Hz to 3400 Hz voice band)

### 1.4 Two Sub-Implementations of Circuit Switching

| Variant | Full Name | Path Used |
|---|---|---|
| **FDM** | Frequency Division Multiplexing | Dedicated **frequency band** per call |
| **TDM** | Time Division Multiplexing | Dedicated **time slot** per call |

Both variants are explicitly listed in the **KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus** and must be explained in descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Phase Analysis

The lifecycle of a circuit-switched connection is rigorously divided into three logical phases. Each phase contributes a measurable delay component to the total communication time.

**Phase 1 — Circuit Establishment:**
The source sends a connection-request signaling packet to the destination. Every intermediate switch along the path **reserves an outgoing channel** and forwards the request. The destination replies with an acknowledgment, completing the reservation. This phase takes **$t_{setup}$** seconds.

**Phase 2 — Data Transfer:**
Once the circuit is established, the source transmits data at the full link bandwidth. Since the path is dedicated, **no queuing delay** occurs at intermediate nodes (theoretically). The switches forward bits as a continuous stream.

**Phase 3 — Circuit Teardown:**
After transmission, the source sends a teardown signal. Each switch releases the reserved resources, making them available to other calls.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step "Why & How" Logic

- **Why is a dedicated path needed?**
  Because real-time voice traffic is **delay-sensitive** but tolerant of occasional bit errors. A dedicated, predictable path guarantees uniform delivery latency — essential for conversational telephony.

- **How is the path established?**
  Using a **signaling protocol**. Historically, **Signaling System No. 7 (SS7)** was used in global PSTN networks; in modern KTU textbook examples, the switches simply reserve a free channel in either the FDM frequency slot or the TDM time slot.

- **How is bandwidth shared across multiple calls?**
  Either by **FDM** (dividing the spectrum into narrow bandpass channels) or **TDM** (dividing time into recurring frames of $N$ slots, one per call).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmission Time ($T_t$) | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds | $L$ = message length (bits), $R$ = link bandwidth (bps) |
| 2 | Propagation Delay ($T_p$) | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | seconds | $d$ = link length (m), $s$ = propagation speed (m/s) |
| 3 | Setup Time | $t_{setup}$ | seconds | Total signaling round-trip time across $k$ links |
| 4 | Total End-to-End Delay | $T_{total} = t_{setup} + k \cdot T_p + T_t$ | seconds | $k$ = number of links on the path |
| 5 | Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{L}{T_{total}}$ | bits/sec | Effective data rate seen by user |
| 6 | TDM Frame Duration | $T_{frame} = N \cdot \tau$ | seconds | $N$ = number of slots, $\tau$ = slot duration |
| 7 | Per-User Bandwidth in TDM | $R_{user} = \dfrac{R_{link}}{N}$ | bps | If TDM divides bandwidth equally |
| 8 | FDM Channel Bandwidth | $B_{ch} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{N}$ | Hz | Guard bands may reduce usable $B_{ch}$ |
| 9 | End-to-End Delay (Single Link) | $T_{e2e} = \dfrac{L}{R} + \dfrac{d}{s}$ | seconds | Simplified two-term model |
| 10 | Link Efficiency in TDM | $\eta_{TDM} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | unitless | Each user gets $\frac{1}{N}$ of frame time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Do **not** confuse **Transmission Delay** ($L/R$) with **Propagation Delay** ($d/s$). Transmission delay is the time taken to push all bits of a packet onto the wire; propagation delay is the time taken by one bit to physically travel across the medium.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Circuit switching still underpins the global **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)**. It is also used in **ISDN (Integrated Services Digital Network)** and in **traditional leased-line enterprise networks** where a guaranteed Constant Bit Rate (CBR) is required. Although modern data networks (Internet) have largely migrated to packet switching, the principles taught in this module form the conceptual foundation for understanding **QoS reservation protocols** like **RSVP (Resource Reservation Protocol)** in modern IP networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Delay in a Multi-Link Circuit-Switched Path

Consider a circuit-switched network with **$k$ links** in series between source $S$ and destination $D$. Each link $i$ has:
- Bandwidth $R_i$ (bps)
- Physical length $d_i$ (meters)
- Propagation speed $s_i$ (m/s)

**Step 1 — Define the Setup Phase Delay**

Let the signaling packet traverse $k$ links one-way, and the acknowledgment return via the same $k$ links. The total setup time is:

$$
t_{setup} = 2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{d_i}{s_i}
$$

**Explanation:** The factor of 2 accounts for the forward signaling message and the reverse acknowledgment. Summation aggregates the propagation delay across each of the $k$ links.

**Step 2 — Define the Per-Link Propagation Delay**

For data transmission, bits travel across each link once (end-to-end, but accumulate one propagation hop per link):

$$
T_{p,\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{d_i}{s_i}
$$

**Explanation:** Once the circuit is set up, the data flows as a continuous stream. The first bit traverses all $k$ links sequentially, so total propagation time is the sum of per-link propagation delays.

**Step 3 — Define the Transmission Time**

Assuming all links have the same bandwidth $R$ and the message length is $L$ bits:

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R}
$$

**Explanation:** The source pushes $L$ bits onto the first link at rate $R$. Since the link is dedicated, the source can finish transmitting the entire message in $L/R$ seconds.

**Step 4 — Combine All Delay Components**

The total end-to-end delay experienced by the **last bit** of the message is:

$$
T_{total} = t_{setup} + T_{p,\text{total}} + T_t
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
T_{total} = 2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{d_i}{s_i} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{d_i}{s_i} + \frac{L}{R}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 3 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{d_i}{s_i} + \frac{L}{R}
$$

**Explanation:** The "3" factor arises because the signal goes forth, the acknowledgment returns, and then data is sent. This is the canonical KTU derivation.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A circuit-switched network connects host A to host B via **3 links** in series. Each link is **1000 km** long and has a bandwidth of **8 kbps**. The message length is **64,000 bits**. Propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Setup signaling time is negligible. Compute the end-to-end delay.

**Given:**
- $k = 3$
- $d_i = 1000$ km $= 10^6$ m per link
- $R = 8000$ bps
- $L = 64000$ bits
- $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Step 1 — Compute Per-Link Propagation Delay**

$$
T_{p,i} = \frac{10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Explanation:** A bit takes 5 milliseconds to cross each 1000 km link.

**Step 2 — Compute Total Propagation Delay Across 3 Links**

$$
T_{p,\text{total}} = 3 \times 5 \text{ ms} = 15 \text{ ms}
$$

**Explanation:** Three links in series = three propagation hops.

**Step 3 — Compute Transmission Delay**

$$
T_t = \frac{64000}{8000} = 8 \text{ s}
$$

**Explanation:** Pushing 64,000 bits onto the first link at 8000 bps takes 8 seconds.

**Step 4 — Compute End-to-End Delay (Setup = 0)**

$$
T_{total} = 0 + 15 \text{ ms} + 8 \text{ s} \approx 8.015 \text{ s}
$$

**Explanation:** The first bit arrives at the destination after 15 ms; the source finishes transmitting after 8 s. The receiver obtains the entire message approximately 8.015 seconds after the call began.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Circuit Switching | Datagram Packet Switching | Virtual Circuit Packet Switching |
|---|---|---|---|
| Path Setup Required | Yes | No | Yes (logical only) |
| Resource Reservation | Dedicated | Best-effort | Logical (no bandwidth lock) |
| Packets Follow Same Path | Always | No | Always |
| Out-of-Order Delivery | Impossible | Possible | Impossible |
| Robustness to Link Failure | Entire call fails | Packets reroute | Entire VC fails |
| Suitability for Voice | Excellent | Poor (jitter) | Good |
| Typical Application | PSTN, ISDN | Internet (IP) | ATM, Frame Relay, X.25 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When comparing circuit switching with virtual-circuit packet switching, emphasize that **virtual circuit is NOT circuit switching** — the underlying network resources are still shared and packets are still buffered at routers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Circuit Establishment & Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host A Source] -->|1 Signaling Request| B[Switch 1]
    B -->|2 Reserve Channel| C[Switch 2]
    C -->|3 Reserve Channel| D[Switch 3]
    D -->|4 Forward Request| E[Host B Destination]
    E -->|5 ACK Backward| D
    D -->|6 Confirm Reservation| C
    C -->|7 Confirm Reservation| B
    B -->|8 Confirm to Source| A
    A -->|9 Data Stream Begins| B
    B -->|10 Continuous Stream| C
    C -->|11 Continuous Stream| D
    D -->|12 Delivered| E
    E -->|13 Teardown Signal| D
    D -->|14 Release Resources| C
    C -->|15 Release Resources| B
    B -->|16 Release Resources| A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — TDM Frame Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph F[TDM Frame of Duration Tframe]
        S1[Slot 1 User A]
        S2[Slot 2 User B]
        S3[Slot 3 User C]
        S4[Slot 4 User D]
        S5[Slot N User N]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    S1 -.Repeats Periodically.-> S1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — FDM Spectrum Allocation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Total Link Bandwidth Btotal] --> CH1[Channel 1 f1 to f2]
    L1 --> CH2[Channel 2 f3 to f4]
    L1 --> CH3[Channel 3 f5 to f6]
    L1 --> CH4[Channel N fKminus1 to fK]
    CH1 -.Guard Band.-> CH2
    CH2 -.Guard Band.-> CH3
    CH3 -.Guard Band.-> CH4
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC[Source Side]
        APP[Application Layer Data]
        ENC[Encoder and Serializer]
        SIG[Signaling Module]
    end
    subgraph NET[Network Core Circuit Switched]
        SW1[Switch 1 with Channel Allocator]
        SW2[Switch 2 with Channel Allocator]
        SW3[Switch 3 with Channel Allocator]
        SW1 <--> SW2
        SW2 <--> SW3
    end
    subgraph DST[Destination Side]
        DEC[Decoder and Deserializer]
        APP2[Application Layer Receiver]
    end
    APP --> ENC --> SIG
    SIG --> SW1
    SW3 --> DEC --> APP2
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams are valid KTU-compliant illustrations. In your exam scripts, you may reproduce simplified versions manually — the markers reward **clear labeling** of phases 1, 2, and 3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define circuit switching. List its three phases.*

**Model Answer:**

Circuit switching is a switching technique in which a dedicated physical communication path is established between the source and the destination **before** data transfer begins, and this path is held exclusively for the entire duration of the session.

The three phases are:
1. **Circuit Establishment** — A signaling message reserves a path through all intermediate switches.
2. **Data Transfer** — Data is transmitted as a continuous stream at full link bandwidth.
3. **Circuit Teardown** — A disconnect signal releases all reserved resources.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing the three phases.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare FDM and TDM as circuit-switching implementations.*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | FDM | TDM |
|---|---|---|
| Resource Divided | Frequency spectrum | Time axis |
| Allocation | Simultaneous | Sequential (round-robin) |
| Guard Band | Required | Not required |
| Synchronization | Simpler | Requires strict clock sync |
| Application | Analog telephony | Digital telephony (PCM) |

Both allocate dedicated portions of the link's capacity to each user for the duration of the call.

*(3 marks: 2 marks comparison + 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the three phases of circuit switching with a suitable diagram. Describe how FDM and TDM are used to share bandwidth among multiple calls.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A circuit-switched network has **4 links** in series between hosts A and B. Each link is **500 km** long with bandwidth **4 kbps**. A message of **32,000 bits** is to be transmitted. Propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. The call setup time is **300 ms**. Compute:
(i) End-to-end delay.
(ii) Effective throughput.
(iii) What percentage of the total time is spent in setup?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Phases Explanation:**

**Phase 1 — Circuit Establishment:**
The source sends a request signal through intermediate switches. Each switch, upon receiving the request, **reserves an available channel** (either an FDM frequency band or a TDM time slot) on the outgoing link and forwards the request. When the destination acknowledges, the dedicated path is confirmed.

**Phase 2 — Data Transfer:**
The source transmits data as a continuous bit stream at full link bandwidth. Since the path is dedicated, **no queuing or contention** occurs at intermediate switches. Data flows with predictable latency.

**Phase 3 — Circuit Teardown:**
After transmission, the source sends a teardown signal. Each switch releases the reserved channel, returning it to the pool of available resources.

**FDM Implementation:** The link's frequency spectrum is divided into $N$ non-overlapping bands, each carrying one call. Guard bands separate adjacent channels.

**TDM Implementation:** Time is divided into recurring frames; each frame contains $N$ slots, with one slot permanently assigned to each call.

*(7 marks distributed as: 2 marks per phase + 2 marks for FDM/TDM explanation + 1 mark for diagram.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $k = 4$ links
- $d_i = 500$ km $= 5 \times 10^5$ m per link
- $R = 4000$ bps
- $L = 32000$ bits
- $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $t_{setup} = 300$ ms $= 0.3$ s

**Step 1 — Per-Link Propagation Delay**

$$
T_{p,i} = \frac{5 \times 10^{5}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Propagation Delay Across 4 Links**

$$
T_{p,\text{total}} = 4 \times 2.5 \text{ ms} = 10 \text{ ms} = 0.01 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Transmission Delay**

$$
T_t = \frac{32000}{4000} = 8 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — End-to-End Delay**

$$
T_{total} = t_{setup} + T_{p,\text{total}} + T_t
$$

$$
T_{total} = 0.3 + 0.01 + 8 = 8.31 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Effective Throughput**

$$
\eta = \frac{L}{T_{total}} = \frac{32000}{8.31} \approx 3851.4 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 6 — Setup Time Percentage**

$$
\%_{setup} = \frac{0.3}{8.31} \times 100 \approx 3.61\%
$$

*(7 marks: 1 mark each for steps 1–4, 2 marks for steps 5–6.)*

---

#### **OR**

#### **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With neat diagrams, explain how circuit switching is implemented using **TDM**. A TDM system has **32 users** sharing a link of **1.544 Mbps**. Compute (i) duration of one frame, (ii) bits per slot, (iii) duration of one slot.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast circuit switching with **datagram packet switching** in seven points. State one real-world example for each.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TDM Diagram & Calculation:**

In TDM, the timeline is divided into recurring **frames**, each containing $N$ time slots (one per user). Each user gets exclusive access to the full link bandwidth **only during its assigned slot**.

```
[Slot1][Slot2][Slot3]...[Slot32]
   |---- Repeats every frame ----|
```

**Given:**
- $N = 32$ users
- $R_{link} = 1.544 \times 10^6$ bps
- Assume standard PCM frame duration $T_{frame} = 125 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Frame Duration:** (Standard for T1/E1 PCM systems)

$$
T_{frame} = 125 \text{ }\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Bits per Frame**

$$
\text{Bits/frame} = R_{link} \times T_{frame} = 1.544 \times 10^6 \times 125 \times 10^{-6} = 193 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Bits per Slot**

$$
\text{Bits/slot} = \frac{193}{32} \approx 6.03 \text{ bits}
$$

Rounded to standard PCM: **8 bits per slot**.

**Step 4 — Slot Duration**

$$
T_{slot} = \frac{T_{frame}}{N} = \frac{125 \text{ }\mu\text{s}}{32} \approx 3.9 \text{ }\mu\text{s}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks diagram, 1 mark each for 4 computations.)*

---

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Sl. No. | Circuit Switching | Datagram Packet Switching |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dedicated path | No fixed path |
| 2 | Resource reserved | Best-effort, no reservation |
| 3 | Setup phase needed | No setup required |
| 4 | No queuing delay | Queuing at every router |
| 5 | In-order delivery guaranteed | Out-of-order possible |
| 6 | Failure breaks entire call | Robust, packets reroute |
| 7 | Example: PSTN Telephone | Example: Internet (IP) |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per comparison row.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner Notes):**
> 1. **Mixing up Transmission and Propagation delays** — marks are heavily docked if these two are interchanged or summed without explanation.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply propagation delay by the number of links $k$** — a single-link assumption in a multi-link problem loses 2 to 3 marks instantly.
> 3. **Ignoring the setup phase** — many students compute only transmission + propagation, completely omitting $t_{setup}$. Always re-read the problem statement for setup-time values.
> 4. **Confusing TDM with statistical multiplexing** — TDM gives each user a *fixed* slot regardless of activity. Statistical multiplexing allocates slots dynamically. This distinction is a favourite KTU trick question.
> 5. **Writing "Virtual Circuit = Circuit Switching"** — this is incorrect. Virtual circuit is a *logical* path over a packet-switched network, NOT a dedicated physical circuit.
> 6. **Unit conversion errors** — converting km to m (multiply by $10^3$), ms to s (divide by $10^3$), kbps to bps (multiply by $10^3$). Mismatched units cause numerical answer marks to be zero.

---

### TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Circuit switching establishes a **dedicated physical path** before transmission.
- **Three Phases:** Establishment → Transfer → Teardown.
- **Two Implementations:** **FDM** (frequency-based) and **TDM** (time-slot-based).
- **Key Formulas:**
  - Transmission delay: $T_t = L/R$
  - Propagation delay: $T_p = d/s$
  - Total delay: $T_{total} = t_{setup} + k \cdot T_p + T_t$
  - Throughput: $\eta = L / T_{total}$
- **Propagation Speed Constants:** Copper $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s; Fiber $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s; Air $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **TDM Rule:** Each user gets exactly $1/N$ of the link's bandwidth.
- **PSTN:** The canonical real-world example of circuit switching.
- **Comparison with Packet Switching:** Circuit switching **wastes bandwidth during silence**; packet switching is more efficient but introduces variable delay.
- **Virtual Circuit $\neq$ Circuit Switching** — keep this distinction crisp in board answers.
- **Standard T1 PCM frame:** $T_{frame} = 125 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$, 193 bits, 32 slots.
- **Common Units in Exam:** kbps, Mbps for bandwidth; km, m for distance; ms, $\mu$s, s for delay.
- **Examiner Loves:** Asking the difference between FDM and TDM, or computing end-to-end delay with multiple links.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit Switching Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Circuit Switching Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Circuit Switching** is a connection-oriented network communication paradigm in which a dedicated physical communication path (called a **circuit**) is established between the sender and the receiver **before** any data transfer begins, the path remains continuously reserved for the exclusive use of the two communicating parties for the **entire duration** of the session, and is explicitly torn down only after the conversation concludes.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, Circuit Switching is studied as the foundational **legacy switching architecture** (originating from the Public Switched Telephone Network — PSTN) that conceptually underpins how early telecom networks guaranteed **Quality of Service (QoS)** through resource reservation. It is one of the **three canonical switching techniques** alongside **Packet Switching** and **Message Switching**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dedicated Highway Lane"

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Trivandrum** on NH-66. Before your trip, you reserve an **entire toll-road lane** exclusively for your car. The lane:
- Is **physically blocked off** for all other vehicles,
- Remains yours **24×7** until you finish your journey,
- And is **decommissioned** only after you reach your destination.

This is exactly how Circuit Switching works. Even if you are silent and not "driving" (i.e., there is a pause in speech or data), the lane is **still blocked and unusable** for anyone else. This wasted idle time is the **principal drawback** of circuit switching and the reason modern data networks (which are "bursty") prefer packet switching.

## 1.3 The Three Mandatory Phases

> [!NOTE]
> **Every** circuit-switched communication follows these **three sequential phases** — examiners frequently award 2 marks just for listing them correctly.

| Phase # | Phase Name | Duration | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Circuit Establishment** | Slow | A dedicated path is set up end-to-end through switches. |
| 2 | **Data Transfer** | Fast | Information flows over the established path. |
| 3 | **Circuit Teardown / Disconnection** | Slow | Resources are released back to the network pool. |

### Key Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlights)

- **End-to-End Delay (Setup Delay + Propagation + Transmission)**
- **Throughput during transfer phase** = Link Bandwidth (since the circuit is dedicated)
- **Call Blocking Probability (B)** — probability that a new call request is **denied** because all circuits are busy
- **Call Setup Time** — typically in the order of **milliseconds to seconds**
- **Bandwidth Guarantee** = **100%** (no statistical multiplexing)
- **Propagation Delay** = constant for the lifetime of the call

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fact that **throughput equals link bandwidth during data transfer** is the single most tested property in KTU Part A questions on this topic.

## 1.4 Why Study Circuit Switching in a Data Communications Course?

Although modern IP networks use **packet switching**, circuit switching is **not obsolete**. The KTU 2024 syllabus retains this module to:
1. Build **conceptual contrast** — students must understand *what packet switching improves upon*.
2. Cover **SONET/SDH** and **ISDN** legacy architectures that still operate over optical circuit-switched backbones.
3. Introduce **switching fabric mathematics** (Clos networks, blocking probability) that are reused in modern **data center switches** and **FPGA routers**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Three Phases (Detailed Walkthrough)

### Phase 1 — Circuit Establishment
- The source sends a **call request / setup message** containing the destination address.
- Each intermediate switch examines the address, picks an **outgoing trunk**, and reserves a **time slot / physical channel** on that trunk.
- If **all channels** on a chosen trunk are occupied → **blocking occurs** and the call is rejected (or queued depending on policy).
- When the request reaches the destination, it acknowledges back, completing the **end-to-end dedicated path**.
- **Time cost**: propagation delay × number of hops + per-switch processing delay.

### Phase 2 — Data Transfer
- Once the circuit is established, **data is transmitted as a continuous stream** (analog voice in PSTN, or digital bitstream in ISDN/SONET).
- No addressing is required in each packet because the path is already known.
- **No store-and-forward delay** at intermediate nodes — bits flow through.
- **Throughput is fixed** and equals the channel capacity of the narrowest link along the path.

### Phase 3 — Circuit Teardown
- A **teardown signal** is sent in **both directions** (asymmetric vs symmetric release).
- All switches **release the reserved channels** back to the free pool.
- **Asymmetric release** (used in PSTN): the sender hangs up → network releases.
- **Symmetric release**: both ends must confirm termination.

## 2.2 Classification of Circuit Switching — The Two Switching Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 4 explicitly requires students to **compare Space Division vs Time Division Switching** and derive the **Clos non-blocking condition**.

### A. Space Division Switching (SDS)
A physical **dedicated path** is established through a matrix of **crosspoints** (electromechanical relays or semiconductor gates).

**Sub-types:**

1. **Crossbar Switch** — N×N matrix with N² crosspoints.
2. **Multistage Switch** — reduces crosspoint count at the cost of internal blocking.

### B. Time Division Switching (TDS)
Time is **sliced into slots**, and a connection is realized by **reassigning a time slot** from input to output. Implemented using a **TDM bus** combined with a **time-slot interchanger (TSI)**.

**Sub-types:**
1. **TDM Bus Switching** (single-stage)
2. **TDM Space Switching** (TDM bus + crossbar)
3. **Combined TDM (TST / STS)** — used in real telecom exchanges

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU Part B (14-mark) problems. Memorize the boundary conditions column — examiners love testing them.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Boundary / Condition | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Crosspoints in Crossbar** | $C_{xb} = N^2$ | For an $N \times N$ switch | Switching-fabric complexity |
| 2 | **Crosspoints in 3-Stage Clos** | $C_{clos} = 2kN + k\left(\frac{N}{n}\right)^2$ | $k$ input modules, $n$ middle modules | Data-center fabric scaling |
| 3 | **Clos Strict Non-Blocking** | $n \geq 2m - 1$ | $m$ = links per input module | Non-blocking design |
| 4 | **Clos Rearrangeable Non-Blocking** | $n \geq m$ | Weaker guarantee | Cheaper fabric design |
| 5 | **Erlang-B Blocking Probability** | $B(C, A) = \dfrac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^i / i!}$ | $C$ = circuits, $A$ = offered traffic (Erlangs) | PSTN trunk sizing |
| 6 | **Circuit Capacity** | $\text{Capacity} = R \times t$ | $R$ = bit rate, $t$ = duration | Total bits delivered |
| 7 | **End-to-End Delay** | $T_{e2e} = T_{setup} + \dfrac{L}{R} + n \cdot T_{prop}$ | $L$ = message length, $R$ = link rate | Voice-call QoS |
| 8 | **Efficiency of Circuit Switching** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{active data time}}{\text{total connection time}}$ | Always $\leq 1$ | Bursty traffic penalty |
| 9 | **3-Stage Blocking Probability** | $P_{B} \approx \left[1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho^{m}}{m!}\right)^{k}\right]^{n}$ | $\rho$ = link utilization | Clos network dimensioning |
| 10 | **Blocking probability (crossbar, $N \times N$)** | $B = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{N-1}$ | $N$ ports, uniform traffic | Approx. $\to 1/e$ as $N \to \infty$ |

> **Note on Erlang B usage in KTU**: $A$ is the offered traffic in **Erlangs** (average number of simultaneous calls). $C$ is the total number of circuits. Always check if the question says "**trunk efficiency**" (use Erlang B with trunk count) or "**blocking probability**" (use Erlang B directly).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Where Circuit Switching Lives On |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Telephony (PSTN)** | Traditional landline voice |
| **ISDN (B-channels)** | 64 kbps circuit-switched digital voice |
| **SONET/SDH** | Optical ring networks (E1/T1 leased lines) |
| **Optical WDM** | Wavelength-routed optical networks |
| **Satellite / SCPC** | Single Channel Per Carrier uplinks |
| **5G Voice (VoNR fallback)** | Circuit-Switched Fallback (CSFB) for legacy LTE/voice |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Crosspoint Count in a Crossbar Switch

We want to prove that an $N \times N$ crossbar requires exactly $N^2$ crosspoints.

**Step 1 — Define the matrix structure.**
A crossbar switch has $N$ input lines and $N$ output lines. Every possible input-to-output pair must be connectable.

**Step 2 — Count crosspoints per row.**
Each of the $N$ inputs must have the ability to connect to all $N$ outputs.
Therefore, the number of crosspoints in one row is $N$.

**Step 3 — Count rows.**
There are exactly $N$ such rows (one per input line).

**Step 4 — Multiply.**
Total crosspoints $C_{xb}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{xb} &= \underbrace{N}_{\text{rows}} \times \underbrace{N}_{\text{crosspoints per row}} \\
       &= N^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check.**
For $N = 8$ ports → $C_{xb} = 64$ crosspoints. For $N = 1024$ ports → $C_{xb} = 1{,}048{,}576$, illustrating the **scalability problem** that motivates multistage Clos networks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Clos Network Non-Blocking Condition (Strict Sense)

**Problem Setup.**
A 3-stage Clos network uses:
- $k$ first-stage modules, each of size $m \times n$
- $n$ middle-stage modules, each of size $k \times k$
- $k$ third-stage modules, each of size $n \times m$
- Total inputs = $N = km$, total outputs = $km$.

**Goal:** Find the **minimum** $n$ (middle-stage modules) such that an incoming call can **always** find a free path, regardless of existing connections (strict-sense non-blocking).

**Step 1 — Worst case analysis.**
A new call enters input module $I_1$ and must reach output module $O_1$. In the **worst case**, all other $m - 1$ inputs of $I_1$ are already busy and all other $m - 1$ outputs of $O_1$ are already busy.

**Step 2 — Count middle-stage links already in use.**
Each of the $m - 1$ busy inputs of $I_1$ can use **at most one** middle-stage link.
Each of the $m - 1$ busy outputs of $O_1$ can use **at most one** middle-stage link.
So the **maximum** number of middle-stage modules already "blocked off" is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Blocked middle modules} &\leq (m - 1) + (m - 1) \\
                              &= 2m - 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the condition.**
To guarantee a free middle module for the new call, the total number of middle modules $n$ must exceed the blocked count by at least 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &> 2m - 2 \\
n &\geq 2m - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clos Strict Non-Blocking Theorem:**
> A 3-stage Clos network with parameters $(k, m, n)$ is **strict-sense non-blocking** if and only if
> $$n \geq 2m - 1$$

**Step 5 — Compare crosspoint count.**
- Crossbar: $C_{xb} = (km)^2 = k^2 m^2$
- Clos (strict): $C_{clos} = 2km \cdot n + n \cdot k^2 = 2k^2m + k^2(2m-1) = k^2(4m - 1)$

For large $m$, the **Clos fabric uses $\approx 4/k$ of the crosspoints of a full crossbar** — a massive saving.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Blocking Probability of a Crossbar (Limiting Case)

**Step 1 — Setup.**
Consider an $N \times N$ crossbar. A new call arrives at a free input and must reach a free output. There are $N - 1$ other inputs, each of which has probability $1/N$ of being connected to the **specific output we need**.

**Step 2 — Probability of a conflict with a single other input.**
The other input is connected to our desired output with probability $1/N$.
Therefore, the probability it is **not** blocking us is $1 - 1/N$.

**Step 3 — Independence assumption.**
Assume the $N - 1$ other inputs act independently. The probability that **none** of them is connected to our target output is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{N-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} B &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{N-1} \\
                      &= e^{-1} \\
                      &= \dfrac{1}{e} \\
                      &\approx 0.3679
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
> [!NOTE]
> Even a **non-blocking crossbar** suffers a 36.8% call-level blocking probability at the **call-arrival level** when traffic is uniformly distributed. This is the *birthday-paradox style* result examiners love to test.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Erlang-B Formula (Statement and Limiting Argument)

The Erlang B formula is **not derived** from first principles in a single semester, but its **structure** can be motivated as follows.

**Step 1 — Define traffic intensity.**
$A$ = offered traffic in Erlangs = (call arrival rate $\lambda$) × (average holding time $\mu^{-1}$).

**Step 2 — State assumption.**
Calls arrive as a **Poisson process** with rate $\lambda$, holding times are **exponentially distributed**, and lost calls are **cleared** (no queue).

**Step 3 — Birth-death equilibrium.**
The system is a **birth-death process**. The steady-state probability $P_n$ of $n$ active calls satisfies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda P_{n-1} = n \mu P_n \quad \text{for } n \geq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Recursively solve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \dfrac{A}{1!} P_0 \\
P_2 &= \dfrac{A^2}{2!} P_0 \\
&\vdots \\
P_n &= \dfrac{A^n}{n!} P_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize using $\sum P_n = 1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 \sum_{i=0}^{C} \dfrac{A^i}{i!} &= 1 \\
P_0 &= \left[\sum_{i=0}^{C} \dfrac{A^i}{i!}\right]^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Blocking probability = probability all $C$ circuits are busy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B(C, A) &= P_C = \dfrac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^i / i!}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Erlang-B formula** — frequently used to dimension PSTN trunk groups.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Switching Fabrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is a **favourite KTU Part B sub-question**. Memorize it column-by-column.

| Parameter | Crossbar | 3-Stage Clos (Strict NB) | 3-Stage Clos (Rearrangeable) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Crosspoint count** | $N^2$ | $k(4m - 1)$ | $k(2m + 1)$ |
| **Non-blocking?** | Yes (always) | Yes (if $n \geq 2m-1$) | Yes (after re-routing) |
| **Internal blocking?** | None | None | Possible until re-route |
| **Cost** | Very high for large $N$ | Moderate | Lowest |
| **Latency** | Uniform | Uniform | Variable |
| **Used in** | Small switches | Data-center fabrics | Telephone exchanges |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Three Phases of Circuit Switching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Host S] --> B{Phase 1: Circuit Establishment}
    B --> C[Send SETUP message with destination address]
    C --> D[Switch 1 reserves a free channel on outgoing link]
    D --> E[Switch 2 reserves a free channel on outgoing link]
    E --> F[Switch k reserves a free channel on outgoing link]
    F --> G{Destination Reachable?}
    G -- No --> X[BLOCKED: Call Rejected]
    G -- Yes --> H[Destination returns CONNECT ACK]
    H --> I[End-to-End Dedicated Path Established]
    I --> J{Phase 2: Data Transfer}
    J --> K[Bits flow continuously over reserved path]
    K --> L[No addressing needed per packet]
    L --> M{All data sent?}
    M -- No --> K
    M -- Yes --> N{Phase 3: Circuit Teardown}
    N --> O[Source sends TEARDOWN signal]
    O --> P[Each switch releases its channel]
    P --> Q[Resources returned to free pool]
    Q --> R[End of Communication]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List and briefly explain the three phases of circuit switching.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Circuit Establishment** (1 mark): A dedicated physical path between source and destination is set up through the switches. A setup signal traverses the network hop-by-hop, reserving a free channel on each outgoing link. If no free channel exists, the call is **blocked**.

2. **Data Transfer** (1 mark): Once the circuit is established, data flows continuously over the reserved path as a steady stream. **No addressing** is required per packet because the path is fixed. Throughput equals the channel bandwidth.

3. **Circuit Teardown** (1 mark): After communication ends, a teardown signal is sent, and all switches release the reserved channels back to the network's free pool for reuse.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a crossbar switch? Why does it not scale to large N?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **crossbar switch** is an $N \times N$ space-division switching fabric formed by $N$ input lines, $N$ output lines, and $N^2$ **crosspoints** (relays/electronic switches) at every input-output intersection (1.5 marks).

It does not scale because the **crosspoint count grows as $N^2$** (1 mark). For $N = 1024$, this requires **1,048,576** physical crosspoints, making the fabric physically large, expensive, and impractical. Multistage Clos networks are used to reduce the crosspoint count to $O(N^{1.5})$ (0.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### **Question A (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the three-stage Clos network. Derive the condition for it to be a strict-sense non-blocking network.**

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (2 marks)** — refer to the 3-stage Clos diagram in Section 4.3.

**Explanation (2 marks):**
A 3-stage Clos network consists of:
- **$k$ first-stage (input) modules**, each of size $m \times n$
- **$n$ second-stage (middle) modules**, each of size $k \times k$
- **$k$ third-stage (output) modules**, each of size $n \times m$
- Total input/output lines = $N = km$.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

In the worst case, a new call from input module $I_1$ to output module $O_1$ finds that all other $m-1$ inputs of $I_1$ and all other $m-1$ outputs of $O_1$ are busy. Each busy input uses one distinct middle-stage link, and each busy output uses one distinct middle-stage link. So the maximum number of middle modules that are "blocked off" is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Blocked} &= (m - 1) + (m - 1) \\
               &= 2m - 2
\end{aligned}
$$

To guarantee a free middle module for the new call:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &> 2m - 2 \\
\therefore n &\geq 2m - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Clos strict-sense non-blocking condition**.

---

### **Question A (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Compare Space Division Switching and Time Division Switching. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Space Division Switching | Time Division Switching |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Resource reserved | Physical path (crosspoints) | A time slot in a TDM frame |
| Implementation | Crossbar / Clos fabric | TDM bus + Time-Slot Interchanger |
| Suitable for | Analog and digital | Digital only |
| Switching speed | Fast (electronic) | Limited by slot duration |

**Space Division — Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Truly non-blocking** (in crossbar): any input can connect to any free output at any time.
2. **No serialization delay** — bits flow through the fabric without waiting for a time slot.

**Space Division — Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. **Crosspoint count grows as $N^2$** — costly and physically large for big switches.

**Time Division — Advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Shared medium** — many calls share one bus, reducing hardware.

**Time Division — Disadvantages (1 mark) [Awarded against the total 7-marks budget]:**
1. **Idle time slots are wasted** when a call is silent.
2. Requires **synchronization** and a Time-Slot Interchanger (TSI).

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's discretion: the two advantages/disadvantages collectively fit the 7-mark budget; distribute as above.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

### **Question B (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**A telephone exchange has 20 incoming trunks and 8 outgoing circuits. If the offered traffic is 5 Erlangs, calculate the blocking probability using the Erlang-B formula. Comment on how this changes if the number of circuits is increased to 12.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Given data (1 mark):**
- Circuits $C = 8$
- Offered traffic $A = 5$ Erlangs
- Formula: $B(C, A) = \dfrac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^i / i!}$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=0}^{8} \dfrac{5^i}{i!} &= 1 + 5 + \dfrac{25}{2} + \dfrac{125}{6} + \dfrac{625}{24} + \dfrac{3125}{120} + \dfrac{15625}{720} + \dfrac{78125}{5040} + \dfrac{390625}{40320} \\
&= 1 + 5 + 12.5 + 20.8333 + 26.0417 + 26.0417 + 21.7014 + 15.5010 + 9.6881 \\
&\approx 138.307
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{A^C}{C!} = \dfrac{5^8}{8!} = \dfrac{390625}{40320} \approx 9.6881
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Blocking probability (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B(8, 5) &= \dfrac{9.6881}{138.307} \\
        &\approx 0.0700 \\
        &\approx 7.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Re-compute for $C = 12$ (1 mark):** Recomputing similarly gives $B(12, 5) \approx 0.00188 \approx 0.19\%$.

**Comment (part of 1 mark):** Increasing circuits from 8 → 12 reduces blocking probability dramatically (from 7% to 0.19%) but doubles the trunk infrastructure. The **trade-off** is between **Grade of Service (GoS)** and **cost**.

---

### **Question B (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain with a neat diagram the working of a Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) bus-based time-slot interchanger (TSI). Why is TSI alone insufficient for large switches, and how is the limitation overcome?**

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the TDM frame diagram in Section 4.4.

**Working (2 marks):**
A TSI contains a **serial-to-parallel converter**, a **random-access control memory** (storing slot reordering), and a **parallel-to-serial converter**. In each frame, incoming bits are written sequentially into the data memory and read out in a **reordered sequence** under the control memory. This effectively **swaps time slots**, achieving switching in the time domain.

**Limitation (1.5 marks):**
A single TSI's control memory size and read/write speed limit its capacity to roughly **120 to 512** time slots. For larger exchanges (e.g., 10,000+ subscribers), a single TSI cannot handle the volume in real time.

**Solution (1.5 marks):**
The limitation is overcome by combining TSI with **space-division switching** to form **TST (Time-Space-Time)** or **STS (Space-Time-Space)** switches. The TSI concentrates/expands slot streams, and the space switch performs the crossbar function. This combination is used in all modern digital telephone exchanges (e.g., Alcatel, Ericsson AXE).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 1–3 marks each in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing "non-blocking" with "no call blocking"** — A non-blocking switch can still *block calls* if all circuits are busy (use Erlang B). Non-blocking only means **internal fabric blocking** is zero.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(m-1)+(m-1) = 2m-2$ trick** in the Clos derivation — examiners specifically test the **worst-case analysis** argument.
> 3. **Writing $N^2$ crosspoints for a Clos network** — that is the crossbar count, not the Clos count.
> 4. **Mixing up space and time division** — TDS uses time slots, not physical crosspoints.
> 5. **Skipping the "rate × time" unit check** in Erlang calculations — traffic must be in Erlangs (dimensionless).
> 6. **Assuming Circuit Switching has no idle time** — voice calls are 60–70% silent, so circuit-switched networks can be **less than 40% efficient** for voice.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — memorize before exam:**

- ☐ **Three phases** of circuit switching: **Establish → Transfer → Teardown** (always in this order).
- ☐ **Crossbar crosspoint count** = $N^2$ (cross product of inputs and outputs).
- ☐ **Clos non-blocking condition (strict)** = $n \geq 2m - 1$.
- ☐ **Clos non-blocking condition (rearrangeable)** = $n \geq m$.
- ☐ **Erlang-B formula**: $B(C, A) = \dfrac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^i / i!}$.
- ☐ **Erlangs** = arrival rate × average holding time (dimensionless).
- ☐ **Efficiency of circuit switching for voice ≤ 40%** because of silence gaps.
- ☐ **TDM uses time slots**; **SDM uses physical paths** — never mix the two.
- ☐ **TST switch** = Time-Space-Time — combination fabric used in real exchanges.
- ☐ **Blocking probability of crossbar** → $1/e \approx 0.368$ as $N \to \infty$.
- ☐ **End-to-end delay** = setup delay + propagation + transmission.
- ☐ **Modern relevance**: SONET/SDH, leased lines, optical WDM, 5G CSFB.
- ☐ **Draw a labeled diagram** in every Part B answer — diagrams alone fetch 2–3 marks.
- ☐ **Always state units**: Erlangs, ms, bps, crosspoints.
- ☐ **Compare** with packet switching when the question says "comment on" or "discuss".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Softswitch Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Softswitch Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Softswitch Architecture — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Softswitch (Software Switch / Call Agent)** is a software-based central call-control element in a packet-switched telecommunications network that decouples the *control plane* (signaling and call logic) from the *bearer plane* (media / voice path). It performs traditional Class-4 / Class-5 switch functions — call setup, teardown, routing, billing, and feature invocation — using IP protocols such as **SIP**, **H.323**, **H.248/MEGACO**, and **SIGTRAN**, instead of dedicated hardware switching matrices.

In KTU 2024-scheme terminology, the softswitch is the **brain of a Next-Generation Network (NGN)** that converts legacy PSTN/ISDN signaling into IP-based signaling while directing Media Gateways to establish, modify, and release voice / multimedia bearers.

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| ITU-T Designation | NGN Functional Block (Y.2011 / Y.2232) |
| Standardization Body | ITU-T, IETF, ETSI TISPAN |
| Typical Call Setup Rate | 1000 – 2000 calls / second |
| Concurrent Active Calls | Up to 1,00,000 BHCA per cluster |
| MTBF | > 1,00,000 hours |
| First Commercial Use | 1998 (Lucent / Telica) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a softswitch as the *Air Traffic Controller (ATC)* of a modern digital airport.**

| Airport Element | Softswitch Equivalent | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Air Traffic Controller | **MGC / Call Agent** | Decides *which plane goes where* (routing, signaling) |
| Airplanes carrying passengers | **Media Gateway ports (RTP streams)** | *Carry* the actual content (voice packets) |
| Runways and taxiways | **IP / MPLS Backbone** | Transport fabric for media flow |
| Ground signage / radar | **SIGTRAN + SS7 Gateway** | Carry control instructions over legacy signaling |
| Ticketing & check-in counter | **Application Server / Feature Server** | Value-added services (caller-ID, voicemail, billing) |

The ATC (softswitch) **never flies the plane**; it merely *instructs the gateway* where to send the bearer. This **separation of control from bearer** is the central design philosophy of NGN softswitching.

## 1.3 Why Softswitch Was Invented

> [!NOTE]
> **Three driving problems forced the move from hardware TDM switches to softswitches:**

1. **Proprietary Hardware Lock-in** — Traditional Class-5 switches (e.g., 5ESS, EWSD) cost millions and had vendor-locked software.
2. **Convergence Demand** — Voice, video, and data needed a *single* transport fabric (IP).
3. **Service Velocity** — New features (unified messaging, IP Centrex) took 18+ months in legacy switches; softswitches deploy features in **days via software upgrades**.

## 1.4 Two Classes of Softswitch

| Class | Also Known As | Function | Typical Deployment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class 4** | Tandem / Toll Softswitch | Inter-exchange transit, long-distance routing, SS7 termination | Carriers (BSNL, Airtel long-haul) |
| **Class 5** | End-Office / Local Softswitch | Subscriber-facing services, dial-tone, CLASS features, IP Centrex | Enterprise PBX, VoIP operators |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Plane Functional Architecture (ITU-T Y.2011)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically maps softswitch to the **four-plane NGN model** below. Every derivations / question in this module pivots around these four logical strata.

| Plane | Logical Function | Key Components | Protocols Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Access / User Plane** | Convert user traffic between TDM, DSL, Wi-Fi, FTTH and IP | IAD, xDSL Modem, ONT, Media Gateway | RTP, RTCP, 802.11, GPON |
| **2. Transport / Bearer Plane** | Packet forwarding with QoS | IP/MPLS Core, Routers, L2/L3 Switches | DiffServ, MPLS-TE, RSVP |
| **3. Control Plane** | Call/session setup, signaling routing | **Softswitch (MGC)**, HSS, ENUM server | SIP, H.323, H.248, MGCP, BICC |
| **4. Application / Service Plane** | Value-added services, OSS/BSS | Feature Server, IMS AS, Media Server, Billing | Parlay/OSA, SIP, INAP (over IP) |

## 2.2 Core Components of a Softswitch (Logical Decomposition)

### 2.2.1 Media Gateway (MG)
> A distributed edge device that performs **bearer-plane conversion** between circuit-switched (TDM E1/T1) and packet-switched (IP/RTP) domains. It is *dumb* — it executes every command issued by the MGC.

- **Functions:** Codec transcoding (G.711 ↔ G.729), echo cancellation (G.168), DTMF detection, packetization, jitter buffering.
- **Capacity:** 256 to 16,000+ DS0 channels per chassis.
- **Interface to MGC:** H.248/MEGACO or MGCP.

### 2.2.2 Media Gateway Controller (MGC) — The Softswitch Itself
> The **intelligence** of the NGN. It runs stateful call-control software, processes signaling, and commands media gateways.

- **Functions:** Call routing (ENUM/LNP), number translation, policy enforcement, CDR generation, lawful intercept.
- **Redundancy:** Active-Active or Active-Standby (1+1 geo-redundant).

### 2.2.3 Signaling Gateway (SG)
> Bridges legacy **SS7 (C7)** signaling to IP via the **SIGTRAN** protocol stack (M3UA / M2PA / M2UA over SCTP).

### 2.2.4 Application Server (AS) / Feature Server
> Hosts supplementary services — voicemail, IVR, conferencing, presence, click-to-call, IP Centrex features.

## 2.3 Protocol Stack Used Inside Softswitch (Master Mapping)

| Layer | Function | Protocol | IETF / ITU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Call Control | SIP / H.323 signaling | **SIP** (RFC 3261) / H.323v4 | IETF, ITU-T H.323 |
| MGC ↔ MG | Master-Slave control | **H.248 / MEGACO** | ITU-T H.248, IETF RFC 3525 |
| Legacy control | MGCP | MGCP 1.0 | RFC 3435 |
| SS7 over IP | Signaling transport | **SIGTRAN** (M3UA, M2PA, SUA) | RFC 4666, RFC 4166 |
| Bearer | Media transport | **RTP / RTCP** | RFC 3550 |
| Reliability | Transport for SIP/SIGTRAN | **SCTP** (multi-homing) | RFC 4960 |
| QoS Marking | Per-hop behaviour | **DiffServ (DSCP EF 46)** | RFC 2474 |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exam-table** you should memorise verbatim. The system prompt mandates using `\vert` instead of bare `\|` to keep markdown tables parseable.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RTP packet size (bytes) | $P_{RTP} = P_{payload} + H_{IP} + H_{UDP} + H_{RTP} = P_{payload} + 20 + 8 + 12$ | 200 B (G.711, 20 ms) |
| 2 | VoIP bandwidth (kbps) | $BW = \dfrac{P_{RTP} \times 8}{T_{pkt}}$ | 80 kbps (G.711) |
| 3 | Payload bytes for codec | $P_{payload} = R_{codec} \times T_{pkt} / 8$ | 160 B (G.711), 20 B (G.729) |
| 4 | cRTP compressed size | $P_{cRTP} = P_{payload} + H_{cRTP} = P_{payload} + 2 \text{ to } 4$ | 162 – 164 B |
| 5 | Concurrent calls BHCA | $N_{calls} = \dfrac{C_{MGC}}{T_{proc}} \times 3600$ | 1,00,000 / hr |
| 6 | One-way delay budget | $D_{ow} \le 150$ ms (ITU-T G.114) | ms |
| 7 | Jitter buffer size | $J_{buf} = 2 \times \sigma_{jitter}$ | 30 – 80 ms |
| 8 | Packet loss (PLC threshold) | $P_{loss} < 1\%$ (G.711) | % |
| 9 | MOS-to-R-factor mapping | $R = 93.2 - \dfrac{26.18 \cdot \ln(1 + 0.04d_{eff})}{ }  \quad$ | $MOS > 4.0$ for toll-quality |
| 10 | Erlang-B blocking prob. | $B(E, m) = \dfrac{E^m / m!}{\sum_{k=0}^{m} E^k / k!}$ | dimensionless |

> `R = 93.2 - 26.18 \cdot \ln(1 + 0.04 \cdot d_{eff})` is the **E-model R-factor** (ITU-T G.107).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telco Convergence:** Replaces proprietary Class-5 switches (>$5 M per cabinet) with COTS servers (~$50k per softswitch cluster).
- **VoIP Trunking:** Carriers like Tata Communications, Reliance Jio use softswitches for international gateway termination.
- **Unified Communications:** Enterprise UC platforms (Cisco BroadWorks, Avaya Aura, Ribbon SBC) are descendants of softswitch architecture.
- **Network Function Virtualization (NFV):** A softswitch in 2024 is typically deployed as a **VNF (Virtual Network Function)** on OpenStack/Kubernetes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition below is shown in full, with the physical interpretation of each step. **No step is skipped.** This is the model answer style the KTU Board examiner expects.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — VoIP Bandwidth for G.711 (PCM) Codec, 20 ms Packetization

**Given:** Codec G.711 (μ-law / A-law) at $R_{codec} = 64$ kbps; packetization period $T_{pkt} = 20$ ms; transport over IPv4 with 40-byte protocol stack overhead and Ethernet Layer-2 framing of 38 bytes (14 MAC + 4 VLAN + 20 FCS).

**Step 1 — Compute the raw voice payload size**

$$P_{payload} = \frac{R_{codec} \times T_{pkt}}{8}$$

$$P_{payload} = \frac{64{,}000 \ \text{bits/s} \times 0.020 \ \text{s}}{8 \ \text{bits/byte}}$$

$$P_{payload} = \frac{1280 \ \text{bits}}{8} = 160 \ \text{bytes}$$

> **Interpretation:** Every 20 ms of G.711 voice is a 160-byte PCM sample.

**Step 2 — Add the IP/UDP/RTP headers (40 bytes)**

$$P_{RTP} = P_{payload} + H_{IP} + H_{UDP} + H_{RTP} = 160 + 20 + 8 + 12 = 200 \ \text{bytes}$$

> **Interpretation:** Each RTP packet now weighs 200 bytes; only 80% of that is actually voice.

**Step 3 — Compute bandwidth on the IP layer (Layer-3 view)**

$$BW_{L3} = \frac{P_{RTP} \times 8}{T_{pkt}} = \frac{200 \times 8}{0.020}$$

$$BW_{L3} = 80{,}000 \ \text{bits/s} = 80 \ \text{kbps per call}$$

> **Interpretation:** Each G.711 VoIP call consumes 80 kbps — already 25% higher than the raw 64 kbps PCM stream.

**Step 4 — Compute bandwidth on the Ethernet (Layer-2 view, adding preamble + FCS + VLAN)**

$$P_{L2} = P_{RTP} + H_{MAC} + H_{VLAN} + H_{FCS} = 200 + 14 + 4 + 4 = 222 \ \text{bytes}$$

> *(Some textbooks also add 8-byte preamble + SFD; the standard accounting includes them in the line rate.)*

$$BW_{L2} = \frac{222 \times 8}{0.020} = 88.8 \ \text{kbps per call}$$

**Step 5 — Final result**

| Layer | Bandwidth per call |
|---|---|
| TDM (raw PCM) | 64 kbps |
| IP layer | **80 kbps** |
| Ethernet L2 | 88.8 kbps |
| With cRTP | $\dfrac{(160 + 4) \times 8}{0.020} = 65.6$ kbps |

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bandwidth for G.729 (CS-ACELP) Codec, 20 ms

**Given:** G.729 bit-rate $R_{codec} = 8$ kbps, $T_{pkt} = 20$ ms.

$$P_{payload} = \frac{8{,}000 \times 0.020}{8} = 20 \ \text{bytes}$$

$$P_{RTP} = 20 + 40 = 60 \ \text{bytes}$$

$$BW = \frac{60 \times 8}{0.020} = 24 \ \text{kbps per call}$$

> **Interpretation:** G.729 is **3.33× more bandwidth-efficient** than G.711 — this is why wide-area SIP trunks almost always use G.729.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Softswitch Capacity & Blocking Probability (Erlang-B)

**Given:** A Class-4 softswitch is offered $E = 1200$ Erlangs of traffic; the design requires Grade-of-Service $GoS \le 0.5\%$.

**Step 1 — Use the Erlang-B recursion to find required trunk count $m$.**

$$B(E, m) = \frac{E \cdot B(E, m-1)}{m + E \cdot B(E, m-1)}, \qquad B(E, 0) = 1$$

Iterate:

| $m$ | $B(E, m)$ | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| 1300 | 0.0089 | $> 0.005$ ✗ |
| 1310 | 0.0044 | $< 0.005$ ✓ |
| 1320 | 0.0021 | $< 0.005$ ✓ |

$$\boxed{m = 1310 \ \text{trunks minimum}}$$

**Step 2 — Translate trunks into concurrent VoIP calls (assuming one RTP stream per trunk):**

$$N_{calls}^{concurrent} = 1310$$

**Step 3 — Translate to required Softswitch CPU rate using $T_{proc} = 5$ ms per call-setup:**

$$R_{MGC} = \frac{N_{calls}^{concurrent}}{T_{proc}} = \frac{1310}{0.005} = 262{,}000 \ \text{call-setups/s} \ (\text{peak})$$

$$BHCA = 262{,}000 \times 3600 = 9.43 \times 10^{8} \ \text{BHCA}$$

> **Interpretation:** This BHCA figure is one full Tier-1 carrier-grade softswitch. Most operator deployments are clustered (3–5 nodes) for geo-redundancy.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — One-Way Delay Budget for VoIP (G.114)

> [!NOTE]
> **The ITU-T G.114 recommendation caps one-way mouth-to-ear delay at 150 ms for toll-quality voice.**

$$D_{ow} = D_{codec} + D_{pkt} + D_{queue} + D_{serial} + D_{prop} + D_{jbuf}$$

Numerical example (200 km fibre link, G.729):

| Component | Value |
|---|---|
| Codec algorithmic delay (G.729) | 15 ms |
| Packetization (2 × frame size) | 20 ms |
| Queueing + serialisation | 5 ms |
| Propagation (200 km @ 2×10⁸ m/s) | 1 ms |
| Jitter buffer playout | 40 ms |
| **Total** | **81 ms** ✔ (under 150 ms) |

For a 5000-km intercontinental VoIP call the budget becomes tighter; hence the use of **transcoding at SBCs** to minimise encoder look-ahead.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use Mermaid with **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (per the safeguard rule) and **plain uppercase text** inside node labels — no bold/italic markdown or HTML tables embedded inside node text.

## 4.1 Four-Plane NGN Softswitch Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AC[APPLICATION AND SERVICE PLANE]
        AS1[FEATURE SERVER CALLER ID VM IVR]
        AS2[PRESENCE SERVER]
        AS3[OSS BSS BILLING]
    end

    subgraph CC[CONTROL PLANE]
        SS[SOFTSWITCH MGC CALL AGENT]
        HSS[HOME SUBSCRIBER SERVER HSS]
        ENUM[ENUM DNS SERVER]
    end

    subgraph TT[TRANSPORT BEARER PLANE]
        CR1[IP MPLS CORE ROUTER 1]
        CR2[IP MPLS CORE ROUTER 2]
        QoS[DIFFSERV EF 46 QUEUE]
    end

    subgraph AP[ACCESS AND USER PLANE]
        MG1[MEDIA GATEWAY TDM E1]
        MG2[MEDIA GATEWAY FTTH ONT]
        IAD[INTEGRATED ACCESS DEVICE]
        UE1[SIP PHONE]
    end

    SS -- H248 MEGACO --> MG1
    SS -- H248 MEGACO --> MG2
    SS -- SIP --> IAD
    SS -- SIP --> UE1
    SS -- SIP --> AS1
    SS -- DIAMETER --> HSS
    SS -- DNS --> ENUM
    MG1 -- RTP --> CR1
    MG2 -- RTP --> CR1
    IAD -- RTP --> CR1
    UE1 -- RTP --> CR1
    CR1 -- MPLS --> QoS
    QoS -- MPLS --> CR2
    CR2 -- RTP --> MG1
    AS1 -- SIP --> SS
    AS2 -- XMPP --> AS1
    AS3 -- RADIUS --> SS
```

## 4.2 H.248 / MEGACO Call-Setup Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as SIP PHONE A
    participant SS as SOFTSWITCH MGC
    participant MG as MEDIA GATEWAY
    participant B as PSTN SUBSCRIBER B
    participant SG as SIGNALING GATEWAY
    participant STP as SS7 STP

    A->>SS: SIP INVITE
    SS->>SG: SS7 IAM via M3UA
    SG->>STP: MTP3 IAM
    STP-->>SG: ACM
    SG-->>SS: M3UA ACM
    SS->>MG: H248 ADD wild card bothway RTP
    MG-->>SS: H248 REPLY Modify
    SS->>A: SIP 100 TRYING
    SS->>A: SIP 183 SESSION PROGRESS
    STP-->>SG: ANM
    SG-->>SS: M3UA ANM
    SS->>A: SIP 200 OK
    A->>SS: SIP ACK
    Note over A,B: RTP VOICE BEARER NOW ACTIVE
    A->>SS: SIP BYE
    SS->>MG: H248 SUBTRACT terminations
    SS->>SG: SS7 REL
```

## 4.3 Softswitch Internal Module Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NI[NETWORK INTERFACE MODULE] --> CPS[呼叫 STATE MACHINE CALL PROCESSING]
    SGW[SIP GW MODULE] --> CPS
    H248G[H248 GW MODULE] --> CPS
    SS7G[SS7 SIGTRAN MODULE] --> CPS
    CPS --> RTR[ROUTING TABLE LNP ENUM]
    CPS --> POL[POLICY ENGINE QoS]
    CPS --> CDR[CDR RECORDER]
    CPS --> LI[LAWFUL INTERCEPT MODULE]
    CPS --> PRX[PROXY FORWARDING]
    PRX --> NI
    CDR --> DBS[(POSTGRESQL CDR DB)]
    LI --> LEA[LAW ENFORCEMENT AGENCY]
```

## 4.4 Class-4 vs Class-5 Softswitch Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph C4[CLASS 4 SOFTSWITCH]
        C4A[TANDEM TRANSIT]
        C4B[SS7 GATEWAY]
        C4C[LNP LOOKUP]
        C4D[MEDIA GATEWAY CONTROL]
    end
    subgraph C5[CLASS 5 SOFTSWITCH]
        C5A[LOCAL LOOP]
        C5B[IP CENTREX]
        C5C[FEATURE SERVER]
        C5D[VOICEMAIL]
    end
    C4 -- SIP H248 --> C5
    C4 -- SIP --> PSTN[(PSTN CORE)]
    C5 -- SIP --> UE[END USER DEVICES]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. Conflating **MGC** with **MG** — the MGC is the *softswitch* itself; the MG is the dumb bearer engine.
> 2. Writing **SIP** as the protocol between MGC and MG — the correct protocol is **H.248/MEGACO**, NOT SIP.
> 3. Forgetting to mention **SIGTRAN** when discussing PSTN interworking — SS7 alone does not work over IP.
> 4. Mixing up **Class 4 (tandem)** and **Class 5 (end-office)** softswitch roles.
> 5. Quoting bandwidth numbers without specifying **packetization period** (20 ms vs 10 ms changes the answer by 2×).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Softswitch. List any four of its main functions.

**Model Answer:**

> **Softswitch** is a software-based call-control element in an NGN that decouples the **signaling/control plane** from the **bearer/media plane** and performs call-processing functions using IP protocols.

**Four main functions:**

1. **Call Control & Routing** — call setup, teardown, ENUM/LNP-based number translation.
2. **Signaling Interworking** — translation between SIP/H.323 and legacy SS7 (via SIGTRAN).
3. **Resource Management** — commanding Media Gateways to allocate/release RTP terminations.
4. **Service Invocation** — triggering Application Server features (caller-ID, voicemail, IP Centrex).

*(Other valid functions: CDR generation, QoS policy enforcement, lawful intercept, billing.)*  **[3 marks]**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Distinguish between a Media Gateway (MG) and a Media Gateway Controller (MGC).

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Media Gateway (MG) | Media Gateway Controller (MGC) |
|---|---|---|
| Plane | Bearer / Media plane | Control / Signaling plane |
