| Function | Converts TDM↔RTP, codecs, echo cancellation | Call logic, routing, signaling |
| Protocol to partner | H.248/MEGACO (slave) | H.248/MEGACO (master) |
| Intelligence | Stateless, command-driven | Stateful, FSM-based |
| Example | AudioCodes Mediant 2600 | Ribbon CSX 9000, Huawei SE2900 |
| Bandwidth handled | Heavy (10 Gbps+) | Light control plane |

**[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 marks)  [KTU ESE — June 2024 expected pattern]

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the four-plane architecture of a softswitch-based NGN. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **four-plane architecture** standardised by ITU-T (Y.2011 / Y.2232) decomposes the NGN into four logical layers:

1. **Access / User Plane (Plane 1)** — Comprises customer-edge devices such as SIP phones, IADs, ONTs, xDSL modems, and Media Gateways. The MG's role here is to convert between subscriber-line signals (analog POTS, E1/T1, GPON) and packetised RTP streams.

2. **Transport / Bearer Plane (Plane 2)** — The QoS-enabled IP/MPLS backbone that carries the RTP media streams. Implements **DiffServ** (EF DSCP 46 for voice), MPLS-TE, and 1:1 redundancy for sub-50 ms failover.

3. **Control Plane (Plane 3)** — Houses the **softswitch (MGC)**, the HSS (subscriber database), ENUM server, and PSTN signaling gateway. This is where SIP / H.323 / H.248 messages terminate and call-state machines execute.

4. **Application / Service Plane (Plane 4)** — Hosts **Application Servers** providing IN-class services (prepaid, free-phone), IP Centrex, unified communications, and OSS/BSS interfaces.

The horizontal decoupling allows the operator to scale each plane independently — adding bandwidth (Plane 2) does not require upgrading the softswitch (Plane 3).  **[7 marks]**

**(b) Explain the H.248/MEGACO protocol. Discuss its role in MGC–MG communication. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**H.248/MEGACO** (ITU-T H.248.1 v3, IETF RFC 3525) is a **master-slave protocol** used between the MGC and the MG. Its salient features:

- **Object Model:** A *Context* contains one or more *Terminations*. A Termination is a logical endpoint (e.g., an RTP port, a DS0 channel). Terminations have *Properties* (signals, events, statistics) and are dynamically created/modified/deleted.

- **Commands:** Eight core commands — **Add, Modify, Subtract, Move, AuditValue, AuditCapabilities, Notify, ServiceChange**. `Add` and `Modify` carry SDP descriptors for codec negotiation.

- **Encoding:** Either **ASN.1 BER** or **text-based ABNF** (more common on Linux softswitches).

- **Transport:** UDP, TCP, or **SCTP** (preferred for reliability).

- **Call Flow Example:**
  1. MGC → MG: `Add (Termination1 = RTP, Termination2 = TDM-E1, Local {rtp-payload=PCMU})`
  2. MG → MGC: `Reply (Add)` with `Local {SDP}`
  3. ... RTP media flows ...
  4. MGC → MG: `Subtract (Termination1, Termination2)` on call release.

**Why it matters:** H.248 is *more powerful* than its predecessor MGCP because it supports multi-point contexts (conferences), text encoding, and explicit transaction grouping. It is the de-facto MGC–MG protocol in every NGN deployment from 2003 onwards.  **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 marks)  [Alternative choice]

**(a) Explain the SIGTRAN protocol stack. How does it enable SS7 signaling over IP? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SIGTRAN** (IETF RFC 4666 series) is a protocol suite that transports legacy **SS7 (Signalling System No. 7)** messages over an IP network.

**Layered Architecture:**

| Layer | Function | Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| **Signaling Application Layer** | SS7 user parts (ISUP, SCCP, TCAP, MAP) | ISUP, SCCP, TCAP, MAP, CAP |
| **SS7 Adaptation Layer** | Maps SS7 semantics onto IP | **M3UA** (MTP3-User), **M2PA** (MTP2-User Peer-to-Peer), **SUA** (SCCP-User), **M2UA** (MTP2-User) |
| **Signaling Transport Layer** | Reliable transport over IP | **SCTP** (RFC 4960) — multi-homing, 4-way handshake |
| **IP Layer** | Network layer | IPv4 / IPv6 |

**Key Protocols:**
- **M3UA** — most widely used; hides MTP3 entirely from the IP node; supports ASP / AS state models.
- **M2PA** — runs a full MTP2/3 peer; replaces SS7 links entirely; required for IP-based STPs.
- **SUA** — carries SCCP user parts (e.g., MAP, CAP) directly over SCTP, bypassing MTP3.

**Why it matters:** A softswitch can communicate with a legacy PSTN STP through a Signaling Gateway running M3UA, *without* changing the SS7 stack. This enables a graceful TDM-to-IP migration.  **[7 marks]**

**(b) Compute the VoIP bandwidth (Layer-3) required for a single G.711 call using 20 ms packetization, with and without cRTP. Also calculate the same for a G.729 call. Comment on the results. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**G.711, 20 ms, no cRTP:**

$$P_{payload} = \frac{64{,}000 \times 0.020}{8} = 160 \ \text{B}$$

$$P_{RTP} = 160 + 40 = 200 \ \text{B}$$

$$BW = \frac{200 \times 8}{0.020} = 80 \ \text{kbps}$$

**G.711, 20 ms, with cRTP** (compressed header reduced to 4 B):

$$P_{cRTP} = 160 + 4 = 164 \ \text{B}$$

$$BW_{cRTP} = \frac{164 \times 8}{0.020} = 65.6 \ \text{kbps}$$

**G.729, 20 ms, no cRTP:**

$$P_{payload} = \frac{8{,}000 \times 0.020}{8} = 20 \ \text{B}$$

$$P_{RTP} = 20 + 40 = 60 \ \text{B}$$

$$BW = \frac{60 \times 8}{0.020} = 24 \ \text{kbps}$$

**Comments:**

1. cRTP saves **~18%** bandwidth on G.711 links — vital on low-speed WAN circuits.
2. G.729 is **3.33× more efficient** than G.711 — preferred for WAN/Trunk links despite slightly lower MOS.
3. Bandwidth on the L2 (Ethernet) side is ~10% higher than L3, due to MAC + VLAN + FCS overhead.
4. A 100-Mbps Ethernet link can carry ≈ **1,250 simultaneous G.711 calls** (100,000/80).  **[7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Re-read 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Softswitch = MGC (Media Gateway Controller)** = software-based call control that separates *control plane* from *bearer plane* in an NGN.
- **Four planes** (in order): Access → Transport → Control → Application.
- **Key components:** MG, MGC, SG, AS.
- **MGC ↔ MG protocol** = **H.248 / MEGACO** (NOT SIP). Master-slave, context/termination model, eight commands.
- **Call signaling to endpoints** = **SIP** (or H.323).
- **SS7 over IP** = **SIGTRAN = SCTP + M3UA/M2PA/UA**.
- **Media transport** = **RTP / RTCP** over UDP.
- **Class 4** softswitch = tandem/transit; **Class 5** = end-office / local exchange.
- **G.711 bandwidth** = 80 kbps (20 ms pkt); **G.729** = 24 kbps; **cRTP** = 65.6 kbps for G.711.
- **One-way delay budget** = 150 ms (ITU-T G.114); jitter buffer ≈ 2× jitter std-dev.
- **Bandwidth formula:** $BW = (P_{payload} + 40) \times 8 \, / \, T_{pkt}$.
- **Erlang-B** is used to size trunk count $m$ for a given offered load $E$ and GoS.
- **SIGTRAN stack:** ISUP/SCCP/TCAP → M3UA → SCTP → IP.
- **PSTN migration pattern:** Legacy TDM switch → Signaling Gateway + Media Gateway + Softswitch → full NGN/IMS.
- **Real-world examples:** Ribbon CSX 9000, Cisco BTS 10200, Huawei SE2900, Metaswitch (now Microsoft Azure Communication Gateway).
- **2024-era deployment:** Softswitch is now typically a **VNF** running on OpenStack or **CNF** on Kubernetes, managed by an NFV orchestrator (ETSI MANO).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packet Switching Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Packet Switching Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Packet Switching Principles — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Packet Switching** is a digital networking communication paradigm in which a source message (payload) is fragmented into discrete, independently routed units of data called **packets** at the network layer (Layer 3 of the OSI/TCP-IP model). Each packet carries a **header** containing control metadata (source address, destination address, sequence number, protocol identifiers) and a **payload** containing the user data. Packets are forwarded hop-by-hop through intermediate routers using the **store-and-forward** transmission discipline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Packet switching is a connectionless (or logical-connection-oriented) message-delivery technique where resources (link bandwidth and switch buffers) are allocated on demand, only when a packet is being transmitted, in contrast to circuit switching which pre-allocates a dedicated end-to-end channel.

The two principal variants mandated by the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Datagram Packet Switching** — connectionless service.
2. **Virtual Circuit Packet Switching** — connection-oriented service (SVC & PVC).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Mail" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine you are sending a 100-page book to a friend across the country, but the courier company only accepts envelopes holding 5 pages each.
> 
> - **You (Source):** Break the book into 20 envelopes (packets). Stamp each with the destination address and a serial number (1 of 20, 2 of 20, ...).
> - **Post Office Hubs (Routers):** Each hub decides independently which truck (link) to put your envelope on based on current traffic. Your 20 envelopes may travel on **different trucks, different highways, and arrive out of order**.
> - **Your Friend (Destination):** Rearranges the envelopes in serial-number order to reconstruct the book.
> 
> This is **Datagram Packet Switching** — every envelope is treated as an independent letter. If you first called the post office and asked them to "open a special route" for your 20 envelopes, that special route is the **Virtual Circuit** — same destination, but a pre-planned logical path is reserved.

## 1.3 Why Packet Switching? — The Engineering Motivation

- **Statistical Multiplexing:** Multiple users share link capacity dynamically; idle time is reclaimed (improved utilization).
- **Resilience & Robustness:** No single point of failure; packets can be re-routed dynamically.
- **Efficient for Bursty Traffic:** Data applications (web, email) are bursty — not suited for circuit's fixed allocation.
- **Store-and-Forward Discipline:** Allows buffering, error control, and address-based forwarding.
- **Standard Metrics:** Packet size is typically bounded by the **Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU)** of the underlying link layer — commonly **1500 bytes** for Ethernet.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 outcomes for Module 4 require you to clearly distinguish *datagram* vs *virtual circuit* switching, compute *end-to-end delays* in store-and-forward networks, and analyze the *trade-off between delay and throughput*. These are the three high-weight assessment targets.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Store-and-Forward Discipline

Under store-and-forward, a router must **receive the entire packet into its buffer** before it can begin forwarding the first bit onto the outgoing link. This is the fundamental mechanism that introduces the four canonical types of delay at every node:

1. **Processing Delay ($d_{proc}$):** Time to inspect the header and decide the outgoing link. Typically **microseconds**, treated as constant or zero in KTU numericals.
2. **Queuing Delay ($d_{queue}$):** Time the packet waits in the router buffer if the outgoing link is busy. Depends on traffic intensity $\rho = La/R$.
3. **Transmission Delay ($d_{trans}$):** Time to push all $L$ bits of the packet onto the link at rate $R$. Equals $L/R$.
4. **Propagation Delay ($d_{prop}$):** Time for one bit to physically travel across the link of length $d$ at propagation speed $s$. Equals $d/s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Nodal Delay:**
> $$d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$$

## 2.2 Datagram Packet Switching (Connectionless)

- **No prior connection setup.** Each packet is independently routed.
- Routers use a **routing table** to decide the next hop on a **per-packet** basis.
- **Packets may arrive out of order** because they may traverse different paths.
- The destination reassembles packets using the **sequence number** in the header.
- Used by the **Internet Protocol (IP)**.

## 2.3 Virtual Circuit Packet Switching (Connection-Oriented)

A **Virtual Circuit (VC)** is a logical, predetermined path through the network that all packets of a session follow.

| Feature | Switched VC (SVC) | Permanent VC (PVC) |
|---|---|---|
| Setup | Per-session signalling (call setup + teardown) | Manually pre-configured by network operator |
| State | Held only for the session duration | Permanent in router tables |
| Analogy | Telephone call | Leased line |
| Used In | X.25, Frame Relay, ATM SVCs | ATM PVCs, Frame Relay PVCs, MPLS LSPs |

A virtual circuit proceeds in **three phases**:
1. **VC Setup:** Source sends a *CONNECT* request; each router along the chosen path allocates a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** for that flow.
2. **Data Transfer:** Each packet carries the VCI (not the full destination address); routers perform fast table lookups using VCI.
3. **VC Teardown:** Source sends a *DISCONNECT*; routers release the VCI.

## 2.4 Comparison: Datagram vs Virtual Circuit

| Parameter | Datagram | Virtual Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Connection Setup | Not required | Required |
| Addressing | Full source & destination in every packet | Short VCI in every packet |
| Routing Decision | Per-packet, independent | Per-VC, fixed at setup |
| Path Followed | May differ per packet | Same for all packets |
| Out-of-order Delivery | Possible | Not possible |
| Router State | Stateless (or soft-state) | Stateful (VC table) |
| Congestion Handling | Difficult (no advance reservation) | Easier (resources reserved at setup) |
| Example Protocol | IPv4, IPv6, UDP | X.25, ATM, Frame Relay, MPLS |
| Failure Recovery | Robust — alternate route auto | All VCs on failed link broken |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 80% of KTU Module-4 numericals.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmission delay (per packet) | $d_{trans} = L / R$ | seconds | $L$ = packet length in **bits**, $R$ = link rate in **bits/sec** |
| 2 | Propagation delay (per link) | $d_{prop} = d / s$ | seconds | $d$ = link length (m), $s \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper/fiber |
| 3 | End-to-end delay for **1 packet** through **N links** | $D = N \times (L/R + d/s)$ | seconds | Processing/queueing ignored (default assumption) |
| 4 | End-to-end delay for **P packets**, **store-and-forward**, **N links** | $D = (N + P - 1) \times (L/R)$ | seconds | $d_{prop}$ ignored; assumes all hops identical rate $R$ |
| 5 | Full formula (trans + prop) for P packets, N links | $D = N \times (L/R + d/s) + (P-1) \times (L/R)$ | seconds | The "pipelined store-and-forward" formula |
| 6 | Traffic intensity at a router | $\rho = (L \times a) / R$ | dimensionless | $a$ = average packet arrival rate (pkts/sec); $\rho \to 1$ means queue explodes |
| 7 | Bottleneck throughput | $Throughput = \min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N)$ | bits/sec | Slowest link in the path limits throughput |
| 8 | Total message bits transmitted | $B_{total} = P \times L$ | bits | Number of packets $\times$ size per packet |
| 9 | Number of packets | $P = \lceil M / (L - H) \rceil$ | packets | $M$ = message bits, $H$ = header bits per packet, $L$ = total packet bits |
| 10 | Time to transmit a message of $M$ bits at rate $R$ (no switching) | $T = M / R$ | seconds | Direct circuit-switched transmission time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to memorize:** Propagation speed in vacuum $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; in copper/fiber $s \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s; speed of light is never used in KTU terrestrial problems.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **Internet backbone** uses datagram switching (IP).
- **MPLS** (Multiprotocol Label Switching) and **ATM** historically used virtual circuits.
- **5G core networks** use a hybrid — GTP-U tunnels are essentially virtual circuits over an IP datagram substrate.
- Modern **SDN (Software-Defined Networking)** controllers dynamically establish virtual circuits (flows) over an IP datagram underlay.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Store-and-Forward Delay for Multiple Packets Across Multiple Hops

**Problem Setup (standard KTU 2019 / 2023 / 2024 exam type):**
- A message of $M$ bits is split into $P$ packets, each of $L$ bits.
- The path has $N$ links (i.e., $N-1$ intermediate routers, source to destination counts as link 1).
- Each link has identical transmission rate $R$ bits/sec.
- Ignore propagation delay and processing/queueing delay for clarity.

**Step 1 — Time to push one packet onto link 1 (transmission delay at source):**
$$T_1 = \frac{L}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Time for that one packet to traverse N links and reach destination:**
At every hop, the packet must be fully received before being forwarded (store-and-forward). So with $N$ links each contributing $L/R$:
$$T_{one\ packet} = N \cdot \frac{L}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Now consider the second packet. It can be transmitted onto link 1 only AFTER the first packet has fully left link 1.** So the second packet starts $L/R$ seconds after the first.
$$Gap\ between\ packet\ starts = \frac{L}{R}$$

**Step 4 — After the first packet arrives at the destination (after $N \cdot L/R$ seconds), the remaining $(P-1)$ packets are still "in the pipeline" — each separated by $L/R$ from the next.** The last (i.e., $P$-th) packet will arrive $L/R$ seconds after packet $(P-1)$ finishes crossing the last link.

$$T_{last\ packet\ arrives} = N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + (P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Final General Formula:**
$$D_{total} = (N + P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is the **single most tested equation** in KTU Module 4. Verify that for $P=1$ it reduces to $N \cdot L/R$ (correct) and for $N=1$ it reduces to $P \cdot L/R$ (correct — single-hop serialization).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Full Formula Including Propagation Delay

**Now include propagation delay $d/s$ per link, where $d$ is the link length and $s$ is propagation speed.**

**Step 1 — Time for the first bit of packet 1 to emerge from the source:** $0$.

**Step 2 — Time for the last bit of packet 1 to be pushed onto link 1:** $L/R$.

**Step 3 — Time for that last bit to physically reach router 2 (propagation across link 1):** $d/s$.

**Step 4 — Router 2 must finish receiving the last bit (which is at time $L/R + d/s$) and then start re-transmitting. After $L/R$ more seconds, the last bit of packet 1 is on link 2.** Then it propagates for $d/s$ more seconds.

**Step 5 — In general, packet 1 fully arrives at the destination after $N$ hops of $(L/R + d/s)$:**
$$T_{packet\ 1\ arrives} = N \cdot \left(\frac{L}{R} + \frac{d}{s}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Meanwhile, the source has been transmitting packets 2, 3, ..., P at intervals of $L/R$ seconds. The P-th packet begins transmission at time $(P-1) \cdot L/R$.**

**Step 7 — The P-th packet then takes $N \cdot (L/R + d/s)$ to traverse the full path.**

**Step 8 — Total End-to-End Delay (all packets received):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{full} &= N \cdot \left(\frac{L}{R} + \frac{d}{s}\right) + (P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Sanity check:** If $d = 0$ (no propagation), this collapses to the basic formula $(N + P - 1) \cdot L/R$. ✓

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Answer Style)

**Question (KTU-style):** A message of $M = 7,200$ bits is to be sent from Host A to Host B across a network with $N = 3$ links. The message is packetized into $P = 3$ packets of $L = 2,400$ bits each. Each link has rate $R = 1,200$ bps. Ignore propagation, processing, and queueing delays. Compute the total end-to-end delay for the message to fully reach Host B using store-and-forward.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify variables. $M = 7200$ bits, $P = 3$ packets, $L = 2400$ bits, $N = 3$ links, $R = 1200$ bps.

**Step 2:** Compute transmission delay per packet per link.
$$d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2400}{1200} = 2\ \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the store-and-forward formula.
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{total} &= (N + P - 1) \cdot d_{trans} \\
&= (3 + 3 - 1) \cdot 2 \\
&= 5 \cdot 2 \\
&= 10\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Cross-verify by timeline:
- $t = 0$ to $2$ s: Packet 1 on Link 1.
- $t = 2$ to $4$ s: Packet 1 on Link 2, Packet 2 on Link 1.
- $t = 4$ to $6$ s: Packet 1 on Link 3, Packet 2 on Link 2, Packet 3 on Link 1.
- $t = 6$ to $8$ s: Packet 2 on Link 3, Packet 3 on Link 2.
- $t = 8$ to $10$ s: Packet 3 on Link 3.
- $t = 10$ s: All three packets received at Host B. ✓

**Answer:** $D_{total} = 10$ seconds.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Formula

| Scenario | Variables Given | Formula to Apply |
|---|---|---|
| Single packet, single link | $L, R$ | $D = L/R$ |
| Single packet, multiple links, no prop delay | $L, R, N$ | $D = N \cdot L/R$ |
| Multiple packets, single link | $L, R, P$ | $D = P \cdot L/R$ |
| Multiple packets, multiple links, no prop delay | $L, R, P, N$ | $D = (N + P - 1) \cdot L/R$ |
| Same as above **with** propagation delay | $L, R, P, N, d, s$ | $D = N(L/R + d/s) + (P-1)L/R$ |
| Throughput of multi-link path | $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_N$ | $Th = \min(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_N)$ |
| Number of packets for given message | $M, L, H$ | $P = \lceil M/(L-H) \rceil$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Datagram vs Virtual Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Host S] -->|Packet 1, Seq 1| R1A[Router R1]
    A -->|Packet 2, Seq 2| R1B[Router R1]
    R1A --> R2A[Router R2]
    R1B --> R2B[Router R2]
    R2A --> D1[Destination D]
    R2B --> D2[Destination D]
    D1 --> RASS[Reassembly at Destination]
    D2 --> RASS
    RASS --> OUT[Complete Message]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Caption:** In **datagram switching**, the two packets take **different paths** through routers (R1→R2 and R1→R2 alternatively shown for emphasis). The destination reassembles them using the sequence number. No prior connection setup existed.

## 4.2 Virtual Circuit Setup — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Phase 1: VC Setup] --> P2[Phase 2: Data Transfer]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: VC Teardown]
    P1A[S sends CONNECT to R1] --> P1B[R1 forwards to R2]
    P1B --> P1C[R2 forwards to R3]
    P1C --> P1D[R3 reaches D, VCI 22 assigned]
    P2A[S sends Data with VCI 22] --> P2B[Each router forwards based on VCI 22]
    P2B --> P2C[D receives all data via same logical path]
    P3A[S sends DISCONNECT] --> P3B[Routers release VCI 22]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Caption:** A **Virtual Circuit** reserves a logical path and a VCI tag (here, **22**) at every router. All subsequent packets carry only the short VCI, not the full destination address — enabling **faster forwarding**.

## 4.3 Generic Packet Structure (Network Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PKT[Packet at Network Layer] --> H[Header]
    PKT --> PAY[Payload Data]
    PKT --> T1[Trailer optional]
    H --> H1[Source Address - 32 bits for IPv4]
    H --> H2[Destination Address - 32 bits for IPv4]
    H --> H3[Sequence Number]
    H --> H4[Protocol Field]
    H --> H5[TTL or Hop Limit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Caption:** A network-layer packet (e.g., IPv4) has a **header** (control info), a **payload** (upper-layer data), and sometimes a **trailer** (CRC / checksum). The header is the source of all routing decisions.

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix Visual: Datagram vs Virtual Circuit Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DATAGRAM[Datagram Mode]
        DS[Source S] --> DR1[Router 1]
        DR1 --> DR2A[Router 2 path A]
        DR1 --> DR2B[Router 2 path B]
        DR2A --> DD[Destination D]
        DR2B --> DD
    end
    subgraph VC[Virtual Circuit Mode]
        VS[Source S] --> VR1[Router 1 - VCI 22]
        VR1 --> VR2[Router 2 - VCI 22]
        VR2 --> VR3[Router 3 - VCI 22]
        VR3 --> VD[Destination D]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** In datagram mode, both packets may diverge. In virtual circuit mode, every packet of the session traverses the *exact same* set of routers in the *exact same order*, identified by the common VCI label.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions expect a crisp **definition + 2 supporting points + a one-line example** in **3-mark** answers. Avoid rambling — be precise.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Distinguish between Datagram and Virtual Circuit packet switching. Give one example protocol for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Datagram | Virtual Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Connectionless — no setup | Connection-oriented — setup required |
| Packet Path | Each packet routed independently | All packets follow the same pre-established path |
| Header | Full source + destination address in every packet | Short VCI label in every packet |
| Example | **IP (Internet Protocol)** | **X.25, ATM, MPLS** |

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is store-and-forward transmission? Why is it essential in packet switching?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Store-and-forward** is the transmission discipline in which a router must **receive the entire packet into its local buffer** before it can begin transmitting the first bit onto the outgoing link.

**Why essential:**
1. Enables the router to **inspect the header and make a routing decision** (so it must have the full packet).
2. Allows **error checking** on the incoming link before re-transmission.
3. Provides **buffering** so that packets can wait their turn on a busy outgoing link.
4. Simplifies **reliability and flow control** at the link layer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q1):** Students often write "datagram is faster than VC" — this is **not always true** and not accepted. Stick to *connectionless vs connection-oriented* and *per-packet vs per-flow routing*. Mention the protocol example — without it, you lose 1 mark.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions of Module 4 carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks). Solve both sub-parts completely. Show all units in numerical answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, repeated in 2024 series]

**A message of $M = 8,000$ bits is to be transmitted from Source to Destination across a network with $N = 4$ links. The message is divided into $P = 4$ packets of equal size. Each link has transmission rate $R = 2$ kbps.**

**(a)** Compute the total end-to-end delay for the entire message to reach the destination using **store-and-forward** packet switching. Assume propagation, processing, and queueing delays are negligible. (7 marks)

**(b)** Now assume that instead of packet switching, the network uses **circuit switching** with a circuit-setup time of $T_s = 0.5$ seconds. Compute the total time to deliver the message. Compare and comment on which method is faster for this scenario. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Packet Switching:**

**Step 1:** Packet length.
$$L = \frac{M}{P} = \frac{8000}{4} = 2000\ \text{bits}$$

**Step 2:** Transmission delay per packet per link.
$$d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2000}{2000} = 1\ \text{second}$$

**Step 3:** Apply store-and-forward formula.
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{packet} &= (N + P - 1) \cdot d_{trans} \\
&= (4 + 4 - 1) \cdot 1 \\
&= 7\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (a):** $\boxed{D_{packet} = 7\ \text{seconds}}$

**Part (b) — Circuit Switching:**

**Step 1:** Circuit setup time.
$$T_s = 0.5\ \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Transmission time of the full message on one dedicated channel of rate $R$.
$$T_{tx} = \frac{M}{R} = \frac{8000}{2000} = 4\ \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3:** Total time.
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{circuit} &= T_s + T_{tx} \\
&= 0.5 + 4 \\
&= 4.5\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Comparison.
$$D_{packet} = 7\ \text{s} \quad \text{vs} \quad D_{circuit} = 4.5\ \text{s}$$

**Comment:** In this specific scenario, **circuit switching is faster** (by 2.5 s). However, circuit switching wastes the dedicated channel for the entire duration even if data arrives in bursts. Packet switching is preferred when traffic is **bursty** or when multiple users share the link (statistical multiplexing). For this small message, the overhead of pipelining across 4 links dominates, making circuit switching momentarily better.

**Answer (b):** $\boxed{D_{circuit} = 4.5\ \text{seconds, faster by 2.5 s, but less efficient for bursty traffic.}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question A):**
> 1. Students forget to convert **kbps to bps**. Here 2 kbps = 2000 bps, not 2000 bits/ms. Always show the unit conversion step.
> 2. Using the wrong formula — the trap is using $N \cdot P \cdot L/R = 16$ s. That is **WRONG**. The correct pipeline formula gives **7 s**.
> 3. In part (b), forgetting to add the setup time $T_s$ — you lose 1 mark for the missing 0.5 s.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, repeated pattern in 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Virtual Circuit (VC)** approach to packet switching with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** and the three phases of a virtual circuit. (7 marks)

**(b)** A network path has 5 links. Each link is 1,000 km long. The propagation speed in the medium is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. A packet of size 1,500 bytes is sent. Compute: (i) propagation delay per link, (ii) transmission delay per link (rate = 1 Mbps), (iii) total time for the packet to reach the destination. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Virtual Circuit Explanation (7 marks):**

A **Virtual Circuit (VC)** is a logical connection established between source and destination before any data packet is transmitted. It mimics circuit switching's guarantee of a fixed path, but achieves it through **VCI labels** at each router rather than a physical dedicated channel.

**Three Phases of a Virtual Circuit:**

1. **VC Setup Phase:** Source sends a *CONNECT* request. Each router along the chosen path assigns a unique **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** for this flow and stores a forwarding entry: *incoming link + VCI → outgoing link + VCI*. Once the *CONNECT* reaches the destination, an acknowledgement is returned.

2. **Data Transfer Phase:** Each data packet carries the **VCI** (typically 8–16 bits) in its header. At each router, a simple VCI-table lookup determines the outgoing link and outgoing VCI (which may be translated). The destination address is not consulted per-packet, making forwarding very fast.

3. **VC Teardown Phase:** Source sends a *DISCONNECT* signal. Each router along the path deletes the VCI entry and frees resources.

**Role of VCI:**
- Acts as a **short local tag** that uniquely identifies the flow within a single router.
- Avoids the overhead of full destination-address lookups in every packet.
- Enables **fast hardware-based forwarding** (foundational principle behind MPLS).

**Diagram:** (Show the setup–transfer–teardown flow as in Section 4.2 of these notes.)

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Given values.
- $N = 5$ links
- $d = 1,000$ km $= 10^6$ m per link
- $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Packet size $L = 1,500$ bytes $= 1,500 \times 8 = 12,000$ bits
- $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps

**(i) Propagation delay per link:**
$$d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 5\ \text{ms}$$

**(ii) Transmission delay per link:**
$$d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12,000}{10^6} = 12 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 12\ \text{ms}$$

**(iii) Total time for the packet to traverse all 5 links (single packet, $P = 1$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{total} &= N \cdot (d_{trans} + d_{prop}) \\
&= 5 \cdot (12\ \text{ms} + 5\ \text{ms}) \\
&= 5 \cdot 17\ \text{ms} \\
&= 85\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (b):**
- (i) $d_{prop} = 5$ ms
- (ii) $d_{trans} = 12$ ms
- (iii) $\boxed{D_{total} = 85\ \text{ms}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. In part (a), **not drawing a diagram** costs at least 2 marks out of 7. Always include a labelled block/flow diagram.
> 2. Confusing the **three phases of VC** (Setup, Data, Teardown) with the **layers of OSI model** — they are entirely different concepts.
> 3. In part (b), **not converting bytes to bits** ($1500$ bytes = $12,000$ bits) is the single most common arithmetic error. Show the conversion explicitly.
> 4. Using the **multi-packet formula** $(N + P - 1) \cdot L/R$ when only a **single packet** is being sent. The correct single-packet formula is $N \cdot (L/R + d/s)$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Packet Switching Principles**

- **Core Definition:** Packet switching transmits data as **independent, addressed, store-and-forward units** over a shared network.
- **Two Flavors:** **Datagram** (connectionless, e.g., IP) and **Virtual Circuit** (connection-oriented, e.g., ATM, MPLS, X.25).
- **Store-and-Forward Formula (most important):** $D = (N + P - 1) \cdot (L/R)$ for $P$ packets, $N$ links, identical rate $R$, ignoring propagation.
- **Full Formula with Propagation:** $D = N \cdot (L/R + d/s) + (P-1) \cdot (L/R)$.
- **Nodal Delay:** $d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$.
- **VC has 3 phases:** Setup → Data Transfer → Teardown.
- **VCI (Virtual Circuit Identifier):** short local label that identifies a flow at a router; replaced at every hop (in/out VCI may differ).
- **Packet Format:** Header (addresses + control) + Payload + (optional) Trailer.
- **Constants:** $s \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber/copper; never use $3 \times 10^8$ m/s unless specified.
- **Unit Conversions:** $1$ byte $= 8$ bits; $1$ kbps $= 10^3$ bps; $1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps; $1$ km $= 10^3$ m.
- **MTU:** Maximum packet size on a link; Ethernet MTU = **1500 bytes**.
- **Common Comparison Traps:** Datagram is *not always* faster than VC; the comparison depends on message length, link count, and traffic burstiness.
- **Throughput Bottleneck:** Slowest link in the path limits the end-to-end throughput.
- **Reliability:** Datagram is robust to link failure (alternate routes); VC on a single failed link breaks the whole circuit.
- **Address Lookup Cost:** Datagram → full address per packet (slower); VC → short VCI per packet (faster).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing in Packet-Switched Networks: Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Routing in Packet-Switched Networks: Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Routing in packet-switched networks** is the process of selecting a path (or set of paths) along which data packets are forwarded from a source node to a destination node across one or more intermediate routers. The **characteristics of routing** refer to the qualitative and quantitative attributes that define how well a routing mechanism performs its function. These characteristics are categorized into two broad groups:

1. **Qualitative (Correctness) Characteristics** – Desirable properties that a routing algorithm must possess.
2. **Quantitative (Performance) Characteristics** – Measurable metrics used to compare different routing strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEITT412 - Module 4):** The syllabus specifically mandates the study of **routing characteristics** which is a high-weightage, frequently asked concept in university examinations, often appearing as a 3-mark or 7-mark sub-question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forouzan & Kurose-Ross Convention:** In the KTU-recommended textbooks, routing characteristics are typically enumerated as **Correctness, Simplicity, Robustness, Stability, Fairness, and Optimality**, alongside performance metrics like **Delay, Throughput, and Hop Count**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving in an unfamiliar city. You have two tools available:
- A **paper map** (static routing) – you pre-decide the path.
- A **GPS navigation app** like Google Maps (dynamic routing) – it constantly recomputes the route based on current traffic.

A good GPS app must be:
- **Correct** – It must not guide you to a closed road or wrong city.
- **Simple** – The interface must be easy to understand.
- **Robust** – If the GPS loses signal in a tunnel, it must still get you home.
- **Stable** – The route shouldn't keep flipping back and forth between two highways every 5 seconds.
- **Fair** – It should not deliberately direct you into a 2 km traffic jam when alternatives exist.
- **Optimal** – The chosen path should minimize your travel time (a quantitative metric).

Packet routing works **exactly the same way**: a router is the "GPS" of the network, and packets are the "cars." The characteristics of routing define the **rules of good GPS behavior** in the digital highway system.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Performance Metrics** used in routing include **Hop Count (H)**, **End-to-End Delay (D)**, **Throughput (T)**, **Network Load (L)**, and **Path Cost (C)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Routing Characteristics

Routing characteristics are divided into two primary pillars:

#### A. Qualitative Characteristics (Correctness Properties)
These are desirable **design goals** that a routing algorithm should fulfill.

1. **Correctness**
   - The routing algorithm must be able to **deliver every packet to its correct final destination**.
   - A routing scheme that loses packets or delivers them to the wrong node is fundamentally broken, regardless of how fast it is.
   - **Why?** Without correctness, no higher-level application can function reliably.

2. **Simplicity**
   - The routing algorithm should be **easy to implement** and computationally light.
   - Routers often have constrained CPU and memory resources, especially in the network core.
   - A simple algorithm means less processing delay at each hop, leading to faster forwarding.

3. **Robustness (Reliability)**
   - The routing algorithm must function correctly even in the face of **hardware failures, link failures, or topological changes**.
   - If a link goes down, the routing protocol should reconverge to a new valid path automatically.
   - **How?** Through distributed computation and regular exchange of topology information (e.g., in OSPF, IS-IS).

4. **Stability**
   - The algorithm must **converge to a steady state** and stay there as long as the topology does not change.
   - A stable algorithm does not suffer from *routing oscillations* (where packets keep being looped between two routers forever).
   - **Real-world utility:** Essential for Voice over IP (VoIP) and video conferencing, where oscillating routes cause jitter.

5. **Fairness**
   - The algorithm should **treat every source-destination pair equitably**.
   - A path that is optimal for one flow must not be repeatedly chosen at the cost of starving another flow.
   - **Engineering trade-off:** Sometimes maximum overall throughput is preferred over strict fairness (e.g., in backbone networks).

6. **Optimality**
   - The chosen path should be the **best path** with respect to some criterion (minimum hops, minimum delay, minimum cost).
   - **The "Why" behind optimality:** Networks are shared resources, and a sub-optimal path wastes bandwidth, increases delay, and reduces user experience.

#### B. Quantitative Characteristics (Performance Metrics)
These are **measurable quantities** used to numerically compare routing decisions.

1. **Number of Hops (H)**
   - The number of intermediate routers a packet must traverse to reach the destination.
   - **Unit:** Pure integer count.
   - **Why it matters:** More hops = more processing delay and more points of failure.

2. **Delay (D)**
   - The time taken for a packet to travel from source to destination.
   - It has four components: **Transmission delay, Propagation delay, Processing delay, and Queuing delay**.
   - **Unit:** **Seconds (s)** or **milliseconds (ms)**.

3. **Throughput (T)**
   - The number of bits successfully delivered per unit time across the network.
   - **Unit:** **bits per second (bps)** or its derivatives (kbps, Mbps, Gbps).
   - **Why it matters:** A high-throughput route is preferred for bulk data transfers.

4. **Network Load (L)**
   - The fraction of the network's total capacity currently being used.
   - **Unit:** **Percentage (%)** or **Erlangs** (in teletraffic engineering).
   - Routing algorithms should **avoid congested paths** to maintain low delay.

5. **Path Cost (C)**
   - A generalized metric (often a weighted sum of delay, bandwidth, and hops) assigned by the network administrator.
   - **Unit:** **Dimensionless cost units** (administrative weight).

### 2.2 Decision Time & Place of Routing Information

A second layer of characteristics describes **how** routing decisions are made:

- **Packet-level vs. Session-level (Time-based):**
  - In *datagram networks* (e.g., the Internet), each packet is routed independently → **per-packet decision**.
  - In *virtual-circuit networks* (e.g., ATM, Frame Relay), a path is set up once for the entire session → **per-session decision**.

- **Local vs. Global (Information-based):**
  - **Local decisions:** Made by each router using only its own table.
  - **Global decisions:** Made with knowledge of the entire network topology (e.g., link-state routing).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Symbol | Defining Equation | Standard Unit | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total End-to-End Delay | $D_{total}$ | $D_{total} = D_{trans} + D_{prop} + D_{proc} + D_{queue}$ | **seconds (s)** | $\geq 0$ |
| Transmission Delay | $D_{trans}$ | $D_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds | $L \to$ packet length (bits), $R \to$ link rate (bps) |
| Propagation Delay | $D_{prop}$ | $D_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | seconds | $d \to$ link length (m), $s \to$ signal speed (m/s) |
| Hop Count | $H$ | $H = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} 1$ along the path | integer | $H \geq 0$ |
| Throughput | $T$ | $T = \min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N)$ for a path of $N$ links | **bps** | $T \leq \min(R_i)$ |
| Path Cost (weighted) | $C_{path}$ | $C_{path} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} w_i$ | dimensionless | $C \geq 0$ |
| Network Load | $L$ | $L = \dfrac{\text{Traffic Carried}}{\text{Total Capacity}}$ | ratio (0 to 1) | $0 \leq L \leq 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The unit of **Throughput is bps (bits per second)**, NOT packets per second. Many students incorrectly write "pps" and lose the unit-mark.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern networks, these characteristics directly influence **Quality of Service (QoS)** provisioning:
- **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)** prioritizes *stability* and *policy* over raw speed.
- **OSPF** uses *link cost* (inverse of bandwidth) to balance load and improve *optimality*.
- **MPLS Traffic Engineering** explicitly optimizes *path cost* to guarantee *fairness* across competing flows.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum-Cost Routing Equation

In a packet-switched network, we represent the topology as a **weighted graph** $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ is the set of routers (vertices).
- $E$ is the set of links (edges).
- Each edge $(i, j)$ has a cost $c_{ij}$ (representing delay, hop count, or administrative weight).

The problem of optimal routing is: **Find the minimum-cost path $P_{s \to d}$ between a source $s$ and destination $d$.**

Formally, the minimum cost is:

$$
C_{min}(s, d) = \min_{P \in \mathcal{P}_{s,d}} \left( \sum_{(i,j) \in P} c_{ij} \right)
$$

where $\mathcal{P}_{s,d}$ is the set of all possible paths from $s$ to $d$.

### 3.2 Derivation of End-to-End Delay

For a packet traversing a path of $N$ links, the total delay is the **sum of the delays introduced at each link** plus the processing delays at each of the $(N-1)$ intermediate routers:

$$
D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( D_{trans,i} + D_{prop,i} \right) + \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} D_{proc,j}
$$

Now, expand each component:

**Step 1 — Transmission Delay on link $i$:**
The transmission delay is the time taken to push all the packet's bits onto the physical medium. If the packet has length $L$ bits and the link bandwidth is $R_i$ bps:
$$
D_{trans,i} = \frac{L}{R_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Propagation Delay on link $i$:**
The propagation delay is the time for a single bit to physically travel across the medium. If the physical length is $d_i$ meters and the signal speed is $s_i$ m/s:
$$
D_{prop,i} = \frac{d_i}{s_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Processing Delay at router $j$:**
The processing delay $D_{proc,j}$ depends on the router's CPU, lookup table size, and queue management. For an analytical model, we often approximate it as a constant $\tau_j$ per router.

**Step 4 — Queuing Delay:**
The queuing delay $D_{queue,j}$ at router $j$ depends on the traffic load. By Little's Law applied to an M/M/1 queue:
$$
D_{queue,j} = \frac{\rho_j}{\mu_j - \lambda_j} = \frac{\lambda_j}{\mu_j(\mu_j - \lambda_j)}
$$
where $\lambda_j$ is the arrival rate, $\mu_j$ is the service rate, and $\rho_j = \lambda_j / \mu_j$ is the utilization.

**Step 5 — Final Combined Equation:**

$$
D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{L}{R_i} + \frac{d_i}{s_i} \right) + \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} \left( D_{proc,j} + D_{queue,j} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for routing:** An "optimal" route in a delay-sensitive application (e.g., video call) is the one that **minimizes this entire expression**, not just the hop count. This is the foundation of QoS routing.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Routing Strategies

A KTU-favorite analytical question asks students to **compare** routing characteristics. The following matrix is a high-yield comparison:

| Characteristic | Static (Non-Adaptive) Routing | Dynamic (Adaptive) Routing |
|---|---|---|
| **Correctness** | Yes (if configured correctly) | Yes |
| **Simplicity** | **Very High** | Moderate to Low |
| **Robustness** | **Low** (manual reconfiguration) | **High** (auto-reconverges) |
| **Stability** | **Very High** (no oscillation) | Moderate (risk of oscillation) |
| **Fairness** | Moderate | **High** |
| **Optimality** | Sub-optimal (pre-computed) | Near-optimal (adapts to load) |
| **Overhead** | **Zero** (after setup) | High (control traffic) |
| **Delay** | Fixed | Variable (improves with time) |
| **Example Protocol** | Static routes in Linux | **OSPF, BGP, RIP** |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A packet of length **1500 bytes** travels across 3 links. Link rates are $R_1 = 1$ Mbps, $R_2 = 100$ Mbps, $R_3 = 10$ Mbps. Physical distances are $d_1 = 1000$ m, $d_2 = 500$ m, $d_3 = 2000$ m. Signal speed $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Processing delay per router $= 1$ ms. Find the total delay, assuming negligible queuing.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units:** $L = 1500 \times 8 = 12000$ bits.

**Step 2 — Transmission delays:**
$$
D_{trans,1} = \frac{12000}{1 \times 10^6} = 12 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
D_{trans,2} = \frac{12000}{100 \times 10^6} = 0.12 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
D_{trans,3} = \frac{12000}{10 \times 10^6} = 1.2 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Propagation delays:**
$$
D_{prop,1} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \, \mu s = 0.005 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
D_{prop,2} = \frac{500}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \, \mu s = 0.0025 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
D_{prop,3} = \frac{2000}{2 \times 10^8} = 10 \, \mu s = 0.01 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Processing delays:** 2 intermediate routers $\times$ 1 ms = $2$ ms.

**Step 5 — Total delay:**
$$
D_{total} = (12 + 0.12 + 1.2) + (0.005 + 0.0025 + 0.01) + 2
$$
$$
D_{total} = 13.32 \text{ ms} + 0.0175 \text{ ms} + 2 \text{ ms} = 15.3375 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Insight:** Always show unit conversion in Step 1. KTU examiners award a partial mark for unit awareness even if the final number is wrong.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Routing Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Packet Arrives at Router] --> B{Valid IP Header?}
    B -- No --> X1[Drop Packet, Send ICMP Error]
    B -- Yes --> C{Longest Prefix Match in Routing Table}
    C -- No Match --> Y1[Use Default Route or Drop]
    C -- Match Found --> D[Apply Routing Characteristics]
    D --> E1[Correctness: Verify Next-Hop is Reachable]
    D --> E2[Simplicity: Single Table Lookup]
    D --> E3[Robustness: Check Link State via OSPF/IS-IS]
    D --> E4[Optimality: Choose Min-Cost Path from LSDB]
    E1 --> F[Decrement TTL]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Forward Packet to Output Interface]
    G --> H[Update Performance Metrics: Delay, Throughput, Load]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Classification of Routing Characteristics

```mermaid
graph LR
    RC[Routing Characteristics]
    RC --> QC[Qualitative Correctness Properties]
    RC --> QM[Quantitative Performance Metrics]
    QC --> QC1[Correctness]
    QC --> QC2[Simplicity]
    QC --> QC3[Robustness]
    QC --> QC4[Stability]
    QC --> QC5[Fairness]
    QC --> QC6[Optimality]
    QM --> QM1[Hop Count H]
    QM --> QM2[End-to-End Delay D]
    QM --> QM3[Throughput T]
    QM --> QM4[Network Load L]
    QM --> QM5[Path Cost C]
    QM1 --> APP[Used in RIP]
    QM2 --> APP2[Used in OSPF Cost Calculation]
    QM3 --> APP
    QM4 --> APP2
    QM5 --> APP2
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Trade-off Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[Correctness Pillar]
        A1[Packet Delivered to Right Destination]
    end
    subgraph T2[Simplicity Pillar]
        A2[Low CPU, Low Memory Footprint]
    end
    subgraph T3[Robustness Pillar]
        A3[Auto-Recovery from Link Failure]
    end
    subgraph T4[Optimality Pillar]
        A4[Minimum Cost Path Computed]
    end
    A1 --- X1{Trade-off Hub}
    A2 --- X1
    A3 --- X1
    A4 --- X1
    X1 --> O1[Choose Routing Algorithm: Static or Dynamic]
    X1 --> O2[Choose Routing Metric: Hops, Delay, Cost]
    X1 --> O3[Choose Decision Mode: Local or Global]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In KTU board exams, schematic block diagrams (especially the Trade-off Hub model) are worth **2-3 extra marks** if drawn neatly with labels. They demonstrate *systems thinking* which examiners specifically reward under the CO (Course Outcome) framework.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List and briefly explain the **performance criteria** used in routing of packet-switched networks.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The performance criteria (quantitative characteristics) of routing in packet-switched networks are:

1. **Number of Hops (H):** The number of intermediate routers a packet must traverse. A path with fewer hops is generally preferred, as it reduces processing delays and points of failure.

2. **Delay (D):** The total time taken for a packet to travel from source to destination, comprising transmission, propagation, processing, and queuing delays. Lower delay is critical for real-time applications.

3. **Throughput (T):** The number of bits successfully delivered per unit time (in **bps**). A route with higher throughput is preferred for bulk data transfer.

4. **Network Load (L):** The fraction of the network's capacity currently being used. Routing algorithms should avoid heavily loaded paths to prevent congestion.

5. **Path Cost (C):** A weighted metric combining delay, bandwidth, and administrative policy. The minimum-cost path is selected for forwarding.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Differentiate between **correctness** and **optimality** as routing characteristics with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Correctness** is a *qualitative* property that ensures the routing algorithm delivers every packet to its **intended destination** without loss or misrouting. For example, if a packet from Host A is destined for Host B, the algorithm must ensure it reaches B, not C.

- **Optimality** is a *quantitative performance goal* that ensures the chosen path is the **best** among all correct paths, based on a metric. For example, between two correct paths from A to B (one with 3 hops, one with 5 hops), the optimal algorithm selects the 3-hop path.

**Key distinction:** Correctness is a *necessary* condition (binary: works or does not), while optimality is a *quality* measure (degree of efficiency).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often lose marks by **listing characteristics without examples**. The board requires at least one real-world example or diagram for full marks in the qualitative questions. Also, units in numerical answers (especially ms vs. s) must match the standard KTU answer key.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **desirable characteristics** of a routing algorithm in packet-switched networks. Discuss each characteristic with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A good routing algorithm must possess the following **six characteristics**:

1. **Correctness:** The algorithm must deliver every packet to its **correct final destination**. For example, in an e-commerce transaction, the order packet must reach the order-processing server, not the inventory server.

2. **Simplicity:** The algorithm should be computationally efficient. For example, **RIP** uses a simple hop-count metric, requiring minimal CPU and memory, suitable for small LANs.

3. **Robustness:** The algorithm must adapt to **link or router failures**. For example, in **OSPF**, if a router fails, the link-state advertisements (LSAs) trigger a re-convergence within seconds, and packets are rerouted automatically.

4. **Stability:** The algorithm must **converge to a fixed routing table** and avoid oscillations. For example, BGP uses a path-vector mechanism with route damping to suppress oscillating routes, ensuring stable inter-AS routing.

5. **Fairness:** The algorithm should not **starve any source-destination pair**. For example, in MPLS Traffic Engineering, multiple flows are load-balanced across equal-cost paths to ensure fair resource allocation.

6. **Optimality:** The algorithm should choose the **minimum-cost path**. For example, OSPF computes the shortest path using Dijkstra's algorithm, where the cost is the inverse of link bandwidth, ensuring the optimal use of fast links.

---

**(b)** A packet of length **2000 bytes** is transmitted over 4 links with bandwidths $R_1 = 500$ kbps, $R_2 = 2$ Mbps, $R_3 = 1$ Mbps, and $R_4 = 4$ Mbps. Each link has a propagation delay of **5 ms**. There are 3 intermediate routers, each with a processing delay of **2 ms**. Calculate the **total end-to-end delay** assuming zero queuing delay. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert packet length to bits: $L = 2000 \times 8 = 16000$ bits.

**Step 2:** Compute transmission delay for each link:
$$
D_{trans,1} = \frac{16000}{500 \times 10^3} = 32 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
D_{trans,2} = \frac{16000}{2 \times 10^6} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
D_{trans,3} = \frac{16000}{1 \times 10^6} = 16 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
D_{trans,4} = \frac{16000}{4 \times 10^6} = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3:** Total transmission delay:
$$
\sum D_{trans} = 32 + 8 + 16 + 4 = 60 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4:** Total propagation delay: $4 \times 5 = 20$ ms.

**Step 5:** Total processing delay: $3 \times 2 = 6$ ms.

**Step 6:** Apply the total delay formula:
$$
D_{total} = \sum D_{trans} + \sum D_{prop} + \sum D_{proc}
$$
$$
D_{total} = 60 + 20 + 6 = 86 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $D_{total} = 86$ **milliseconds (ms)**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **static (non-adaptive) routing** and **dynamic (adaptive) routing** in terms of the **qualitative and quantitative characteristics** of routing. Use a comparative table in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Characteristic | Static Routing | Dynamic Routing |
|---|---|---|
| **Correctness** | Correct if manually configured | Correct (self-updates) |
| **Simplicity** | Very simple (manual table) | Complex (requires protocol) |
| **Robustness** | **Poor** (no auto-failover) | **Excellent** (re-converges on failure) |
| **Stability** | Very high (no oscillation) | Moderate (possible transient loops) |
| **Fairness** | Limited (no load awareness) | High (responds to congestion) |
| **Optimality** | Sub-optimal (pre-computed) | Near-optimal (real-time updates) |
| **Hop Count** | Fixed | Minimized dynamically |
| **Delay** | Fixed (often high) | Adapts to traffic conditions |
| **Throughput** | Limited by static path | Maximized by adaptive selection |
| **Network Load** | Ignored (may overload links) | Balanced via load-aware metrics |
| **Path Cost** | Administrator-defined only | Computed using live metrics |
| **Example** | Linux `route add` command | **OSPF, BGP, RIP** |

**Conclusion:** Static routing is preferred in small, stable networks where simplicity is paramount. Dynamic routing is essential in large, dynamic networks like the Internet, where robustness, fairness, and optimality are critical.

---

**(b)** Derive the expression for **end-to-end delay** in a packet-switched network for a packet traversing $N$ links. Identify the **routing characteristics** that influence each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:** End-to-end delay is the time elapsed between the first bit of the packet being transmitted at the source and the last bit being received at the destination.

**Step 2 — Decomposition:** The total delay has four components:
- **Transmission delay** ($D_{trans}$) — depends on packet size and link bandwidth.
- **Propagation delay** ($D_{prop}$) — depends on physical distance and signal speed.
- **Processing delay** ($D_{proc}$) — depends on router CPU and routing table lookup speed (linked to **Simplicity**).
- **Queuing delay** ($D_{queue}$) — depends on traffic load and congestion (linked to **Network Load**).

**Step 3 — Formulation:**
$$
D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} D_{trans,i} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} D_{prop,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} D_{proc,j} + \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} D_{queue,j}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand each term:**
$$
D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{L}{R_i} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{d_i}{s_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} \tau_j + \sum_{j=1}^{N-1} D_{queue,j}
$$

**Step 5 — Routing characteristics mapping:**

| Component | Influencing Characteristic |
|---|---|
| $D_{trans} = L / R$ | **Throughput, Path Cost** |
| $D_{prop} = d / s$ | **Hop Count** (longer paths = more propagation) |
| $D_{proc} = \tau$ | **Simplicity** of routing algorithm |
| $D_{queue}$ | **Robustness, Fairness, Network Load** |

**Conclusion:** A **minimum-delay route** minimizes this entire expression, which is the goal of optimal routing in delay-sensitive applications.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams**

- ✅ **Six Qualitative Characteristics** (memorize in order): **Correctness, Simplicity, Robustness, Stability, Fairness, Optimality**.
- ✅ **Five Quantitative Metrics**: **Hop Count, Delay, Throughput, Network Load, Path Cost**.
- ✅ **Delay Formula (must memorize):** $D_{total} = D_{trans} + D_{prop} + D_{proc} + D_{queue}$.
- ✅ **Transmission Delay Unit:** $D_{trans} = L / R$ → result in **seconds**.
- ✅ **Throughput Unit:** Always **bps** (bits per second), not packets per second.
- ✅ **Static vs. Dynamic Routing** — Static = simple but not robust; Dynamic = robust but complex.
- ✅ **Decision Modes** — Per-packet (datagram) vs. Per-session (virtual circuit).
- ✅ **Information Modes** — Local (distance-vector like RIP) vs. Global (link-state like OSPF).
- ✅ **Hop Count** is an integer; it cannot be negative or fractional.
- ✅ **Path Cost** is a dimensionless administrative metric — the minimum-cost path is optimal.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Higher optimality often comes at the cost of simplicity and stability.
- ✅ Always specify **units** in numerical answers — examiners award partial credit for correct units.
- ✅ For diagrams in the exam, use **block diagrams** (input → process → output) rather than network topology drawings for routing characteristic questions.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Routing in Packet-Switched Networks: Characteristics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Routing Strategies # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Routing** is the process of selecting a path for traffic in a network, or in multiple networks, between or among devices. A **routing strategy** (or routing algorithm) is the systematic set of rules and computations used by routers to determine the optimal path for forwarding data packets from a source to a destination across a packet-switched network. In the context of the **OSI Reference Model** and the **TCP/IP Architecture**, routing is a core function of the **Network Layer (Layer 3)**. The router — a Layer 3 device — uses a routing strategy to maintain a **Routing Table** that maps destination networks to outgoing interfaces and next-hop addresses. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic is foundational to Module 4, where students are expected to understand the difference between *forwarding* (local action on a packet) and *routing* (network-wide path computation), along with classification of routing algorithms and the working of classical algorithms such as **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF)** and the **Bellman-Ford Distance Vector** method. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Highway Navigator Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Delhi** for the first time. Before you start, you have two options: 1. **Static Approach (Non-Adaptive):** You memorize a single fixed route (e.g., via NH44) printed on a paper map. If there is a landslide on that highway, you are stranded — your "routing table" is hard-coded. 2. **Dynamic Approach (Adaptive):** You turn on a **GPS navigator (Google Maps)**. It constantly receives live traffic updates from other vehicles, recomputes the shortest time-path in real time, and reroutes you dynamically if congestion occurs. A **router** behaves exactly like the GPS navigator. Each router in a network is like a city intersection; the **routing algorithm** is the logic that decides which intersection (neighbor router) the packet should be sent to next, in order to reach the destination efficiently. | Term | Real-World Analogy | |---|---| | Packet | A letter being delivered | | Router | A post office that sorts mail | | Routing Table | A postal directory listing destinations and outgoing trucks | | Routing Algorithm | The decision rule used by the postmaster to pick the best truck | | Link Cost | Toll, distance, or traffic on a road segment | ## 1.3 Routing vs. Forwarding — The Critical Distinction | Aspect | Routing | Forwarding | |---|---|---| | **Scope** | Network-wide (control plane) | Per-packet, per-router (data plane) | | **Frequency** | Computed periodically / on topology change | Performed on every arriving packet | | **Output** | Updates the routing table | Moves packet from input to output port | | **Time Scale** | Seconds to minutes | Microseconds to nanoseconds | > [!TIP] > **Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain forwarding vs routing," KTU examiners expect students to clearly state that **routing = building the map**, while **forwarding = following the map**. ## 1.4 Why Routing Strategies Matter In modern networks, no single router knows the entire topology. Routing strategies allow distributed decision-making so that the network: - **Adapts** to failures (link down, router crash). - **Scales** to millions of nodes (hierarchical routing). - **Optimizes** for metrics like **delay**, **hop count**, **bandwidth**, or **cost**. - **Avoids congestion** by distributing load across multiple paths. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Routing Strategies Routing strategies are classified along **two orthogonal axes**: ### Axis 1: Centralization of Information - **Centralized Routing:** One central controller (e.g., Software-Defined Networking controller) computes paths for the whole network. Modern variant: **SDN Routing**. - **Distributed Routing:** Each router computes its own routing table by exchanging information with neighbors. Classical algorithms. ### Axis 2: Adaptivity - **Non-Adaptive (Static):** Routes are computed offline and loaded into routers. No response to traffic or topology changes. - **Adaptive (Dynamic):** Routes change in response to topology changes, traffic load, or both. ### Adaptive Sub-Classification | Sub-Type | Trigger | Information Used | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Locally Adaptive** | Local queue length | Local | Backpressure routing | | **Centrally Adaptive** | Global state from one node | Central controller | SDN | | **Distributed Adaptive** | Neighbor exchange | Neighbor info | Distance Vector, Link State | ## 2.2 Key Routing Strategies — Operational Logic ### 2.2.1 The Shortest Path Problem (Foundation) Every routing strategy ultimately reduces to finding a **shortest path** in a graph $G = (V, E)$ where: - $V$ = routers (vertices) - $E$ = links (edges) - Each edge has a **weight (cost)** $c(i,j) \geq 0$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Insight:** The word "shortest" is a *misnomer*. The path is not necessarily shortest in physical distance; it is the path that **minimizes a chosen cost metric** (delay, hops, dollars, congestion, etc.). The cost of path $P = (i_0, i_1, \ldots, i_p)$ is: $$C(P) = \sum_{k=0}^{p-1} c(i_k, i_{k+1})$$ The **Shortest Path** between source $s$ and destination $t$ is: $$D(s,t) = \min_{P \in \mathcal{P}_{s,t}} C(P)$$ where $\mathcal{P}_{s,t}$ is the set of all valid paths from $s$ to $t$. ### 2.2.2 Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) Algorithm **Why it works:** Dijkstra's algorithm solves the *single-source shortest path* problem for graphs with **non-negative edge weights**. It is a **greedy algorithm** that builds the shortest-path tree incrementally. **Operational Steps:** 1. Initialize the distance to source $D(s) = 0$ and $D(v) = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$. 2. Mark all nodes as **unvisited**. Create a set $S$ of visited nodes, initially empty. 3. While unvisited nodes remain: - Pick the unvisited node $u$ with the **smallest tentative distance** $D(u)$. - Add $u$ to $S$. - For each unvisited neighbor $v$ of $u$, perform **relaxation**: $$D(v) = \min\left(D(v),\; D(u) + c(u,v)\right)$$ 4. When all nodes are in $S$, the algorithm terminates. **Time Complexity:** $O(V^2)$ for the naive implementation; $O((V+E)\log V)$ with a min-heap (Fibonacci heap gives $O(E + V\log V)$). ### 2.2.3 Bellman-Ford Algorithm (Foundation of Distance Vector) **Why it works:** It uses **dynamic programming**. It solves shortest path even when **negative edge weights** exist (no negative cycles) and is the basis for the **Distance Vector Routing Protocol** (e.g., RIP). **Bellman-Ford Equation (Fundamental of Distributed Routing):** $$D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left[ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \right]$$ where: - $D_x(y)$ = current best estimate of distance from node $x$ to destination $y$ - $c(x,v)$ = cost of direct link from $x$ to neighbor $v$ - $N(x)$ = set of $x$'s neighbors - $D_v(y)$ = neighbor $v$'s current best distance to $y$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** This single equation is worth 3–4 marks in KTU ESE. Memorize the notation $D_x(y)$ and the minimization structure. **Operational Steps:** 1. Initialize: $D_x(y) = c(x,y)$ if direct neighbor, else $\infty$. 2. Repeat for $V-1$ iterations: each node sends its distance vector to neighbors and updates using the Bellman-Ford equation. 3. Detect negative cycles (if any update still occurs in iteration $V$, a negative cycle exists). **Time Complexity:** $O(V \cdot E)$. ### 2.2.4 Flooding **Concept:** Every incoming packet is sent out on **every outgoing link** except the one it arrived on. - **Pure Flooding:** Inefficient — generates $O(\deg^{N})$ copies. - **Controlled Flooding:** Use **sequence numbers** or **hop counters** to prune duplicates. > [!CAUTION] > Flooding is **guaranteed** to find the shortest path in a static network (because the first packet to arrive has taken the minimum-hop route), but it is **terrible** for scalability. Used in **military / emergency networks** and historically in **ARP** within broadcast domains. ### 2.2.5 Distance Vector Routing (RIP-style) - Each router maintains a vector $D = [D(x,A), D(x,B), \ldots]$ for all destinations. - **Periodic exchange** (every 30 s in RIP) with **directly connected** neighbors only. - Prone to **Count-to-Infinity Problem** — solved by **split horizon** and **poison reverse** heuristics. ### 2.2.6 Link State Routing (OSPF-style) - Each router **floods Link State Packets (LSPs)** describing its direct neighbors to **all** routers. - Every router builds an **identical Link State Database (LSDB)**. - Each router independently runs **Dijkstra's algorithm** on the LSDB to compute its routing table. - **Advantages:** Fast convergence, no count-to-infinity. **Disadvantages:** Memory and CPU intensive. ### 2.2.7 Hierarchical Routing Used when the network is too large to maintain a flat routing table (e.g., the Internet). - Routers are grouped into **Autonomous Systems (AS)**. - Within an AS: **Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP)** — OSPF, RIP. - Between ASes: **Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP)** — BGP. **Optimal Number of Levels:** For a network with $N$ routers grouped into clusters of $k$ routers each: $$k \approx \sqrt{N}$$ This minimizes the total table size: $$\text{Table Size} = (N/k) + (k - 1)$$ ### 2.2.8 Broadcast Routing - **Flooding** (uncontrolled, wasteful). - **Multi-Destination Routing:** Each packet contains a list of destinations. - **Spanning Tree:** Send packet only along edges of a spanning tree — exactly one copy per link. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > The following table contains every formula, definition, and bound a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Validity / Boundary | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Path cost | $C(P) = \sum_{k=0}^{p-1} c(i_k, i_{k+1})$ | Scalar (metric unit) | Any non-negative weight graph | | 2 | Shortest path | $D(s,t) = \min_{P} C(P)$ | Scalar | Unique if all weights strictly positive | | 3 | Bellman-Ford equation | $D_x(y) = \min_v \{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ | Scalar | All finite positive weights | | 4 | Dijkstra relaxation | $D(v) \leftarrow \min(D(v), D(u) + c(u,v))$ | Scalar | **Non-negative** weights only | | 5 | Dijkstra complexity | $O((V+E)\log V)$ with min-heap | Operations | Non-negative weights | | 6 | Bellman-Ford complexity | $O(VE)$ | Operations | Tolerates negative weights | | 7 | Flooding copies (pure) | $N_{\text{copies}} \leq \sum_{i=0}^{H} d^i$ where $d$ = avg degree, $H$ = hops | Count | Exponential — uncontrollable | | 8 | Hierarchical table size | $T = (N/k) + (k-1)$ | Entries | Optimal $k = \sqrt{N}$ | | 9 | Hop limit (TTL) | Decremented at each router; packet dropped at 0 | Integer | Prevents infinite loops | | 10 | Routing metric types | Hop count, delay, bandwidth, load, reliability, cost | Mixed | Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) uses weighted sum | | 11 | Count-to-Infinity bound | Converges in at most $\infty$ without split horizon; $\leq$ hop count with poison reverse | Hops | Distributed DV without loop prevention | | 12 | LSDB size (Link State) | Sum of all LSPs = $\Theta(E)$ | Packets | Grows with topology | | 13 | Convergence time (DV) | $O(\text{diameter})$ iterations | Iterations | Slow on large networks | | 14 | Convergence time (LS) | $O(\log V)$ flooding + Dijkstra | Iterations | Fast, scales well | | 15 | Spanning tree edges | Exactly $V - 1$ edges for connected graph | Edges | Broadcast routing optimum | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Routing Strategy Used | |---|---| | Internet backbone (BGP) | Path Vector (hierarchical + policy) | | Enterprise LAN (OSPF) | Link State (Dijkstra) | | Small office (RIP) | Distance Vector (Bellman-Ford) | | Military / disaster networks | Flooding with TTL | | Data center fabrics (VXLAN-EVPN) | BGP + centralized controller (SDN) | | Wireless sensor networks | Hierarchical / cluster-based (LEACH) | | Satellite / DTN | Store-carry-forward (epidemic routing) | > [!NOTE] > Modern hybrid approaches combine classical algorithms with **machine learning** (e.g., Google B4 uses reinforcement learning on top of link-state-style forwarding). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Dijkstra's Algorithm (Single-Source Shortest Path) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** Given the network below, use Dijkstra's algorithm to compute the shortest path from node **A** to all other nodes. > > **Link costs:** A–B = 2, A–D = 1, B–C = 3, B–D = 5, C–D = 2, C–E = 4, D–E = 6. ### Step 1 — Initialize the Table Create a table with columns: *Visited (V), Node, D(A), D(B), D(C), D(D), D(E), Predecessor*. Set $D(A) = 0$ (source) and $D(\text{all others}) = \infty$. | V | Node | D(A) | D(B) | D(C) | D(D) | D(E) | Pred | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | – | Start | 0 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | – | ### Step 2 — Visit Node A (smallest tentative distance = 0) Relax neighbors of A: B and D. - For B: $D(B) = \min(\infty, 0 + 2) = 2$, Pred(B) = A. - For D: $D(D) = \min(\infty, 0 + 1) = 1$, Pred(D) = A. | V | Node | D(A) | D(B) | D(C) | D(D) | D(E) | Pred | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | A | 0 | 2 | $\infty$ | 1 | $\infty$ | – | ### Step 3 — Visit Node D (smallest unvisited = 1) Relax neighbors of D: A (already visited), B, C, E. - B: $D(B) = \min(2, 1 + 5) = 2$ (no change). - C: $D(C) = \min(\infty, 1 + 2) = 3$, Pred(C) = D. - E: $D(E) = \min(\infty, 1 + 6) = 7$, Pred(E) = D. | V | Node | D(A) | D(B) | D(C) | D(D) | D(E) | Pred | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | A, D | D | 0 | 2 | 3 | 1 | 7 | – | ### Step 4 — Visit Node B (smallest unvisited = 2) Relax neighbors of B: A, D (both visited), C. - C: $D(C) = \min(3, 2 + 3) = 3$ (no change, equal). | V | Node | D(A) | D(B) | D(C) | D(D) | D(E) | Pred | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | A,D,B | B | 0 | 2 | 3 | 1 | 7 | – | ### Step 5 — Visit Node C (smallest unvisited = 3) Relax neighbors of C: B, D (both visited), E. - E: $D(E) = \min(7, 3 + 4) = 7$ (no change, equal). ### Step 6 — Visit Node E (smallest unvisited = 7) No unvisited neighbors. Algorithm terminates. ### Final Shortest Paths from A | Destination | Distance | Path (via predecessors) | |---|---|---| | A | 0 | A | | B | 2 | A → B | | C | 3 | A → D → C | | D | 1 | A → D | | E | 7 | A → D → C → E | > [!TIP] > **Verification by inspection:** A → D → C → E gives cost $1 + 2 + 4 = 7$. Any other path (e.g., A → B → C → E gives $2 + 3 + 4 = 9$) is more expensive. ✓ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Bellman-Ford / Distance Vector Update > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** Router **X** has three neighbors **A, B, C** with link costs $c(X,A)=2$, $c(X,B)=5$, $c(X,C)=1$. The current distance vectors received from neighbors (to destination **Z**) are: > $D_A(Z) = 3$, $D_B(Z) = 1$, $D_C(Z) = 7$. > Compute the new distance from X to Z. ### Step 1 — List all candidate paths For each neighbor $v$ of X, compute the cost to reach Z through $v$: $$\text{Path via A: } c(X,A) + D_A(Z) = 2 + 3 = 5$$ $$\text{Path via B: } c(X,B) + D_B(Z) = 5 + 1 = 6$$ $$\text{Path via C: } c(X,C) + D_C(Z) = 1 + 7 = 8$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Bellman-Ford Equation $$D_X(Z) = \min_v \{c(X,v) + D_v(Z)\}$$ $$D_X(Z) = \min\{5, 6, 8\} = 5$$ ### Step 3 — Identify the next hop The minimum is achieved via neighbor **A**. So X will route packets to Z through A with total cost **5**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state **(a)** the minimization formula, **(b)** the computation for *each* candidate neighbor, and **(c)** the chosen next hop. Skipping (c) loses 1–2 marks. ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Hierarchical Routing Table Size > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A network has $N = 100$ routers. Using hierarchical routing with $k$ routers per cluster, derive the formula for the table size and find the optimal $k$. ### Step 1 — Derive the Table Size Formula Each router stores: - **1 entry** for each of the other $(k-1)$ routers in its own cluster. - **1 entry** for each of the $(N/k)$ clusters (treating all routers in another cluster as one destination). $$T(k) = (k - 1) + \frac{N}{k}$$ ### Step 2 — Minimize T(k) by Calculus $$\frac{dT}{dk} = 1 - \frac{N}{k^2} = 0$$ $$k^2 = N \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \sqrt{N}$$ ### Step 3 — Plug in N = 100 $$k = \sqrt{100} = 10$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Optimal Table Size $$T_{\min} = (10 - 1) + \frac{100}{10} = 9 + 10 = 19 \text{ entries}$$ Compare to flat routing: $T_{\text{flat}} = N - 1 = 99$ entries. **Reduction:** $\frac{99 - 19}{99} \approx 80.8\%$. > [!TIP] > KTU examiners love this "show derivation + compute value" pattern. Set up $T(k)$, take derivative, solve, plug numbers, interpret. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Distance Vector vs. Link State | Property | Distance Vector (RIP) | Link State (OSPF) | |---|---|---| | **Information shared** | Whole routing table with neighbors | LSPs with all routers | | **Scope of exchange** | Direct neighbors only | Flooded network-wide | | **Algorithm used at node** | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra | | **Convergence speed** | Slow (count-to-infinity) | Fast | | **Loop formation** | Yes (count-to-infinity) | Rare; LSPs are sequenced | | **CPU & Memory** | Low | High (maintains full LSDB) | | **Bandwidth use** | Low per exchange, frequent | High during flood, rare | | **Scalability** | Limited (max 15 hops in RIP) | Good (used in large ISPs) | | **Robustness** | Slow to detect failures | Fast detection via hello packets | | **Standard examples** | RIP, IGRP | OSPF, IS-IS | > [!CAUTION] > In KTU 14-mark questions, students often **confuse** DV and LS. The single most distinguishing feature is: **DV uses Bellman-Ford; LS uses Dijkstra on a globally synchronized LSDB.** ## 3.5 Convergence Property — Count-to-Infinity Consider a 3-router line: A — B — C. A and C are connected via B. Initially: $D_A(C) = 2$, $D_B(C) = 1$, $D_C(C) = 0$. **Failure:** Link B–C goes down. B updates $D_B(C) = \infty$ (after timeout). But before timeout expires, A might still advertise $D_A(C) = 2$. B then "thinks" A has a path of cost 2 to C, so B updates $D_B(C) = 1 + 2 = 3$. A hears B's $D_B(C) = 3$ and updates $D_A(C) = 1 + 3 = 4$.
