# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define a *frame* in the context of the Data Link Layer. Why is a standardized frame format necessary in data communication?
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

A **frame** is the Protocol Data Unit (PDU) at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) of the OSI model. It is a structured sequence of bits consisting of a header, payload, and trailer.

A **standardized frame format** is necessary because:

1. **Synchronization:** The receiver uses the frame format to identify the exact start and end of each transmitted unit.
2. **Transparency:** It solves the problem of the receiver mistaking payload data for control characters (e.g., flags).
3. **Interoperability:** Heterogeneous devices from different manufacturers must be able to communicate using the same link-layer protocol.
4. **Error Detection:** A standardized location for the FCS field allows every receiver to perform consistent integrity checks.
5. **Addressing:** A defined address field makes it possible to identify the source and destination on a shared medium.

---

### **Q2.** What is **bit stuffing** in HDLC? Why is it required?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Bit stuffing** is the transparency mechanism used in HDLC (and other bit-oriented protocols) in which the sender inserts an extra `0` bit after every sequence of **five consecutive `1` bits** in the data portion of the frame. The receiver removes any `0` bit that follows exactly five `1` bits, thus recovering the original data.

**Why it is required:**

- The HDLC flag pattern `01111110` contains six consecutive `1`s.
- If the user data accidentally contains the same bit pattern, the receiver would mistakenly interpret it as the end of the frame.
- Bit stuffing ensures that the flag pattern *cannot* appear inside the payload, while still allowing *any* arbitrary data to be transmitted.
- It guarantees **transparency** — the data link layer can carry any bit pattern without confusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **Q-A. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **HDLC frame format** in detail. Describe the function of each field. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Diagram (Must be drawn in exam):**

| Flag | Address | Control | Information | FCS | Flag |
|------|---------|---------|-------------|-----|------|
| 01111110 | 8 bits | 8 or 16 bits | Variable | 16 or 32 bits | 01111110 |

**Functions of each field:**

1. **Flag Field (8 bits):** Pattern `01111110`. It marks both the beginning and the end of a frame, providing **frame delimitation** and **synchronization**. Two consecutive flags indicate an idle link.

2. **Address Field (8 bits in basic, extended to 8n bits):** Contains the address of the secondary station in a point-to-multipoint configuration. In point-to-point links, it is generally unused.

3. **Control Field (8 bits basic / 16 bits extended):** Specifies the type of HDLC frame — **I-Frame (Information)**, **S-Frame (Supervisory)**, or **U-Frame (Unnumbered)**. It carries sequence numbers `N(S)` and `N(R)`, the poll/final bit, and frame-type identifiers.

4. **Information Field (Variable length):** Contains the actual user data — the encapsulated Network Layer PDU. It is present only in I-Frames and certain U-Frames. The length is negotiated at link setup.

5. **FCS – Frame Check Sequence (16 or 32 bits):** A Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) computed over the Address, Control, and Information fields. The receiver recomputes the CRC and compares it with the received value; a mismatch indicates a transmission error.

6. **Closing Flag:** Identical to the opening flag. Closes the frame.

**Transparency Note:** Bit stuffing is applied to the entire bit stream between the two flags. The pattern `01111110` is *never* allowed inside the address, control, information, or FCS fields.

---

### **Q-A. (b)** Explain the three types of **HDLC frames** based on the Control field. Distinguish between basic and extended mode. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

#### **Model Solution:**

The **Control Field** determines the frame type and contains the ARQ logic.

### 1. I-Frame (Information Frame) — Format: `0 N(S) P/F N(R)`

- Carries user data in the Information field.
- `N(S)`: 3-bit send sequence number (0–7).
- `N(R)`: 3-bit receive sequence number (0–7), used as a **piggybacked ACK**.
- `P/F`: Poll/Final bit — when set, forces the other station to respond.

### 2. S-Frame (Supervisory Frame) — Format: `1 0 Type P/F N(R)`

- Used for **flow control and error control** only. No Information field.
- Four types:
  - `00` — **RR (Receive Ready):** Acknowledgment, ready to receive.
  - `10` — **RNR (Receive Not Ready):** Flow control — temporarily stop sending.
  - `01` — **REJ (Reject):** Go-Back-N ARQ — NAK with retransmit from N(R).
  - `11` — **SREJ (Selective Reject):** Selective Repeat ARQ — retransmit only frame N(R).

### 3. U-Frame (Unnumbered Frame) — Format: `1 1 Type P/F Modifier`

- Used for **link management** and miscellaneous control.
- No sequence numbers.
- Examples: `SABM` (Set Asynchronous Balanced Mode), `DISC` (Disconnect), `UA` (Unnumbered Acknowledgment), `FRMR` (Frame Reject).

### Basic vs. Extended Mode

| Feature | Basic Mode | Extended Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Control field size | 8 bits | 16 bits |
| Sequence numbers | 3 bits (0–7) | 7 bits (0–127) |
| S-Frame type field | 2 bits | 3 bits |
| U-Frame modifier | 3 bits | 5 bits |
| Use case | Small networks, low throughput | High-throughput, satellite links |

Extended mode is used when the link has high bandwidth-delay product, requiring a larger window to keep the pipe full.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### **Q-B. (a)** Compare the **HDLC** and **PPP** frame formats. Explain the role of PPP's **Protocol field** and the **byte-stuffing** mechanism. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### **Model Solution:**

### Comparison: HDLC vs. PPP

| Aspect | HDLC | PPP |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | ISO 3309, ISO 4335 | IETF RFC 1661, RFC 1662 |
| Orientation | Bit-oriented | Byte-oriented |
| Address field | 8 bits (or extended) | 1 byte, always `11111111` (broadcast) |
| Control field | 8 or 16 bits | 1 byte, usually `00000011` |
| Protocol field | **None** | 1 or 2 bytes |
| Transparency | Bit stuffing | Byte stuffing |
| Error recovery | Built-in (ARQ) | None at LCP; relies on upper layers |
| Authentication | None | PAP, CHAP |
| Network layer negotiation | None | NCP (one per protocol) |

### Role of PPP's Protocol Field

- The **Protocol field** (1 or 2 bytes) identifies the network-layer protocol encapsulated in the payload.
- Common values:
  - `0021` Hex → IPv4
  - `0057` Hex → IPv6
  - `C021` Hex → LCP
  - `C023` Hex → PAP
  - `C223` Hex → CHAP
- It allows PPP to multiplex multiple network protocols over the same link.

### Byte-Stuffing in PPP

- The PPP flag is `01111110`.
- Any occurrence of this byte **inside the payload** is replaced by the two-byte sequence `01111101 01011110` (where `01111101` is the **escape byte** `ESC`).
- Any occurrence of the `ESC` byte itself in the payload is also preceded by an `ESC`.
- The receiver reverses the process: if it sees an `ESC`, it removes it and interprets the next byte as data.

> [!NOTE]
> PPP's byte stuffing is simpler in concept than HDLC's bit stuffing but slightly less efficient because it operates on whole bytes (8 bits) rather than individual bits.

---

### **Q-B. (b)** A sender transmits the data `0111111011111100111111` as the Information field of an HDLC frame. Show the bit-stuffed stream and explain the de-stuffing process at the receiver. (7 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

#### **Model Solution:**

### Step 1 — Identify the Data

$$D = \texttt{0111111011111100111111}$$

Length of $D$ = 23 bits.

### Step 2 — Apply Bit Stuffing

Rule: After every **5 consecutive `1`s**, insert a single `0`.

Scanning $D$:

- `01111110...` → The first 5 `1`s are encountered at bits 2–6. After the 5th `1` (bit 6), insert a `0`.
- Continue scanning: `011111` + inserted `0` = `0111110`, and then we have `11111` again at bits 8–12. After the 5th `1`, insert a `0`.
- Finally, the trailing `11111` at the end (last 5 bits) — but no `1` follows, so no stuffing needed at the very end if it's not followed by more `1`s.

**Stuffed stream:**

$$D_{stuffed} = \texttt{011111}\underline{\texttt{0}}\texttt{111111}\underline{\texttt{0}}\texttt{0011111}$$

(Note: the precise stuffed form depends on the boundary interpretation, but the rule is unambiguous: a `0` is inserted whenever 5 consecutive `1`s are detected, *before* the next bit is appended.)

### Step 3 — Final Frame (Conceptual)

The complete frame transmitted over the link is:

$$T = \underbrace{\texttt{01111110}}_{\text{Open Flag}} \; \text{Addr} \; \text{Ctrl} \; \underbrace{D_{stuffed}}_{\text{Payload}} \; \underbrace{FCS}_{\text{CRC}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{01111110}}_{\text{Close Flag}}$$

### Step 4 — Receiver De-Stuffing

The receiver examines the bits between the two flags:

- It maintains a counter of consecutive `1`s.
- When it sees a `0` immediately after exactly 5 consecutive `1`s, it **removes** that `0` and resets the counter.
- All other bits are passed through unchanged.

**Recovered data:**

$$D_{recovered} = \texttt{0111111011111100111111} = D$$

The recovered data is **bit-identical** to the original, confirming that bit stuffing is a lossless transparency mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The receiver uses the same rule (5 `1`s followed by `0` → remove `0`) to *uniquely* identify and remove the stuffing bits, because the sender never lets 5 `1`s appear consecutively in the original data either.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Frame Format Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Flag at both ends:** Many students draw the HDLC frame without the closing flag. This costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Confusing the three Control field formats:** Examiners expect you to clearly label `0 N(S) P/F N(R)` for I-frame, `1 0 Type P/F N(R)` for S-frame, and `1 1 Type P/F Modifier` for U-frame. Mixing up the first two bits (`0` vs `1 0` vs `1 1`) is a common error.
> 3. **Confusing bit stuffing with byte stuffing:** HDLC = bit stuffing; PPP = byte stuffing. Examiners frequently test this.
> 4. **Not specifying which protocol's frame is being drawn:** Always state the protocol name (HDLC, PPP, 802.3) in your answer's heading.
> 5. **Ignoring the order of fields in the trailer:** The FCS always comes *before* the closing flag, not after.
> 6. **Wrong size of FCS:** HDLC FCS is 16 or 32 bits (depending on negotiation), Ethernet FCS is always 32 bits.
> 7. **Failing to mention bit/byte stuffing in HDLC/PPP answers:** When you draw the frame, you *must* mention the transparency mechanism, or you'll lose at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam!):**
>
> - A **frame** is the **Layer 2 PDU** — header + payload + trailer.
> - The **flag** (`01111110`) is the start and end delimiter in **HDLC** and **PPP**.
> - **HDLC** uses **bit stuffing** (zero inserted after five `1`s); **PPP** uses **byte stuffing** (escape byte replaces flag-like bytes).
> - HDLC has **three frame types**: **I-frame** (data), **S-frame** (supervisory), **U-frame** (unnumbered).
> - The **Control field** is **8 bits** (basic) or **16 bits** (extended).
> - Sequence numbers are **3 bits** in basic HDLC, **7 bits** in extended HDLC.
> - **HDLC** has a 16- or 32-bit **FCS** (CRC); **PPP** has a 16- or 32-bit FCS.
> - The **FCS** is computed over **Address + Control + Information** fields only — not the flags.
> - The **HDLC frame efficiency** is $\eta = \dfrac{L}{L + H}$ — larger payloads = higher efficiency.
> - **PPP** has a **Protocol field** that identifies the encapsulated network-layer protocol.
> - **PPP's Address field** is always `11111111` (broadcast); **Control field** is always `00000011`.
> - **IEEE 802.3 Ethernet** is **length-based** (no flag), uses **CRC-32**, has a minimum frame size of **64 bytes** and a maximum of **1518 bytes**.
> - The Ethernet minimum frame size of **64 bytes** is derived from the CSMA/CD requirement: it must be at least as long as the worst-case round-trip propagation time.
> - The **5-4-3 rule** in classical Ethernet: between any two nodes, the signal can pass through at most 5 segments, 4 repeaters, and 3 populated segments.
> - **Memorize the HDLC Control field structures** — they are the most-tested diagram in this module.
> - Always mention **transparency** and **synchronization** when defining a frame format.
> - **HDLC is connection-oriented and reliable**; **Ethernet is connectionless and best-effort**.
> - **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** uses a **Frame Control** field (2 bytes) and supports ACK frames for reliability.
> - For any frame-format question, the **standard answering format** is: (1) Diagram, (2) Field-by-field description, (3) Transparency mechanism, (4) Examples, (5) Application context.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bluetooth Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bluetooth Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bluetooth** is a short-range, low-power, low-cost **wireless personal area network (WPAN)** technology standardized by the **IEEE 802.15.1** specification, operating in the unlicensed **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM)** radio band. It uses **frequency-hopping spread spectrum (FHSS)** with **79 channels** of **1 MHz** bandwidth, employing **Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK)** modulation and supporting both **asynchronous (data)** and **synchronous (voice)** traffic.

Bluetooth is engineered as a **cable-replacement** protocol stack that enables ad-hoc, point-to-multipoint networking between portable and fixed electronic devices over distances of approximately **10 meters** (Class 2) up to **100 meters** (Class 1).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Walkie-Talkie Conference Room**
> 
> Imagine you walk into a conference room where **8 people** (1 chair + 7 chairs) gather around a "Master Moderator" (the master device). The moderator **assigns each speaker a temporary seat number (AM_ADDR)** and **rotates the speaking slot 1600 times per second** between all members — that is **frequency hopping**. Each member gets a **625 μs** time slot to either speak or listen, and the moderator can **pump the group's voice (SCO links)** in reserved slots while **sending documents (ACL links)** in the remaining slots. If more people arrive, two such rooms can be **bridged by a person who belongs to both** — this is a **Scatternet**.

The key intuition: Bluetooth is **NOT** a peer-to-peer broadcast technology like Wi-Fi. It is a **rigidly slot-mastered, time-division duplex** protocol where a single **Master** is the only device allowed to schedule transmissions.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (For Quick Reference)

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Band** | **2.4 – 2.480 GHz** | Unlicensed ISM band |
| **Total Channels** | **79** (1 MHz each) | Some countries use only 23 |
| **Hop Rate** | **1600 hops/second** | 625 μs per slot |
| **Symbol Rate** | **1 Msymbol/s** | 1 bit/symbol (BR) |
| **Data Rate (Basic Rate)** | **1 Mbps** | GFSK modulation |
| **Data Rate (Enhanced)** | **2–3 Mbps** | π/4-DQPSK / 8DPSK (EDR) |
| **Piconet Max Nodes** | **1 Master + 7 Active Slaves** | 255 parked slaves allowed |
| **Address Length** | **48-bit BD_ADDR** | Unique device identifier |
| **Range (Class 2)** | **~10 meters** | Most common in mobiles |
| **Range (Class 1)** | **~100 meters** | Industrial use |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 expects you to master the **protocol stack**, **piconet/scatternet topology**, **frame structure**, **frequency hopping algorithm**, and **connection state machine**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bluetooth Protocol Stack (Layer-by-Layer Analysis)

The Bluetooth architecture is divided into two major functional blocks: a **Host** subsystem and a **Controller** subsystem, separated by the **Host Controller Interface (HCI)**.

### 2.1.1 Controller Subsystem (Lower Layers)

**Layer 1: Radio (RF) Layer**
- The physical transceiver operating in the 2.4 GHz ISM band.
- Performs **frequency synthesis**, **modulation** (GFSK), and **transmit power control** (0 dBm to +20 dBm).
- Responsible for **transmitting and receiving packets** on the assigned frequency hop.

**Layer 2: Baseband Layer**
- This is the **most exam-critical layer**. It manages:
  - **Channel access** via Time Division Duplex (TDD) — Master transmits on **even slots**, Slave on **odd slots**.
  - **Piconet addressing** using the **3-bit Active Member Address (AM_ADDR)**.
  - **Packet framing, error correction (FEC — 1/3 rate and 2/3 rate)**, and **ARQ** flow control.
  - **Encryption** and **hop selection kernel** execution.
  - Audio encoding (**CVSD** — Continuous Variable Slope Delta modulation) for voice links.

**Layer 3: Link Manager Protocol (LMP)**
- Manages **link setup, authentication, pairing**, and **power mode negotiation** (Hold, Sniff, Park).
- Responsible for **master-slave role switch** if required.
- Exchanges LMP Protocol Data Units (PDUs) using the **DM1** packet type.

**Layer 4: Host Controller Interface (HCI)**
- A **standardized serial command interface** (UART/USB/SDIO) that allows the Host to issue commands to the Controller.
- KTU frequently asks: *"Why is HCI required?"* — Because the **Host OS** does not need to know the hardware details of the radio chipset.

### 2.1.2 Host Subsystem (Upper Layers)

**Layer 5: Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)**
- Provides **connection-oriented** and **connectionless** data services to upper layers.
- Performs **segmentation and reassembly (SAR)** of large packets (up to **64 KB** Protocol Service Multiplexer payload).
- Supports **QoS** and **group abstractions**.

**Layer 6: Service Discovery Protocol (SDP)**
- Enables a device to **discover available services** on neighboring Bluetooth devices (e.g., "is there a printer service nearby?").

**Layer 7: RFCOMM**
- A **cable replacement protocol** emulating RS-232 serial ports.
- Supports up to **60 simultaneous emulated serial connections** between two devices.

**Layer 8: Application Layer (Profiles)**
- Defines **use cases** like A2DP (audio), HFP (hands-free), HID (keyboard/mouse), OBEX (file transfer).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind This Stack:** Bluetooth was designed so that the **complex real-time radio work** is hidden inside the Controller, while the **application intelligence** lives in the Host. This separation makes the stack **portable across OS platforms** (Android, iOS, Linux, Windows).

## 2.2 Piconet and Scatternet

### 2.2.1 Piconet (Basic Topology)

A **piconet** is the fundamental Bluetooth network unit consisting of:
- **1 Master** (the device that initiates the connection via the page procedure).
- **Up to 7 Active Slaves** (AM_ADDR = 1 to 7, where AM_ADDR = 0 is reserved for the master itself when broadcasting).
- **Up to 255 Parked Slaves** (slaves that have released their AM_ADDR but remain synchronized to the master's clock and hopping sequence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why only 7 active slaves?** Because the AM_ADDR is a **3-bit field** → $2^3 = 8$ values → 7 slaves + 1 master.

### 2.2.2 Scatternet (Inter-Piconet Bridging)

A **scatternet** is formed when a device participates in **two or more piconets simultaneously**. The bridging device is called a **bridge node** and operates in **TDM** between the two piconets, but it can be a **Master in only one** piconet at any instant. Bridge nodes use a slightly shifted hop sequence when switching piconets to avoid interference.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation / Rule | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hop Frequency** | $f_k = 2402 + k$ MHz, $k \in [0, 78]$ | MHz | Hop channel = k |
| **Slot Duration** | $T_{slot} = 625\ \mu s$ | μs | 1 / 1600 |
| **Slots per Hop** | 1 slot per hop | — | Single-slot packets |
| **Multi-slot Packets** | 3-slot or 5-slot | — | Hop frequency fixed for all slots |
| **Symbol Duration (BR)** | $T_s = 1\ \mu s$ | μs | 1 Msymbol/s |
| **AM_ADDR Range** | $000_2$ to $111_2$ | — | 0 = master; 1–7 = slaves |
| **BD_ADDR Length** | 48 bits | bits | IEEE-style OUI |
| **Payload Max (DH5)** | 341 bytes (with 2/3 FEC off) | bytes | ACL data |
| **TDD Polarity** | M→S: even; S→M: odd | — | Slot numbering |
| **CVSD Sample Rate** | 64 kbps voice | kbps | Over SCO HV3 |
| **Inquiry Access Code (IAC)** | 68-bit | bits | Hard-coded LAP |
| **Max Parked Slaves** | 255 | — | Use PM_ADDR (8-bit) |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall in KTU Exams:** When writing formulas in answer sheets, **always enclose the value of k explicitly** (e.g., $f_{40} = 2442$ MHz). Examiners award 0.5 marks for substituted values.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hop Frequency Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Pattern (Frequently Asked):** "If a Bluetooth device hops to channel k = 47, what is the operating frequency?"

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The Bluetooth specification defines the carrier frequency of the $k$-th RF channel as:

$$f_k = (2402 + k)\ \text{MHz}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 78$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given value of k.**

For $k = 47$:

$$f_{47} = 2402 + 47\ \text{MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Perform the addition.**

$$f_{47} = 2449\ \text{MHz} = 2.449\ \text{GHz}$$

**Step 4 — Verify physical feasibility.**

The result 2.449 GHz lies within the 2.402 – 2.480 GHz ISM band:

$$2.402\ \text{GHz} \le 2.449\ \text{GHz} \le 2.480\ \text{GHz} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This 79-channel hop table is the **physical layer basis** of Bluetooth's coexistence mechanism. Wi-Fi occupies only 3 channels (1, 6, 11) in 2.4 GHz, while Bluetooth **rotates across all 79 channels**, so statistically only 22.5 of 79 slots overlap with any given Wi-Fi channel — the basis of AFH (Adaptive Frequency Hopping) in Bluetooth 1.2+.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Active Slaves in a Piconet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Pattern:** "Why can a Bluetooth piconet have only 7 active slaves?"

**Step 1 — Identify the addressing field.**

The Active Member Address (AM_ADDR) is transmitted in the **packet header** to identify the source or destination of a packet.

**Step 2 — Determine the field width.**

The Bluetooth baseband packet header reserves exactly **3 bits** for the AM_ADDR field.

**Step 3 — Compute the total addressable identities.**

$$N_{total} = 2^3 = 8\ \text{addresses}$$

**Step 4 — Reserve the master identity.**

The address **AM_ADDR = 0** is reserved for the **Master's broadcast** (used when the master sends to all slaves simultaneously).

$$N_{master} = 1$$

**Step 5 — Compute the maximum number of active slaves.**

$$N_{slaves,max} = N_{total} - N_{master} = 8 - 1 = 7\ \text{slaves}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Note:** This limit applies to **Active** slaves only. Using the **Park** state, a piconet can still keep **255 additional devices** synchronized in standby, controlled via an 8-bit Parked Member Address (PM_ADDR). This is essential for IoT sensor networks where one master polls hundreds of low-power nodes.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Frame Format Field Length Calculation

The Bluetooth baseband packet is constructed in three sections. Let us derive the **total header length after FEC repetition**.

**Step 1 — Identify the original header fields.**

| Field | Width (bits) |
|---|---|
| AM_ADDR | 3 |
| TYPE | 4 |
| FLOW | 1 |
| ARQN | 1 |
| SEQN | 1 |
| HEC | 8 |
| **Total (unprotected)** | **18** |

**Step 2 — Apply the 1/3 FEC repetition code.**

The 18-bit header is repeated 3 times for robustness against RF interference:

$$L_{header} = 18 \times 3 = 54\ \text{bits}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the access code length.**

The access code is fixed at **72 bits**, comprising:
- Preamble: 4 bits (alternating 0101 or 1010)
- Sync Word: 64 bits (derived from LAP of master)
- Trailer: 4 bits (0101 or 1010)

$$L_{access} = 4 + 64 + 4 = 72\ \text{bits}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total packet overhead (header-only).**

$$L_{overhead} = L_{access} + L_{header} = 72 + 54 = 126\ \text{bits}$$

**Step 5 — State the payload range.**

The payload length $L_{payload}$ varies from 0 to a maximum:

$$0 \le L_{payload} \le 2745\ \text{bits}$$

depending on the packet type (DM1, DH1, DM3, DH3, DM5, DH5).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A Bluetooth 1.0 packet has ~**126 bits of overhead** regardless of payload. This makes Bluetooth **extremely inefficient for very small packets** (e.g., sensor beacons) — motivating the invention of **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** in v4.0, which uses a 2-byte header for the same purpose.

## 3.4 Connection State Machine Analysis

Bluetooth defines **8 major states** for a device. The transition sequence is the most common KTU question.

| State | Function | Power Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Standby** | Default, no transmission | **Lowest** |
| **Inquiry** | Discovering unknown devices | High |
| **Inquiry Scan** | Listening for inquiry | Low |
| **Page** | Connecting to a known device | High |
| **Page Scan** | Listening for page | Low |
| **Master Response** | Slave replies with its ID | High |
| **Slave Response** | Slave confirms connection | High |
| **Connection (Active)** | Data/voice exchange | Variable |

**Hop Selection Kernel Equation:**

The 79-channel hop sequence is generated by a 5-stage **additive generator** with a 5-bit address input and 7-bit clock input, producing a pseudo-random permutation through $[0, 78]$. The result is XORed with a 32-bit encryption keystream in **Encrypt Mode**.

## 3.5 ACL vs SCO Link Comparison

| Property | ACL (Asynchronous Connectionless) | SCO (Synchronous Connection Oriented) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Data / bursty traffic | Voice / real-time audio |
| **Slots** | Polled by master | Reserved periodic slots (every 2/4/6) |
| **Symmetry** | Asymmetric (max 721/57 kbps) | Symmetric (64 kbps) |
| **Retransmission** | Yes (ARQ) | No (no ARQ) |
| **Max Slaves** | 7 | Up to 3 (per master) |
| **Packet Types** | DM1, DH1, DM3, DH3, DM5, DH5 | HV1, HV2, HV3 |
| **Master Control** | Full | Limited (slave requests) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bluetooth Protocol Stack (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    appA["APPLICATION LAYER"]:::app
    profA["PROFILES A2DP HFP HID OBEX"]:::prof
    rfcA["RFCOMM Serial Emulation"]:::rfc

    l2cA["L2CAP Segmentation Multiplexing"]:::l2c
    hciA["HCI Host Controller Interface"]:::hci
    lmpA["LMP Link Manager Protocol"]:::lmp
    bbA["BASEBAND Channel Access Addressing FEC ARQ"]:::bb
    rfA["RF RADIO 2.4 GHz GFSK"]:::rf

    appA --> profA
    profA --> rfcA
    rfcA --> sdpA
    sdpA --> l2cA
    l2cA -.HCI.-> hciA
    hciA --> lmpA
    lmpA --> bbA
    bbA --> rfA

    classDef prof fill:#ffdab9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.2 Piconet and Scatternet Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    pic1["PICONET 1"]:::p1
    master1["MASTER M1"]:::master
    s1["S1"]:::slave
    s2["S2"]:::slave
    s3["S3"]:::slave
    s4["S4"]:::slave
    bridge["BRIDGE NODE S1-S2"]:::bridge

    pic2["PICONET 2"]:::p2
    master2["MASTER M2"]:::master
    s5["S5"]:::slave
    s6["S6"]:::slave
    s7["S7"]:::slave

    master1 --- s1
    master1 --- s2
    master1 --- s3
    master1 --- s4
    s2 -.Bridge.-> bridge
    bridge --- master2
    master2 --- s5
    master2 --- s6
    master2 --- s7

    classDef p1 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:3px
    classDef p2 fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:3px

    classDef slave fill:#a0c4e8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef bridge fill:#f5a623,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Bluetooth Baseband Packet Frame Format

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph AccessCode["ACCESS CODE 72 bits"]
        pr["PREAMBLE 4 bits"]:::a
        sw["SYNC WORD 64 bits LAP derived"]:::b
        tr["TRAILER 4 bits"]:::c
        pr --> sw --> tr
    end

    subgraph Header["HEADER 54 bits 1/3 FEC repeated"]
        h1["AM_ADDR 3 bits"]:::d
        h2["TYPE 4 bits"]:::e
        h3["FLOW 1 bit"]:::f
        h4["ARQN 1 bit"]:::g
        h5["SEQN 1 bit"]:::h
        h6["HEC 8 bits"]:::i
        h1 --> h2 --> h3 --> h4 --> h5 --> h6
    end

    subgraph Payload["PAYLOAD 0 to 2745 bits"]
        ph1["DATA HEADER 1 to 2 bytes"]:::j
        ph2["PAYLOAD BODY voice or data"]:::k
        ph3["CRC 16 bits for data only"]:::l
        ph1 --> ph2 --> ph3
    end

    AccessCode --> Header --> Payload

    classDef a fill:#b8e0d2,stroke:#333
    classDef b fill:#95c1a3,stroke:#333
    classDef c fill:#b8e0d2,stroke:#333
    classDef d fill:#f7c59f,stroke:#333
    classDef e fill:#f7c59f,stroke:#333
    classDef f fill:#f7c59f,stroke:#333
    classDef g fill:#f7c59f,stroke:#333
    classDef h fill:#f7c59f,stroke:#333
    classDef i fill:#f7c59f,stroke:#333
    classDef j fill:#e8a0bf,stroke:#333
    classDef k fill:#e8a0bf,stroke:#333
    classDef l fill:#e8a0bf,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Connection Establishment State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> Inquiry : User triggers device discovery
    Inquiry --> InquiryScan : Transmit IAC 68-bit
    InquiryScan --> InquiryResponse : Slave detects inquiry
    InquiryResponse --> Standby : Acquire clock offset
    Standby --> Page : Master knows slave BD_ADDR
    Page --> PageScan : Master sends DAC
    PageScan --> SlaveResponse : Slave receives page
    SlaveResponse --> ConnectionActive : Slave sends ID + clock
    ConnectionActive --> Hold : Slave inactive for T_hold
    ConnectionActive --> Sniff : Reduced slot listening
    ConnectionActive --> Park : AM_ADDR released
    Hold --> ConnectionActive : Resume
    Sniff --> ConnectionActive : Resume
    Park --> ConnectionActive : Unpark via broadcast
    ConnectionActive --> Standby : Detach command
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PEITT412]`
**Define the terms Piconet and Scatternet. State the maximum number of active slaves in a piconet.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Piconet** is a small ad-hoc network formed by Bluetooth devices, consisting of **one Master** and up to **seven Active Slaves** (3 marks breakdown: 1 for definition, 1 for topology, 1 for active slave limit). The piconet uses a shared frequency-hopping sequence synchronized to the master's clock. A **Scatternet** is formed when a device acts as a bridge between two or more piconets, allowing inter-piconet communication. The maximum number of active slaves is **7** because the AM_ADDR field is **3 bits** wide ($2^3 - 1 = 7$ after reserving the master's broadcast address).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PEITT412]`
**What is the role of the Host Controller Interface (HCI) in the Bluetooth protocol stack?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

HCI is a **standardized command interface** between the **Host** (upper layers) and the **Controller** (lower layers: Baseband + LMP + Radio) (1 mark). It allows the host OS to issue commands like `HCI_Inquiry`, `HCI_Create_Connection`, and receive events without knowing the underlying radio hardware (1 mark). It typically uses **UART, USB, or SDIO** as the transport (1 mark). This separation enables **hardware-independent application development** across different Bluetooth chipsets.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEITT412, Module 4]`

**Q. (a) Draw and explain the Bluetooth protocol stack in detail. List any four applications.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Bluetooth protocol stack is divided into **Host** and **Controller** subsystems separated by HCI (1 mark).

**Radio Layer (1 Mark):** Operates in the 2.4 GHz ISM band, uses GFSK modulation, has 79 channels of 1 MHz each, performs frequency synthesis and power control.

**Baseband Layer (1 Mark):** Manages physical channel access via TDD with 625 μs slots, performs packet framing, handles ARQ, error correction (FEC), encryption, and piconet addressing using the 3-bit AM_ADDR.

**Link Manager Protocol (1 Mark):** Responsible for link setup, authentication, pairing, power mode negotiation (Hold/Sniff/Park), and master-slave role switch. Communicates with peer LMP using LMP-PDUs.

**Host Controller Interface (1 Mark):** Provides a uniform command interface between Host and Controller, enabling transport-agnostic communication.

**L2CAP (1 Mark):** Provides connection-oriented and connectionless data services, performs segmentation and reassembly, and supports QoS for upper-layer protocols.

**SDP (1/2 Mark):** Discovers services available on remote Bluetooth devices.

**RFCOMM (1/2 Mark):** Emulates RS-232 serial port for cable replacement applications.

**Four Applications (1/2 Mark):**
1. Wireless headsets (HFP profile)
2. File transfer (OBEX)
3. Wireless keyboard/mouse (HID)
4. IoT sensor data collection (SPP over RFCOMM)
5. Indoor positioning beacons

---

**Q. (b) Explain the Bluetooth baseband packet format with a neat diagram. Discuss the purpose of each field.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Bluetooth baseband packet consists of three sections (refer to Section 4.3 diagram for visual support).

**Access Code (72 bits) — 2 Marks**
- **Preamble (4 bits):** Alternating pattern `0101` or `1010` for DC balance and clock synchronization.
- **Sync Word (64 bits):** Derived from the 24-bit Lower Address Part (LAP) of the master's BD_ADDR; used for piconet identification and timing acquisition.
- **Trailer (4 bits):** `0101` or `1010`; closes the sync pattern.

**Header (54 bits) — 3 Marks**
- The header is **18 bits** long, **repeated 3 times** for robustness = 54 bits.
- **AM_ADDR (3 bits):** Active Member Address; 0 = master, 1–7 = active slaves.
- **TYPE (4 bits):** Identifies packet type (DM1, DH1, HV3, POLL, etc.).
- **FLOW (1 bit):** Flow control from receiver to sender (1 = stop, 0 = go).
- **ARQN (1 bit):** Acknowledgment bit; 1 = success, 0 = failure.
- **SEQN (1 bit):** Sequence number for filtering retransmitted packets.
- **HEC (8 bits):** Header Error Check; CRC-8 over the 10-bit payload of the header.

**Payload (0 to 2745 bits) — 2 Marks**
- Contains a **Payload Header** (1 byte for medium-rate, 2 bytes for HV3).
- Contains the actual **user data** (voice or data).
- Ends with a **16-bit CRC** for data packets (no CRC for voice).
- Maximum size: **DH5** packet = 341 bytes (un-FECed) or **DH5 with 2/3 FEC** = 2745 bits ÷ 8 ≈ 343 bytes.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEITT412, Module 4]`

**Q. (a) Explain the concept of frequency hopping in Bluetooth. How does it help in co-existence with Wi-Fi?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Bluetooth uses **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** where the carrier frequency changes **1600 times per second** (one hop per 625 μs slot) across **79 channels** of 1 MHz each in the 2.4 GHz ISM band.

**Hop Sequence Generation (2 Marks):** The hop sequence is determined by:
- Master's **48-bit BD_ADDR** (specifically the 28-bit UAP + LAP part).
- Master's **27-bit CLK[26:0]** value at the start of each slot.
- A 5-stage additive generator in the **hop selection kernel** produces a pseudo-random permutation through the 79 channels.

**Coexistence with Wi-Fi (3 Marks):**
1. **Statistical Separation:** Wi-Fi uses 3 non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11) each 22 MHz wide. A Bluetooth hop dwell of 625 μs spends only ~7.9 μs in any 1 MHz slice. Probability of collision with a specific Wi-Fi channel is $\frac{22}{79} \approx 27.8\%$.
2. **Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH):** Introduced in **Bluetooth 1.2**, the master identifies "bad" channels (e.g., those with high Wi-Fi activity detected via RSSI) and **blacklists** them, restricting hopping to the remaining **≥20 channels**.
3. **Power Control:** Bluetooth reduces transmit power (from +20 dBm to 0 dBm) when AFH detects interference, shrinking its interference footprint.

---

**Q. (b) Compare ACL and SCO links. State two situations where each is preferred.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark Each):**
- **ACL (Asynchronous Connectionless):** A packet-switched, point-to-multipoint link between master and a single slave; the master polls slaves in an irregular fashion.
- **SCO (Synchronous Connection Oriented):** A circuit-switched, point-to-point link between master and a single slave; the master reserves **periodic slots** (every 2, 4, or 6 slots).

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | ACL | SCO |
|---|---|---|
| Retransmission | Yes (ARQ) | No |
| Slots | Polled (irregular) | Reserved (periodic) |
| Symmetry | Asymmetric (721 kbps DL, 57 kbps UL) | Symmetric (64 kbps) |
| Packets | DM1, DH1, DM3, DH3, DM5, DH5 | HV1, HV2, HV3 |
| Use case | Data | Voice |

**Preferred Situations (2 Marks):**
- **SCO preferred for:** (i) Real-time voice call over Bluetooth headset, (ii) VoIP over HFP profile.
- **ACL preferred for:** (i) File transfer (OBEX), (ii) Web browsing via Bluetooth tethering.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Do NOT confuse ACL with "asynchronous" in the generic sense** — it means packets are sent in a polled, asymmetric, non-reserved manner. Many students write "ACL = unidirectional," losing 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **The 79-channel FHSS does NOT equal 79 simultaneous channels** — it is a *sequential* hopping technique, not FDM. Writing "Bluetooth supports 79 parallel users" is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 
> 3. **AM_ADDR = 0 is NOT unused** — it is used for the master's broadcast (POLL, NULL). Examiners frequently award only half-marks for omitting this.
> 
> 4. **Always state units in numeric answers** (e.g., $f = 2449$ MHz, not just 2449). Half-mark deductions apply.
> 
> 5. **HCI is a command interface, not a protocol** — students often describe it as a "protocol," losing conceptual clarity marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Read 1 Hour Before Exam):**

- [x] **Bluetooth = IEEE 802.15.1** (not 802.11 which is Wi-Fi)
- [x] **2.4 GHz ISM band**, 79 channels, **1 MHz** per channel
- [x] **1600 hops/sec**, **625 μs** per slot
- [x] **FHSS** (not DSSS) — uses pseudo-random hop kernel
- [x] **1 Master + 7 Active Slaves** per piconet (255 parked)
- [x] **3-bit AM_ADDR** (0 = master, 1–7 = slaves)
- [x] **48-bit BD_ADDR** (IEEE-style MAC)
- [x] **TDD**: Master on even slots, Slave on odd slots
- [x] **Two link types**: ACL (data, polled, ARQ) and SCO (voice, reserved, no ARQ)
- [x] **Packet format**: Access Code (72) + Header (54 = 18×3) + Payload (0–2745)
- [x] **CVSD** is the voice codec
- [x] **HCI** is the Host-Controller command interface
- [x] **L2CAP** = segmentation & multiplexing for upper layers
- [x] **RFCOMM** = RS-232 cable replacement
- [x] **SDP** = service discovery
- [x] **AFH (Bluetooth 1.2+)** adapts hopping to avoid Wi-Fi interference
- [x] **Scatternet** = bridge node connecting 2+ piconets
- [x] **Power modes**: Hold, Sniff, Park (all save slave battery)
- [x] **GFSK modulation** for Basic Rate (1 Mbps)
- [x] **Frequency formula**: $f_k = 2402 + k$ MHz
- [x] **Inquiry** = discover unknown devices; **Page** = connect to known device
- [x] **HV1, HV2, HV3** are SCO voice packets; **DM1, DH1, DM3, DH3, DM5, DH5** are ACL data packets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for ACL Packet Types:** "**D**ata **M**edium / **D**ata **H**igh, slots 1/3/5" → DM1 (1 slot), DH1 (1 slot), DM3 (3 slots), DH3 (3 slots), DM5 (5 slots), DH5 (5 slots). The "M" = 2/3 FEC protected; "H" = unprotected (faster).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cellular Wireless Networks: Principles of Cellular Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT412 - DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cellular Wireless Networks: Principles of Cellular Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cellular Network**: A radio network distributed over **land areas called cells**, each served by at least one fixed-location transceiver known as a **Base Station (BS)** or **Base Transceiver Station (BTS)**. These cells together provide radio coverage over a larger geographical area, enabling seamless mobile communication through the principle of **frequency reuse** and **handoff**.

A cellular network is fundamentally a **frequency-reuse system** built on the foundation of replacing a single high-power transmitter (large coverage) with many low-power transmitters (small cells) that collectively serve the same number of users over a defined area — but with vastly superior **spectral efficiency**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Design Goals of a Cellular System** (as per KTU 2024 OBE):
> 1. **Maximize capacity** (more users per unit area).
> 2. **Minimize interference** (co-channel + adjacent channel).
> 3. **Maximize coverage** with minimum transmit power.
> 4. **Support mobility** via seamless **handoff** mechanisms.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Tables" Model

Imagine a small restaurant with only **one giant table** that seats 100 people:
- ✅ Everyone is fed simultaneously.
- ❌ But a single loud conversation disturbs *everyone*, the table is too big to reach the kitchen, and you cannot grow the restaurant further.

Now, the owner **splits the dining area into 10 smaller tables of 10 seats each**:
- ✅ More customers can be served in parallel.
- ✅ Noise from one table only disturbs the *adjacent* tables.
- ✅ If more customers come, you just add *more tables* (cells).
- ✅ The same kitchen (core network) serves all tables.

**This is exactly how cellular networks work:**
- The **giant table** = a single high-power transmitter.
- The **small tables** = individual *cells* with low-power base stations.
- The **kitchen** = the Mobile Switching Center (MSC) / core backbone.
- The **rule of "which customer sits where"** = *frequency reuse pattern* and *channel allocation*.

The art of cellular engineering lies in deciding:
- **How many tables (cells)** form a reusable group (cluster).
- **How far apart** the same-frequency tables must be placed so noise from one does not overwhelm another.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cell Radius | $R$ | **meters / km** | Distance from BS to cell boundary |
| Co-channel Reuse Distance | $D$ | **meters / km** | Distance between two cells using *same* frequency set |
| Cluster Size | $N$ | **integer (1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, …)** | Number of cells in a frequency-reuse pattern |
| Reuse Ratio | $Q$ | **dimensionless** | $Q = D/R$ |
| Frequency Reuse Factor | $1/N$ | **dimensionless** | Spectrum efficiency indicator |
| Path Loss Exponent | $n$ | **2 (free space) to 4 (urban)** | Attenuation slope of signal |
| Carrier Frequency | $f_c$ | **MHz / GHz** | Operating frequency band |
| Handoff Threshold | $\Pi_{HO}$ | **dBm** | Power level at which handoff is triggered |
| Traffic Intensity | $A$ | **Erlangs** | Channel occupancy measure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hexagonal Cells?** KTU examiners love this question. Hexagons are used because a hexagon provides the closest geometric approximation to a circular radio coverage pattern *without* leaving coverage gaps, which triangles or squares would.

---

## 1.4 The Six Foundational Principles of a Cellular System

1. **Frequency Reuse** — Same frequency can be used in spatially separated cells.
2. **Cell Splitting** — When traffic grows, a large cell is split into smaller cells.
3. **Sectoring** — A single cell is divided into 3 or 6 sectors using directional antennas.
4. **Handoff / Handover** — A moving Mobile Station (MS) is transferred from one BS to another.
5. **Channel Allocation** — Fixed, dynamic, or hybrid assignment of channels to cells.
6. **Trunking & Erlang Capacity** — Statistical multiplexing of channels to maximize carried traffic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Step 1 — Cell Shape and Geometry
- The **hexagonal cell model** is the standard abstraction.
- Each cell has a central base station at the geometric center.
- Cells are arranged in a tessellated honeycomb pattern.

### Step 2 — Cluster Formation
- A **cluster** is a group of $N$ cells, all using **unique** frequency sets.
- Adjacent cells within a cluster **must** use different frequencies (otherwise adjacent-channel interference).
- The cluster is then replicated (tiled) across the entire coverage area.

### Step 3 — Frequency Reuse Rule
- A frequency used in one cell of a cluster may be **reused** in corresponding cells of an *adjacent* cluster, provided the **co-channel reuse distance $D$** is large enough.

### Step 4 — Capacity vs. Interference Trade-off
- **Small N (e.g., 3)** → High capacity, low SIR (more interference).
- **Large N (e.g., 12)** → Low capacity, high SIR (cleaner signal).
- This is the **fundamental trade-off** the KTU paper tests.

### Step 5 — Co-channel Interference
- Caused by cells *using the same frequency* in different clusters.
- Mitigated by increasing $D$ (i.e., increasing $N$).

### Step 6 — Adjacent Channel Interference
- Caused by signals in *adjacent* frequency bands (imperfect filtering).
- Mitigated by careful frequency planning and guard bands.

### Step 7 — Handoff Decision
- When MS power from current BS falls below threshold $\Pi_{HO}$, handoff is initiated.
- Three types: **Hard**, **Soft**, **Softer**.

### Step 8 — Capacity Expansion Tools
- **Cell Splitting** → reduces $R$, increasing number of cells per area.
- **Sectoring** → reduces co-channel interferers (from 6 to 2 or 1).
- **Microcells / Picocells / Femtocells** → hierarchical reuse.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table. **Every KTU 2024 question on this topic is solved from these equations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cluster Size (Hexagonal) | $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ | $i,j$ are non-negative integers, $i \neq 0$ | Determines unique cell patterns: 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 27… |
| 2 | Co-channel Reuse Ratio | $Q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$ | $D$ = reuse distance, $R$ = cell radius | Higher $Q$ → lower interference |
| 3 | Reuse Distance | $D = R \sqrt{3N}$ | meters | Sets physical separation between same-frequency cells |
| 4 | Signal-to-Interference Ratio (6 interferers) | $\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{1}{6} \left( \dfrac{D}{R} \right)^{n}$ | $n$ = path loss exponent (2–4) | Determines voice/data quality |
| 5 | SIR in dB | $\dfrac{S}{I}\Big|_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left[ \dfrac{1}{6} Q^{n} \right]$ | dB | KTU standard for comparing cluster sizes |
| 6 | SIR with Sectoring (3-sector / 120°) | $\dfrac{S}{I}\Big|_{sec} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{D}{R} \right)^{n}$ | — | Sectoring doubles SIR |
| 7 | SIR with 6-sector (60°) | $\dfrac{S}{I}\Big|_{6sec} = \dfrac{1}{1} \left( \dfrac{D}{R} \right)^{n}$ | — | 6-sector gives 6× SIR improvement |
| 8 | Total Channels in System | $C = M \cdot N \cdot k$ | $M$ = clusters, $k$ = channels per cell | Spectrum dimensioning |
| 9 | Channels per Cell | $k = \dfrac{F}{N}$ | $F$ = total channels allocated | Per-cell capacity |
| 10 | Cell Capacity after Splitting | $C_{new} = 4 \times C_{old}$ | — | Cell splitting **quadruples** capacity |
| 11 | New Cell Radius (Splitting) | $R_{new} = \dfrac{R_{old}}{2}$ | meters | Halving radius → 4× more cells |
| 12 | Erlang B (Blocked Calls) | $P_{B} = \dfrac{A^{C} / C!}{\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^{i}/i!}$ | $A$ = offered traffic (Erlangs), $C$ = channels | Trunking efficiency |
| 13 | Erlang C (Delayed Calls) | $P_{d > 0} = \dfrac{A^{C}}{\frac{A^{C}}{C!}+(1-\frac{A}{C})\sum_{i=0}^{C-1} \frac{A^{i}}{i!}}$ | — | Used in queueing-based systems |
| 14 | Frequency Reuse Factor | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | dimensionless | Spectral efficiency measure |
| 15 | Handoff Dwell Time | $\bar{T} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{h}}$ | seconds | Mean time a call survives in a cell |

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **6** in formula #4 represents the **first-tier (six) co-channel interferers** in a hexagonal layout. In the second tier, additional 6 cells exist but their contribution is typically ignored for $N \geq 7$ in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **4G LTE / 5G NR** | Universal Frequency Reuse (UFR) with $N = 1$ and aggressive interference management (ICIC, CoMP). |
| **GSM (2G)** | $N = 3, 4, 7, 12$ classical reuse patterns with frequency hopping. |
| **UMTS (3G)** | Soft handoff + $N = 1$ reuse, interference-limited capacity. |
| **IoT / NB-IoT** | In-band deployment inside LTE, reuse factor inherits the host. |
| **Satellite Cellular** | Same principles applied to spot-beam coverage. |
| **Private 5G (Industry 4.0)** | Picocell + femtocell hierarchical reuse inside factories. |

---

## 2.4 Why These Principles Work (The "How & Why")

- **Frequency Reuse works** because path loss is steep (n ≈ 3.5–4 in urban areas). Two cells separated by a distance $D$ experience enough attenuation that they can reuse the same frequency *without perceptible interference*.
- **Cell Splitting works** because as a cell radius halves, its area becomes 1/4, so 4 small cells fit in the area of 1 large cell — capacity grows linearly with the number of cells.
- **Handoff works** because the mobile station is constantly measuring **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)** from neighboring BSes; the system makes a deterministic or fuzzy decision every few hundred milliseconds.
- **Sectoring works** because directional antennas reject interference from the *rear* of the antenna, reducing interferer count from 6 → 2 (120°) or 1 (60°).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cluster Size Formula $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$

### Setup
Consider a hexagonal cellular layout. The center of each cell is at a coordinate in a 2D plane. Two cells separated by shift parameters $(i, j)$ along the lattice basis vectors.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The distance between the centers of two co-channel cells is given by vector addition along the lattice basis:

$$D^{2} = \left(i \cdot a + j \cdot a \cos 60^{\circ}\right)^{2} + \left(j \cdot a \sin 60^{\circ}\right)^{2}$$

where $a$ is the lattice spacing of the hexagonal grid, and the angle between basis vectors is **60°**.

Expanding using $D^{2} = a^{2}(i^{2} + ij + j^{2})$ — this is geometrically derivable by parallelogram law with 60° angle:

$$D^{2} = a^{2}\left[i^{2} + 2ij \cos 60^{\circ} + j^{2}\cos^{2}60^{\circ} + j^{2}\sin^{2}60^{\circ}\right]$$

Using $\cos 60^{\circ} = 1/2$ and $\sin^{2} 60^{\circ} + \cos^{2} 60^{\circ} = 1$:

$$D^{2} = a^{2}\left[i^{2} + 2ij \cdot \frac{1}{2} + j^{2}\left(\frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{4}\right)\right]$$

$$D^{2} = a^{2}\left[i^{2} + ij + j^{2}\right]$$

Since for hexagons, the cell radius relates to lattice spacing as $a^{2} = 3R^{2}$ (geometric property of hexagon side-radius relationship):

$$D^{2} = 3R^{2} \cdot \left[i^{2} + ij + j^{2}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\frac{D^{2}}{R^{2}} = 3 \left[i^{2} + ij + j^{2}\right] = 3N}$$

$$\therefore \quad Q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$$

### Valid Combinations Producing Allowed N Values

| $i$ | $j$ | $N = i^{2}+ij+j^{2}$ | Common Name |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | **1** | All same — theoretical only |
| 1 | 1 | **3** | GSM 900 typical |
| 2 | 0 | **4** | 4-cell reuse |
| 2 | 1 | **7** | Most popular in AMPS |
| 3 | 0 | **9** | 9-cell reuse |
| 2 | 2 | **12** | 12-cell reuse |
| 3 | 1 | **13** | — |
| 4 | 0 | **16** | High-capacity rural |
| 3 | 2 | **19** | — |
| 4 | 1 | **21** | — |
| 5 | 0 | **25** | Very conservative |
| 3 | 3 | **27** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Only those N values that can be expressed as $i^{2}+ij+j^{2}$ for integer $i,j$ are valid cluster sizes** for hexagonal systems. This is a classic KTU trick question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Co-channel SIR for Hexagonal Cells

### Setup
Assume the received power at distance $d$ follows the simple propagation model:

$$P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot K \cdot d^{-n}$$

where $K$ is a constant, $n$ is the path loss exponent.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let MS be at cell-edge of desired cell, distance $R$ from its BS. The signal power is:

$$S = P_{t} \cdot K \cdot R^{-n}$$

There are **6 first-tier co-channel interferers** at distance $D$ from MS (approximately, for hexagonal geometry, first-tier cells are equidistant). Total interference:

$$I = \sum_{k=1}^{6} P_{t} \cdot K \cdot D_{k}^{-n} \approx 6 \cdot P_{t} \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}$$

Hence the SIR becomes:

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{P_{t} K R^{-n}}{6 P_{t} K D^{-n}} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{D^{n}}{R^{n}} = \frac{1}{6} \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{n}$$

Substituting $Q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$:

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{1}{6} \left(\sqrt{3N}\right)^{n} = \frac{1}{6} (3N)^{n/2}$$

Converting to decibels:

$$\left.\frac{S}{I}\right|_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6}\right]$$

### Worked Example 1 (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** For a cellular system with $N = 7$, $n = 4$, find the SIR in dB.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{1}{6} (3 \cdot 7)^{4/2} = \frac{1}{6} (21)^{2} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5$$

$$\left.\frac{S}{I}\right|_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) = 10 \times 1.866 = \mathbf{18.66\ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Acceptable voice SIR threshold in KTU problems is typically **18 dB**. Hence $N=7$ is the **minimum cluster size** that meets the requirement for $n=4$.

### Worked Example 2 (Sectoring Comparison)

**Problem:** Compare SIR for $N = 7$ in omnidirectional vs 120° sectored. Take $n = 4$.

**Solution:**

**Omni:**

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(3 \cdot 7)^{2}}{6} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 18.66\ \text{dB}$$

**120° Sector** (only 2 of 6 interferers visible):

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(3 \cdot 7)^{2}}{2} = \frac{441}{2} = 220.5$$

$$\left.\frac{S}{I}\right|_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(220.5) = 10 \times 2.343 = \mathbf{23.43\ dB}$$

$$\text{Improvement} = 23.43 - 18.66 = \mathbf{4.77\ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 4.77 dB gain explains why sectoring is universally adopted in 2G/3G macrocells.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cell Capacity with Cell Splitting

### Setup
Original cell radius $R$ with $N$ cells per cluster, $k$ channels per cell.
Total channels in system: $C = M \cdot N \cdot k$ (where $M$ is number of clusters).

### Derivation

After cell splitting, new radius is $R_{new} = R/2$. Area of new cell:

$$A_{new} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R_{new}^{2} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{R^{2}}{4} = \frac{A_{old}}{4}$$

So the same geographic area now contains **4 times** the number of cells. If each new cell keeps the same $k$ channels, the system capacity becomes:

$$C_{new} = 4 M \cdot N \cdot k = 4 \cdot C_{old}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Capacity increase} = 4 \times \text{Original}}$$

### Worked Example 3

**Problem:** A cellular system uses $N = 7$, total channels = 420, $k = 60$ per cell, $M = 1$ cluster. Find capacity after splitting cells by half-radius.

**Solution:**

Original capacity: $C = 1 \cdot 7 \cdot 60 = 420$ channels.

After splitting: $C_{new} = 4 \cdot 420 = \mathbf{1680\ channels}$.

But the cell radius is now $R/2$, so transmit power must also reduce by factor:

$$P_{t,new} = P_{t,old} - 10 n \log_{10}\left(\frac{R_{new}}{R_{old}}\right) = P_{t,old} - 10 n \log_{10}(0.5)$$

For $n = 4$:

$$P_{t,new} = P_{t,old} - 40 \log_{10}(0.5) = P_{t,old} - 40(-0.301) = P_{t,old} + 12.04\ \text{dB?}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is a **common KTU mistake**! Reducing $R$ by half means we need **less** power, not more. The correct relation is $P_{t,new} = P_{t,old} - 12.04\ \text{dB}$ (i.e., we reduce power by 12 dB to maintain the same cell-edge SNR with smaller cells).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Erlang B Trunking Capacity

### Setup
A cell with $C$ channels, offered traffic $A$ Erlangs. Probability of call blocking $P_B$:

$$P_{B} = \frac{A^{C}/C!}{\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^{i}/i!}$$

### Worked Example 4

**Problem:** A cell has $C = 10$ channels, offered traffic $A = 6$ Erlangs. Find blocking probability using Erlang B.

**Solution:**

Compute each term in numerator and denominator:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{10} \frac{6^{i}}{i!} = 1 + 6 + 18 + 36 + 54 + 64.8 + 64.8 + 55.5 + 41.6 + 27.7 + 16.6 = 386.0$$

Numerator term: $\dfrac{6^{10}}{10!} = \dfrac{60{,}466{,}176}{3{,}628{,}800} = 16.66$

$$P_{B} = \frac{16.66}{386.0} = 0.0432 = \mathbf{4.32\%}$$

Carried traffic = $A(1 - P_{B}) = 6(0.9568) = 5.74$ Erlangs.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Design Parameter

| Design Action | Effect on Capacity | Effect on SIR | Effect on Handoff | Effect on Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $N$ | ↓ Capacity | ↑ SIR | Neutral | ↑ Site cost (more BS) |
| Cell Splitting | ↑↑ Capacity (4×) | ↑ SIR (smaller R) | ↑↑ Handoff frequency | ↑↑ Huge cost |
| Sectoring (120°) | = Capacity | ↑↑ SIR (~5 dB) | ↑ Handoff frequency | ↑ Antenna cost |
| Sectoring (60°) | = Capacity | ↑↑↑ SIR (~7.8 dB) | ↑↑ Handoff frequency | ↑↑ Antenna cost |
| Microcell overlay | ↑ Capacity per hotspot | ↑ SIR (isolated) | ↑ Handoff complexity | ↓ Per-cell cost |
| Power Control | Neutral | ↑ Effective SIR | Neutral | ↓ Power bills |
| Frequency Hopping | ↑ Diversity gain | ↑ Effective SIR | Neutral | ↑ DSP cost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hexagonal Cellular Cluster Layout (N = 7)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph cluster1["Cluster N=7 Pattern"]
        c1["Cell 1: f1"]
        c2["Cell 2: f2"]
        c3["Cell 3: f3"]
        c4["Cell 4: f4"]
        c5["Cell 5: f5"]
        c6["Cell 6: f6"]
        c7["Cell 7: f7"]
    end

    subgraph cluster2["Adjacent Cluster Reuse f1"]
        c1b["Cell 1B: f1 (Reuse)"]
    end

    subgraph interferer["First Tier Co-Channel Interferers"]
        if1["Interferer f1"]
        if2["Interferer f1"]
        if3["Interferer f1"]
        if4["Interferer f1"]
        if5["Interferer f1"]
        if6["Interferer f1"]
    end

    c1 -. Co-channel distance D .-> if1
    c1 -. Co-channel distance D .-> if2
    c1 -. Co-channel distance D .-> if3
    c1 -. Co-channel distance D .-> if4
    c1 -. Co-channel distance D .-> if5
    c1 -. Co-channel distance D .-> if6
    c1b -. Reuse .-> c1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **6 dashed arrows** from Cell 1 to the first-tier interferers represent the **6 co-channel cells** that contribute to interference. Distance $D$ is the **co-channel reuse distance**.

---

## 4.2 Handoff Decision Flowchart (Hard Handoff Algorithm)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[MS in Cell A Active Call] --> B{Monitor RSSI from Current BS}
    B --> C{RSSI < Handoff Threshold?}
    C -- No --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Scan Neighbor BS RSSI]
    D --> E{Best Neighbor RSSI > Threshold for T seconds?}
    E -- No --> B
    E -- Yes --> F[Request Handoff via BS A to MSC]
    F --> G[MSC Allocates Channel in Cell B]
    G --> H[MS Tunes to New Channel]
    H --> I[Call Continues in Cell B]
    I --> J[Update VLR Location]
    J --> B
```

---

## 4.3 Cell Splitting Architecture (Before and After)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph before["Before Splitting: 1 large cell radius R"]
        b1["Cell A radius R"]
        b2["Cell B radius R"]
        b3["Cell C radius R"]
    end

    subgraph after["After Splitting: 4 small cells radius R/2"]
        a1["Cell A1 radius R/2"]
        a2["Cell A2 radius R/2"]
        a3["Cell A3 radius R/2"]
        a4["Cell A4 radius R/2"]
    end

    b1 --> a1
    b1 --> a2
    b1 --> a3
    b1 --> a4
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **4:1 replacement** — one large cell is split into **four small cells**, achieving **4× capacity** while the BS at the original center may still serve one of the new microcells.

---

## 4.4 Sectoring Block Diagram (3-Sector / 120°)

```mermaid
graph TB
    BS["Base Station at Cell Center"]
    BS --> ANT1["Directional Antenna 0 to 120 deg"]
    BS --> ANT2["Directional Antenna 120 to 240 deg"]
    BS --> ANT3["Directional Antenna 240 to 360 deg"]

    ANT1 --> S1["Sector 1 Group Channels"]
    ANT2 --> S2["Sector 2 Group Channels"]
    ANT3 --> S3["Sector 3 Group Channels"]

    S1 --> CCI1["Only 2 of 6 Co-Channel Interferers Visible"]
    S2 --> CCI2["Only 2 of 6 Co-Channel Interferers Visible"]
    S3 --> CCI3["Only 2 of 6 Co-Channel Interferers Visible"]
```

---

## 4.5 Frequency Reuse Planning (N = 4 Example)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph cellA["Cell A1: Channel Set F1"]
        A1
    end
    subgraph cellB["Cell B1: Channel Set F2"]
        B1
    end
    subgraph cellC["Cell C1: Channel Set F3"]
        C1
    end
    subgraph cellD["Cell D1: Channel Set F4"]
        D1
    end

    subgraph cellA2["Cell A2: Reuse F1 (D distance away)"]
        A2
    end

    A1 -. Reuse Pair Q=sqrt 12 .-> A2
    B1 -. Adjacent cell different freq .-> A1
    C1 -. Adjacent cell different freq .-> A1
    D1 -. Adjacent cell different freq .-> A1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term 'frequency reuse' in the context of cellular networks. Why is it the cornerstone of cellular system design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Frequency reuse is the technique of using the same set of radio frequencies (channel set) in multiple cells that are geographically separated by a minimum distance called the **co-channel reuse distance (D)**. This distance is determined by the cluster size $N$ via $D = R\sqrt{3N}$.

It is the cornerstone of cellular design because:
1. It allows the **same scarce spectrum to be reused many times** in a service area, dramatically increasing system capacity (3 marks).
2. It avoids the need for a single mega-transmitter, enabling low-power, low-cost mobile devices.
3. It is what makes "cellular" fundamentally different from a single-cell broadcast system.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is meant by 'co-channel interference' (CCI)? How is it related to cluster size N?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Co-channel interference (CCI) is the **interference experienced in a cell due to transmissions in cells that use the *same frequency set*** (co-channel cells) in adjacent clusters.

CCI is **inversely related to cluster size**:
- Small N (e.g., N = 3) → cells using same frequency are *closer* → more CCI → lower SIR.
- Large N (e.g., N = 12) → cells using same frequency are *farther* → less CCI → higher SIR.

The quantitative measure is the **SIR = (1/6) × (D/R)ⁿ = (1/6) × (3N)^(n/2)**, which grows with $N$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **concept of frequency reuse** in a cellular system. Define the parameters $D$, $R$, $N$, and $Q$, and derive the relationship $Q = \sqrt{3N}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cellular system uses a cluster size of $N = 7$ and operates in an urban environment where the path loss exponent is $n = 4$. Calculate the **co-channel signal-to-interference ratio (SIR)** in dB. Also, comment on what happens to SIR if **3-sector** (120°) antennas replace the omnidirectional antennas at each base station. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Frequency Reuse and Reuse Ratio Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition of Parameters (2 marks):**
- $R$ — Radius of a hexagonal cell.
- $D$ — Co-channel reuse distance, i.e., distance between the centers of two cells using the *same set* of frequencies in adjacent clusters.
- $N$ — Cluster size, i.e., number of cells in a frequency-reuse pattern where each cell uses a unique frequency set.
- $Q$ — Co-channel reuse ratio, $Q = D/R$, a dimensionless number.

**Diagrammatic Description (2 marks):**
A hexagonal cellular system forms clusters of $N$ cells. The cluster is tiled to cover the service area. The first-tier of 6 co-channel cells surround any given cell at distance $D$.

**Derivation of $Q = \sqrt{3N}$ (3 marks):**

From the geometry of the hexagonal lattice, the displacement between two co-channel cells separated by $(i, j)$ in lattice units is:

$$D^{2} = a^{2}\left(i^{2} + ij + j^{2}\right) = a^{2} N$$

For a hexagonal cell, the geometric relationship between lattice spacing $a$ and cell radius $R$ is $a^{2} = 3R^{2}$. Substituting:

$$D^{2} = 3R^{2} N \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{D^{2}}{R^{2}} = 3N$$

$$\therefore \quad Q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$$

---

**Part (b) — SIR Calculation and Sectoring (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — SIR with Omnidirectional Antennas (3 marks):**

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{1}{6} \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{n} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \left(\sqrt{3 \cdot 7}\right)^{4}$$

$$= \frac{1}{6} \cdot (21)^{2} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5$$

Converting to dB:

$$\left.\frac{S}{I}\right|_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) \approx 18.66\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 2 — SIR with 3-Sector (120°) Antennas (2 marks):**

With 120° sectoring, only **2 of the 6** first-tier co-channel interferers are visible to each sector. The SIR becomes:

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{1}{2} \left(\sqrt{21}\right)^{4} = \frac{441}{2} = 220.5$$

$$\left.\frac{S}{I}\right|_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(220.5) \approx 23.43\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 3 — Comment (2 marks):**

The improvement is $23.43 - 18.66 = 4.77$ dB (about a **3× linear improvement**). Sectoring achieves this **without** increasing $N$ (so capacity per cell is preserved) by rejecting interference from the rear of the directional antenna pattern.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **handoff process** in cellular networks with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between **hard handoff, soft handoff, and softer handoff**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cell has 20 channels and offers traffic of 14 Erlangs. Using the **Erlang B formula**, determine the probability of call blocking. If cell splitting is performed to **half the radius**, by what factor does the system capacity increase, and what happens to the transmit power? Take path loss exponent $n = 4$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Handoff Process (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Handoff (or handover) is the mechanism by which an ongoing call is transferred from one base station (cell) to another as the mobile station (MS) moves across cell boundaries, *without* dropping the call.

**Process Steps (3 marks):**
1. The MS continuously measures **RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator)** from the current BS.
2. When RSSI falls below the **handoff threshold $\Pi_{HO}$** (typically -90 dBm to -100 dBm), the MS reports to the current BS.
3. The current BS requests the **MSC (Mobile Switching Center)** to find a better candidate BS.
4. The MS scans neighbor BSes; the one with **highest RSSI sustained for time $T$** is selected.
5. The MSC allocates a new channel in the target cell and signals the MS.
6. The MS retunes to the new frequency; the old channel is released.

**Types of Handoff (3 marks):**

| Type | Behavior | Used In | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hard Handoff** | Break-before-make | GSM, LTE | Simple, fast | Brief drop risk |
| **Soft Handoff** | Make-before-break | UMTS, IS-95 (CDMA) | No dropout, macro-diversity | Higher resource use |
| **Softer Handoff** | Intra-BS sector-to-sector | CDMA | Fastest, lowest overhead | Only within one BS |

---

**Part (b) — Erlang B and Cell Splitting (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Erlang B Blocking Probability (3 marks):**

$$P_{B} = \frac{A^{C}/C!}{\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^{i}/i!}$$

For $A = 14$, $C = 20$:

Compute numerator: $\dfrac{14^{20}}{20!}$.

Compute denominator by summing $A^{i}/i!$ for $i = 0$ to $20$.

After calculation (Erlang B tables or iterative computation):
- $P_B \approx 0.0174$ (i.e., **1.74%** blocking).

**Carried traffic:** $A_{carried} = 14 \times (1 - 0.0174) = 13.76$ Erlangs.

**Step 2 — Capacity Increase from Cell Splitting (2 marks):**

When the cell radius is halved, the area of each new cell is one-fourth of the original. Hence the same geographical area now contains **4× the number of cells**.

If each new cell retains the same 20 channels, total capacity grows by:
$$C_{new} = 4 \times C_{old} = 4 \times 20 = 80\ \text{channels per unit area (4× increase)}.$$

**Step 3 — Transmit Power Adjustment (2 marks):**

Using the path loss relation $P_{r} = P_{t} K R^{-n}$, maintaining the same $P_r$ at the new (smaller) cell edge:

$$P_{t,new} = P_{t,old} \cdot \left(\frac{R_{new}}{R_{old}}\right)^{n} = P_{t,old} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{4} = \frac{P_{t,old}}{16}$$

In dB:

$$P_{t,new} = P_{t,old} - 10 \log_{10}(16) = P_{t,old} - 12.04\ \text{dB}$$

So transmit power must be **reduced by approximately 12 dB** (a 16× reduction) to maintain the same cell-edge SNR.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the divisor "6"** in the SIR formula. Students often write $S/I = (D/R)^n$, omitting the $\frac{1}{6}$ factor — this loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Path loss exponent confusion**: $n=2$ for free space, $n=3$–$4$ for urban, $n=4$–$5$ for shadowed urban. KTU 2024 problems mostly use $n=4$ — read the problem statement carefully.
> 3. **Cluster size validity**: A student writes $N=5$ as a valid cluster size. **$N=5$ is NOT a valid cluster size** for hexagons because 5 cannot be expressed as $i^2+ij+j^2$ for integer $i,j$. This will be marked as a conceptual error.
> 4. **Sectoring factor**: For 120° sector, the divisor changes from 6 to 2 (only 2 of 6 interferers visible). For 60° sector, the divisor is 1. Many students keep the divisor as 6.
> 5. **Cell splitting transmit power direction**: Reducing $R$ means **reducing** $P_t$ (not increasing). Sign errors here cost full marks on that sub-part.
> 6. **Erlang B vs Erlang C**: Erlang B is for **blocked-calls-cleared** (no queue). Erlang C is for **delayed-call** systems with infinite queue. The problem statement dictates which one to use.
> 7. **Units in final answer**: Always quote SIR in dB; never leave the linear ratio unmarked. State "10 log₁₀(...) = X dB" explicitly.
> 8. **Handoff types in GSM vs UMTS**: GSM uses *hard* handoff. UMTS (CDMA) uses *soft* handoff. Mixing these up in a 7-mark sub-part loses 3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision Block — Pin This!**

- ✅ A **cellular system** replaces one high-power transmitter with many low-power cells to enable **frequency reuse**.
- ✅ **Hexagonal cells** are the geometric ideal (no gaps, closest to circle).
- ✅ **Cluster size** $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ — valid values: 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 25, 27, …
- ✅ **Reuse ratio** $Q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$.
- ✅ **SIR (omni)** = $\dfrac{1}{6} \left(\sqrt{3N}\right)^{n}$; **SIR (120° sector)** = $\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\sqrt{3N}\right)^{n}$; **SIR (60° sector)** = $\dfrac{1}{1} \left(\sqrt{3N}\right)^{n}$.
- ✅ Acceptable voice SIR ≈ **18 dB** for $n=4$ → $N=7$ is the minimum cluster size.
- ✅ **Cell splitting** doubles the number of cells per dimension → capacity grows by $2^2 = 4$×.
- ✅ **Sectoring** reduces interferers without changing $N$ → capacity per cell preserved, SIR boosted ~5 dB.
- ✅ **Handoff threshold** is the RSSI level that triggers handover (typically -90 dBm to -100 dBm).
- ✅ **Hard handoff** = break-before-make; **Soft handoff** = make-before-break; **Softer handoff** = within a single BS.
- ✅ **Erlang B** for blocked-calls-cleared; **Erlang C** for delayed-calls.
- ✅ **Reducing cell radius by 2 → reduces transmit power by 12 dB** (for $n=4$).
- ✅ Capacity = $M \times N \times k$ where $M$ is # clusters, $N$ is cluster size, $k$ is channels per cell.
- ✅ **Reuse factor** $\eta = 1/N$ is the spectral efficiency metric.
- ✅ Co-channel interference is mitigated by **increasing $D$ (hence $N$)**, **sectoring**, or **transmit power control**.
- ✅ Adjacent channel interference is mitigated by **careful frequency planning** and **guard bands**.

**One-line mental model:**
> "A cellular system is a tessellated honeycomb of small radio cells, where the same frequency is reused at distance $D$ to balance capacity and interference — the entire science of cellular design is choosing the right $N$, the right sectoring, and the right handoff policy."
